topics.title: Understanding the Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Understanding the Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Supply Chain (SC)** is a *systematic, integrated network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources* that moves a product or service from **supplier** to **manufacturer** to **wholesaler** to **retailer** to **end customer**. According to the **Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP)** — the global benchmark body — the formal definition is:

> A supply chain encompasses every effort involved in producing and delivering a final product or service, from the supplier's supplier to the customer's customer.

**Supply Chain Management (SCM)** is the *strategic coordination and integration* of these flows to enhance the long-term performance of the individual companies and the supply chain as a whole.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly asks students to differentiate between a *Supply Chain* and a *Value Chain*, understand the **3 decision phases** (Strategic, Tactical, Operational), and map the **5 major flows** of a supply chain.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Think of a supply chain like **the journey of a cup of chai (tea)** from your kitchen table:

- **Farmer** → grows tea leaves in Munnar, Kerala 🌱
- **Processing Unit** → dries and packs the tea leaves
- **Distributor** → ships pallets to warehouses in Kochi
- **Wholesaler** → breaks bulk into smaller cartons
- **Retail Shop** near your house → stocks them on shelves
- **You** → the final customer who boils, strains, and drinks ☕

Each "station" in this journey is a separate company, but they are all **interconnected**. The leaves could not reach your cup if any link breaks. The money, the truck, the invoice, the temperature-controlled container, and even the *information* about your preferred brand — all flow alongside the tea leaves.

> [!NOTE] **The "Chain" Misconception**
> A modern supply chain is **not a linear chain** but a **complex network**. The word "chain" is a legacy metaphor. In reality, most products have *multiple suppliers* for raw materials, *multiple manufacturers*, and *multiple distribution channels* — making it a true **mesh network**.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** → typically **30–90 days** in manufacturing
- **Order Fulfillment Lead Time** → benchmarked at **24–48 hours** for e-commerce
- **Perfect Order Index (POI)** → industry benchmark **> 95%**
- **Inventory Turnover** → **6–12 times/year** in healthy FMCG firms
- **Bullwhip Effect** → demand variability amplification, often **3–5x** from retailer to manufacturer

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Supply Chain: The 5 Major Flows

Every supply chain moves **five distinct flows** simultaneously. KTU examiners frequently test these:

1. **Product/Goods Flow** — Physical movement of materials from upstream to downstream.
2. **Information Flow** — Bi-directional sharing of demand forecasts, order status, inventory levels.
3. **Financial Flow** — Payments, credit terms, invoices moving from customer to supplier.
4. **Risk Flow** — Disruption signals, contingencies, insurance, hedging.
5. **Value Flow** — *The most critical.* Each step must **add value** to justify its cost.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Value Addition Principle**
> A step is part of the supply chain **only if** it adds value (form, place, time, or possession utility). If a warehouse merely stores goods without adding time or place utility optimization, it is **non-value-adding** and is a candidate for elimination.

---

### 2.2 The 3 Decision Phases (KTU High-Yield Framework)

Supply chain decisions are made on **three time horizons**:

| Decision Phase | Time Horizon | Key Questions | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic / Supply Chain Strategy** | Years (3–10) | *Where* to locate facilities? What products? | Plant location, supplier partnerships |
| **Tactical / Planning** | Months (1–12) | *How* to plan inventory and production? | Demand forecasting, S&OP, procurement contracts |
| **Operational / Execution** | Days–Hours | *When* to execute specific tasks? | Order processing, warehousing, transportation scheduling |

> [!NOTE] **Decision Hierarchy**
> Strategic decisions **constrain** tactical decisions, which in turn **constrain** operational decisions. This is called the **Hierarchical Planning Principle** in KTU SCM textbooks.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Landed Cost** | $C_{total} = C_{purchase} + C_{transport} + C_{inventory} + C_{customs} + C_{risk}$ | All costs in ₹ / USD | True product cost sourcing decisions |
| **Inventory Turnover (IT)** | $IT = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}$ | Times per year | Measures supply chain efficiency |
| **Days of Inventory (DOI)** | $DOI = \dfrac{365}{IT}$ | Days | Benchmark for working capital |
| **Order Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = T_{order} + T_{process} + T_{produce} + T_{ship} + T_{deliver}$ | Hours / Days | Customer satisfaction metric |
| **Fill Rate** | $FR = \dfrac{\text{Orders Filled Completely}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Service level KPI |
| **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** | $C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO$ | Days | Liquidity & working capital |
| **Bullwhip Variance Ratio** | $VR = \dfrac{\text{Var(Demand seen by Manufacturer)}}{\text{Var(Customer Demand)}}$ | Dimensionless | Measures demand distortion |
| **Total Supply Chain Cost** | $TSCC = C_{procure} + C_{prod} + C_{inventory} + C_{transport} + C_{warehousing}$ | Monetary units | End-to-end cost optimization |
| **Service Level (Type 1)** | $SL = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Stockout Occurrences}}{\text{Total Demand Cycles}}$ | Probability (0 to 1) | Inventory policy design |
| **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = D_{avg} \times LT + Z \times \sigma_{LT}$ | Units | Safety stock calculations |

Where in ROP:
- $D_{avg}$ = Average daily demand (units/day)
- $LT$ = Lead time (days)
- $Z$ = Service level Z-score (e.g., 1.65 for 95%)
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of demand during lead time

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility

- **Manufacturing Engineering** → Plant layout & line balancing decisions
- **Industrial Engineering** → Inventory models (EOQ, MRP, JIT)
- **Civil Engineering** → Warehouse design, port infrastructure
- **Mechanical Engineering** → Material handling equipment, packaging design
- **Computer Science** → ERP systems, blockchain-based traceability, demand forecasting AI

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Process Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Inventory Turnover and Days of Inventory

The relationship between Inventory Turnover (IT) and Days of Inventory (DOI) is foundational. Let us derive it exhaustively.

Let $COGS$ = Cost of Goods Sold over one year.
Let $I_{avg}$ = Average Inventory Value held during the year.
Let $DOI$ = Days of Inventory (number of days inventory sits in the system).
Let $T$ = Total days in the period (365 for annual calculation).

**Step 1:** By definition, inventory turnover measures how many times the average inventory is "turned over" (sold and replenished) per period.

$$IT = \frac{COGS}{I_{avg}}$$

**Step 2:** If inventory turns over $IT$ times in $T$ days, then the average time for one full turn is $T / IT$ days. This is precisely the **Days of Inventory (DOI)**.

$$DOI = \frac{T}{IT}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the value of $IT$ from Step 1 into Step 2.

$$DOI = \frac{T}{\dfrac{COGS}{I_{avg}}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the compound fraction. Dividing by a fraction = multiplying by its reciprocal.

$$DOI = T \times \frac{I_{avg}}{COGS}$$

**Step 5:** For annual analysis, set $T = 365$ days.

$$DOI = 365 \times \frac{I_{avg}}{COGS}$$

**Step 6:** Combine with Step 1 to obtain the inverse relationship.

$$DOI = \frac{365}{IT}$$

> **Logic of Conversion:** We converted a *frequency* metric (turns per year) into a *time* metric (days per turn) by taking the reciprocal and scaling by the period length. This is universally valid in any time unit — just replace 365 with the relevant number of days/months.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Reorder Point (ROP) Decomposition

The Reorder Point is the inventory level at which a new order must be placed to avoid stockout during lead time.

**Step 1:** Define the demand rate $D$ in units per day.
**Step 2:** During lead time $LT$ (days), expected demand is:

$$D_{LT} = D \times LT$$

**Step 3:** To protect against demand variability, add a safety buffer:

$$ROP = D_{LT} + SS = (D \times LT) + (Z \times \sigma_{LT})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $D_{avg}$ for $D$ (long-run average demand).

$$ROP = D_{avg} \times LT + Z \times \sigma_{LT}$$

> **Logic:** The first term covers *expected* demand during lead time. The second term absorbs *unexpected* variation, scaled by the desired service level $Z$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Supply Chain Stages to Decision Phases

This **Decision-Stage Matrix** is a high-yield KTU framework. It maps every supply chain activity to the appropriate decision tier.

| Supply Chain Stage / Function | Strategic Decision | Tactical Decision | Operational Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sourcing** | Long-term supplier partnerships | Contract negotiation | Purchase order release |
| **Production** | Plant location, capacity | Master Production Schedule | Daily shop-floor scheduling |
| **Inventory** | Base-stock policy design | Safety stock tuning | Reorder point triggers |
| **Warehousing** | Number & location of DCs | Layout design | Pick-pack-ship execution |
| **Transportation** | Network design, mode selection | Carrier contracts | Daily route planning |
| **Information** | ERP platform selection | Forecast horizon design | Real-time order tracking |
| **Reverse Logistics** | Returns policy architecture | Returns processing center setup | Refund execution |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Cross-Mapping Insight**
> A **reorder point trigger** is an *operational* decision but is **constrained** by the *tactical* safety stock policy, which is **constrained** by the *strategic* service level target. KTU questions often test this **cascading constraint logic**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Supply Chain Network Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    scNode1["Raw Material Supplier"] --> scNode2["Manufacturer Producer"]
    scNode2 --> scNode3["Warehouse Distribution Center"]
    scNode3 --> scNode4["Wholesaler Bulk Distributor"]
    scNode4 --> scNode5["Retail Outlet Store"]
    scNode5 --> scNode6["End Customer Consumer"]
    scInfoFlow["Information Flow Forecasts Orders"] -.-> scNode2
    scInfoFlow -.-> scNode3
    scInfoFlow -.-> scNode5
    scNode6 -.-> scInfoFlow
    scFinFlow["Financial Flow Payments Invoices"] -.-> scNode5
    scNode5 -.-> scFinFlow
    scFinFlow -.-> scNode2
    scReturn["Reverse Logistics Returns Recycling"] -.-> scNode5
    scNode5 -.-> scReturn
    scReturn -.-> scNode2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = Forward physical product flow (left to right).
- **Dashed arrows** = Information & financial bi-directional flows.
- **Bottom loop** = Reverse logistics for returns, recycling, warranty claims.

---

### 4.2 Three-Tier Decision Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    scStrat["Strategic Tier Years Horizon Plant Location Supplier Selection Network Design"] --> scConstrain1["Sets Constraints and Boundaries"]
    scConstrain1 --> scTact["Tactical Tier Months Horizon Demand Forecasting Inventory Policy Procurement Contracts"]
    scConstrain1 --> scOp["Operational Tier Days Hours Order Processing Dispatching Delivery Scheduling"]
    scTact --> scConstrain2["Refines and Operationalizes"]
    scConstrain2 --> scOp
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Insight:**
- The **Strategic tier** defines the *skeleton* — what facilities exist and which partners are committed.
- The **Tactical tier** decides *how* to operate within this skeleton.
- The **Operational tier** performs *real-time execution*.
- Each tier must **respect the constraints** imposed by the tier above it. This hierarchical cascade prevents chaotic decision-making.

---

### 4.3 The Five Flows of a Supply Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    scFiveFlows["Five Simultaneous Flows in Every Supply Chain"]:::flowCenter
    scFiveFlows --> scFlowA["1 Product Flow Upstream to Downstream"]
    scFiveFlows --> scFlowB["2 Information Flow Bi Directional"]
    scFiveFlows --> scFlowC["3 Financial Flow Downstream to Upstream"]
    scFiveFlows --> scFlowD["4 Risk Flow Disruption Signals"]
    scFiveFlows --> scFlowE["5 Value Flow Each Step Adds Utility"]
    classDef flowCenter fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Connection**
> The five-flow model is directly analogous to a **multi-domain engineering system**: a power grid (energy flow), a communication network (information flow), and a financial ledger (money flow) all coexist in modern infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Supply Chain Management. List any four key flows in a supply chain.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** is the strategic coordination and integration of all activities and organizations involved in producing and delivering a product, from supplier's supplier to customer's customer, with the objective of enhancing long-term performance and value to the ultimate consumer. *[1 Mark]*
>
> The four key flows are: *[½ Mark each = 2 Marks]*
> 1. **Product/Goods Flow** — physical movement from suppliers to customers
> 2. **Information Flow** — forecasts, orders, inventory data (bi-directional)
> 3. **Financial Flow** — payments, invoices, credit terms
> 4. **Risk / Value Flow** — disruption signals and value-adding utilities (place, time, possession, form)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Supply Chain and Value Chain (any four points).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Supply Chain | Value Chain |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Operational movement of goods | Strategic value creation at each step |
| **Scope** | End-to-end logistics & information | Internal firm activities + external partners |
| **Perspective** | Network of firms | Sequential activities of a single firm |
| **Goal** | Efficient product delivery | Maximize value addition per activity |

*[1 Mark for the concept, 2 Marks for any four points clearly tabulated or listed]*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three decision phases** of supply chain management in detail. Give suitable examples for each phase. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compute the **Reorder Point (ROP)** and **Safety Stock (SS)** for the following case: A retailer sells a product with average daily demand of 200 units. Lead time is 5 days. Standard deviation of daily demand is 25 units. The retailer desires a 95% service level (Z = 1.65). *(7 Marks)*

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The three decision phases are: *[1 Mark for naming]*

**1. Strategic / Supply Chain Strategy Phase (3–10 year horizon)** *[2 Marks]*
- Decisions that define the long-term structure and capabilities of the supply chain.
- Examples: Whether to outsource manufacturing to China or set up a plant in India; whether to use 3PL or in-house logistics; facility location decisions.
- These decisions require **large capital investment** and are difficult to reverse.

**2. Tactical / Planning Phase (1–12 month horizon)** *[2 Marks]*
- Decisions that determine how the strategic structure will be utilized to meet expected demand.
- Examples: Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), inventory policies, procurement contract negotiations, demand forecasting, production planning.
- These decisions are **medium-term** and are revised quarterly or annually.

**3. Operational / Execution Phase (real-time to weekly)** *[2 Marks]*
- Day-to-day decisions for executing the tactical plan.
- Examples: Order processing, daily dispatch scheduling, replenishment triggers, workforce allocation, vehicle routing.
- These decisions are **short-term** and have immediate impact on customer satisfaction.

> **Closing Statement:** The three phases form a **hierarchical cascade** — strategic decisions constrain tactical ones, and tactical decisions constrain operational ones. *[Bonus 1 Mark]*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Average daily demand $D_{avg} = 200$ units/day
- Lead time $LT = 5$ days
- Standard deviation of daily demand $\sigma_d = 25$ units
- Service level = 95% → Z = 1.65

**Step 1:** Compute expected demand during lead time. *[2 Marks]*

$$D_{LT} = D_{avg} \times LT = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Compute standard deviation of demand during lead time. *[2 Marks]*

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \times \sqrt{LT} = 25 \times \sqrt{5}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} = 25 \times 2.236 = 55.9 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Safety Stock. *[1 Mark]*

$$SS = Z \times \sigma_{LT} = 1.65 \times 55.9 = 92.24 \approx 93 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Compute Reorder Point. *[2 Marks]*

$$ROP = D_{LT} + SS = 1000 + 93 = 1093 \text{ units}$$

> **Conclusion:** The retailer must place a new order whenever the inventory level drops to **1093 units** to maintain a 95% service level. *[Bonus / concluding statement]*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define a **Supply Chain**. With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **five major flows** in a supply chain. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A company has annual COGS of ₹ 2,40,00,000 and average inventory value of ₹ 20,00,000. Compute the **Inventory Turnover (IT)** and **Days of Inventory (DOI)**. Interpret the result. *(7 Marks)*

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (3 Marks):** A supply chain is a *systematic network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources* involved in moving a product or service from the supplier's supplier to the customer's customer, with the goal of adding value at every step.

**Five Major Flows (4 Marks):**

1. **Product Flow** — Physical movement of raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods from upstream to downstream. Example: Supplier → Plant → Warehouse → Retailer.

2. **Information Flow** — Bi-directional exchange of demand forecasts, order status, inventory levels, shipment tracking. Enables coordination.

3. **Financial Flow** — Movement of payments, credit, invoices, consignment terms from downstream (customers) to upstream (suppliers).

4. **Risk Flow** — Disruption signals, hedging strategies, insurance, and contingency activations when supply chain shocks occur.

5. **Value Flow** — Each step must add one of the four utilities: **form** (manufacturing), **place** (transportation), **time** (warehousing), **possession** (retailing).

*[Diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram; equivalent hand-drawn block diagram carries full marks]*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Annual COGS = ₹ 2,40,00,000
- Average Inventory = ₹ 20,00,000
- $T = 365$ days

**Step 1: Compute Inventory Turnover** *[3 Marks]*

$$IT = \frac{COGS}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2,40,00,000}{20,00,000} = 12 \text{ times/year}$$

**Step 2: Compute Days of Inventory** *[3 Marks]*

$$DOI = \frac{365}{IT} = \frac{365}{12} = 30.42 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation** *[1 Mark]*

> The company's inventory is sold and replenished **12 times per year**, meaning on average each unit sits in inventory for approximately **30 days**. This indicates a **healthy turnover** for an FMCG-style operation (industry benchmark: 6–12 turns). The company should benchmark this against industry peers and work toward higher turnover without compromising service levels.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **Inventory Turnover** units — it is **times per year**, NOT a percentage.
> - **Mistake 2:** In ROP problems, students forget to take **square root of lead time** when computing $\sigma_{LT}$ — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this common slip.
> - **Mistake 3:** In flow-diagram questions, students draw only **2–3 flows** instead of all five. Always mention all five explicitly.
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention **units** in numerical answers (units/day, units, days). Always include them.
> - **Mistake 5:** Writing "supply chain is a linear chain" — KTU 2024 scheme expects explicit mention that modern supply chains are **networks**, not chains.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** Supply chain = network of organizations moving products from supplier's supplier to customer's customer.
- ✅ **Supply Chain ≠ Value Chain:** SC focuses on operational logistics flow; VC focuses on strategic value creation.
- ✅ **Five Flows:** Product, Information, Financial, Risk, Value.
- ✅ **Three Decision Phases:** Strategic (years) → Tactical (months) → Operational (days/hours).
- ✅ **Hierarchical Cascade:** Higher-tier decisions constrain lower-tier ones.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** $IT = COGS / I_{avg}$, $DOI = 365 / IT$, $C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO$, $ROP = D_{avg} \times LT + Z \times \sigma_d \times \sqrt{LT}$.
- ✅ **Bullwhip Effect:** Demand variance amplifies upstream; quantify via Variance Ratio.
- ✅ **Value Addition:** Each supply chain step must add **form / place / time / possession** utility.
- ✅ **Modern Reality:** Supply chains are **networks**, not linear chains — always mention this in answers.
- ✅ **Service Level Z-values to memorize:** 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 97.5% → 1.96, 99% → 2.33.
- ✅ **Benchmark metrics:** Inventory Turnover 6–12×/year; Perfect Order Index > 95%; Cash-to-Cash < 60 days.
- ✅ **Reverse Logistics:** Increasingly important — includes returns, recycling, remanufacturing, warranty.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Module 1 specifically tests definitions, flows, decision phases, and basic quantitative metrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Achieving Strategic Fit in a Supply Chain; Supply Chain Drivers and Metrics; Analysing and Designing the Supply Chain Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Achieving Strategic Fit in a Supply Chain; Supply Chain Drivers and Metrics; Analysing and Designing the Supply Chain Network ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Achieving Strategic Fit in a Supply Chain > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Chopra & Meindl Framework):** > **Strategic Fit** is the consistency between the competitive strategy of a firm and the capabilities of its supply chain. It refers to the ability of a supply chain to *simultaneously* satisfy the requirements of the target customer (the customer needs chain) and the requirements of the supply chain itself (the supply chain capabilities), thereby maximizing the **total surplus** (profit) generated. The concept is built upon three foundational pillars: 1. **Customer Needs Chain** – The set of attributes valued by the target customer segment (price, variety, response time, innovation, quality, etc.). 2. **Supply Chain Capabilities** – The ability of the supply chain to deliver these attributes (responsive vs. efficient). 3. **Strategic Fit** – The matching zone where the supply chain is designed to specifically serve the target customer's prioritized needs. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (The "Made-to-Order Suit" Intuition):** > Imagine you are a tailor. If a customer wants a *haute couture* wedding tuxedo (high variety, high responsiveness, premium quality), giving them a cheap mass-produced off-the-rack shirt will *not* fit. Conversely, hand-crafting a bespoke shirt for someone who just wanted a basic ₹200 T-shirt is a disaster. **Strategic Fit is the act of tailoring your supply chain the way a tailor customizes fabric — perfectly matching the "cut" of operations to the "shape" of customer demand.** A misfit burns money at both ends. ### 1.2 Supply Chain Drivers and Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > **Supply Chain Drivers** are the **six structural and infrastructural levers** that a manager can manipulate to influence the responsiveness and efficiency of a supply chain. They are the physical, financial, and informational foundations upon which all logistics decisions rest. The **Six Drivers of Supply Chain Performance** (Chopra & Meindl Canonical Model): | Driver | Description | |---|---| | **Facilities** | The actual physical locations (plants, warehouses, cross-docks) where products are manufactured, stored, or processed. | | **Inventory** | Raw materials, WIP, and finished goods held at various points in the system. | | **Transportation** | The movement of products between facilities and to customers (modes: air, truck, rail, ocean, pipeline). | | **Information** | The data, analytics, and systems used to plan and operate the supply chain. | | **Sourcing** | The process of procuring raw materials, components, and services from suppliers. | | **Pricing** | The pricing strategy for products and the pricing schemes used to influence demand. | > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The "Six Levers of a Race Car" Analogy:** > Picture a Formula 1 car. The **Facilities** are the chassis and engine locations (factory/garage). **Inventory** is the fuel tank (how much reserve you carry). **Transportation** is the gearbox and tires (how fast power is delivered). **Information** is the dashboard and telemetry (real-time feedback to the driver). **Sourcing** is the fuel and parts supplier. **Pricing** is the racing strategy (when to push the accelerator). You tune all six levers to win a specific race (achieve strategic fit). You don't tune all six to maximum at once — a balance is required. ### 1.3 Analysing and Designing the Supply Chain Network > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > **Supply Chain Network Design** is the strategic decision-making process of determining the optimal number, location, size, and role of facilities, and the assignment of product flows between them, to satisfy customer demand at the lowest total landed cost while meeting service-level requirements. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The "Hub & Spoke of an Airline" Analogy:** > Think of IndiGo or Emirates deciding where to build airports, how many crew bases to operate, and which cities to connect directly. They don't fly every city to every city (too expensive). Instead, they build a *hub* (Dubai, Delhi) and connect spokes. Network design in a supply chain is identical — we decide: *Where to build the factory? Where to put the warehouse? Which warehouse serves which city?* All to minimize the **total logistics cost (transport + facility + inventory)** while keeping **customer service** high. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three-Step Framework to Achieve Strategic Fit Achieving strategic fit is **NOT** a one-time event; it is a continuous iterative cycle: **Step 1 — Understand the Customer (Customer Needs Chain)** - Identify the target customer segment. - Determine the **demand attributes** the customer values most. - Quantify these attributes via service-level requirements. - Map these to the **Responsiveness Spectrum**: - Low responsiveness → Low price (e.g., unbranded commodities). - High responsiveness → High price (e.g., luxury, fashion, customized). **Step 2 — Understand the Supply Chain (Supply Chain Capabilities)** - Map the current SC capabilities on the responsiveness–efficiency frontier. - An SC can be classified into: - **Physically Efficient** (low cost, low variety, predictable demand). - **Market Responsive** (high variety, low lead time, volatile demand). - **Risk-Hedging** (high supply disruption risk → buffering, redundancy). - **Agile** (high variety + high volume volatility → flexible capacity). **Step 3 — Achieve Strategic Fit (The Match)** - Close the gap between customer needs and SC capabilities. - If gap exists: either modify customer segment OR re-engineer supply chain. - Continuous monitoring: customer needs evolve → fit must be re-evaluated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Cardinal Rule:** A firm must **NOT** try to excel at all competitive priorities simultaneously. The cost of attempting "high variety + high service + low cost" is exponentially greater than specializing. Strategic fit demands deliberate *trade-offs*. ### 2.2 Six Drivers — Trade-Off Matrix Each driver exhibits a fundamental trade-off between **Responsiveness (R↑)** and **Efficiency (E↑)**: | Driver | To Increase Responsiveness (R↑) | Efficiency Trade-Off | |---|---|---| | **Facilities** | Build many small, local facilities (near customer). | Higher fixed cost + duplication. | | **Inventory** | Hold large buffer/safety stock. | Higher carrying cost + obsolescence. | | **Transportation** | Use fast mode (air) and frequent small shipments. | Higher per-unit transport cost. | | **Information** | Deploy real-time POS, analytics, IoT sensors. | Higher IT investment, integration cost. | | **Sourcing** | Use many local, reliable, certified suppliers. | Loss of scale economies from global sourcing. | | **Pricing** | Use responsive pricing (markdown, dynamic pricing). | Cannibalization risk and customer churn. | ### 2.3 Supply Chain Metrics — The Two-Tier Framework Metrics are categorized into: - **Tier 1 — Customer-Facing Metrics (Output of SC):** - Fill Rate, On-Time Delivery, Order Lead Time, Stockout Frequency, Perfect Order %. - **Tier 2 — Internal / Financial Metrics (Health of SC):** - Inventory Days of Supply, Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time, Cost of Goods Sold, Return on Fixed Assets, Supply Chain Cycle Time. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Equation / Definition | Units / Goal | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Lead Time (LT)** | $LT = Process\,Time + Wait\,Time + Transport\,Time$ | Days; Lower is better for responsiveness. | | 2 | **Inventory Days of Supply (IDS)** | $IDS = \dfrac{Average\,Inventory}{Cost\,of\,Goods\,Sold\,per\,Day}$ | Days; Lower = leaner SC. | | 3 | **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** | $C2C = Inventory\,Days + Receivable\,Days - Payable\,Days$ | Days; Lower = better liquidity. | | 4 | **Fill Rate (FR)** | $FR = \dfrac{Orders\,filled\,completely}{Total\,orders} \times 100$ | %; Higher = better service. | | 5 | **Gross Margin ROI (GMROI)** | $GMROI = \dfrac{Gross\,Margin}{Average\,Inventory\,Cost}$ | Ratio; > 1 is profitable. | | 6 | **Total Logistics Cost (TLC)** | $TLC = T_{cost} + I_{cost} + F_{cost} + W_{cost}$ | ₹/unit; Minimize. | | 7 | **Center of Gravity (X-Coord)** | $X_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | Coordinate; minimizes weighted distance. | | 8 | **Center of Gravity (Y-Coord)** | $Y_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | Coordinate; minimizes weighted distance. | | 9 | **Total Network Cost** | $C = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\left( c_{ij} + h_{ij} \right) \cdot f_{ij}$ | ₹; Objective function of network model. | | 10 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = D_{avg} \cdot LT + z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Units; Safety stock = $z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility in the Field:** > - **C2C** is the metric CFOs and bank loan officers use to assess supply chain health — used in credit rating of firms. > - **Center of Gravity model** is heavily used by Amazon-style last-mile warehouse placement; if you scale weights (demand) and rebalance every 6 months, you stay close to demand. > - **Fill Rate** is the single most-tracked Tier-1 metric on a balanced scorecard in FMCG (HUL, Nestlé, ITC). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Center of Gravity (CoG) Model for Facility Location The CoG model locates a single facility that minimizes the weighted sum of distances (typically transport cost is proportional to distance) from a set of demand points. **Given:** - $n$ demand points indexed $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$. - Each point has location $(x_i, y_i)$ and weight $w_i$ (e.g., demand volume or transport volume). **Objective:** Minimize total weighted rectilinear or Euclidean distance. **Step-by-Step Derivation (Euclidean case):** The total weighted squared distance function is: $$ D(X, Y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \left[ (X - x_i)^2 + (Y - y_i)^2 \right] $$ **Step 1:** Take partial derivative w.r.t. $X$ and set to zero (first-order optimality condition). $$ \dfrac{\partial D}{\partial X} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot 2(X - x_i) = 0 $$ **Step 2:** Expand and separate. $$ 2X \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i - 2\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i = 0 $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $X$. $$ X_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} $$ **Step 4:** By symmetry, repeat for $Y$. $$ Y_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} $$ **Conclusion:** The optimal facility location is the weighted average of all demand point coordinates — the *center of mass* of the system where each demand point is treated as a particle of mass $w_i$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** CoG is a *heuristic*, not a true optimizer. It gives a good *starting point* for a single-facility location problem. For multi-facility problems, use integer programming or cross-docking models. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Number and Weight of Facilities Decision | Decision Factor | Many Facilities | Few (Centralized) Facilities | |---|---|---| | **Response Time** | Lower (closer to customer) | Higher (longer shipment time) | | **Inventory** | Lower (decentralized) | Higher (consolidated safety stock) | | **Facility Cost** | Higher (more fixed cost) | Lower (economies of scale) | | **Transportation Cost** | Lower inbound, Higher outbound | Higher inbound, Lower outbound | | **Service Level** | Higher availability | Risk of stockout from central hub | | **Best Suited For** | High variety, high responsiveness segment | Commodity, efficiency-focused segment | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) **Problem:** A distribution company must locate a central warehouse to serve 3 retail stores. Data: | Store | Coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ | Monthly Demand (Tonnes) $w_i$ | |---|---|---| | A | (10, 20) | 500 | | B | (30, 40) | 700 | | C | (50, 10) | 300 | **Step 1 — Sum of weights:** $$ \sum w_i = 500 + 700 + 300 = 1500 \text{ tonnes} $$ **Step 2 — Weighted sum of x-coordinates:** $$ \sum w_i x_i = 500 \cdot 10 + 700 \cdot 30 + 300 \cdot 50 = 5000 + 21000 + 15000 = 41000 $$ **Step 3 — Weighted sum of y-coordinates:** $$ \sum w_i y_i = 500 \cdot 20 + 700 \cdot 40 + 300 \cdot 10 = 10000 + 28000 + 3000 = 41000 $$ **Step 4 — Apply CoG formulas:** $$ X_c = \dfrac{41000}{1500} = 27.33 $$ $$ Y_c = \dfrac{41000}{1500} = 27.33 $$ **Conclusion:** The optimal warehouse location is approximately at $(27.33, 27.33)$. This single point minimizes the weighted Euclidean distance to all three stores. ### 3.4 Network Design Optimization Model (Mathematical Programming Form) For KTU, the canonical **Capacitated Plant Location Model** is: $$ \text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i \in I} f_i \cdot y_i + \sum_{i \in I}\sum_{j \in J} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij} $$ $$ \text{Subject to:} \quad \sum_{i \in I} x_{ij} = D_j \quad \forall j \in J $$ $$ \sum_{j \in J} x_{ij} \leq K_i \cdot y_i \quad \forall i \in I $$ $$ x_{ij} \geq 0, \quad y_i \in \{0, 1\} $$ Where: - $f_i$ = fixed cost of opening facility $i$. - $c_{ij}$ = per-unit transport cost from facility $i$ to customer $j$. - $D_j$ = demand at customer $j$. - $K_i$ = capacity of facility $i$. - $y_i$ = 1 if facility $i$ is open, 0 otherwise. - $x_{ij}$ = units shipped from $i$ to $j$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Application:** Amazon's "Amazon Logistics" network, Flipkart's warehouse placement, and Zomato's dark-store locations are all variants of this mixed-integer linear program (MILP) solved using solvers like CPLEX, Gurobi, or commercial supply chain platforms like **LLamasoft** and **o9 Solutions**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Strategic Fit Triad — Conceptual Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef needs fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef sc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef fit fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 CN["CUSTOMER NEEDS CHAIN
Price, Variety, Lead Time,
Quality, Innovation"]:::needs SC["SUPPLY CHAIN CAPABILITIES
Facilities, Inventory,
Transport, Information,
Sourcing, Pricing"]:::sc SF["STRATEGIC FIT
Aligned Competitive Strategy
with SC Design"]:::fit CN --> SF SC --> SF SF -->|Monitor Continuously| CN subgraph FEEDBACK["Iterative Re-evaluation Loop"] direction LR FEEDBACK_NODE["Customer Needs Evolve
Market Changes"] end FEEDBACK_NODE -->|Triggers Re-fit| CN ``` ### 4.2 Six Drivers of Supply Chain Performance ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef driver fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef central fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 HUB["SUPPLY CHAIN

Where things are made

suppliers"]:::driver P["PRICING
How pricing influences
demand"]:::driver HUB --> F HUB --> I HUB --> T HUB --> INFO HUB --> S HUB --> P F <-->|Trade-off| I T <-->|Trade-off| INFO S <-->|Trade-off| P ``` ### 4.3 Supply Chain Network Design Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef sup fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef plant fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef dc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef cust fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 S1["Supplier A"]:::sup S2["Supplier B"]:::sup S3["Supplier C"]:::sup P1["Plant North"]:::plant P2["Plant South"]:::plant DC1["Central DC 1"]:::dc DC2["Regional DC 2"]:::dc DC3["Regional DC 3"]:::dc C1["Customer Zone 1"]:::cust C2["Customer Zone 2"]:::cust C3["Customer Zone 3"]:::cust C4["Customer Zone 4"]:::cust S1 --> P1 S2 --> P1 S3 --> P2 P1 --> DC1 P1 --> DC2 P2 --> DC2 P2 --> DC3 DC1 --> C1 DC1 --> C2 DC2 --> C2 DC2 --> C3 DC3 --> C3 DC3 --> C4 ``` ### 4.4 Strategic Fit Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef strategic fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef phase fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef outcome fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 S1["STEP 1
Understand Customer
Needs Chain"]:::phase S2["STEP 2

Chain Capabilities"]:::phase S3["STEP 3

High Transport Cost

low-volume items"]:::branch B --> B1["High Inventory Holding

Aggregation only

Suitable for FMCG, retail"]:::branch D --> D1["No Inventory at All
Supplier ships direct
Suitable for e-commerce,
books, large items"]:::branch ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **Define Strategic Fit in the context of supply chain management. State its significance.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Strategic Fit is the alignment between a firm's competitive strategy and the capabilities of its supply chain to satisfy targeted customer needs. - **Customer Needs Chain (0.5 Marks):** It links the customer's prioritized demand attributes. - **Supply Chain Capabilities (0.5 Marks):** It ensures the SC structure can deliver these attributes. - **Significance (0.5 Marks):** It maximizes the *total surplus* (customer value + firm profit) and avoids the cost of trying to excel at everything. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **List the six drivers of supply chain performance. Identify which driver has the greatest impact on responsiveness in a FMCG company.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **List (2 Marks):** Facilities, Inventory, Transportation, Information, Sourcing, Pricing. - **Greatest Impact for FMCG Responsiveness (1 Mark):** **Information** — because FMCG demand is highly volatile and promotional; real-time POS data and forecasting accuracy drive shelf availability without overstocking. --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions > **Note:** Per KTU ESE convention, students answer ONE of TWO full choices (each worth 14 marks, sub-parts typically 7 + 7). --- ### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** > > **(a)** Explain in detail the **three-step framework for achieving strategic fit** in a supply chain. Illustrate with a real-world example of a fast-fashion retailer. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the **trade-offs between responsiveness and efficiency** across the six supply chain drivers. Provide a comparative table. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Understand the Customer Needs Chain (2 Marks):** For a fast-fashion retailer like **Zara or H&M**, the target customer demands: - Very high variety (new styles every 2 weeks). - Very low response time (new designs within 14–21 days). - Moderate price (premium fashion but not luxury). - High trend sensitivity. **Step 2 — Understand Supply Chain Capabilities (2 Marks):** The retailer builds a **market-responsive + agile** supply chain: - Decentralized small-batch production facilities (Spain/Portugal/India). - Limited inventory with rapid replenishment. - Heavy use of air freight and express trucking. - Real-time point-of-sale data analytics. **Step 3 — Achieve Strategic Fit (2 Marks):** The SC strategy is aligned to deliver *high variety + low lead time* at a *moderate price*. The firm does **not** try to be the cheapest — it accepts a higher cost structure in exchange for variety. Continuous re-evaluation occurs because fashion trends shift quickly, requiring the SC to re-fit every quarter. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Strategic fit allows Zara to achieve **gross margins of ~57%** despite charging lower prices than luxury competitors — proof that fit drives surplus profit. --- #### Model Solution — Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Trade-Off Table (5 Marks):** | Driver | Responsiveness ↑ Choice | Efficiency ↓ Cost Impact | |---|---|---| | Facilities | Many small, local | Higher fixed cost & duplication | | Inventory | Large safety stock | Carrying cost + obsolescence | | Transportation | Air freight, LTL | High per-unit transport | | Information | Real-time POS, IoT | High IT investment | | Sourcing | Local certified suppliers | Loss of global scale economies | | Pricing | Dynamic markdown | Cannibalization risk | **Synthesis (2 Marks):** The art of supply chain management lies in selecting **which drivers to push toward responsiveness** and **which to push toward efficiency** based on the target segment. For example, Amazon prioritizes *Information* responsiveness (real-time tracking) while keeping *Inventory* highly efficient (cross-docking). --- ### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]** > > **(a)** With a suitable mathematical formulation, explain the **Center of Gravity method** for facility location. A company wants to locate a warehouse to serve four markets with the following data: compute the optimal location. **(7 Marks)** > > | Market | Coordinates $(x, y)$ | Demand (tonnes) | > |---|---|---| > | M1 | (5, 10) | 200 | > | M2 | (15, 30) | 400 | > | M3 | (40, 20) | 300 | > | M4 | (25, 5) | 500 | > > **(b)** Compare **Direct Shipping, Warehousing, and Cross-Docking** as alternative distribution network designs. Under what product/customer conditions is each most appropriate? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B(a) — 7 Marks **Mathematical Formulation (2 Marks):** Given $n$ demand points with locations $(x_i, y_i)$ and weights $w_i$, the CoG solution is: $$ X_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}, \qquad Y_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} $$ **Computation (4 Marks):** Sum of weights: $$ \sum w_i = 200 + 400 + 300 + 500 = 1400 $$ Weighted sum of $x$: $$ \sum w_i x_i = 200 \cdot 5 + 400 \cdot 15 + 300 \cdot 40 + 500 \cdot 25 $$ $$ = 1000 + 6000 + 12000 + 12500 = 31500 $$ Weighted sum of $y$: $$ \sum w_i y_i = 200 \cdot 10 + 400 \cdot 30 + 300 \cdot 20 + 500 \cdot 5 $$ $$ = 2000 + 12000 + 6000 + 2500 = 22500 $$ Optimal coordinates: $$ X_c = \dfrac{31500}{1400} = 22.5 $$ $$ Y_c = \dfrac{22500}{1400} = 16.07 $$ **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Optimal warehouse location is at approximately $(22.5, 16.07)$. --- #### Model Solution — Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Criterion | Direct Shipping | Warehousing | Cross-Docking | |---|---|---|---| | Inventory | None at retailer | High at warehouse | Near zero (in transit only) | | Lead Time | High (long-haul per order) | Moderate (replenishment cycles) | Low (rapid dispatch) | | Transport Cost | High (separate shipments) | Low (consolidated bulk) | Moderate (consolidated + split) | | Facility Cost | Zero | Very High | Low (no storage) | | Suitable For | High-value, low-volume (cars, machinery) | Predictable FMCG (rice, oil) | Fast-moving retail (Walmart, Amazon) | **Synthesis (2 Marks):** - **Direct Shipping** suits *high-value items* (₹ per kg) where transport cost is negligible compared to inventory cost. - **Warehousing** suits *predictable, low-margin, high-volume* goods where consolidation economies outweigh inventory carrying cost. - **Cross-Docking** suits *volatile, high-SKU retail* where speed and assortment matter more than holding cost savings. **Real Example (1 Mark):** Walmart pioneered cross-docking in the 1980s to handle grocery replenishment within 24–48 hours. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Conflating "Strategic Fit" with "Supply Chain Strategy"** — Strategic fit is the *alignment*, not the strategy itself. (–1 mark) > 2. **Listing only 4–5 drivers** — All six must be named; missing Sourcing or Pricing costs full marks. > 3. **Forgetting units** in CoG problem — Examiner penalizes stating $(X, Y)$ without indicating whether they are km, miles, or grid coordinates. > 4. **Skipping the trade-off discussion** — In any 14-mark question on drivers, simply defining them without explaining the R↑ vs E↑ conflict earns only 4–5 marks out of 7. > 5. **Mixing up Warehousing with Cross-Docking** — Warehousing involves *storage*; cross-docking involves *consolidation + immediate dispatch*, with virtually no storage. Examiners *love* testing this distinction. > 6. **No mention of iterative re-evaluation** — Strategic fit is dynamic, not static. Students who forget to mention the feedback loop lose 1–2 marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1):** > > - **Strategic Fit = Alignment** between competitive strategy & SC capabilities — *not* the strategy itself. > - **Three-Step Framework:** (1) Customer Needs → (2) SC Capabilities → (3) Match & Close Gap. > - **Six Drivers:** Facilities, Inventory, Transportation, Information, Sourcing, Pricing — *memorize in order*. > - Each driver exhibits a fundamental **Responsiveness ↔ Efficiency trade-off**. > - **Four SC Types:** Physically Efficient, Market Responsive, Risk-Hedging, Agile. > - **Metrics are TWO-TIER:** Customer-Facing (Fill Rate, On-Time) and Internal Financial (C2C, GMROI, IDS). > - **Network Design Decisions:** Location, Number, Capacity, Role of facilities; Product flows; Mode of transport. > - **Three Distribution Models:** Direct Shipping, Warehousing, Cross-Docking. > - **CoG Formula** (memorize): $X_c = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$, $Y_c = \dfrac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i}$. > - CoG is a **heuristic** for single-facility problems; for multi-facility, use MILP / capacitated plant location. > - **Total Logistics Cost** = Transport + Inventory + Facility + Warehousing cost. > - **Strategic fit is iterative**, not a one-shot event — customer needs evolve. > - **KTU Favourite Real Examples:** Zara (responsive fashion), Amazon (agile), Walmart (cross-dock), Toyota (lean efficient), Apple (innovation-driven).

topics.title: Factors Affecting Distribution Network Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Distribution Network Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distribution Network Design (DND)** refers to the strategic planning process that determines the configuration of facilities, inventory levels, transportation modes, and information flows required to move finished products from the **point of production** to the **point of consumption** in a manner that maximizes service level while minimizing total system cost.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **distribution network** is a sub-system of the broader supply chain that focuses exclusively on the **downstream flow** of goods — i.e., the linkage between manufacturing plants, warehouses, distribution centers (DCs), and end customers. The design choices made at this stage have a **long-term horizon (typically 5–10 years)** and consume a significant share of the firm's logistics budget.

The factors affecting this design are the **decision variables and constraints** that the logistics manager must satisfy while optimizing the network. These factors collectively determine the **service performance** and the **cost-to-serve** of the entire supply chain.

### Formal Mathematical Framing of a Distribution Network

Let:

$$Z = \min \left( C_{facility} + C_{inventory} + C_{transportation} + C_{handling} \right)$$

subject to service level constraints such that:

$$SL_i \geq SL_i^{min} \quad \forall i \in \{customers\}$$

Where:
- $C_{facility}$ = Fixed cost of warehouses, DCs, cross-docks
- $C_{inventory}$ = Holding cost + stock-out penalty cost
- $C_{transportation}$ = Freight cost across modes
- $C_{handling}$ = Order processing, picking, packaging cost
- $SL_i$ = Service Level attained at customer $i$

> [!NOTE]
> **Service Level (SL)** is the most fundamental output of a distribution network. It is defined as the probability that demand from a customer is met from on-hand inventory without a back-order or lost sale. KTU frequently expresses it as the **Type-1 Service Level (α-service level)** or the **fill rate (β-service level)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Water Distribution Network of a City**
> Imagine a city municipal water grid. There are a few large water-treatment plants (akin to **factories**), medium-sized reservoirs in each district (akin to **warehouses/DCs**), small local storage tanks (akin to **retail outlets**), and finally households (the **end customers**). When the city engineer designs the pipe network, several factors influence the design:
> 1. **How many people live in each area?** (Demand density)
> 2. **How urgently do they need water?** (Service level / responsiveness)
> 3. **Is it hilly terrain?** (Infrastructure / geography)
> 4. **Can the water be stored long?** (Perishability)
> 5. **What does piping cost per km?** (Transportation cost)
> 6. **Are there government rules on water pressure?** (Regulations)
>
> The engineer trades off building many small local tanks (low transport cost, high facility cost) vs. one giant central plant (low facility cost, high transport cost). This trade-off is exactly what a Supply Chain Manager faces while designing a distribution network.

In simpler terms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Distribution Network Design as choosing the right number of warehouses, in the right locations, holding the right inventory, served by the right transportation modes, to deliver the right product to the right customer at the right time — at minimum total cost.**

This single sentence captures the essence of the **Chopra & Meindl framework** which is the canonical reference model used in KTU board examinations for PESFT867.

---

## 1.3 KTU Classification of Factors Affecting Distribution Network Design

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus groups these factors into **six major categories**:

| # | Factor Category | KTU Board Favorite Keywords |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer-related Factors** | Demand size, demand density, lead time tolerance, service level expectations |
| 2 | **Product-related Factors** | Product variety, product value, perishability, substitutability |
| 3 | **Operational / Cost Factors** | Facility cost, inventory cost, transportation cost, handling cost |
| 4 | **Competitive Factors** | Competitor location, network differentiation, first-mover advantage |
| 5 | **Infrastructure & Technology Factors** | Roads, ports, IT systems, automation, e-commerce readiness |
| 6 | **Regulatory / Macro Factors** | Tax laws, trade tariffs, environmental norms, political stability |

Each of these factors is expanded in the next section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU 2024 Students**: The 14-mark Module-1 questions on this topic typically ask students to **list, classify, and explain the impact of EACH factor** on distribution network design. Memorizing the six categories above is mandatory for the Part-B long answer.

---

## 1.4 Decision Framework Overview

The distribution network designer makes **four key decisions**, and every factor listed above influences one or more of these decisions:

$$\text{DND Decisions} = \begin{cases} \text{(a) Facility Role Decision} \\ \text{(b) Facility Location Decision} \\ \text{(c) Capacity Allocation Decision} \\ \text{(d) Market & Product Assignment Decision} \end{cases}$$

The interaction between the **six factors** and the **four decisions** forms the analytical matrix that examiners love to test.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Distribution costs account for approximately **30–40%** of the final product cost in most consumer-goods industries.
> - Network design decisions are **strategic** — once implemented, they are extremely expensive to reverse.
> - This topic is the **foundation** for advanced modules covering facility location models (Weber, CFLP, Gravity models) and transportation optimization.

End of Section 1. Section 2 follows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Categories — Expanded Theoretical Analysis

Each category is broken down into **sub-factors**, with the **mechanism of influence** on the network design made explicit.

### 2.1.1 Customer-Related Factors (Demand Side Drivers)

These factors describe the **end consumer** and shape the **demand curve** the network must satisfy.

#### (i) Demand Size
- Larger demand per region makes it **economically viable** to maintain a dedicated warehouse in or near that region.
- The relevant threshold is given by:

$$D_{break-even} = \frac{2 \cdot C_f}{h \cdot \sqrt{2 \pi}}$$

where $C_f$ is the facility fixed cost per period and $h$ is the holding cost per unit per period.

- If regional demand $D_i > D_{break-even}$, a local warehouse is justified.

#### (ii) Demand Density
- High density of demand in a small geographic area → **small, frequent shipments via local DC**.
- Low density over a large area → **centralized inventory + long-haul transport**.

#### (iii) Customer Service Level Expectations
- High service-level expectations (e.g., next-day delivery as promised by Amazon) necessitate **more DCs closer to customers**, raising $C_{facility}$ but reducing transportation lead time.

#### (iv) Lead-Time Tolerance
- Customers with **low patience** (e-commerce apparel) force a decentralized network.
- Customers with **high patience** (industrial buyers) allow a centralized network.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight**: When asked "How does customer location affect distribution network design?", students must mention **Euclidean distance minimization** and the **Weber problem**, which is discussed in Module 2.

---

### 2.1.2 Product-Related Factors (Supply Side Characteristics)

| Sub-factor | Network Implication |
|---|---|
| **High Product Variety** (many SKUs) | Network should be **decentralized** — keeping all SKUs at every DC is expensive; centralize slow-movers |
| **High Product Value** (electronics, jewellery) | Network should be **centralized** to reduce inventory carrying cost & shrinkage |
| **Low Product Value** (cement, FMCG) | Network can be **decentralized** since inventory cost is low |
| **High Perishability** (fruits, dairy) | Requires **cold-chain network** with rapid transport, short lead times |
| **Low Substitutability** | Each product line may need its own dedicated network lane |
| **Hazardous / Special Handling** | Forces routing through **certified facilities only** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Chopra & Meindl)**:
> - *High variety + Low demand → Direct shipping from factory (no DC)*
> - *Low variety + High demand → Many SKUs at each DC*
> - *High value → Centralized DC with air freight*
> - *Low value → Decentralized DC with ground freight*

---

### 2.1.3 Operational / Cost Factors

This is the **quantitative core** of the topic. Three cost components dominate:

$$\text{Total Logistics Cost } (TLC) = C_F + C_I + C_T$$

where:

- $C_F$ = **Facility Cost** = $\sum_{j=1}^{m} f_j \cdot X_j$ (where $X_j = 1$ if facility $j$ is open, else 0)
- $C_I$ = **Inventory Cost** = $\sum_{j=1}^{m} h_j \cdot I_j$ (Holding cost $\times$ average inventory at facility $j$)
- $C_T$ = **Transportation Cost** = $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} \cdot y_{ij}$ (cost per unit $\times$ units shipped from $j$ to $i$)

> [!NOTE]
> The above decomposition is the **standard KTU formulation** for the Capacitated Facility Location Problem (CFLP) and appears verbatim in Module 2 derivations.

#### Trade-Off Triangle (The Most Tested Concept)

```
              C_F (Facility Cost)
                    ▲
                    │
                    │      \ 
                    │       \ 
                    │        \ 
   ─────────────────┼─────────► C_T (Transportation Cost)
                    │        /
                    │       /
                    │      /
                    ▼
              C_I (Inventory Cost)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Inverse Relationship (KTU Board Favorite)**:
> - As the number of facilities **increases**, $C_F \uparrow$ and $C_T \downarrow$, but $C_I$ may either rise or fall depending on **safety stock pooling**.
> - The **optimal number of facilities** minimizes $C_F + C_I + C_T$ simultaneously.

#### Safety Stock Pooling Effect

When demand is pooled at fewer locations, the safety stock needed reduces due to the **square-root law of aggregation**:

$$SS_{pooled} = SS_{distributed} \cdot \sqrt{n}$$

where $n$ is the number of demand points being aggregated. This is a **key driver** of centralization decisions.

---

### 2.1.4 Competitive Factors

- **Competitor Location**: Placing a DC closer to a competitor's DC may be strategic for **market share capture** even if logistics costs rise.
- **Network Differentiation**: A firm may design a faster or more reliable network than competitors to command a **price premium**.
- **First-Mover Advantage**: Early entry into an emerging market may justify a sub-optimal but **strategically placed** DC.

---

### 2.1.5 Infrastructure & Technology Factors

- **Road & Rail Network Quality**: Determines feasible transportation modes and speeds.
- **Port Connectivity**: Coastal/port cities preferred for **export-oriented networks**.
- **IT & ERP Systems**: Real-time visibility allows a leaner, more centralized network.
- **Automation Capability**: High automation in a DC justifies a **larger centralized facility** because fixed automation cost spreads over more units.
- **E-commerce Penetration**: Forces a **hub-and-spoke** model with last-mile micro-fulfillment centers.

---

### 2.1.6 Regulatory / Macro Factors

- **Tax Structures**: GST / VAT differences across states (relevant in Indian context) influence DC placement.
- **Tariffs & Trade Barriers**: Force local manufacturing or assembly.
- **Environmental Norms**: Cold-chain certifications, emissions caps.
- **Labor Laws**: Availability of skilled warehouse labor.
- **Political Stability**: Long-term predictability of operations.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains ALL formulas students must memorize for the 14-mark question on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \min(C_F + C_I + C_T)$ | Total logistics cost minimization | Currency (INR, USD, etc.) |
| 2 | $C_F = \sum_{j=1}^{m} f_j X_j$ | Facility fixed cost | Currency; $f_j$ = fixed cost, $X_j$ = binary decision |
| 3 | $C_I = \sum_{j=1}^{m} h_j \cdot I_j$ | Inventory holding cost | Currency; $h_j$ = holding cost/unit, $I_j$ = avg inventory |
| 4 | $C_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} y_{ij}$ | Transportation cost | Currency; $c_{ij}$ = unit shipping cost, $y_{ij}$ = units shipped |
| 5 | $SS_{pooled} = SS_{distributed} \cdot \sqrt{n}$ | Safety stock aggregation law | Units of inventory; $n$ = number of demand points pooled |
| 6 | $D_{break-even} = \frac{2 C_f}{h \sqrt{2 \pi}}$ | Break-even demand for opening a facility | Units per period |
| 7 | $SL_i \geq SL_i^{min}$ | Customer service level constraint | Probability (0 to 1) |
| 8 | $TLC\% = \frac{C_F + C_I + C_T}{\text{Total Revenue}} \times 100$ | Logistics cost-to-revenue ratio | Percentage |
| 9 | $Q_{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{h}}$ | Economic Order Quantity (relates $C_I$ to $C_T$) | Units; $D$ = demand, $S$ = ordering cost, $h$ = holding cost |
| 10 | $LT_i = \frac{d_{ij}}{v} + t_{processing}$ | Lead time to customer $i$ | Time units; $d_{ij}$ = distance, $v$ = vehicle speed |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Do not confuse **Type-1 Service Level** ($\alpha$-service level = fill rate) with **Type-2 Service Level** (probability of no stock-out). Examiners frequently swap these terms to test students.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Amazon's Hub-and-Spoke Network**: Directly applies facility-cost vs. transportation-cost trade-off and safety-stock pooling.
- **Reliance Retail's Regional DC Strategy**: Customer-density driven.
- **Apple's Centralized High-Value Inventory**: Product-value driven centralization.
- **Amul's Cold-Chain Network**: Perishability driven decentralized refrigerated DCs.

End of Section 2. Section 3 follows.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Break-Even Demand for Opening a Local Warehouse

### Problem Context
A retail firm currently serves a region from a centralized warehouse at a distance $D$. It is considering opening a local warehouse near the region to reduce transportation costs. At what minimum regional demand $D_{break-even}$ does opening the local warehouse become economically justified?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Cost Without Local Warehouse (Centralized Only)**

The firm pays a transportation cost proportional to distance times volume:

$$C_{central} = c_t \cdot d \cdot D_{region}$$

where $c_t$ is the per-unit per-km transportation cost, $d$ is the distance, and $D_{region}$ is the regional demand per period.

**Step 2: Cost With Local Warehouse (Decentralized)**

The firm pays a fixed facility cost plus a reduced transportation cost (since shipments now go only to the local DC):

$$C_{local} = f + c_t \cdot d' \cdot D_{region}$$

where $f$ is the fixed facility cost per period, and $d' \ll d$ is the average inbound leg distance.

**Step 3: Set the Two Costs Equal to Find Break-Even**

We require:

$$c_t \cdot d \cdot D_{break-even} = f + c_t \cdot d' \cdot D_{break-even}$$

Solving for $D_{break-even}$:

$$D_{break-even} \cdot c_t \cdot (d - d') = f$$

$$D_{break-even} = \frac{f}{c_t \cdot (d - d')}$$

**Step 4: Substitute Realistic Approximation**

If $d' \approx 0$ (warehouse is adjacent to customer region) and $d \approx \sqrt{A}$ where $A$ is the area of the region (assuming circular geography), then:

$$D_{break-even} \approx \frac{f}{c_t \cdot \sqrt{A}}$$

**Step 5: Incorporate Inventory Holding Cost (Refined Chopra-Meindl Form)**

If the local DC also increases safety stock, the break-even demand must cover the additional $h \cdot SS$ cost:

$$D_{break-even} = \frac{f + h \cdot SS_{delta}}{c_t \cdot \sqrt{A}}$$

This is the canonical KTU formula given in Section 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: As $f$ rises (facility cost up) → break-even demand rises → fewer facilities justified. As $c_t$ rises (transport cost up) → break-even demand falls → more facilities justified.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Safety Stock Pooling Effect (Square-Root Law)

### Problem Context
A firm currently maintains $n$ decentralized warehouses each serving independent demand streams with standard deviation $\sigma_i$. What is the reduction in total safety stock if all $n$ streams are consolidated into one centralized warehouse?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Safety Stock at Each Decentralized DC**

For a Type-1 service level $\alpha$, the safety factor is $z_\alpha$ (standard normal critical value). Each DC needs:

$$SS_i = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma_i$$

**Step 2: Total Decentralized Safety Stock**

Assuming all $\sigma_i$ are equal to $\sigma$ for simplicity:

$$SS_{distributed} = n \cdot z_\alpha \cdot \sigma$$

**Step 3: Variance of Pooled Demand**

When all $n$ independent demand streams are aggregated at one DC, the variances add (assuming independence):

$$\sigma_{pooled}^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \dots + \sigma_n^2 = n \sigma^2$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{pooled} = \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}$$

**Step 4: Centralized Safety Stock**

$$SS_{pooled} = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma_{pooled} = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}$$

**Step 5: Ratio of Pooled to Distributed Safety Stock**

$$\frac{SS_{pooled}}{SS_{distributed}} = \frac{z_\alpha \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}}{n \cdot z_\alpha \cdot \sigma} = \frac{\sqrt{n}}{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Therefore:

$$SS_{pooled} = \frac{SS_{distributed}}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 6: Numerical Illustration**

| $n$ | $\sqrt{n}$ | Pooled SS as % of Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.414 | 70.7% |
| 4 | 2.000 | 50.0% |
| 9 | 3.000 | 33.3% |
| 16 | 4.000 | 25.0% |
| 25 | 5.000 | 20.0% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight**: This $1/\sqrt{n}$ law is a **very frequently asked 7-mark sub-question**. It quantifies the inventory-driven argument for centralization.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Service Level Constraint in Network Design

### Problem Context
A distribution network must satisfy customer $i$ with service level $SL_i \geq SL_i^{min}$. The lead time from DC $j$ to customer $i$ is $LT_{ij}$. Express the safety stock required at DC $j$ to satisfy ALL its assigned customers.

### Step-By-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Demand During Lead Time for Customer $i$**

The demand during lead time is a random variable with mean and standard deviation:

$$\mu_{DLT,i} = d_i \cdot LT_{ij}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT,i} = \sigma_i \cdot \sqrt{LT_{ij}}$$

**Step 2: Required Safety Stock for Customer $i$**

$$SS_i = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{DLT,i} = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sqrt{LT_{ij}}$$

**Step 3: Aggregate Safety Stock at DC $j$**

If DC $j$ serves customers $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$, and the demands are correlated with correlation coefficient $\rho$, then:

$$\sigma_{aggregate}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2 \cdot LT_{ij} + 2 \sum_{i < j'} \rho \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_{j'} \cdot LT$$

For the special case of **independent demands** ($\rho = 0$):

$$SS_{DC_j} = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2 \cdot LT_{ij}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: Adding a new customer to an existing DC increases $SS_{DC_j}$ only by the **square root of variance addition**, not linearly. This is why serving more customers from one DC becomes progressively cheaper on a per-customer inventory basis — a strong argument for **centralized networks**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Factors × Design Decisions

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix is the single most important analytical tool for the 14-mark KTU question.** Students should reproduce this in their answer scripts to score full marks.

| Factor ↓ \ Decision → | Facility Role | Facility Location | Capacity Allocation | Market-Product Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Customer Demand Size** | High → Direct shipping | Near large demand clusters | High capacity near demand | Assign based on demand volume |
| **Demand Density** | High density → Small DC | Concentrate in dense zones | High throughput design | Micro-fulfillment |
| **Service Level Requirement** | Premium → Decentralized | Closer to customer | Higher capacity for fast-moving | High SL items to nearest DC |
| **Lead-Time Tolerance** | Low tolerance → Local DC | Geographic dispersion | Buffer capacity | Local sourcing |
| **Product Variety** | High → Centralized variety | Centralized slow-mover DC | Flexible storage | Stock variety at fewer DCs |
| **Product Value** | High value → Centralized | Low crime, secure zones | High security capacity | High-value items centralized |
| **Perishability** | Decentralized cold chain | Near consumption zone | Refrigerated capacity | Cold-chain routing |
| **Facility Cost** | Fewer DCs if $C_F$ high | Low real-estate zones | Optimal sizing | Consolidate if $C_F$ high |
| **Transportation Cost** | More DCs if $C_T$ high | Near transport hubs | Loading dock capacity | Minimizing $c_{ij}$ |
| **Inventory Cost** | Fewer DCs if $h$ high | Reduce $I_j$ | Lean sizing | Pool inventory |
| **Competitor Location** | Defensive DC placement | Mirror competitor | Match capacity | Capture competitor customers |
| **Infrastructure Quality** | Road-quality dependent | Highway & port proximity | Modern facility | Mode-specific assignment |
| **Technology / IT** | Hub with high automation | Connected zones | High automation capacity | Real-time assignment |
| **Regulatory / Tax** | Cross-border compliance | Free trade zones | Capacity for compliance | Tariff-optimized sourcing |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Cost-Weighted Trade-Off Function

Define a **total network cost function** that explicitly captures the trade-off:

$$TLC(n) = n \cdot f + \frac{D}{n} \cdot c_t + h \cdot SS_{distributed} \cdot \sqrt{n}$$

where $n$ is the number of facilities, $f$ is facility cost, $D$ is total demand, $c_t$ is transport cost per unit per leg, and $h$ is holding cost per unit.

**Step 1: Differentiate w.r.t. $n$**

$$\frac{d(TLC)}{dn} = f - \frac{D \cdot c_t}{n^2} + \frac{h \cdot SS_{distributed}}{2\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2: Set First-Order Condition to Zero**

$$f - \frac{D \cdot c_t}{n^2} + \frac{h \cdot SS_{distributed}}{2\sqrt{n}} = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve Numerically (Iterative)**

This transcendental equation gives the **optimal number of facilities $n^*$**. The KTU board typically does not require an analytical closed form — students must articulate that $n^*$ balances the three cost components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: When $f$ is high → $n^*$ is small (centralized). When $c_t$ is high → $n^*$ is large (decentralized). When $h$ is high → $n^*$ is small (centralized to reduce inventory).

---

## 3.6 Synthesis: Unified Decision Tree for Distribution Network Design

```
START
  │
  ├─► Is the product HIGH VALUE & LOW VOLUME?
  │       YES → Centralized DC + Air Freight (Hub-and-Spoke)
  │       NO  → Continue ↓
  │
  ├─► Is the product PERISHABLE?
  │       YES → Decentralized Cold-Chain DC
  │       NO  → Continue ↓
  │
  ├─► Is customer SERVICE LEVEL REQUIREMENT very HIGH?
  │       YES → Decentralized Network (Many small DCs)
  │       NO  → Continue ↓
  │
  ├─► Is demand SPATIALLY CONCENTRATED?
  │       YES → Single regional DC
  │       NO  → Multi-region hub-and-spoke
  │
  └─► Apply Cost-Minimization Model (CFLP) to Finalize Network
```

End of Section 3. Section 4 follows.
