## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question carries **3 marks** and tests direct recall or short application. Model answers are provided.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Distribution Network Design and list any FOUR factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Distribution Network Design** is the strategic process of configuring the number, location, and capacity of warehouses/distribution centers along with transportation and inventory policies to deliver products from plants to customers at minimum total cost while satisfying service level requirements.

**The FOUR factors are:**

1. **Customer-related factors** — Demand size, demand density, service level expectations.
2. **Product-related factors** — Product variety, product value, perishability.
3. **Operational cost factors** — Facility cost, inventory cost, transportation cost.
4. **Regulatory/Macro factors** — Tax structure, environmental norms, trade tariffs.

*(Full 3 marks requires defining DND correctly and listing 4 distinct factor categories.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the safety stock pooling effect and its impact on distribution network design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **safety stock pooling effect** refers to the reduction in total safety stock achieved when demand from multiple decentralized locations is consolidated at fewer centralized facilities.

When $n$ independent demand streams with standard deviation $\sigma$ each are pooled at one DC, the pooled standard deviation becomes $\sigma \sqrt{n}$, and hence the pooled safety stock is:

$$SS_{pooled} = \frac{SS_{distributed}}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Impact on Network Design:**
- Centralized networks require **less aggregate safety stock** to serve the same demand.
- This **lowers inventory cost** $C_I$ and favors **centralization**.
- However, centralization increases transportation cost $C_T$ and may reduce service level — hence a **trade-off** is necessary.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU ESE Module pattern, **students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B**. Both choices below carry **14 marks each** with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Explain in detail the various factors affecting distribution network design. Discuss how customer-related and product-related factors influence the choice between a centralized and decentralized network.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Comprehensive List of All Factors

**Model Solution:**

The factors affecting distribution network design can be classified into **six broad categories**:

**1. Customer-Related Factors:**
- **Demand size** — Larger per-customer demand justifies local DCs.
- **Demand density** — High density favors small regional DCs.
- **Service level expectations** — High expectations (next-day delivery) favor decentralization.
- **Lead-time tolerance** — Low patience drives network dispersion.

**2. Product-Related Factors:**
- **Product variety** — High variety favors centralization of slow-movers.
- **Product value** — High-value items need centralized secure storage.
- **Perishability** — Requires cold-chain decentralized network.
- **Substitutability** — Low substitutability demands dedicated lanes.

**3. Operational/Cost Factors:**
- **Facility cost ($C_F$)** — High $C_F$ discourages multiple facilities.
- **Inventory cost ($C_I$)** — High $C_I$ encourages centralization (pooling).
- **Transportation cost ($C_T$)** — High $C_T$ encourages decentralization.
- **Handling cost** — Depends on automation level.

**4. Competitive Factors:**
- Competitor location, network differentiation, first-mover advantage.

**5. Infrastructure & Technology Factors:**
- Road/rail quality, port connectivity, IT/ERP systems, automation, e-commerce readiness.

**6. Regulatory/Macro Factors:**
- Tax structures, tariffs, environmental laws, labor regulations, political stability.

*(7 marks: ~1 mark per category with 2-3 sub-factors each.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Centralized vs Decentralized Trade-Off

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Centralized Network | Decentralized Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Demand size per region** | Low | High |
| **Demand density** | Sparse | Concentrated |
| **Service level** | Standard (3-5 days) | Premium (same/next day) |
| **Lead-time tolerance** | High (industrial buyers) | Low (e-commerce, perishables) |
| **Product variety** | High SKU count at central DC | Limited SKUs at local DCs |
| **Product value** | High (electronics, jewellery) | Low (FMCG, cement) |
| **Perishability** | Non-perishable | Highly perishable |
| **Facility cost ($C_F$)** | High (single large facility) | Low (smaller multiple DCs) |
| **Transportation cost ($C_T$)** | High (long-haul) | Low (short-haul) |
| **Inventory cost ($C_I$)** | Low (pooling effect) | High (distributed safety stock) |
| **Inventory pooling $1/\sqrt{n}$** | Strong benefit | Limited benefit |

**Synthesis:**
- **Centralized networks** are favored when: product value is high, variety is high, demand is sparse, customers tolerate lead time, inventory cost is high.
- **Decentralized networks** are favored when: service level must be very high, product is perishable, demand density is high, transportation cost is high.

*(7 marks: table + synthesis explanation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Discuss the role of operational cost factors in distribution network design. Explain with suitable diagrams and the cost trade-off between facility, inventory, and transportation costs. Derive the safety stock pooling formula.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Operational Cost Factors and Trade-Off Triangle

**Model Solution:**

The **three primary operational cost components** that drive distribution network design are:

**(i) Facility Cost ($C_F$):**
- Fixed cost of operating warehouses, distribution centers, and cross-docks.
- Includes rent, utilities, labor, equipment depreciation.
- Increases linearly with the number of facilities.

$$C_F = \sum_{j=1}^{m} f_j X_j$$

**(ii) Inventory Cost ($C_I$):**
- Cost of holding safety stock, cycle stock, and pipeline stock.
- Includes capital cost, obsolescence, shrinkage, insurance.
- Affected by service level and demand variability.

$$C_I = \sum_{j=1}^{m} h_j I_j$$

**(iii) Transportation Cost ($C_T$):**
- Outbound freight from DC to customer.
- Depends on distance, volume, mode, and rate.
- Increases as the network becomes more centralized (longer hauls).

$$C_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} c_{ij} y_{ij}$$

**Trade-Off Relationship:**

```
        C_F goes UP with more facilities
                       ▲
                       │
   ────────────────────┼─────────────────►
   C_T goes DOWN       │     C_T goes UP with fewer facilities
   with more           │     C_I is LOWER with fewer facilities
   facilities          │     (pooling effect)
                       ▼
        C_I goes DOWN with fewer facilities
        (pooling effect)
```

**Optimal Network:** The optimal number of facilities $n^*$ minimizes:

$$Z(n) = C_F(n) + C_I(n) + C_T(n)$$

*(7 marks: explanation of three costs + trade-off diagram + minimization objective.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Safety Stock Pooling Derivation

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider $n$ decentralized DCs each serving independent demand streams with common standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 2:** Each DC's safety stock for service level $\alpha$ is:

$$SS_i = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma$$

**Step 3:** Total distributed safety stock:

$$SS_{distributed} = n \cdot z_\alpha \cdot \sigma$$

**Step 4:** When consolidated at one DC, the variance of pooled demand is:

$$\sigma_{pooled}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2 = n\sigma^2$$

Hence:

$$\sigma_{pooled} = \sigma\sqrt{n}$$

**Step 5:** Pooled safety stock:

$$SS_{pooled} = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma\sqrt{n}$$

**Step 6:** Ratio:

$$\frac{SS_{pooled}}{SS_{distributed}} = \frac{z_\alpha \sigma\sqrt{n}}{n z_\alpha \sigma} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{SS_{pooled} = \frac{SS_{distributed}}{\sqrt{n}}}$$

**Numerical illustration:**

| $n$ | Reduction in Safety Stock |
|---|---|
| 2 | 29.3% |
| 4 | 50.0% |
| 9 | 66.7% |
| 16 | 75.0% |

**Network Implication:** Pooling provides a strong economic incentive for **centralization**, especially when demand variability is high. However, this must be weighed against the rising $C_T$ of centralization.

*(7 marks: derivation + numerical table + network implication.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):**

1. **Confusing Distribution Network Design with Logistics Management.** DND is a **strategic, long-term** decision; logistics is **operational, day-to-day**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.

2. **Listing factors without explaining their impact.** Simply writing "Customer demand" is worth 0.5 marks. Writing "High customer demand density in a region justifies a local DC because the increased throughput offsets the fixed facility cost" is worth 2 marks.

3. **Forgetting to mention the trade-off.** The KTU board loves the **centralized vs. decentralized trade-off**. Any answer that doesn't mention this earns only a partial score.

4. **Mixing up Type-1 and Type-2 service levels.** Examiners specifically swap terms; know both definitions:
   - **Type-1 (α):** Probability of no stock-out in a replenishment cycle.
   - **Type-2 (β):** Fraction of demand met from stock on hand (fill rate).

5. **Skipping the safety stock pooling derivation.** This is the **single most-tested formula**. Memorize $SS_{pooled} = SS_{distributed} / \sqrt{n}$ and its derivation steps.

6. **Not drawing a diagram.** KTU valuation gives **extra 1-2 marks** for a clear, labeled trade-off triangle or centralized-vs-decentralized comparison chart.

7. **Ignoring Indian context examples.** Where relevant, citing GST states, Indian logistics corridors (Delhi-Mumbai, Chennai-Bengaluru), or specific firms (Reliance, Amazon India, Flipkart) scores bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Students:**

### Core Concept
- [ ] Distribution Network Design = strategic configuration of facilities + inventory + transportation to deliver products at minimum cost with required service level.
- [ ] Six factor categories: **Customer, Product, Operational Cost, Competitive, Infrastructure/Technology, Regulatory/Macro**.

### Key Formulas (MUST memorize)
- [ ] Total Cost: $Z = \min(C_F + C_I + C_T)$
- [ ] Facility Cost: $C_F = \sum f_j X_j$
- [ ] Inventory Cost: $C_I = \sum h_j I_j$
- [ ] Transportation Cost: $C_T = \sum \sum c_{ij} y_{ij}$
- [ ] Safety Stock Pooling: $SS_{pooled} = SS_{distributed} / \sqrt{n}$
- [ ] Break-even demand: $D_{break-even} = f / [c_t \sqrt{A}]$
- [ ] EOQ: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/h}$
- [ ] Lead time: $LT_i = d_{ij}/v + t_{processing}$

### Trade-Off Logic
- [ ] More facilities → $C_F \uparrow$, $C_T \downarrow$, $C_I \uparrow$ (more distributed safety stock).
- [ ] Fewer facilities → $C_F \downarrow$, $C_T \uparrow$, $C_I \downarrow$ (pooling effect).
- [ ] High product value → Centralize.
- [ ] High service level → Decentralize.
- [ ] Perishable products → Decentralize (cold chain).
- [ ] High variety + low demand → Direct shipping.

### Decision Tree
- [ ] Is product high value? → Centralized.
- [ ] Is product perishable? → Decentralized.
- [ ] High service level demand? → Decentralized.
- [ ] Demand concentrated? → Single regional DC.
- [ ] Demand dispersed? → Hub-and-spoke.

### Numerical / Derivable
- [ ] Be ready to derive safety stock pooling from first principles.
- [ ] Be ready to compute break-even demand with given $f$, $c_t$, and region area.
- [ ] Be ready to compute total logistics cost for a 2-facility scenario with given parameters.

### Exam Strategy
- [ ] Always **define** the term in the first sentence of your answer.
- [ ] Always **classify** factors into categories.
- [ ] Always **explain the mechanism** by which each factor affects design.
- [ ] Always **include a diagram** (trade-off triangle or network architecture).
- [ ] Always **mention the safety stock pooling law** when discussing cost factors.
- [ ] Always **conclude** with a synthesis or decision framework.

### Real-World Anchors to Cite
- [ ] Amazon's hub-and-spoke global network.
- [ ] Walmart's cross-docking strategy.
- [ ] Apple's centralized high-value inventory.
- [ ] Amul's decentralized cold-chain.
- [ ] Flipkart/Amazon India's hyperlocal delivery.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Factors Affecting Distribution Network Design.**

**All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE examination.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Network Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Network Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Supply Chain Network Design** is the strategic process of configuring the long-term physical structure of a supply chain — determining the **number, location, size, and assignment** of facilities (plants, warehouses, distribution centers, cross-docks, and retail outlets) — and the **flow of materials, information, and cash** between them, in order to optimize total landed cost while meeting target customer service levels.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, network design sits at the **strategic decision layer** of supply chain planning. It answers four foundational questions:

1. **Where** should facilities be located?
2. **How many** facilities are required?
3. **What** capacity and capability should each facility possess?
4. **Which** customers, products, and suppliers should be assigned to each facility?

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The *role* of network design emphasizes that nearly every operational decision — inventory, transportation, sourcing, and information systems — is **constrained and enabled** by the underlying network configuration. A poor network design locks the firm into decades of sub-optimal cost; a good one compounds value over time.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **water supply system for Kerala** — building dams, treatment plants, and pipeline connections to every household. 

- If you build one giant dam in the north and pipe water 400 km down to Trivandrum, **transport cost** is enormous even if the dam is cheap.
- If you build a small dam in every district, **fixed cost** explodes and maintenance becomes a nightmare.
- The *right* answer lies in a balanced **hub-and-spoke architecture** — a few large plants connected to regional depots that fan out to retailers.

**Supply Chain Network Design is exactly this problem** — but for products, components, and finished goods flowing through factories, warehouses, and stores. The "pipes" are trucks, ships, and planes; the "tanks" are warehouses; the "pressure" is demand. Designing this topology correctly is the single most leveraged decision a supply chain executive ever makes.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terms to Master for KTU Exams:**
> - **Facility** – Any node in the network (plant, DC, warehouse, retailer).
> - **Flow** – Movement of product/information/cash between nodes.
> - **Hub** – A consolidation/transshipment point serving multiple spokes.
> - **Spoke** – A point served by a hub (typically retail or customer).
> - **Service Level** – Often measured as **fill rate** or **order cycle time**; usually **> 95%** in KTU benchmark cases.
> - **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** – Procurement + Production + Inventory + Transportation + Facility + Sourcing cost.
> - **Strategic Horizon** – **3 to 10 years** (network decisions are capital-intensive and irreversible).
> - **Lead Time** – Time between order placement and receipt; a key driver of safety stock and number of facilities.

---

## 1.4 Why "Role" — and Not Just "Network Design"?

The word **role** in the KTU syllabus is deliberate. The examiner is testing whether the student understands **why** the topic is positioned as the *first* topic in a supply chain course. Network design is the **upstream constraint** for every downstream decision:

> A **15% reduction in network cost** typically yields a **2–5% reduction** in final product cost. By contrast, a **15% reduction in transportation cost alone** yields only a **0.5–1%** reduction in final product cost (Chopra & Meindl, framework adopted in KTU pedagogy).

This **leverage asymmetry** is precisely the *role* of network design — it is the highest-leverage decision in supply chain management.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Decision Levels in Network Design

Network design decisions are classified by **time horizon and reversibility**:

| Decision Level | Time Horizon | Reversibility | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | 3–10 years | **Highly irreversible** | Number & location of plants, DCs; supply base structure |
| **Tactical** | 1 quarter – 1 year | Partially reversible | Warehouse layout, inventory targets, transportation mode choice |
| **Operational** | Real-time / daily | Fully reversible | Specific shipment routing, daily dispatch, picker assignment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The examiner frequently tests whether students can correctly classify a given decision. *Example Q: "Deciding the number of warehouses" — Answer: **Strategic** (because of high capital cost and long useful life of 10–25 years).*

---

## 2.2 The Role of Network Design — Six Functional Roles

Network design plays **six interlocking roles** in any supply chain:

1. **Cost Anchor Role** – Determines the baseline cost structure. Facility fixed costs and transportation distances are baked in for years.
2. **Service Enablement Role** – The number and location of facilities dictate achievable **order cycle time** and **fill rate**.
3. **Inventory Buffer Role** – More facilities reduce safety stock at each node (risk pooling inverted), but raise in-transit pipeline inventory.
4. **Strategic Flexibility Role** – A well-designed network can absorb demand surges, new product launches, and supplier disruptions.
5. **Sourcing Enablement Role** – Plant locations define feasible sourcing regions and dictate inbound freight cost.
6. **Information & Coordination Role** – Geographic spread determines the latency, accuracy, and cost of information flows.

---

## 2.3 Factors Influencing Network Design (High-Yield List)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to know **the strategic factors** that shape network decisions. These are best memorized as the **S.T.E.E.P.L.E.** mnemonic:

> [!NOTE]
> **S.T.E.E.P.L.E. Factors in Network Design**
> - **S** – Strategic Objectives (cost leadership vs. responsiveness)
> - **T** – Technology (automation, WMS, ERP)
> - **E** – Economic conditions (land cost, tax incentives, labor rates)
> - **E** – Environmental & Sustainability regulations
> - **P** – Political & Trade Policy (tariffs, FTAs, GST in India context)
> - **L** – Labor availability & skill profile
> - **E** – Customer expectations (delivery speed, omni-channel)

Other critical operational factors include:
- **Demand characteristics** — volume, variability, geographic spread.
- **Product characteristics** — value density, shelf life, perishability.
- **Cost structure** — fixed facility cost vs. variable transport cost.
- **Competitive positioning** — proximity to competitors' customers.

---

## 2.4 The Cost–Service Trade-Off (Central Tension)

The *role* of network design is best understood as the **explicit balancing** of two competing forces:

$$C_{total} = C_{facility} + C_{transport} + C_{inventory} + C_{sourcing} + C_{information}$$

As the **number of facilities increases**:
- **Facility cost ↑** (more fixed cost: rent, salaries, utilities)
- **Transportation cost ↓** (customers are closer)
- **Inventory cost ↓** (smaller, more responsive buffers)
- **Service level ↑** (shorter delivery lead times)

The **optimal number of facilities** is where the **marginal cost of opening one more facility equals the marginal cost saved** on transport + inventory + service penalty.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Network Design Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are examinable under KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_x = \dfrac{\sum_i d_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_i d_i}$ | Center of Gravity (x-coordinate) | km / coordinate units |
| 2 | $C_y = \dfrac{\sum_i d_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_i d_i}$ | Center of Gravity (y-coordinate) | km / coordinate units |
| 3 | $LD = \sum_i \sum_j d_{ij} \cdot v_{ij}$ | Load-Distance Score (network efficiency) | ton·km |
| 4 | $TC = F + v \cdot D$ | Total Cost (fixed + variable × distance) | ₹ |
| 5 | $N^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_c \cdot N_0}{V_c}}$ | Optimal number of facilities (cost-balance) | dimensionless (round up) |
| 6 | $TL = LT_{processing} + LT_{transport} + LT_{waiting}$ | Total Lead Time | days / hours |
| 7 | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Safety Stock (driven by lead-time variability) | units |
| 8 | $Fill \, Rate = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Units Short}}{\text{Units Demanded}}$ | Service Level Metric | % (decimal) |
| 9 | $TLC = C_{proc} + C_{prod} + C_{inv} + C_{trans} + C_{fac}$ | Total Landed Cost | ₹ / unit |
| 10 | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Euclidean distance between node i and j | km |

> **Notation key:** $d_i$ = demand (or volume) at location *i*; $v_{ij}$ = volume shipped between *i* and *j*; $F$ = fixed facility cost (₹/yr); $v$ = variable cost (₹/ton-km); $D$ = total distance traveled; $N_0$ = baseline number of facilities; $F_c$ = fixed cost of one facility; $V_c$ = variable cost saving per unit distance reduction (₹/ton-km); $z$ = standard normal value for service level (e.g., $z = 1.65$ for 95%).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Network Design Models

| Industry | Typical Network Design Question |
|---|---|
| **FMCG (e.g., Hindustan Unilever)** | How many C&F agents should cover Kerala? Where should the regional DC be? |
| **E-commerce (e.g., Amazon India)** | How many sort centers and last-mile delivery stations are needed? |
| **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors)** | Plant location for new EV line; assignment of suppliers to plants. |
| **Pharma (e.g., Cipla)** | Cold-chain warehouse location; regulatory zone mapping. |
| **Apparel (e.g., Myntra)** | Drop-ship vs. centralized fulfillment trade-off. |

In every case, the **role** of network design is to translate business strategy into a **physical logistics footprint** that minimizes total cost subject to service constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Center of Gravity (CoG) Method

The CoG method treats the optimal facility location as the **weighted average position** of all demand (or supply) points, where the weights are the volumes handled. It minimizes the **load-distance score** under a linear (or Euclidean) distance assumption.

### 3.1.1 Objective Function

Minimize the total load-distance:

$$LD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot d_{i,facility}$$

For Euclidean distance:

$$LD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot \sqrt{(x_i - X)^2 + (y_i - Y)^2}$$

where $(X, Y)$ is the candidate facility location and $(x_i, y_i)$ is the location of demand point *i* with volume $d_i$.

### 3.1.2 First-Order Optimality Conditions

To minimize, take partial derivatives w.r.t. *X* and *Y* and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial LD}{\partial X} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot \frac{(x_i - X)}{d_{i,facility}} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial LD}{\partial Y} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot \frac{(y_i - Y)}{d_{i,facility}} = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for X and Y

Recognizing that the term $\frac{1}{d_{i,facility}}$ is cumbersome, the **approximate closed-form** (which is what KTU students should memorize) is obtained by assuming $d_{i,facility} \approx \bar{d}$ (average distance), yielding:

$$\boxed{X^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i}, \quad Y^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i}}$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A logistics manager must locate a central warehouse to serve three retail outlets. The data is given below:

| Outlet | x-coordinate (km) | y-coordinate (km) | Monthly Demand (tons) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 50 | 100 | 1000 |
| B | 100 | 150 | 1500 |
| C | 75 | 125 | 2000 |

**Find the optimal warehouse location using the Center of Gravity method.**

**Step 1:** Compute the total demand.

$$\sum_{i} d_i = 1000 + 1500 + 2000 = 4500 \text{ tons}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the weighted sum of x-coordinates.

$$\sum_i d_i \cdot x_i = (1000 \times 50) + (1500 \times 100) + (2000 \times 75)$$

Evaluating term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
1000 \times 50 &= 50{,}000 \\
1500 \times 100 &= 150{,}000 \\
2000 \times 75 &= 150{,}000 \\
\text{Sum} &= 350{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the weighted sum of y-coordinates.

$$\sum_i d_i \cdot y_i = (1000 \times 100) + (1500 \times 150) + (2000 \times 125)$$

Evaluating term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
1000 \times 100 &= 100{,}000 \\
1500 \times 150 &= 225{,}000 \\
2000 \times 125 &= 250{,}000 \\
\text{Sum} &= 575{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Center of Gravity formulas.

$$\begin{aligned}
X^* &= \frac{350{,}000}{4500} = 77.78 \text{ km} \\
Y^* &= \frac{575{,}000}{4500} = 127.78 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation. The optimal warehouse should be located at **(77.78, 127.78) km** in the given coordinate frame. The manager can then use this as a starting point for practical site selection, adjusting for land cost, highway access, and zoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Calculation Tip:** The CoG gives a **theoretical** optimum. In practice, an iterative refinement is needed because real distances are **not Euclidean** (they follow road networks). KTU exam problems, however, typically use the closed-form CoG as above.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Load-Distance Score (Network Efficiency)

The load-distance score quantifies **how efficient** an existing or proposed network is. Lower scores mean more efficient networks.

### 3.2.1 General Form

For a network with $m$ supply points and $n$ demand points:

$$LD = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$$

where $v_{ij}$ is the volume shipped from supply point *i* to demand point *j*, and $d_{ij}$ is the distance.

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A firm has 2 plants serving 3 warehouses. Compute the load-distance score.

| Plant → Warehouse | Volume $v_{ij}$ (tons) | Distance $d_{ij}$ (km) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 → W1 | 200 | 50 |
| P1 → W2 | 100 | 100 |
| P1 → W3 | 50 | 150 |
| P2 → W1 | 0 | 200 |
| P2 → W2 | 150 | 30 |
| P2 → W3 | 200 | 80 |

**Step 1:** Compute each lane contribution $v_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P1 \to W1 &: \quad 200 \times 50 = 10{,}000 \\
P1 \to W2 &: \quad 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 \\
P1 \to W3 &: \quad 50 \times 150 = 7{,}500 \\
P2 \to W1 &: \quad 0 \times 200 = 0 \\
P2 \to W2 &: \quad 150 \times 30 = 4{,}500 \\
P2 \to W3 &: \quad 200 \times 80 = 16{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Sum all contributions.

$$LD = 10{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 7{,}500 + 0 + 4{,}500 + 16{,}000 = 48{,}000 \text{ ton-km}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. This score can be used to **compare alternative network configurations**; the configuration with the lowest LD is preferred (subject to capacity and service constraints).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimal Number of Facilities (Cost-Balance Approximation)

### 3.3.1 Setting Up the Total Cost Function

Assume:
- $F$ = fixed cost of operating one facility (₹/yr)
- $v$ = variable transportation cost per ton-km (₹/ton-km)
- $T$ = total annual tonnage
- $D(N)$ = average ton-km per ton as a function of N facilities

If doubling the number of facilities roughly halves the average distance (square-root relationship):

$$D(N) \approx \frac{D_0}{\sqrt{N}}$$

Then:

$$TC(N) = N \cdot F + v \cdot T \cdot \frac{D_0}{\sqrt{N}}$$

### 3.3.2 Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$\frac{dTC}{dN} = F - \frac{v \cdot T \cdot D_0}{2 \cdot N^{3/2}} = 0$$

### 3.3.3 Solve for N*

$$F = \frac{v \cdot T \cdot D_0}{2 \cdot N^{*3/2}}$$

$$\boxed{N^* = \left(\frac{v \cdot T \cdot D_0}{2 F}\right)^{2/3}}$$

### 3.3.4 Quick Numerical Example

Given: $v = 5$ ₹/ton-km, $T = 100{,}000$ tons/yr, $D_0 = 500$ km, $F = 20{,}00{,}000$ ₹/yr per facility.

Compute numerator:

$$v \cdot T \cdot D_0 = 5 \times 100{,}000 \times 500 = 25 \times 10^7 = 2.5 \times 10^8$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{v \cdot T \cdot D_0}{2F} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^6} = 62.5$$

Raise to the power 2/3:

$$N^* = (62.5)^{2/3} = (62.5^{1/3})^2$$

Since $62.5^{1/3} \approx 3.97$, we have $N^* \approx (3.97)^2 \approx 15.7$. Round up to **16 facilities**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The $N^* = (\cdot)^{2/3}$ form shows **diminishing returns** — quadrupling the budget does not quadruple the number of facilities. This is the **cost-service scale economy** that justifies hub-and-spoke designs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Choice Summary

| Decision Type | When to Add a Facility | When to Close a Facility | Role in Network |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity-driven** | Existing facilities > **85% utilization** | Utilization < **40%** for > 2 years | Cost-anchor |
| **Service-driven** | Lead time > SLA, fill rate < target | Lead time < SLA by wide margin, inventory pile-up | Service enablement |
| **Risk-driven** | Single-source dependency on one region | Geopolitical concentration risk high | Resilience |
| **Strategic** | New market entry / new product launch | Exit from a market | Strategic flexibility |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
> **Define Supply Chain Network Design. List any two strategic decisions involved in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Supply Chain Network Design is the strategic process of configuring the long-term physical structure of a supply chain, including the **number, location, size, and capacity** of facilities (plants, warehouses, distribution centers) and the **assignment of customers and products** to these facilities, with the objective of optimizing total landed cost while meeting target service levels.

**Two strategic decisions (1 mark):**
1. Number and geographic location of plants and warehouses.
2. Choice between centralized vs. decentralized distribution structure.
3. (Any other valid: capacity sizing, supplier base selection.)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> **State the Center of Gravity method. Write its formula for finding the x-coordinate of the optimal location.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Center of Gravity method is an **analytical technique** used to find the optimal geographic location of a facility by treating it as the **weighted average of the coordinates of demand (or supply) points**, where the weights are the volumes handled at each point.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$X^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i}$$

**Explanation (1 mark):** Here $d_i$ is the demand (or volume) at point $i$ and $x_i$ is its x-coordinate. The analogous formula for $Y^*$ replaces $x_i$ with $y_i$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A [14 Marks] — KTU ESE Module 1 Choice Option 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **strategic role** of network design in supply chain management. Discuss how it influences cost, service level, and inventory. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Three retail outlets are located at coordinates **(20, 30), (50, 80), and (90, 40)** with monthly demands of **500, 800, and 700 tons** respectively. Using the **Center of Gravity method**, determine the optimal location for a new warehouse. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The strategic role of network design is foundational because it determines the **physical footprint** over which all other supply chain decisions operate. The major roles are:

1. **Cost Anchor Role (2 marks):** Network design fixes facility costs (rent, salaries, utilities) and inbound/outbound transportation distances for years. Chopra & Meindl's framework states that a **5–10% improvement in network design** typically yields a **2–5% reduction in total supply chain cost**, which is the highest leverage of any SC decision.

2. **Service Enablement Role (2 marks):** The number and proximity of facilities directly determine the achievable **order cycle time** and **fill rate**. A widely distributed network gives 24–48 hour delivery; a centralized one may take 5–7 days. Network design therefore sets the **upper bound** of customer service.

3. **Inventory Buffer Role (1 mark):** More facilities reduce safety stock at each node but raise in-transit pipeline inventory. The total inventory is a function of network topology (square-root law for centralized vs. decentralized).

4. **Strategic Flexibility Role (1 mark):** A well-designed network can absorb new product launches, demand surges, and supplier disruptions without re-architecting.

5. **Sourcing Enablement Role (1 mark):** Plant location defines the feasible supplier base and inbound freight cost.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**

| Outlet | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $d_i$ (tons) |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | 20 | 30 | 500 |
| O2 | 50 | 80 | 800 |
| O3 | 90 | 40 | 700 |

**Step 1: Total Demand (1 mark)**

$$\sum_i d_i = 500 + 800 + 700 = 2000 \text{ tons}$$

**Step 2: Weighted sum of x-coordinates (1.5 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i d_i x_i &= (500 \times 20) + (800 \times 50) + (700 \times 90) \\
&= 10{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 63{,}000 \\
&= 113{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Weighted sum of y-coordinates (1.5 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i d_i y_i &= (500 \times 30) + (800 \times 80) + (700 \times 40) \\
&= 15{,}000 + 64{,}000 + 28{,}000 \\
&= 107{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply Center of Gravity formulas (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
X^* &= \frac{113{,}000}{2000} = 56.5 \text{ km} \\
Y^* &= \frac{107{,}000}{2000} = 53.5 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark)**

The optimal location for the new warehouse is **(56.5, 53.5) km**. This point is biased toward Outlet O2 (which has the highest demand of 800 tons) but also considers the geographic spread of all three outlets.

---

> ### **Question B [14 Marks] — KTU ESE Module 1 Choice Option 2**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **factors influencing supply chain network design** decisions. Use the **S.T.E.E.P.L.E.** framework for your answer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company ships goods from 2 plants to 3 warehouses as per the table below. Compute the **Load-Distance Score** for the network. If the company is evaluating an alternative configuration with an LD score of **35,000 ton-km**, recommend whether the current or alternative network should be adopted. **(7 marks)**

| Lane | Volume (tons) | Distance (km) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 → W1 | 300 | 40 |
| P1 → W2 | 200 | 90 |
| P1 → W3 | 100 | 120 |
| P2 → W1 | 50 | 180 |
| P2 → W2 | 250 | 30 |
| P2 → W3 | 300 | 70 |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The S.T.E.E.P.L.E. framework captures all major external and internal drivers of network design:

1. **Strategic Objectives (1 mark):** Cost leadership vs. responsiveness vs. differentiation. A cost leader centralizes; a responsiveness leader decentralizes.

2. **Technology (1 mark):** Automation, WMS/ERP maturity, and visibility tools influence how far a single facility can serve.

3. **Economic conditions (1 mark):** Land prices, tax incentives, electricity tariffs, and labor costs in candidate regions.

4. **Environmental regulations (1 mark):** Carbon caps, e-waste norms, plastic-packaging rules affect site selection.

5. **Political & Trade Policy (1 mark):** Tariffs, FTAs, GST zones, and SEZ benefits shape cross-border and inter-state network choices.

6. **Labor availability & skill (1 mark):** Skill-intensive products (e.g., electronics) need clusters with engineering talent.

7. **Customer expectations (1 mark):** Same-day delivery expectations drive the proliferation of forward stocking points.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Lane-wise product $v_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ (3 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P1 \to W1 &: \; 300 \times 40 = 12{,}000 \\
P1 \to W2 &: \; 200 \times 90 = 18{,}000 \\
P1 \to W3 &: \; 100 \times 120 = 12{,}000 \\
P2 \to W1 &: \; 50 \times 180 = 9{,}000 \\
P2 \to W2 &: \; 250 \times 30 = 7{,}500 \\
P2 \to W3 &: \; 300 \times 70 = 21{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sum the contributions (2 marks)**

$$LD_{current} = 12{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 9{,}000 + 7{,}500 + 21{,}000 = 79{,}500 \text{ ton-km}$$

**Step 3: Comparison (1 mark)**

$$LD_{current} = 79{,}500 \text{ ton-km} \quad \text{vs.} \quad LD_{alt} = 35{,}000 \text{ ton-km}$$

The alternative network has a **much lower LD score** (55.97% lower), which means it requires **less ton-km of transport** for the same volume shipped.

**Step 4: Recommendation (1 mark)**

The **alternative network should be adopted**, subject to:
- Capacity feasibility at each warehouse
- Customer service level validation
- Implementation cost comparison (facility relocation cost may offset the transport saving)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing strategic with operational decisions** — *Adding one more truck* is operational; *opening a new warehouse* is strategic. Marks lost in classification questions.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the Center of Gravity answer** — Always state the units (km) of $X^*$ and $Y^*$.
> 3. **In Load-Distance problems, omitting the multiplication** — Students often sum only distances or only volumes. Each lane must be $v_{ij} \times d_{ij}$.
> 4. **Recommending a network based solely on LD score** — Always caveat with service level, capacity, and implementation cost. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for one-sided answers.
> 5. **In Part B (a) on S.T.E.E.P.L.E., students often list only 4–5 factors** — Examiners expect all 7 (or at least 6). Each factor is worth 1 mark.
> 6. **Calculation errors in the weighted sum** — Double-check the term-by-term evaluation. Even one wrong product (e.g., $700 \times 90 = 63{,}000$) loses 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Role of Network Design**
>
> **Core Concepts (Must Memorize):**
> - Network design = **strategic, long-horizon, capital-intensive** decision on number, location, size, and assignment of facilities.
> - Network design is the **highest-leverage** SC decision (5–10% improvement → 2–5% cost reduction).
> - Three decision levels: **Strategic (3–10 yrs)**, **Tactical (1 qtr – 1 yr)**, **Operational (daily)**.
> - Six functional roles: Cost Anchor, Service Enabler, Inventory Buffer, Strategic Flexibility, Sourcing Enabler, Information Hub.
> - **S.T.E.E.P.L.E.** factors: Strategic, Technology, Economic, Environmental, Political, Labor, Expectations.
> - Central trade-off: **More facilities → ↑ facility cost, ↓ transport & inventory cost, ↑ service level**.
>
> **Key Formulas (Must Memorize for Numerical Questions):**
> - Center of Gravity: $X^* = \frac{\sum d_i x_i}{\sum d_i}$, $Y^* = \frac{\sum d_i y_i}{\sum d_i}$
> - Load-Distance Score: $LD = \sum_i \sum_j v_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$
> - Total Cost: $TC = N \cdot F + v \cdot T \cdot D_0 / \sqrt{N}$
> - Optimal facilities: $N^* = (v T D_0 / 2F)^{2/3}$ (round up to integer)
> - Service Level Fill Rate: $1 - (\text{Units Short} / \text{Units Demanded})$
>
> **Exam-Day Quick Wins:**
> - Always state **units** in numerical answers.
> - Always include a **caveat** when recommending a network ("subject to capacity, service, and implementation cost").
> - Use **block diagrams or tables** to show the data clearly before calculation.
> - Use the **S.T.E.E.P.L.E.** acronym verbatim in theory questions.
> - For "role of network design" questions, always lead with the **leverage / cost-anchor** argument.
>
> **Common Conceptual Confusions (Avoid):**
> - Network design ≠ Transportation planning (one is strategic, the other is tactical/operational).
> - CoG gives a **theoretical** optimum — real-world adjustment needed.
> - LD score measures **transport efficiency**, not total cost — must combine with facility cost for full evaluation.
> - "More warehouses is always better for service" — FALSE; total cost may explode. Balance is the *role* of network design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models for Designing Regional Network Configuration; Impact of Globalisation on Supply Chain Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Models for Designing Regional Network Configuration; Impact of Globalisation on Supply Chain Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Regional Network Configuration

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, **Regional Network Configuration** refers to the strategic planning framework that determines the optimal placement, capacity, and role of facilities (plants, warehouses, distribution centers, cross-docks) within a defined geographic region to serve customer demand at minimum total cost while satisfying service-level constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A regional supply chain network configuration model is a mathematical optimization framework that simultaneously decides the *number*, *location*, *capacity allocation*, and *flow assignment* of facilities across a region such that total logistics costs (production + inventory + transportation + facility fixed costs) are minimized subject to demand satisfaction and capacity constraints.

The three fundamental decisions in network design are:
1. **Facility Role Assignment** — Whether a facility acts as a *manufacturing plant*, *finished-goods warehouse*, or *distribution hub*.
2. **Facility Location** — The geographic coordinates where each facility will be sited.
3. **Capacity & Flow Allocation** — How much each facility produces/holds and how it serves demand zones.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Kerala Spice Route

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine you are the CEO of a Kochi-based spice export company. Your turmeric is sourced from Erode, your cardamom from Idukki, and your pepper from Wayanad. You can either:
> - **Option A:** Build one huge central processing plant in Kochi and ship raw spices inward (Hub-and-Spoke).
> - **Option B:** Build three mini processing plants in each growing district and ship finished powder outward (Decentralized).
> - **Option C:** Build one mega plant + two regional warehouses for value-added services (Hybrid).
>
> Each choice has different costs, lead times, and risks. **Regional Network Configuration Models** are the mathematical tools that help you pick the best option using real data (demand, distances, costs).

## 1.2 Defining Globalisation in Supply Chain Context

**Globalisation** is the integration of economic activities across national borders through trade liberalization, reduced trade barriers, advances in ICT, and standardized logistics protocols — leading firms to source, produce, and distribute on a worldwide scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Chopra & Meindl Framework):** Globalisation in supply chain networks refers to the strategic decision of a firm to *locate facilities*, *source materials*, and *serve markets* across multiple countries to leverage cost differentials, market access, and competitive advantages — fundamentally altering the structure, length, and risk profile of the supply chain.

### Intuitive Overview of Globalisation's Impact

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation:** Think of globalisation as the difference between a fisherman selling only at the local Cochin beach versus exporting to supermarkets in Dubai, Singapore, and London. His supply chain grows longer, more complex, and more profitable — but also more vulnerable to currency swings, port strikes, and foreign regulations. Modern supply chains (think Apple iPhones or Kitex garments) span 40+ countries, requiring sophisticated models to design them.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Network Models

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed facility cost | $f_i$ | ₹/year |
| Variable production cost | $c_i$ | ₹/unit |
| Transportation cost | $t_{ij}$ | ₹/unit |
| Demand at zone $j$ | $D_j$ | units/year |
| Distance | $d_{ij}$ | km |
| Facility capacity | $K_i$ | units/year |
| Service-level constraint | $s$ | days / hours |
| Total network cost | $Z$ | ₹/year |

These constants/metrics appear repeatedly in KTU board exam problems, so commit them to memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Network Configuration Models

Network design models can be classified along three primary axes:

### A. By Decision Type

| Model Type | Decisions Made | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Facility Location Model** | Where to open facilities | Set Covering, Maximal Covering |
| **Capacity Allocation Model** | How much capacity at each site | Linear Programming (LP) |
| **Network Flow Model** | Which routes, how much flow | Transportation / Transshipment |
| **Integrated Network Model** | All of the above simultaneously | Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) |

### B. By Solution Approach

1. **Optimization Models** — Provide provably optimal solutions (LP, MILP).
2. **Heuristic Models** — Provide good solutions quickly (Greedy, Genetic Algorithms).
3. **Simulation Models** — Evaluate a given configuration under uncertainty.

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU Cares:** The Module 1 syllabus explicitly emphasises *models* — this means you must understand the mathematical formulation, not just the strategic intuition. Expect derivation questions in Part B.

## 2.2 The Four Classical Models for Regional Network Design

### Model 1: The Gravity Location Model (Single Facility Placement)

Used when a company must locate **one** new facility (e.g., a warehouse) to serve existing demand points with minimum weighted distance.

The core principle is the **Center of Gravity (CoG) Method** borrowed from physics: the optimal location is the weighted centroid of all demand points.

### Model 2: The Plant Location (Capacitated) Model

Decides *which* of $n$ candidate plants to open and *how much* to ship from each to $m$ markets, given capacity limits. Formulated as a **Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP)**.

### Model 3: The Warehouse Location Model

A specialised version where only warehouses are sited (plants exist). Solved using **Branch-and-Bound** or **Lagrangian Relaxation**.

### Model 4: The Hub-and-Spoke Network Model

Used in regional designs (e.g., airline networks, e-commerce). Hub facilities consolidate flows from multiple origins before forwarding to destinations.

## 2.3 The Center of Gravity (CoG) Method — Detailed Theory

Given $n$ demand points with locations $(x_j, y_j)$ and weights $w_j$ (typically demand volume), the optimal facility coordinates are:

$$X^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j \cdot w_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \qquad Y^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j \cdot w_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$$

### Why Does This Formula Work?

- It is the **2-D extension of the arithmetic mean**, weighted by importance.
- It minimises $\sum w_j \cdot \sqrt{(X-x_j)^2 + (Y-y_j)^2}$, the weighted Euclidean distance.
- It assumes *equal transportation cost per km* across all directions (rarely true in practice — a major limitation).

### Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Center of Gravity

1. Plot all demand points on a Cartesian grid (longitude on X, latitude on Y).
2. Multiply each coordinate by its demand (weight).
3. Sum the weighted X values; divide by total demand.
4. Repeat for Y.
5. The result $(X^*, Y^*)$ is the cost-minimising single-facility location.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation:** CoG assumes (a) Euclidean distance = transport cost, (b) flat geography, (c) no facility fixed cost. Real Indian terrain (Western Ghats, NE India) breaks these assumptions. Always comment on limitations in your KTU answer.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Center of Gravity X** | $$X^* = \frac{\sum x_j w_j}{\sum w_j}$$ | Single-facility placement |
| **Center of Gravity Y** | $$Y^* = \frac{\sum y_j w_j}{\sum w_j}$$ | Single-facility placement |
| **MILP Objective (Plant Loc.)** | $$\min Z = \sum_i f_i y_i + \sum_i \sum_j c_{ij} x_{ij}$$ | Multi-facility selection |
| **Capacity Constraint** | $$\sum_j x_{ij} \leq K_i y_i, \quad \forall i$$ | Plant location |
| **Demand Satisfaction** | $$\sum_i x_{ij} = D_j, \quad \forall j$$ | All market models |
| **Binary Variable** | $$y_i \in \{0, 1\}$$ | Open/close decision |
| **Total Logistics Cost** | $$TC = TC_p + TC_t + TC_w + TC_i$$ | Network evaluation |
| **Landed Cost** | $$LC = \text{Cost} + \text{Freight} + \text{Duty} + \text{Insurance}$$ | Global sourcing |
| **Pipeline Inventory** | $$I_p = D \cdot T$$ | Global supply chains |
| **Total Cost Curve** | $$TC(Q) = a + bQ + cQ^2$$ | Capacity sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, note that all mathematical notation uses LaTeX-safe delimiters inside markdown cells. The pipe character $\vert$ is rendered as `$\vert$` only when explicitly needed — never use a raw $\vert$ inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Industrial Engineering:** Plant layout and facility planning.
- **Civil Engineering:** Optimal placement of material yards for highway/metro projects.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Optimal location of after-sales service centres.
- **Computer Science:** Solving MILPs using solvers like CPLEX, Gurobi (operational research).
- **Management Consulting:** Retail site selection (Starbucks, DMart use CoG variants).

## 2.6 Impact of Globalisation — Theoretical Framework

Globalisation restructures supply chains in six measurable ways:

| Dimension | Pre-Globalisation | Post-Globalisation |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Span** | Single country | Multi-continent |
| **Sourcing Strategy** | Domestic only | Global multi-sourcing |
| **Lead Time** | Short, predictable | Long, variable |
| **Risk Profile** | Low (single jurisdiction) | High (currency, political) |
| **Cost Structure** | High labor, low transport | Low labor, high transport |
| **Information Flow** | Manual, slow | Digital, real-time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:** Globalisation is *not* just about offshoring — it is about *networking* decisions across borders. The same MILP framework applies, but with added layers: tariffs, currency, lead-time variability, and trade agreements.

## 2.7 Drivers and Barriers of Globalisation

**Enablers:**
- WTO trade liberalisation
- Containerisation (post-1956)
- IT/Internet (post-1995)
- Falling communication costs (telecom bandwidth)
- Standardised logistics (ISO containers, INCOTERMS)

**Barriers:**
- Tariffs and non-tariff barriers
- Exchange rate volatility
- Political instability (sanctions, war)
- ESG and sustainability pressures
- Pandemic-style disruptions (COVID-19 effect)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Center of Gravity Formula

### Problem Setup
Given $n$ demand points at $(x_j, y_j)$ with weights $w_j$ ($j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$), find the point $(X, Y)$ that minimises the weighted total Euclidean distance:

$$\min \; D(X,Y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \sqrt{(X - x_j)^2 + (Y - y_j)^2}$$

### Step 1 — Take the Partial Derivative with Respect to $X$

$$\frac{\partial D}{\partial X} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \frac{X - x_j}{\sqrt{(X - x_j)^2 + (Y - y_j)^2}}$$

**Logic:** Differentiate each term $\sqrt{(X-x_j)^2 + (Y-y_j)^2}$ with respect to $X$. By chain rule, the derivative of $\sqrt{u}$ is $\frac{1}{2\sqrt{u}} \cdot \frac{du}{dX}$, and $\frac{d}{dX}(X-x_j)^2 = 2(X-x_j)$.

### Step 2 — Set the Derivative to Zero (First-Order Optimality)

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \frac{X - x_j}{\sqrt{(X-x_j)^2 + (Y-y_j)^2}} = 0$$

**Logic:** At a minimum, the gradient must equal zero (no further descent possible).

### Step 3 — Approximate Using a Key Assumption

Assume the weights and distances are such that the denominator can be approximated by a constant $\lambda$ (or, in the simplest case, drop the denominator — equivalent to using *Manhattan distance* in the limit):

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot (X - x_j) = 0$$

**Logic:** This simplification is the standard *unweighted-by-distance* Center of Gravity used in textbooks. It produces a closed-form solution.

### Step 4 — Expand and Solve for $X$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j X - \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j = 0$$

$$X \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j$$

$$\boxed{X^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}}$$

**Logic:** Factor out the constant $X$ and isolate. The result is the *demand-weighted mean* of the X-coordinates.

### Step 5 — Repeat for Y (by Symmetry)

By identical derivation with respect to $Y$:

$$\boxed{Y^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j y_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "derive the CoG formula" in a 7-mark question, write all five steps above. Even if you skip Step 3, mention the assumption explicitly — KTU examiners reward methodological awareness.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A firm plans to build a regional warehouse to serve four retail stores in Kerala. Demand and location data:

| Store $j$ | Location $(x_j, y_j)$ in km | Demand $w_j$ (tonnes/year) |
|---|---|---|
| A (Kochi) | (10, 8) | 500 |
| B (Thrissur) | (20, 25) | 300 |
| C (Kozhikode) | (5, 40) | 400 |
| D (Trivandrum) | (15, 5) | 600 |

**Total Demand:** $\sum w_j = 500 + 300 + 400 + 600 = 1800$ tonnes.

### Step 1 — Compute $\sum w_j x_j$

$$\sum w_j x_j = (500)(10) + (300)(20) + (400)(5) + (600)(15)$$
$$= 5000 + 6000 + 2000 + 9000 = 22000$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\sum w_j y_j$

$$\sum w_j y_j = (500)(8) + (300)(25) + (400)(40) + (600)(5)$$
$$= 4000 + 7500 + 16000 + 3000 = 30500$$

### Step 3 — Apply the CoG Formula

$$X^* = \frac{22000}{1800} = 12.22 \; \text{km}$$

$$Y^* = \frac{30500}{1800} = 16.94 \; \text{km}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

The optimal warehouse should be located approximately at coordinates **(12.22, 16.94)**, which lies between Kochi and Thrissur (closer to Kochi due to higher demand — note how Trivandrum's high demand (600) pulls the location southward despite Trivandrum being far south).

> [!NOTE]
> **Observational Insight:** Compare $X^* = 12.22$ to the unweighted average $X_{\text{avg}} = \frac{10+20+5+15}{4} = 12.5$. The weighted result is very close — but $Y^* = 16.94$ vs $Y_{\text{avg}} = 19.5$. Trivandrum's low $y$-coordinate (5) and high demand (600) pulls the location *south*, making $Y^* < Y_{\text{avg}}$. This is the entire practical value of CoG.

## 3.3 Formulation of the Plant Location MILP

### Sets and Indices
- $i \in I$: candidate plant sites, $i = 1, \ldots, m$
- $j \in J$: market/demand zones, $j = 1, \ldots, n$

### Parameters
- $f_i$ = annual fixed cost of opening plant $i$ (₹)
- $c_{ij}$ = per-unit production + transport cost from $i$ to $j$ (₹/unit)
- $K_i$ = annual capacity of plant $i$ (units)
- $D_j$ = annual demand at zone $j$ (units)

### Decision Variables
$$y_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if plant } i \text{ is opened} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$x_{ij} = \text{quantity shipped from plant } i \text{ to market } j \; (\text{units})$$

### Complete Formulation

$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

**Subject to:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \leq K_i y_i, \quad \forall i \in I \quad \text{(Capacity)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = D_j, \quad \forall j \in J \quad \text{(Demand satisfaction)}$$

$$x_{ij} \geq 0, \quad y_i \in \{0, 1\} \quad \text{(Non-negativity & binary)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The product $K_i y_i$ in the capacity constraint is the trick — it forces $x_{ij} = 0$ if $y_i = 0$. This is the standard MILP "big-M" logic and is a frequent 2-mark question in KTU Part A.

## 3.4 Globalisation Impact — Quantitative Example

**Scenario:** An Indian garment firm (e.g., Kitex Garments, Kerala) sources cotton from Gujarat, dyes it in Tirupur, stitches in Kerala, and exports to the EU.

### Total Cost Computation

$$TC_{\text{domestic}} = c_{\text{prod}}^{\text{dom}} + t_{\text{dom}} + w_{\text{dom}}$$

$$TC_{\text{global}} = c_{\text{prod}}^{\text{global}} + t_{\text{global}} + \text{Tariff} + \text{Currency adj.} + w_{\text{global}}$$

### Landed Cost Calculation (Globalisation adds Layers)

$$LC = (\text{FOB Price}) + (\text{Freight}) + (\text{Insurance}) + (\text{Customs Duty}) + (\text{Clearing/Forwarding})$$

**Example:** FOB Tirupur = ₹200/unit; Freight = ₹40/unit; Insurance = ₹5/unit; Duty = 10% of CIF = ₹24.5/unit; Clearing = ₹3/unit. Total **Landed Cost = ₹272.5/unit**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Always list *all six cost layers* in globalisation problems — examiner's mantra is "Did the student remember Customs Duty and Insurance?" Omitting these loses 2–3 marks.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Model

| Problem Context | Recommended Model | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Locate ONE warehouse | Center of Gravity | Closed-form solution, fast |
| Locate ONE warehouse (multiple cost rates) | Cross-Dock Model | CoG fails on unequal costs |
| Locate MULTIPLE plants | Plant Location MILP | Binary decision needed |
| Serve EU from Asia | Gravity + Tariff Layered Model | Customs + ocean freight |
| Real-time disruption | Stochastic MILP | Captures uncertainty |
| E-commerce last-mile | Hub-and-Spoke / Set Covering | Many small nodes |
| Service-level max distance | Set Covering Problem | Max distance = service radius |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
