# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Regional Network Configuration in supply chain management. List the three primary decisions involved. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Regional Network Configuration refers to the strategic planning process of determining the optimal number, location, capacity, and role of facilities (plants, warehouses, distribution centres) within a geographic region to serve customer demand at minimum total logistics cost while satisfying service-level constraints.

The three primary decisions are:
1. **Facility role assignment** (manufacturing vs warehouse vs cross-dock).
2. **Facility location** (geographic coordinates).
3. **Capacity and flow allocation** (how much to produce/hold and which market to serve).

*(Examiner note: All three components must be present for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Center of Gravity Method? State its key assumption. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Center of Gravity (CoG) method is a single-facility location technique used in regional network design that identifies the cost-minimising coordinates of a new facility by computing the demand-weighted centroid of all customer locations.

$$X^* = \frac{\sum w_j x_j}{\sum w_j}, \qquad Y^* = \frac{\sum w_j y_j}{\sum w_j}$$

**Key Assumption:** Transportation cost is directly proportional to Euclidean (straight-line) distance and is uniform in all directions — i.e., there are no geographic barriers, tolls, or differentiated freight rates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain with a diagram the four classical models used for designing a regional supply chain network. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four classical models for designing a regional supply chain network are:

**1. Gravity Location Model:** Used when locating a *single* facility. Uses weighted centroid based on demand and coordinates. Best for warehouses or single plant decisions. Limitation: ignores cost variation.

**2. Plant Location Model (Capacitated):** A MILP that simultaneously decides which plants to open (binary) and how much flow to assign. Subject to capacity and demand constraints. Optimal for multi-plant networks.

**3. Warehouse Location Model:** Variant of plant location model where plants are pre-existing and warehouses are the decisions. Used in retail and FMCG sectors (e.g., DMart, BigBasket).

**4. Hub-and-Spoke Model:** Concentrates flows through central hubs (e.g., airline networks, Amazon FBA). Reduces long-haul transportation costs at the expense of increased handling at hubs.

| Model | Output Type | Solution Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Gravity | Single point $(X^*, Y^*)$ | Closed-form |
| Plant Location | Set + flow matrix | MILP / Branch-and-Bound |
| Warehouse Location | Set of warehouses | Lagrangian Relaxation |
| Hub-and-Spoke | Hub locations + allocation | Combinatorial Heuristics |

*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 for the block diagram.)*

---

#### (b) A company must locate a single regional warehouse to serve four markets. Data given: Use the Center of Gravity method to find the optimal location. **(7 marks)**

**Data Table:**

| Market | X (km) | Y (km) | Demand (tonnes/year) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | 5 | 10 | 400 |
| M2 | 15 | 8 | 600 |
| M3 | 20 | 25 | 300 |
| M4 | 10 | 30 | 500 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total demand = $400 + 600 + 300 + 500 = 1800$ tonnes.

**Step 2:** Compute $\sum w_j x_j$:

$$\sum w_j x_j = (400)(5) + (600)(15) + (300)(20) + (500)(10)$$
$$= 2000 + 9000 + 6000 + 5000 = 22000$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\sum w_j y_j$:

$$\sum w_j y_j = (400)(10) + (600)(8) + (300)(25) + (500)(30)$$
$$= 4000 + 4800 + 7500 + 15000 = 31300$$

**Step 4:** Apply formulas:

$$X^* = \frac{22000}{1800} = 12.22 \; \text{km}$$

$$Y^* = \frac{31300}{1800} = 17.39 \; \text{km}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — The warehouse should be located at approximately **(12.22, 17.39)**, closer to M2 and M4 due to their high combined demand (1100 of 1800 tonnes).

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss the impact of globalisation on supply chain networks with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Globalisation has fundamentally restructured supply chain networks in six measurable ways:

**1. Extended Geographic Reach:**
Modern firms source from 30+ countries. Example: Apple iPhone components come from 43 countries. This necessitates multi-region network models.

**2. Sourcing Strategy Shift:**
From single-source domestic to multi-source global. Example: Toyota sources steel from India, Japan, and Brazil simultaneously.

**3. Cost Structure Transformation:**
Labor cost arbitrage drives offshoring. Example: Kitex Garments (Kerala) produces for 40+ global brands due to lower labor costs vs Europe.

**4. Increased Lead Time and Pipeline Inventory:**
Pipeline inventory $I_p = D \times T$ grows with lead time. Example: Shipping from Shenzhen to Rotterdam takes 28 days; pipeline inventory equals one month of sales.

**5. Risk Profile Changes:**
Currency, geopolitical, and regulatory risks emerge. Example: 2018 US-China tariff war forced Apple to consider India production.

**6. Service-Level Expectations:**
Amazon, Flipkart force 1–2 day global delivery, requiring regional warehouse networks close to consumers.

**Strategic Responses to Globalisation:**
- Offshoring production to low-cost countries.
- Nearshoring to balance cost and lead time.
- Reshoring critical components post-COVID.
- Digitalisation for visibility (Blockchain, IoT).
- Hedging currency via forward contracts.

---

#### (b) Formulate the Capacitated Plant Location Problem as a MILP. Explain each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sets:**
- $I = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$: candidate plant locations.
- $J = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$: demand markets.

**Parameters:**
- $f_i$: annual fixed cost to open plant $i$ (₹).
- $c_{ij}$: per-unit cost (production + transport) from plant $i$ to market $j$.
- $K_i$: capacity of plant $i$ (units/year).
- $D_j$: annual demand at market $j$ (units).

**Decision Variables:**
- $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$: 1 if plant $i$ is opened, 0 otherwise.
- $x_{ij} \geq 0$: units shipped from plant $i$ to market $j$.

**Objective Function (Minimize Total Cost):**

$$\min Z = \sum_{i \in I} f_i y_i + \sum_{i \in I} \sum_{j \in J} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

**Subject to:**

1. **Capacity Constraint:**

$$\sum_{j \in J} x_{ij} \leq K_i \cdot y_i \quad \forall i \in I$$

*Explanation:* Total outflow from plant $i$ cannot exceed its capacity; if $y_i = 0$, all flow must be zero.

2. **Demand Satisfaction:**

$$\sum_{i \in I} x_{ij} = D_j \quad \forall j \in J$$

*Explanation:* Each market's demand must be fully met (equality assumes backorders not allowed).

3. **Non-negativity and Binary:**

$$x_{ij} \geq 0, \quad y_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Why MILP?** The presence of binary variables ($y_i$) combined with continuous variables ($x_{ij}$) and linear constraints makes it a *Mixed Integer Linear Program*. Solvers used: CPLEX, Gurobi, or open-source CBC.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Capacity Multiplier $K_i y_i$** — writing $\sum x_{ij} \leq K_i$ alone is wrong because it allows flow from a *closed* plant. Always multiply by $y_i$. *(Loss: 2 marks)*
> 2. **Omitting Demand Equality vs Inequality** — equality ($\sum x_{ij} = D_j$) assumes perfect demand fulfillment. If backorders are allowed, use $\geq$. Mention your assumption! *(Loss: 1 mark)*
> 3. **CoG Assumption Omission** — never derive CoG without stating "Euclidean distance and uniform transport cost" as assumptions. *(Loss: 1–2 marks)*
> 4. **Forgetting Customs Duty & Insurance** in globalisation cost problems — landed cost has 5 components. Listing only 2 loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing Up Single-facility vs Multi-facility** — CoG is for ONE facility only. Using it for a multi-plant problem is conceptually wrong. *(Loss: 2 marks)*
> 6. **Not defining variables** before the MILP formulation — KTU examiners deduct marks if $y_i, x_{ij}, I, J$ are not explicitly defined.
> 7. **Confusing "globalisation" with "internationalisation"** — globalisation implies integration of supply chains, not just exports. *(Loss: 1 mark)*

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> **Models for Regional Network Configuration:**
> - Regional network = optimal placement of plants, warehouses, DCs in a geographic region.
> - Four classical models: **Gravity Location**, **Plant Location (MILP)**, **Warehouse Location**, **Hub-and-Spoke**.
> - **Center of Gravity Formula:** $X^* = \frac{\sum w_j x_j}{\sum w_j}$, $Y^* = \frac{\sum w_j y_j}{\sum w_j}$.
> - CoG is for *single* facility only; assumes uniform transport cost.
> - **Plant Location MILP** has three components: Objective (cost minimization), Capacity constraint, Demand satisfaction.
> - Binary variable $y_i \in \{0,1\}$ forces the open/close decision.
> - The "big-M trick": $K_i y_i$ in capacity constraint enforces zero flow when $y_i = 0$.
>
> **Impact of Globalisation:**
> - Globalisation = integration of economic activities across borders.
> - Six measurable impacts: extended reach, multi-sourcing, longer lead times, currency risk, higher pipeline inventory, higher service expectations.
> - **Landed Cost** has 5 components: FOB + Freight + Insurance + Duty + Clearing.
> - Pipeline inventory: $I_p = D \times T$.
> - Total network cost: $TC = TC_{\text{prod}} + TC_{\text{transport}} + TC_{\text{warehouse}} + TC_{\text{inventory}}$.
> - Strategic responses: **Offshoring, Nearshoring, Reshoring, Digitalisation, Hedging**.
>
> **KTU Board Exam Triggers:**
> - "Derive CoG" → write all 5 derivation steps + assumption.
> - "Formulate plant location" → write objective + capacity + demand + binary constraints.
> - "Impact of globalisation" → use the six-dimension framework + an Indian example (Kitex, Tata, Infosys).
> - Always **define variables and sets** before any MILP formulation.
> - Always **comment on limitations** of CoG in real-world contexts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Demand Forecasting** is the systematic, scientific process of estimating the **future demand** for a product, service, or commodity over a defined time horizon, using historical sales data, market intelligence, statistical models, and causal variables, in order to support strategic, tactical, and operational supply chain decisions.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, demand forecasting is positioned as the **starting node** of the entire Supply Chain Management (SCM) planning cycle. It is the input that drives:

- **Procurement & Sourcing** decisions
- **Production planning** and aggregate scheduling
- **Inventory replenishment** policies
- **Distribution & logistics** capacity planning
- **Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP)** cycles

The KTU module categorizes demand forecasting into three primary classifications:

| Classification | Basis | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative Forecasting** | Expert judgment, market research, Delphi method | New product introduction, no historical data |
| **Quantitative Forecasting (Time Series)** | Historical data patterns | Short-to-medium term planning |
| **Causal / Associative Forecasting** | Relationship between demand and external variables | Medium-to-long term planning |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Predictor" of Business

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of demand forecasting as the **meteorological department of a business**. Just as meteorologists use past temperature data, wind patterns, humidity, and satellite imagery to predict tomorrow's weather, supply chain managers use past sales, seasonality patterns, promotions, and economic indicators to predict next quarter's demand.

If the forecast is **accurate**, the company stocks the right products at the right warehouses — like carrying an umbrella on a rainy day. If the forecast is **wrong**, the company either suffers **stockouts** (empty shelves = lost sales) or **overstocks** (excess inventory = dead capital), exactly like getting drenched without an umbrella or carrying one on a sunny day.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Metrics

- **Forecast Horizon (n)**: The number of future time periods (weeks, months, quarters) for which demand is predicted.
- **Forecast Error (eₜ)**: The deviation between the actual demand `Aₜ` and the forecasted demand `Fₜ` at time `t`, given by `eₜ = Aₜ − Fₜ`.
- **Forecast Bias**: A systematic tendency to over-forecast or under-forecast consistently.
- **Lead Time (L)**: The time gap between placing an order and receiving the goods — closely tied to forecast horizon.
- **Service Level**: The probability of not stockout during lead time, typically set at **95%** or **97%** in KTU numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Benchmarks** used in KTU numerical problems: **MAD**, **MSE**, **MAPE**, and **Tracking Signal (TS)**. These are the four metrics examiners expect students to compute.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven-Step Demand Forecasting Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a structured process flow for demand forecasting:

1. **Determine the purpose** of the forecast (production, inventory, finance, marketing).
2. **Identify the demand variables** (SKU, region, customer segment, time bucket).
3. **Select the forecast horizon** (short-term ≤ 3 months, medium-term 3–12 months, long-term > 1 year).
4. **Collect historical data** (sales records, point-of-sale data, market data).
5. **Choose the forecasting model** (qualitative, time series, or causal).
6. **Generate the forecast** and compute error metrics.
7. **Monitor and refine** the forecast using tracking signals.

## 2.2 Components of a Time Series

A time series is decomposed into four components — this is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU exams:

- **Trend (T)**: Long-term upward or downward movement in demand (e.g., rising smartphone sales over 5 years).
- **Cyclical (C)**: Medium-term oscillations tied to business/economic cycles (e.g., recession-recovery).
- **Seasonal (S)**: Regular, predictable fluctuations within a year (e.g., AC sales peak in April–June).
- **Random/Irregular (I)**: Unpredictable noise (e.g., natural disasters, pandemics).

The two standard additive and multiplicative decomposition models are:

$$
Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t \quad \text{(Additive Model)}
$$

$$
Y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative Model)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the **additive model** when seasonal variation is roughly **constant in magnitude**. Use the **multiplicative model** when seasonal variation **grows proportionally with the trend** (e.g., ice-cream sales).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation you are expected to apply in PESFT867 Module 2 numerical questions.

| # | Method | Formula | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Moving Average (SMA, n-period) | `F_{t+1} = (D_t + D_{t-1} + ... + D_{t-n+1}) / n` | `D` = past demand, `n` = window size | Stable demand, no trend |
| 2 | Weighted Moving Average (WMA) | `F_{t+1} = Σ w_i * D_{t-i+1}`, where `Σ w_i = 1` | `w_i` = weights (recent > older) | Recent data is more relevant |
| 3 | Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) | `F_{t+1} = α·A_t + (1−α)·F_t` | `α` = smoothing constant (0 < α < 1) | No trend, no seasonality |
| 4 | Holt's Linear Trend Method | `L_t = α·A_t + (1−α)·(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})` ; `T_t = β·(L_t − L_{t-1}) + (1−β)·T_{t-1}` | `β` = trend smoothing constant | Linear trend, no seasonality |
| 5 | Linear Regression (Trend Line) | `D_t = a + b·t` ; `b = (n·ΣtD − Σt·ΣD) / (n·Σt² − (Σt)²)` ; `a = D̄ − b·t̄` | `t` = time index | Capturing long-term trend |
| 6 | Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) | `MAD = Σ \vert A_t − F_t \vert / n` | Error magnitudes | Forecast accuracy |
| 7 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | `MSE = Σ (A_t − F_t)² / n` | Squared errors | Penalizes large errors |
| 8 | Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) | `MAPE = (Σ \vert (A_t − F_t) / A_t \vert / n) × 100` | Percentage error | Scale-independent comparison |
| 9 | Tracking Signal (TS) | `TS = Cumulative Sum of Errors (CSE) / MAD` | Cumulative forecast bias | Detecting systematic bias |
| 10 | Seasonal Index | `SI = (Average demand for season) / (Overall average demand)` | Season-level averages | Decomposition & deseasonalization |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Retail (Big-Bazaar, Amazon)**: SES + seasonal indices for daily SKU-level forecasting.
- **Manufacturing (Maruti, Bosch)**: Linear regression on macro-economic indicators for long-term capacity.
- **FMCG (HUL, Nestlé)**: WMA with high weight on the latest month for fast-moving goods.
- **Logistics (BlueDart, DHL)**: Forecast accuracy directly determines hub capacity, fleet sizing, and last-mile staffing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: Whenever a numerical problem provides a series of past demands and asks for the *next period's forecast*, students must (a) state the chosen method, (b) write the formula, (c) substitute values, and (d) produce a numeric answer with units (units/period). Skipping any one of these four steps costs marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Solved Analytical Problem 1 — Simple Moving Average & Forecast Error

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**: A retailer recorded the following monthly sales (in units) for a product over the last 6 months:

| Month | Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr | May | Jun |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand `D_t` | 100 | 120 | 110 | 130 | 125 | 140 |

Compute the **3-month Simple Moving Average (SMA)** forecast for each month from April onwards, then calculate **MAD, MSE, and MAPE** for the forecast.

### Step 1 — Compute the 3-Month SMA Forecast

The SMA forecast for any month `t` is the average of the previous 3 actual demands.

**Forecast for April (using Jan, Feb, Mar):**

$$
F_{Apr} = \frac{D_{Jan} + D_{Feb} + D_{Mar}}{3} = \frac{100 + 120 + 110}{3} = \frac{330}{3} = 110 \text{ units}
$$

**Forecast for May (using Feb, Mar, Apr):**

$$
F_{May} = \frac{120 + 110 + 130}{3} = \frac{360}{3} = 120 \text{ units}
$$

**Forecast for June (using Mar, Apr, May):**

$$
F_{Jun} = \frac{110 + 130 + 125}{3} = \frac{365}{3} \approx 121.67 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 2 — Tabulate Forecast Errors

| Month | Actual `A_t` | Forecast `F_t` | Error `e_t = A_t − F_t` | `\vert e_t \vert` | `e_t²` | `\vert e_t / A_t \vert × 100` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr | 130 | 110 | +20 | 20 | 400 | 15.38 |
| May | 125 | 120 | +5 | 5 | 25 | 4.00 |
| Jun | 140 | 121.67 | +18.33 | 18.33 | 336.0 | 13.09 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | **43.33** | **761.0** | **32.47** |

### Step 3 — Compute MAD, MSE, MAPE

$$
MAD = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{43.33}{3} = 14.44 \text{ units}
$$

$$
MSE = \frac{\sum e_t^2}{n} = \frac{761.0}{3} = 253.67 \text{ units}^2
$$

$$
MAPE = \frac{1}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert \times 100 = \frac{32.47}{3} = 10.82\%
$$

**Interpretation**: The retailer under-forecasted demand every month (positive errors), producing a **positive bias** — they consistently stocked too little. The MAPE of ~10.82% is acceptable in many FMCG contexts (typical industry target: < 10% for stable SKU).

---

## 3.2 Solved Analytical Problem 2 — Simple Exponential Smoothing & Tracking Signal

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**: Demand for a product over 5 months is: `D₁ = 50, D₂ = 54, D₃ = 48, D₄ = 56, D₅ = 52`. Given `α = 0.3` and `F₁ = 50` (initial forecast equals first actual), compute:
1. Forecasts for months 2 through 6 (`F₆` required).
2. MAD and the **Tracking Signal at the end of month 5**.

### Step 1 — Apply SES Recursively

The recursive SES equation:

$$
F_{t+1} = \alpha \cdot A_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot F_t
$$

**Forecast for Month 2:**

$$
F_2 = 0.3 \times 50 + 0.7 \times 50 = 50.00 \text{ units}
$$

**Forecast for Month 3:**

$$
F_3 = 0.3 \times 54 + 0.7 \times 50 = 16.2 + 35.0 = 51.20 \text{ units}
$$

**Forecast for Month 4:**

$$
F_4 = 0.3 \times 48 + 0.7 \times 51.20 = 14.4 + 35.84 = 50.24 \text{ units}
$$

**Forecast for Month 5:**

$$
F_5 = 0.3 \times 56 + 0.7 \times 50.24 = 16.8 + 35.168 = 51.97 \text{ units}
$$

**Forecast for Month 6:**

$$
F_6 = 0.3 \times 52 + 0.7 \times 51.97 = 15.6 + 36.379 = 41.98 \text{ units (≈ 42.0)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Forecast Errors and Cumulative Error

| Month `t` | `A_t` | `F_t` | `e_t = A_t − F_t` | `\vert e_t \vert` | CSE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 54 | 50.00 | +4.00 | 4.00 | +4.00 |
| 3 | 48 | 51.20 | −3.20 | 3.20 | +0.80 |
| 4 | 56 | 50.24 | +5.76 | 5.76 | +6.56 |
| 5 | 52 | 51.97 | +0.03 | 0.03 | +6.59 |

### Step 3 — MAD and Tracking Signal

$$
MAD = \frac{4.00 + 3.20 + 5.76 + 0.03}{4} = \frac{12.99}{4} = 3.25 \text{ units}
$$

$$
TS = \frac{CSE}{MAD} = \frac{6.59}{3.25} \approx 2.03
$$

**Interpretation**: A Tracking Signal within the standard control limits of `±4` (or `±6` in some KTU texts) indicates the forecast is **not biased** — the model is acceptable. If `TS > +4`, the model is **consistently under-forecasting**, and the firm should reset the forecast.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall**: Many students compute `MAD` correctly but **forget to compute the Cumulative Sum of Errors (CSE)** first. CSE = sum of signed errors (`+` and `−`), NOT the sum of absolute errors. Mixing these up destroys the Tracking Signal calculation.

---

## 3.3 Solved Analytical Problem 3 — Linear Regression Trend Line

**Problem Statement**: Annual demand for a product over 6 years is shown below. Fit a **linear trend line** `D_t = a + b·t` (where `t = 1, 2, ..., 6`) and forecast demand for **year 7**.

| Year | `t` | Demand `D_t` |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 2 | 220 |
| 3 | 3 | 245 |
| 4 | 4 | 260 |
| 5 | 5 | 280 |
| 6 | 6 | 295 |

### Step 1 — Compute Sums

$$
\sum t = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 = 21
$$

$$
\sum D = 200 + 220 + 245 + 260 + 280 + 295 = 1500
$$

$$
\sum t^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 = 91
$$

$$
\sum tD = 1(200) + 2(220) + 3(245) + 4(260) + 5(280) + 6(295) = 200 + 440 + 735 + 1040 + 1400 + 1770 = 5585
$$

$$
\bar{t} = 21 / 6 = 3.5, \quad \bar{D} = 1500 / 6 = 250
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Slope `b` and Intercept `a`

$$
b = \frac{n \sum tD - \sum t \cdot \sum D}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2} = \frac{6(5585) - (21)(1500)}{6(91) - (21)^2}
$$

$$
b = \frac{33510 - 31500}{546 - 441} = \frac{2010}{105} = 19.14
$$

$$
a = \bar{D} - b \cdot \bar{t} = 250 - (19.14)(3.5) = 250 - 67.0 = 183.00
$$

### Step 3 — Forecast for Year 7

$$
D_7 = a + b \cdot t = 183.00 + (19.14)(7) = 183.00 + 133.98 = 316.98 \approx 317 \text{ units}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The slope `b ≈ 19.14` indicates demand is growing by roughly **19 units per year**, consistent with a strong upward business trend.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Demand Forecasting Process Flow (KTU Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Historical Demand Data and Market Intelligence"] --> B["STEP 1: Define Forecasting Objective and Horizon"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: Select Forecasting Model Type"]
    C --> D{"DECISION: Data Availability"}
    D -- No Historical Data --> E["QUALITATIVE METHOD: Delphi, Market Research, Expert Judgment"]
    D -- Yes Historical Data --> F["QUANTITATIVE METHOD: Time Series"]
    D -- External Variables Available --> G["CAUSAL METHOD: Regression, Econometric Models"]
    F --> H["CHOOSE TECHNIQUE: SMA, WMA, SES, Holt, Winters"]
    E --> I["GENERATE FORECAST"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["COMPUTE ERROR METRICS: MAD, MSE, MAPE"]
    J --> K{"MONITOR: Tracking Signal within Limits?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["OUTPUT: Validated Forecast for S and OP"]
    K -- No --> M["STEP 3: Recalibrate Model Parameters"]
    M --> H
    L --> N["FEED INTO: Inventory, Production, Procurement, Logistics Planning"]
```

## 4.2 Time Series Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RAW TIME SERIES Y of t"] --> B["TREND COMPONENT T of t"]
    A --> C["CYCLICAL COMPONENT C of t"]
    A --> D["SEASONAL COMPONENT S of t"]
    A --> E["IRREGULAR COMPONENT I of t"]
    B --> F{"MODEL SELECTION"}
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- Additive --> G["Y = T + C + S + I"]
    F -- Multiplicative --> H["Y = T x C x S x I"]
    G --> I["RECONSTRUCTED FORECAST"]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["SUPPLIER SIDE: Production Capacity and Inventory Data"] --> C["JOINT BUSINESS PLAN"]
    B["RETAILER SIDE: POS Sales Data and Customer Insights"] --> C
    C --> D["SALES FORECAST COLLABORATION"]
    D --> E["ORDER FORECAST COLLABORATION"]
    E --> F["ORDER GENERATION AND FULFILLMENT"]
    F --> G["EXCEPTION MANAGEMENT LOOP"]
    G -- Forecast Exception --> D
    G -- Supply Exception --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPFR** is a high-weightage conceptual topic in PESFT867. The block above shows the **closed-loop feedback architecture** that distinguishes CPFR from traditional unilateral forecasting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define demand forecasting. List and briefly explain the three main types of forecasting methods.**

**Model Answer**:
Demand forecasting is the process of estimating the **future demand** for a product or service over a specific time horizon, using historical data, statistical techniques, and market information, to enable effective supply chain planning.

The **three main types** of forecasting methods are:

1. **Qualitative Forecasting**: Based on subjective inputs such as expert opinion, the Delphi technique, market research, and consumer surveys. Used when **no historical data** exists, e.g., new product launch.

2. **Quantitative (Time Series) Forecasting**: Uses **historical demand patterns** projected forward. Includes Simple Moving Average, Weighted Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, and Holt-Winters methods. Suitable for **short-to-medium term** stable demand.

3. **Causal (Associative) Forecasting**: Establishes a cause-effect relationship between demand and **external independent variables** (price, advertising, GDP). Linear regression is the most common method. Used for **medium-to-long term** strategic planning.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the four components of a time series with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer**:
A time series consists of four components:

1. **Trend (T)**: The **long-term persistent upward or downward movement** of demand. Example: Growth in electric vehicle sales in India from 2018 to 2024.

2. **Cyclical Component (C)**: **Multi-year oscillations** linked to macroeconomic business cycles. Example: Decline in real estate demand during 2008–2009 global recession.

3. **Seasonal Component (S)**: **Recurring intra-year fluctuations** of fixed period (12 months, 4 quarters). Example: Spike in umbrella sales during June–September monsoon in Kerala.

4. **Irregular / Random Component (I)**: **Unpredictable one-time shocks**. Example: Sudden surge in sanitiser demand during the COVID-19 outbreak.

Mathematically, these components combine as:
`Y = T + C + S + I` (Additive) or `Y = T × C × S × I` (Multiplicative).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** A bakery recorded the following weekly demand for bread loaves over the past 8 weeks:

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 220 | 240 | 235 | 250 | 245 | 260 | 255 | 270 |

**(i)** Compute the **4-week Simple Moving Average** forecast for weeks 5, 6, 7, and 8.
**(ii)** Calculate **MAD, MSE, and MAPE** for the 4-week SMA forecast.

**(b)** What is the **Bullwhip Effect** in supply chain demand forecasting? Explain **four causes** and **three strategies** to mitigate it.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(a)**

**Step 1 — 4-Week SMA Forecast**

$$
F_5 = \frac{D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + D_4}{4} = \frac{220 + 240 + 235 + 250}{4} = \frac{945}{4} = 236.25 \text{ loaves}
$$

$$
F_6 = \frac{D_2 + D_3 + D_4 + D_5}{4} = \frac{240 + 235 + 250 + 245}{4} = \frac{970}{4} = 242.50 \text{ loaves}
$$

$$
F_7 = \frac{D_3 + D_4 + D_5 + D_6}{4} = \frac{235 + 250 + 245 + 260}{4} = \frac{990}{4} = 247.50 \text{ loaves}
$$

$$
F_8 = \frac{D_4 + D_5 + D_6 + D_7}{4} = \frac{250 + 245 + 260 + 255}{4} = \frac{1010}{4} = 252.50 \text{ loaves}
$$

**Step 2 — Error Computation Table**

| Week | `A_t` | `F_t` | `e_t = A_t − F_t` | `\vert e_t \vert` | `e_t²` | `\vert e_t / A_t \vert × 100` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 245 | 236.25 | +8.75 | 8.75 | 76.56 | 3.57 |
| 6 | 260 | 242.50 | +17.50 | 17.50 | 306.25 | 6.73 |
| 7 | 255 | 247.50 | +7.50 | 7.50 | 56.25 | 2.94 |
| 8 | 270 | 252.50 | +17.50 | 17.50 | 306.25 | 6.48 |
| **Σ** | — | — | — | **51.25** | **745.31** | **19.72** |

**Step 3 — Error Metrics**

$$
MAD = \frac{51.25}{4} = 12.81 \text{ loaves}
$$

$$
MSE = \frac{745.31}{4} = 186.33 \text{ loaves}^2
$$

$$
MAPE = \frac{19.72}{4} = 4.93\%
$$

**Interpretation**: A MAPE of **4.93%** indicates excellent forecast accuracy (industry benchmark < 10% for stable FMCG). The **positive bias** (all errors positive) suggests the bakery should slightly raise the forecast window size or switch to a Weighted MA with higher recent weights.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(b)**

**Bullwhip Effect Definition**:
The **Bullwhip Effect** is the **amplification of demand variability** as it propagates upstream from the customer to the retailer, wholesaler, distributor, and manufacturer in a supply chain. Small fluctuations at the retail level translate into large swings in factory orders.

**Four Causes**:

1. **Demand Forecasting Errors**: Each tier uses its own forecast, compounding errors at every link.
2. **Order Batching**: Buyers place bulk orders periodically to optimize transportation or qualify for quantity discounts.
3. **Price Fluctuations & Promotions**: Forward buying during sales causes artificial demand spikes.
4. **Rationing & Shortage Gaming**: When supply is scarce, customers over-order anticipating rationing, distorting the true demand signal.

**Three Mitigation Strategies**:

1. **Improve Information Sharing** across the supply chain (e.g., POS data sharing, CPFR).
2. **Reduce Lead Time** by improving logistics and using faster transportation modes.
3. **Eliminate Order Batching** through frequent, smaller replenishments (e.g., Vendor-Managed Inventory, Just-In-Time).
4. *(Bonus)* Stabilize pricing to remove promotional artificial demand.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** The monthly demand for a consumer durable is:

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 400 | 420 | 410 | 430 | 425 | 445 |

Using **Simple Exponential Smoothing** with `α = 0.4` and `F₁ = 400`, calculate:
**(i)** Forecasts for months 2 through 7.
**(ii)** **MAD** and **Tracking Signal** at the end of month 6.

**(b)** Explain **Holt's Linear Trend Method** for forecasting demand with a mathematical formulation. Under what conditions is it preferred over SES?

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(a)**

**Step 1 — Apply SES with `α = 0.4`**

$$
F_{t+1} = 0.4 \cdot A_t + 0.6 \cdot F_t
$$

$$
F_2 = 0.4(400) + 0.6(400) = 400.00
$$

$$
F_3 = 0.4(420) + 0.6(400) = 168 + 240 = 408.00
$$

$$
F_4 = 0.4(410) + 0.6(408) = 164 + 244.8 = 408.80
$$

$$
F_5 = 0.4(430) + 0.6(408.8) = 172 + 245.28 = 417.28
$$

$$
F_6 = 0.4(425) + 0.6(417.28) = 170 + 250.37 = 420.37
$$

$$
F_7 = 0.4(445) + 0.6(420.37) = 178 + 252.22 = 430.22 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Error Table**

| `t` | `A_t` | `F_t` | `e_t = A_t − F_t` | `\vert e_t \vert` | CSE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 420 | 400.00 | +20.00 | 20.00 | +20.00 |
| 3 | 410 | 408.00 | +2.00 | 2.00 | +22.00 |
| 4 | 430 | 408.80 | +21.20 | 21.20 | +43.20 |
| 5 | 425 | 417.28 | +7.72 | 7.72 | +50.92 |
| 6 | 445 | 420.37 | +24.63 | 24.63 | +75.55 |

**Step 3 — MAD and Tracking Signal**

$$
MAD = \frac{20.00 + 2.00 + 21.20 + 7.72 + 24.63}{5} = \frac{75.55}{5} = 15.11 \text{ units}
$$

$$
TS = \frac{CSE}{MAD} = \frac{75.55}{15.11} \approx 5.00
$$

**Interpretation**: TS = 5.00 lies **outside the ±4 limit**, indicating a **systematic positive bias** — the model is consistently under-forecasting. The firm should **raise `α`** to give more weight to recent actuals, or switch to Holt's method if a trend exists.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(b)**

**Holt's Linear Trend Method** extends SES to handle data with a **linear trend** (no seasonality). It uses **two smoothing equations** — one for the level and one for the trend.

**Mathematical Formulation**:

$$
L_t = \alpha \cdot A_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})
$$

$$
T_t = \beta \cdot (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \cdot T_{t-1}
$$

**Forecast for `m` periods ahead**:

$$
F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t
$$

Where:
- `L_t` = Smoothed level at time `t`
- `T_t` = Smoothed trend at time `t`
- `α` = Level smoothing constant (0 < α < 1)
- `β` = Trend smoothing constant (0 < β < 1)
- `m` = Number of future periods to forecast

**When Preferred Over SES**:
Holt's method is preferred when:
1. The demand series exhibits a **clear linear upward or downward trend**.
2. SES is producing **systematic forecast bias** (Tracking Signal consistently outside ±4).
3. The business requires **multi-period-ahead forecasts** where the trend extrapolation matters.
4. **Seasonality is absent** — for seasonal data, Holt-Winters is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
>
> 1. **Initial Values**: Students often forget to specify `F₁` or `L₁`. KTU explicitly tests initialization — always state the assumption (e.g., `F₁ = D₁`, `L₁ = D₁`, `T₁ = D₂ − D₁`).
> 2. **Sign Errors in Tracking Signal**: CSE uses **signed errors**, but MAD uses **absolute errors**. Confusing these yields wrong TS.
> 3. **Units in MAPE**: MAPE is always expressed as a **percentage**, not a decimal. Writing `MAPE = 0.1082` instead of `10.82%` costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forecast for Past Months**: Many students incorrectly use `F_t` instead of `A_t` to compute `e_t`. The error `e_t = A_t − F_t` is always anchored on **actual demand**.
> 5. **Skipping the Conclusion**: A 14-mark question requires a closing 1–2 line **interpretation** (what the MAPE/MAD/TS *means* for the business). Skipping it costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PESFT867 Module 2 — Demand Forecasting**

- **Forecasting Purpose**: Feeds **S&OP**, inventory policy, production scheduling, and procurement.
- **Three Method Classes**: **Qualitative** (no data), **Time Series** (past data), **Causal** (external drivers).
- **Time Series Components**: **T + C + S + I** (Additive) or **T × C × S × I** (Multiplicative).
- **SMA Formula**: `F_{t+1} = average of last n demands`.
- **WMA Formula**: Recent weights > older weights, weights sum to 1.
- **SES Recursion**: `F_{t+1} = α·A_t + (1−α)·F_t`; higher `α` = more reactive forecast.
- **Holt's Method**: Two equations for level `L_t` and trend `T_t`; used when trend is present.
- **Linear Regression**: Slope `b = [nΣtD − Σt·ΣD] / [nΣt² − (Σt)²]`.
- **MAD**: Average absolute error — **most common** KTU accuracy metric.
- **MSE**: Average squared error — penalizes large errors.
- **MAPE**: Average percentage error — scale-independent; < 10% is excellent.
- **Tracking Signal**: `CSE / MAD`; acceptable range is **±4** (or ±6 in some texts). Outside = bias.
- **CPFR**: Collaborative planning between retailer and supplier with shared forecast data.
- **Bullwhip Effect**: Demand variability amplification upstream; caused by forecasting errors, batching, price fluctuation, shortage gaming.
- **Mitigation**: Information sharing, lead time reduction, smaller order batches, VMI.
- **Delphi Method**: Iterative anonymous expert consensus for qualitative forecasting.
- **Service Level**: Standard KTU benchmark = **95%** or **97%** — directly tied to safety stock.
- **Numerical Approach**: Always state the **method**, **formula**, **substitution**, **answer with units**, and a one-line **interpretation**.

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — DEMAND FORECASTING IN SUPPLY CHAIN (PESFT867 | KTU 2024 SCHEME)**

topics.title: Role of Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Role of Forecasting ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Forecasting** is the process of predicting future demand for products or services by analyzing historical data, market intelligence, and statistical models. In supply chain management (SCM), it serves as the **foundational input** for strategic, tactical, and operational planning across procurement, production, distribution, and inventory functions. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, forecasting in SCM is **not merely estimation** — it is a structured, systematic, and continuous process that bridges customer demand variability with supply capability, enabling the **Demand-Supply Alignment Cycle**. ### Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Weather App on Your Phone** 📱 > Think of forecasting like the weather app. It does not *guarantee* it will rain tomorrow, but it gives you a *probabilistic estimate* so you carry an umbrella. Similarly, demand forecasting tells a company "how many umbrellas to manufacture" before the monsoon arrives. The umbrella company that ignores the forecast ends up with **10,000 umbrellas in summer** (overstock) or **zero umbrellas in July** (stockout). Forecasting is the supply chain's "weather app" — *imperfect, but indispensable.* ### Core Characteristics of Effective Forecasting | Characteristic | Explanation | |---|---| | **Always Wrong, Always Useful** | No forecast is 100% accurate; value lies in being *less wrong* than random guessing. | | **Aggregated > Disaggregated** | Forecasting total demand for 1,000 SKUs together is more accurate than forecasting each SKU individually. | | **Shorter Horizon > Longer Horizon** | A 1-month forecast is more accurate than a 12-month forecast. | | **Includes Error Estimate** | Every forecast should be quoted as $F \pm e$ (forecast ± error margin). | | **Continuous Process** | Forecasts must be updated regularly (rolling basis), not done once and forgotten. | ### Why Forecasting is the "Heart" of Supply Chain Management > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, forecasting is positioned as the **primary input** to the **Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP)** process. Without a reliable forecast, every downstream activity — purchasing, production, warehousing, transportation, and staffing — becomes *reactive* rather than *proactive*. **Standard Forecast Metrics used in industry (in bold for emphasis):** - **MAD** (Mean Absolute Deviation) - **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) - **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) - **Tracking Signal (TS)** - **Forecast Bias** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Strategic Roles of Forecasting in a Supply Chain Forecasting performs **seven critical roles** in SCM, each cascading into operational decisions: 1. **Demand Planning Driver** — Establishes the baseline against which actual sales are compared. 2. **Production Planning Input** — Determines batch sizes, run-rates, and machine loading. 3. **Inventory Control Foundation** — Sets reorder points (ROP), safety stock (SS), and Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) parameters. 4. **Procurement Trigger** — Drives purchase orders to upstream suppliers; supports long-lead-time ordering. 5. **Capacity & Workforce Planning** — Aligns labor shifts, overtime, and equipment utilization. 6. **Financial Budgeting** — Projects revenues, working capital needs, and cash flow. 7. **Logistics Network Design** — Influences warehouse locations, transportation mode selection, and fleet sizing. ### 2.2 Forecast Time Horizons | Horizon | Duration | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Short-Range** | 0 – 3 months | Production scheduling, replenishment, dispatch | | **Medium-Range** | 3 months – 2 years | Workforce planning, supplier contracts | | **Long-Range** | 2+ years | Capacity expansion, strategic sourcing, capital investment | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** *Short-range forecasts* typically use **quantitative methods** (time-series), while *long-range forecasts* lean on **qualitative methods** (Delphi, market research) because historical patterns become less reliable over extended horizons. ### 2.3 Classification of Forecasting Methods Forecasting methods are broadly classified into **three families**: | Family | Description | Best Suited For | |---|---|---| | **Qualitative Methods** | Based on expert judgment, intuition, market surveys | New products, no historical data, fashion-driven items | | **Quantitative – Time Series** | Uses only past demand patterns | Stable demand, short-term forecasts | | **Quantitative – Associative / Causal** | Links demand to external variables (price, GDP, advertising) | Strategic planning, when causal drivers are known | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this table as your **last-night revision sheet**. All formulas below are exhaustively verified against standard Operations Management references (Chopra & Meindl, Heizer & Render). | # | Method | Formula | Variables | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Naïve Forecast** | $F_{t+1} = A_t$ | $A_t$ = actual demand in period $t$ | Baseline, stable demand | | 2 | **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{t-i+1}}{n}$ | $n$ = window size | Smoothing random fluctuations | | 3 | **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot A_{t-i+1}$ | $\sum w_i = 1$ | Recent data weighted more | | 4 | **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** | $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing constant (0 to 1) | No trend, no seasonality | | 5 | **Trend-Adjusted Exp. Smoothing (Holt's)** | $L_t = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$
$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$
$F_{t+\tau} = L_t + \tau T_t$ | $\alpha, \beta$ = smoothing constants; $\tau$ = periods ahead | Linear trend present | | 6 | **Seasonal Index** | $SI_i = \dfrac{\text{Average demand in season } i}{\text{Average demand across all seasons}}$ | Dimensionaless ratio | Seasonal products | | 7 | **MAD (Error Metric)** | $MAD = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert A_t - F_t \vert}{n}$ | $n$ = number of periods | Measures average error magnitude | | 8 | **MSE (Error Metric)** | $MSE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} (A_t - F_t)^2}{n}$ | Penalizes large errors | When big misses are costly | | 9 | **MAPE (Error Metric)** | $MAPE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$ | Expressed in % | Comparing across products | | 10 | **Cumulative Error (Bias)** | $CE = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (A_t - F_t)$ | Positive → under-forecast, Negative → over-forecast | Detecting systematic bias | | 11 | **Tracking Signal (TS)** | $TS = \dfrac{CE}{MAD}$ | Control limits: $\pm 4$ to $\pm 6$ | Forecast model monitoring | | 12 | **Linear Regression (Causal)** | $F = a + bX$ | $b = \dfrac{n \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$ | Demand vs. price/income | > **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute value expressions use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ### 2.5 Why These Formulas Matter in Practice - The **Naïve method** is the **benchmark** — any sophisticated method must beat it to be considered valuable. - **MAD** is preferred over **MSE** when you want a *linear* sense of error magnitude. - **MAPE** is the **industry standard** for reporting forecast accuracy to non-technical management because it is *scale-independent* (a percentage). - **Tracking Signal** acts as an **early warning system** — when TS exceeds $\pm 4$, the forecasting model must be re-tuned. ### 2.6 The Bullwhip Effect & Forecasting > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Bullwhip Effect** is the phenomenon where demand variability *amplifies* as it moves upstream in the supply chain (from retailer → wholesaler → manufacturer → supplier). **Forecasting errors are a major cause.** KTU 2024 specifically tests this: poor forecasting at the retailer cascades into excessive safety stock, erratic production, and inflated costs upstream. **Counter-measures using better forecasting:** - **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)** — sharing POS data across the chain. - **Use of POS (Point-of-Sale) data** instead of order data. - **Reducing lead times** so forecast horizons shrink. - **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** — supplier uses retailer's data to forecast and replenish. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Smoothing Recursion > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **favourite KTU long-answer question** (often worth 7 marks). Master it. **Starting Point:** Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) is defined as: $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t $$ **Goal:** Show that SES is equivalent to a weighted average of all past observations. **Step 1 — Write the equation for the next forecast period:** $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $F_t$ from its own definition $F_t = \alpha A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-1}$:** $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)\big[\alpha A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-1}\big] $$ **Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side:** $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 F_{t-1} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $F_{t-1}$ similarly: $F_{t-1} = \alpha A_{t-2} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-2}$:** $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 \big[\alpha A_{t-2} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-2}\big] $$ **Step 5 — Continue expanding recursively up to period $t-k$:** $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} (1-\alpha)^i A_{t-i} + (1-\alpha)^k F_{t-k+1} $$ **Step 6 — Take the limit as $k \to \infty$. Since $0 \le \alpha \le 1$, the term $(1-\alpha)^k \to 0$:** $$ \lim_{k \to \infty} F_{t+1} = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^i A_{t-i} $$ **Step 7 — Factor out $\alpha$ and recognize the geometric series of weights:** $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha \left[ A_t + (1-\alpha) A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 A_{t-2} + \cdots \right] $$ **Conclusion:** SES assigns exponentially *decreasing weights* to older observations, with the most recent observation carrying weight $\alpha$, the previous one $\alpha(1-\alpha)$, and so on. The **sum of all weights equals 1**, confirming it is a proper weighted average. $$ \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha (1-\alpha)^i = \frac{\alpha}{1-(1-\alpha)} = 1 $$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 3-Period Moving Average **Given:** Quarterly demand (in units) for a product: | Quarter | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Actual Demand $A_t$ | 120 | 135 | 128 | 142 | 150 | 138 | **Task:** Compute 3-period moving average forecasts for Q4, Q5, Q6. Then calculate MAD and MAPE. **Step 1 — Forecast for Q4** (using Q1, Q2, Q3): $$ F_{Q4} = \frac{A_{Q1} + A_{Q2} + A_{Q3}}{3} = \frac{120 + 135 + 128}{3} = \frac{383}{3} \approx 127.67 $$ **Step 2 — Forecast for Q5** (using Q2, Q3, Q4): $$ F_{Q5} = \frac{135 + 128 + 142}{3} = \frac{405}{3} = 135.00 $$ **Step 3 — Forecast for Q6** (using Q3, Q4, Q5): $$ F_{Q6} = \frac{128 + 142 + 150}{3} = \frac{420}{3} = 140.00 $$ **Step 4 — Tabulate Errors:** | Period | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | Error $A_t - F_t$ | $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | $\dfrac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Q4 | 142 | 127.67 | 14.33 | 14.33 | 10.09% | | Q5 | 150 | 135.00 | 15.00 | 15.00 | 10.00% | | Q6 | 138 | 140.00 | -2.00 | 2.00 | 1.45% | **Step 5 — Compute MAD:** $$ MAD = \frac{14.33 + 15.00 + 2.00}{3} = \frac{31.33}{3} \approx 10.44 \text{ units} $$ **Step 6 — Compute MAPE:** $$ MAPE = \frac{10.09\% + 10.00\% + 1.45\%}{3} = \frac{21.54\%}{3} \approx 7.18\% $$ **Interpretation:** On average, the 3-month moving average forecast is off by **~10.44 units** or **~7.18%** of actual demand — a very respectable accuracy for a manual method. ### 3.3 Worked Example — Tracking Signal and Bias Detection **Given:** The forecast errors $(A_t - F_t)$ over 5 periods are: $+8, +6, +10, +9, +7$. **Step 1 — Compute Cumulative Error (Bias):** $$ CE = \sum (A_t - F_t) = 8 + 6 + 10 + 9 + 7 = 40 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Absolute Errors and MAD:** $$ MAD = \frac{8 + 6 + 10 + 9 + 7}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Tracking Signal:** $$ TS = \frac{CE}{MAD} = \frac{40}{8} = 5 $$ **Step 4 — Decision Rule:** Standard control limits are $\pm 4$ (rigorous) to $\pm 6$ (lenient). > [!WARNING] > Since $TS = 5 > 4$, the forecasting model is producing **systematic under-forecasting** (consistently positive errors). The model must be **re-tuned** or a new forecasting technique adopted. If left unchecked, this will lead to **chronic stockouts**. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Forecasting Method | Product / Demand Characteristic | Recommended Method | Justification | |---|---|---| | Stable demand, no trend | **Naïve or SES** | Simple, low cost, sufficient accuracy | | Random fluctuations around mean | **Simple Moving Average** | Smooths noise effectively | | Recent data more relevant | **Weighted Moving Average** | Higher weights on recent periods | | Linear growth/decline trend | **Holt's Exponential Smoothing** | Explicitly models trend | | Strong seasonality (e.g., AC sales) | **Winter's / Seasonal Index method** | Captures seasonal pattern | | Demand driven by price, ad spend, GDP | **Linear Regression (Causal)** | Models external drivers | | New product, no history | **Delphi / Market Research** | Qualitative expert judgment | | Short life-cycle, fashion items | **Analogous / Test Marketing** | Compares to similar past products | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Central Position of Forecasting in a Supply Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Market Data and Customer Demand Signals] --> B[FORECASTING ENGINE] B --> C[Demand Plan] C --> D[Sales and Operations Planning S and OP] D --> E[Production Planning] D --> F[Inventory and Replenishment] D --> G[Procurement and Supplier Orders] D --> H[Logistics and Transportation] D --> I[Financial Budgeting] E --> J[Finished Goods] F --> J J --> K[Customer Delivery] K --> L[Actual Sales Data] L -.Feedback Loop.-> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **feedback loop** from `Actual Sales Data` back to `Market Data` — this represents the **continuous refinement** of forecasts using real outcomes, a hallmark of the **closed-loop S&OP process**. ### 4.2 Taxonomy of Forecasting Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Forecasting Methods] --> B[Qualitative] A --> C[Quantitative] B --> B1[Delphi Method] B --> B2[Market Research] B --> B3[Sales Force Composite] B --> B4[Panel Consensus] C --> D[Time Series] C --> E[Causal or Associative] D --> D1[Naive] D --> D2[Simple Moving Avg] D --> D3[Weighted Moving Avg] D --> D4[Exponential Smoothing] D --> D5[Holt Winters] E --> E1[Linear Regression] E --> E2[Multiple Regression] E --> E3[Econometric Models] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Forecasting Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Define Objective] --> B[Step 2: Collect Historical Data] B --> C[Step 3: Select Forecasting Method] C --> D[Step 4: Generate Forecast] D --> E[Step 5: Calculate Error Metrics] E --> F{Tracking Signal within Control Limits} F -- Yes --> G[Step 6: Deploy Forecast to Planning Systems] F -- No --> H[Step 7: Re tune Model or Switch Method] H --> C G --> I[Continuous Monitoring and Re forecasting] I --> D ``` **Engineering Significance:** This block diagram maps the *executable control flow* of a forecasting process. The decision block (tracking signal check) acts as a **feedback controller** — analogous to a PID controller in process control — ensuring the forecasting model self-corrects when it drifts out of acceptable accuracy bands. ### 4.4 The Bullwhip Effect — Visual Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Customer Demand] --> R[Retailer Order] R --> W[Wholesaler Order] W --> M[Manufacturer Order] M --> S[Supplier Raw Material Order] S -.Raw Materials.-> M M -.Finished Goods.-> W W -.Bulk Shipments.-> R R -.Packed Goods.-> P P -.Actual Consumption Data.-> A[Forecasting System] A -.Better Forecasts.-> R R -.Information Sharing via CPFR.-> W W -.Information Sharing.-> M M -.Information Sharing.-> S ``` **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **upward amplification of order variability** (left side) versus the **downstream demand signal** (right side). CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment) attempts to break this amplification by **aligning forecasts across all tiers using shared POS data**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **"Explain the role of forecasting in supply chain management. Why is it considered the foundation of all planning activities?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Forecasting plays the role of the **planning cornerstone** in SCM. It serves three foundational purposes: 1. **Demand Estimation** — It estimates future customer demand, which is the primary input for production, inventory, and procurement planning. 2. **Risk Mitigation** — By providing a probabilistic estimate of future needs, it helps the firm prepare for uncertainty in demand, supply, and capacity. 3. **Cost Optimization** — Accurate forecasts reduce both **excess inventory costs** and **stockout costs**, leading to optimized working capital and service levels. Without a reliable forecast, every downstream decision becomes **reactive and costly**, leading to the **Bullwhip Effect**, increased safety stock, and poor customer service. *(1 mark each for the three purposes = 3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** **"Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods. Give two examples of each."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Qualitative Methods | Quantitative Methods | |---|---|---| | **Data Used** | Judgment, opinion, market intelligence | Historical numerical data | | **Best For** | New products, no historical data | Existing products with stable demand | | **Examples** | 1. Delphi Method 2. Market Research / Surveys | 1. Moving Average 2. Exponential Smoothing | *(1.5 marks for the distinction table + 1.5 marks for examples = 3 marks)* --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **various qualitative and quantitative methods of forecasting** used in supply chain management. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A company has recorded the following monthly demand for a product: **120, 130, 125, 140, 135, 145** units. Using **3-month simple moving average**, forecast the demand for the **7th month** and calculate the **MAD** for the forecast period. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks]** **Qualitative Methods (3.5 marks):** - **Delphi Method** — Iterative anonymous expert opinion rounds, used when historical data is unavailable. - **Market Research** — Surveys, focus groups, customer interviews to gauge future intent. - **Sales Force Composite** — Aggregates individual salesperson estimates from the field. - **Panel Consensus** — Open discussion among experts/managers to reach a consensus forecast. **Quantitative Methods (3.5 marks):** - **Time-Series Methods** — Use only past demand data. - *Naïve:* $F_{t+1} = A_t$ - *Simple Moving Average:* $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{\sum A_i}{n}$ - *Exponential Smoothing:* $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ - **Causal / Associative Methods** — Use external variables (price, advertising). - *Linear Regression:* $F = a + bX$ **(b) [7 Marks — Numerical]** **Given Data:** | Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Demand | 120 | 130 | 125 | 140 | 135 | 145 | **Step 1 — Forecast for Month 4** (using months 1, 2, 3): $$ F_4 = \frac{120 + 130 + 125}{3} = \frac{375}{3} = 125 $$ **Step 2 — Forecast for Month 5** (using months 2, 3, 4): $$ F_5 = \frac{130 + 125 + 140}{3} = \frac{395}{3} \approx 131.67 $$ **Step 3 — Forecast for Month 6** (using months 3, 4, 5): $$ F_6 = \frac{125 + 140 + 135}{3} = \frac{400}{3} \approx 133.33 $$ **Step 4 — Forecast for Month 7** (using months 4, 5, 6): $$ F_7 = \frac{140 + 135 + 145}{3} = \frac{420}{3} = 140 \text{ units} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate Absolute Errors and MAD:** | Month | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | |---|---|---|---| | 4 | 140 | 125.00 | 15.00 | | 5 | 135 | 131.67 | 3.33 | | 6 | 145 | 133.33 | 11.67 | $$ MAD = \frac{15.00 + 3.33 + 11.67}{3} = \frac{30.00}{3} = 10.00 \text{ units} $$ **Final Answer:** Forecast for Month 7 = **140 units**; MAD = **10 units**. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **(a)** Explain the **Bullwhip Effect** in supply chains. How does collaborative forecasting (CPFR) help in reducing it? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A firm uses exponential smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$. The forecast for January was 200 units, and the actual demand in January was 220 units. Forecast demand for **February, March, and April** if the actual demands for February and March are 230 and 210 units respectively. Also calculate the **MAPE** for these three periods. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks]** **The Bullwhip Effect (4 marks):** The Bullwhip Effect refers to the **progressive amplification of demand variability** as orders flow upstream from the customer to the manufacturer and supplier. Even a small change in consumer demand can cause large swings in factory orders, leading to excessive inventory, stockouts, and increased costs. **Causes:** Forecast inaccuracy, order batching, price fluctuations, shortage gaming. **CPFR's Role in Mitigation (3 marks):** **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (CPFR)** reduces the bullwhip effect by: - Sharing **Point-of-Sale (POS)** data across the chain. - Jointly developing a **single, unified forecast** among trading partners. - Reducing **forecast uncertainty** and **order batching**. - Improving **service levels** while cutting inventory costs. **(b) [7 Marks — Numerical]** **Given:** $\alpha = 0.3$, $F_{\text{Jan}} = 200$, $A_{\text{Jan}} = 220$, $A_{\text{Feb}} = 230$, $A_{\text{Mar}} = 210$. **Step 1 — Forecast for February:** $$ F_{\text{Feb}} = \alpha \cdot A_{\text{Jan}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot F_{\text{Jan}} = 0.3(220) + 0.7(200) = 66 + 140 = 206 $$ **Step 2 — Forecast for March:** $$ F_{\text{Mar}} = 0.3(230) + 0.7(206) = 69 + 144.2 = 213.2 $$ **Step 3 — Forecast for April:** $$ F_{\text{Apr}} = 0.3(210) + 0.7(213.2) = 63 + 149.24 = 212.24 \text{ units} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate MAPE:** | Period | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | $\dfrac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Feb | 230 | 206.00 | 24.00 | 10.43% | | Mar | 210 | 213.20 | 3.20 | 1.52% | | Apr | 212.24* | 212.24 | 0.24 | 0.11% | *Note: April actual ≈ forecast for MAPE reporting in forecast systems.* $$ MAPE = \frac{10.43 + 1.52 + 0.11}{3} = \frac{12.06}{3} \approx 4.02\% $$ **Final Answer:** $F_{\text{Feb}} = 206$, $F_{\text{Mar}} = 213.2$, $F_{\text{Apr}} = 212.24$ units; **MAPE ≈ 4.02%**. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Forgetting to update $F$ in SES calculations** — Each new forecast uses the *previous* actual and the *previous* forecast. Using stale forecasts is the most common error. > 2. **MAPE vs MAD confusion** — MAPE is a *percentage*; MAD is in *units*. Mixing units costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Bullwhip Effect** — Students often *describe* it but forget to **explain causes** and **mitigation**. Both are required for full marks. > 4. **Tracking Signal signs** — Positive TS = under-forecasting; Negative TS = over-forecasting. Getting this wrong in numerical questions is a frequent error. > 5. **No mention of forecast error** — Always quote forecasts as $F \pm \text{error}$. A bare forecast number loses credit. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > ✅ **Forecasting = Prediction of Future Demand** using past data + judgment. > ✅ **Three Families:** Qualitative | Quantitative-Time Series | Quantitative-Causal. > ✅ **Seven Roles:** Demand planning, production, inventory, procurement, capacity, finance, logistics. > ✅ **Time Horizons:** Short (0–3 mo) → Medium (3 mo – 2 yr) → Long (2+ yr). > ✅ **Core Properties:** Always wrong but useful; aggregated > disaggregated; shorter horizon = more accurate. > ✅ **Key Methods & When to Use:** > - Naïve → Baseline > - SMA / WMA → Smooth random noise > - SES → No trend, no seasonality > - Holt's → Linear trend > - Winter's → Trend + Seasonality > - Regression → Causal drivers > - Delphi → New products > ✅ **Error Metrics:** MAD (units), MSE (penalizes large errors), MAPE (%, scale-free). > ✅ **Bias Detection:** Cumulative Error (CE) and Tracking Signal (TS); control limits $\pm 4$ to $\pm 6$. > ✅ **Bullwhip Effect:** Demand variability amplifies upstream; mitigated by CPFR, POS sharing, VMI, shorter lead times. > ✅ **S&OP Linkage:** Forecasts drive the entire **Sales & Operations Planning** cycle. > ✅ **Golden Rule:** *Aggregated forecasts beat disaggregated ones. Never trust a forecast without an error margin.*

topics.title: Components of Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Components of Forecasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Forecasting** in supply chain management is the systematic process of estimating the future demand for products, services, or resources based on historical data, statistical modeling, and qualitative judgment. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT867)**, forecasting is positioned as the *primary driver* of strategic and operational decisions across procurement, production, distribution, and inventory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *Forecasting is the art and science of predicting future events by systematically analyzing past and present data, often using mathematical models and managerial judgment, to enable proactive supply chain planning.*

### Components of Forecasting – The Formal Framework

The **components of forecasting** refer to the structured, interrelated building blocks that together constitute any forecasting system. These components are:

1. **Time Horizon / Planning Horizon**
2. **Demand Patterns / Time-Series Components**
3. **Forecasting Methods / Techniques**
4. **Data Sources & Information Inputs**
5. **Forecast Error Measurement & Tracking**
6. **Level of Aggregation**
7. **Forecasting Process (Steps)**
8. **Human/Collaborative Judgment (S&OP)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Weather Forecast 🌦️**
> 
> Imagine you are planning a picnic. You look at:
> - **Past data** (last 5 years, the same month was mostly sunny),
> - **Today's signals** (clouds building up, humidity rising),
> - **Expert opinion** (the local meteorologist says "70% rain by evening"),
> - **Pattern recognition** (it's monsoon season, so heavy showers are normal).
> 
> Combining all these, you **forecast** that it will rain at 4 PM.
> 
> This is exactly how supply chain forecasting works. The **components of forecasting** are like the different *instruments* a meteorologist uses — barometer (data), satellite (trends), computer model (quantitative methods), and personal judgment (qualitative input). Each component has a role, and together they build the "weather report" of demand.

### Why Components Matter in KTU Context

In KTU 2024 Scheme, the examiner tests whether you understand that forecasting is **not a single equation** — it is a **system of components** working together. Identifying each component, its purpose, and its interaction is the foundation for 7- and 14-mark questions in Module 2.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook (7 Components) – "D-F-L-E-M-P-T"**
> **D**ata, **F**orecast horizon, **L**evel of aggregation, **E**rror metrics, **M**ethods, **P**rocess steps, **T**ime-series components (demand patterns).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted in Bold

The following **metrics and parameters** are key to KTU-level answers:

- **MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)** – industry-standard accuracy metric
- **MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation)** – average magnitude of forecast error
- **MSE / RMSE** – penalize large errors
- **Tracking Signal (TS)** – detects bias in forecast
- **α, β, γ** – smoothing constants (range **0 to 1**)
- **α₂ (level), α₁ (trend)** – Holt's parameters
- **CYCLE, SEASONAL indices** – multiplicative/additive factors
- **Lead Time (LT)** – critical linkage between forecast and inventory
- **Service Level (β)** – probability of not stocking out, typically **85% – 99%**

These are **the vocabulary markers** KTU examiners scan for in valuation scripts.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Components – Structured Analysis

### Component 1: Time Horizon / Planning Horizon

Forecasts are classified by the time window they cover:

| Horizon Type | Time Range | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Short-term** | 0 – 3 months | Workforce scheduling, replenishment orders |
| **Medium-term** | 3 months – 2 years | Production planning, aggregate planning |
| **Long-term** | 2+ years | Capacity expansion, facility location, R&D |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'How'?** Short-term forecasts need *fine-grained* data (daily/weekly POS data). Long-term forecasts need *macroeconomic indicators* (GDP, industry growth). KTU expects you to **match horizon to method**.

---

### Component 2: Demand Patterns (Time-Series Components)

Every demand pattern is a combination of four fundamental components:

$$D_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$$

Where:
- $L_t$ = **Level** (baseline average demand at period $t$)
- $T_t$ = **Trend** (long-term upward/downward movement)
- $S_t$ = **Seasonality** (regular, predictable fluctuation within a year)
- $C_t$ = **Cyclicality** (longer economic waves, > 1 year)
- $\varepsilon_t$ = **Random / Irregular** (unexplained noise)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Identifying the pattern from a graph/dataset. If a question gives a demand series and asks "identify the components" — you must list all four and justify with numerical evidence.

**Pattern Recognition Checklist:**
- **Horizontal (Stationary):** No trend, no seasonality → use *Moving Average / Simple Exponential Smoothing*.
- **Trend:** Linear upward/downward → use *Holt's Linear Method*.
- **Seasonal:** Repeating peaks/troughs → use *Holt-Winters*.
- **Cyclical:** Long waves (real estate, economy) → use *Econometric Models*.
- **Random:** White noise → use *Naïve Forecast*.

---

### Component 3: Forecasting Methods / Techniques

**Two broad families:**

**A. Qualitative Methods** (used when no data exists — new products):
- Jury of Executive Opinion
- Delphi Method
- Sales Force Composite
- Market Research / Customer Surveys

**B. Quantitative Methods:**

| Sub-class | Methods | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-Series** | Naïve, MA, WMA, ES, Holt, Holt-Winters | Most common in exam |
| **Causal / Regression** | Linear regression, multiple regression, econometric | Linking demand to price/income |
| **Machine Learning (Advanced)** | ARIMA, Neural Nets | Mentioned in 2024 scheme |

> [!TIP]
> **"How to Choose?"** – Decision rule:
> - **Data available + stable demand** → Time-series
> - **Data available + causal driver** → Regression
> - **No data + new product** → Qualitative
> - **Data + uncertainty** → Combination (ensemble) or Collaborative Forecasting (CPFR)

---

### Component 4: Data Sources & Information Inputs

A forecast is only as good as its data. KTU identifies:

- **Internal Data:** POS (Point of Sale), historical sales, inventory records, order pipeline.
- **External Data:** Market reports, macroeconomic indicators, weather, competitor pricing.
- **Promotional Data:** Planned discounts, advertising spend, new product launches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is a 'Component':** The component represents the *raw material* of forecasting. Without clean, relevant data, even the best model fails. KTU often frames a 3-mark question as *"List the data sources required for demand forecasting."*

---

### Component 5: Forecast Error Measurement & Tracking

This is the **most numerically tested component** in KTU papers.

#### 2.5.1 Basic Error Term
$$e_t = A_t - F_t$$

Where $A_t$ = actual demand, $F_t$ = forecast demand at period $t$.

#### 2.5.2 MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation)
$$\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$$

#### 2.5.3 MSE (Mean Squared Error)
$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^{2}$$

#### 2.5.4 RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)
$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$$

#### 2.5.5 MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)
$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$$

#### 2.5.6 Tracking Signal (TS)
$$\text{TS} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$$

**Decision Rules (Memorize for KTU):**
- $\vert \text{TS} \vert \le 4$ → **Forecast is in control** ✅
- $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$ → **Forecast has bias** ⚠️ (re-tune model)

#### 2.5.7 Bias
$$\text{Bias} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t$$

- Bias > 0 → **Under-forecasting** (actual > forecast)
- Bias < 0 → **Over-forecasting** (forecast > actual)

---

### Component 6: Level of Aggregation

Forecasts exist at different levels:
- **Item-level (SKU):** Highly variable, noisy, expensive.
- **Product family / Group level:** More stable, cheaper.
- **Aggregate / Total company level:** Most stable, used for strategic planning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** *Aggregation reduces variability.* Forecasting at the SKU level is hard because of randomness; aggregating up smoothens the pattern. The **square-root law of forecasting** quantifies this:
> 
> $$\sigma_{group} = \frac{\sigma_{item}}{\sqrt{n}}$$
> 
> where $n$ = number of SKUs in the group.

---

### Component 7: Forecasting Process (Standard Steps)

The seven-step process KTU expects:

1. **Define purpose & horizon** of the forecast.
2. **Select data sources** (clean & relevant).
3. **Identify demand pattern** (trend, seasonality, etc.).
4. **Choose forecasting method** matching the pattern.
5. **Generate forecast** $F_{t+1}$.
6. **Measure error** (MAD, MAPE, TS).
7. **Monitor, control & re-forecast** (continuous loop).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **"P-D-P-I-C-G-M"** → Purpose → Data → Pattern → Identification (of method) → Calculate → Gauge (error) → Monitor.

---

### Component 8: Collaborative & Judgmental Input (S&OP / CPFR)

Forecasts are not pure math. They integrate:
- **Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP)** inputs
- **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, Replenishment)** with retailers
- **Managerial override** based on market intelligence

This component ensures forecasts align with **business reality**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Notation | Use in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | Error | Foundation |
| 2 | $\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n}$ | Mean Absolute Deviation | Common |
| 3 | $\text{MSE} = \frac{\sum e_t^{2}}{n}$ | Mean Squared Error | Penalizes outliers |
| 4 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | RMSE | Same units as demand |
| 5 | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}\times 100$ | MAPE (%) | **Most-asked** |
| 6 | $\text{TS} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$ | Tracking Signal | Bias detection |
| 7 | $\text{Bias} = \frac{\sum e_t}{n}$ | Average bias | Direction of error |
| 8 | $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(A_t - F_t)$ | Simple ES | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_{group} = \frac{\sigma_{item}}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Aggregation law | Variance reduction |
| 10 | $\text{Service Level} \propto 1 - \frac{\text{MAD}}{L}$ | Inventory linkage | 7-mark question |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note (per protocol):** All absolute values use `\vert` inside table cells to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No shortcuts, no truncation.** Every algebraic step, every numerical evaluation, and every logical transition is explicitly shown below.

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem – MAPE & Tracking Signal

### Problem Statement
A logistics company recorded the following 6 months of actual demand and forecast demand:

| Month ($t$) | Actual ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 90 |
| 2 | 110 | 105 |
| 3 | 120 | 115 |
| 4 | 130 | 128 |
| 5 | 125 | 130 |
| 6 | 140 | 135 |

**Required:**
1. Compute the **Error** $e_t$ for each month.
2. Compute the **MAD**, **MAPE**, **Bias**, and **Tracking Signal (TS)** for the 6-month period.
3. Comment on whether the forecast is acceptable (use $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ rule).

---

### Step 1: Compute the Error $e_t = A_t - F_t$

For $t = 1$:
$$e_1 = A_1 - F_1 = 100 - 90 = +10$$

For $t = 2$:
$$e_2 = A_2 - F_2 = 110 - 105 = +5$$

For $t = 3$:
$$e_3 = A_3 - F_3 = 120 - 115 = +5$$

For $t = 4$:
$$e_4 = A_4 - F_4 = 130 - 128 = +2$$

For $t = 5$:
$$e_5 = A_5 - F_5 = 125 - 130 = -5$$

For $t = 6$:
$$e_6 = A_6 - F_6 = 140 - 135 = +5$$

**Tabulated error column:**
$$e = [10, 5, 5, 2, -5, 5]$$

---

### Step 2: Compute the Absolute Errors

Taking absolute value of each error:

$$\vert e_1 \vert = 10,\quad \vert e_2 \vert = 5,\quad \vert e_3 \vert = 5,\quad \vert e_4 \vert = 2,\quad \vert e_5 \vert = 5,\quad \vert e_6 \vert = 5$$

Sum:
$$\sum_{t=1}^{6} \vert e_t \vert = 10 + 5 + 5 + 2 + 5 + 5 = 32$$

---

### Step 3: Compute the MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation)

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert = \frac{32}{6} = 5.333\ \text{units}$$

---

### Step 4: Compute the Squared Errors

$$e_1^{2} = 100,\ e_2^{2} = 25,\ e_3^{2} = 25,\ e_4^{2} = 4,\ e_5^{2} = 25,\ e_6^{2} = 25$$

Sum:
$$\sum_{t=1}^{6} e_t^{2} = 100 + 25 + 25 + 4 + 25 + 25 = 204$$

---

### Step 5: Compute MSE and RMSE

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{204}{6} = 34.0$$

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{34.0} \approx 5.831\ \text{units}$$

---

### Step 6: Compute the MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)

Compute the per-period absolute percentage error:
$$\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$$

For $t = 1$:
$$\frac{10}{100} \times 100 = 10.00\%$$

For $t = 2$:
$$\frac{5}{110} \times 100 = 4.545\%$$

For $t = 3$:
$$\frac{5}{120} \times 100 = 4.167\%$$

For $t = 4$:
$$\frac{2}{130} \times 100 = 1.538\%$$

For $t = 5$:
$$\frac{5}{125} \times 100 = 4.000\%$$

For $t = 6$:
$$\frac{5}{140} \times 100 = 3.571\%$$

Sum:
$$\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}\times 100 = 10.000 + 4.545 + 4.167 + 1.538 + 4.000 + 3.571 = 27.821\%$$

Therefore:
$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{27.821}{6} \approx 4.637\%$$

---

### Step 7: Compute the Bias (Mean Error)

$$\sum_{t=1}^{6} e_t = 10 + 5 + 5 + 2 + (-5) + 5 = 22$$

$$\text{Bias} = \frac{22}{6} \approx +3.667\ \text{units}$$

**Interpretation:** Positive bias → the forecast has been **under-forecasting** systematically (actual > forecast). The model is too conservative.

---

### Step 8: Compute the Tracking Signal (TS)

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{22}{5.333} \approx 4.125$$

---

### Step 9: KTU-Style Comment / Decision

The control limits for the Tracking Signal are typically $\pm 4$.

$$\vert \text{TS} \vert = \vert 4.125 \vert = 4.125$$

Since $4.125 > 4$, the forecast is **just outside the acceptable band** and shows a **systematic under-forecasting bias**. **Action required:** Re-tune the model, increase the level/trend parameter, or incorporate a bias-correction factor. The high positive bias (3.667) confirms the issue is not random but structural.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Tip:** A TS value of 4.125 is a *borderline case*. If you write "forecast is acceptable" without checking the limit, you lose the mark. Always apply the rule $\vert \text{TS} \vert \le 4$ explicitly.

---

## 3.3 End-to-End Application – Decision Flow for KTU Case Studies

```
Given a demand series in question paper
            ↓
Step 1: Plot/inspect → identify pattern (trend, seasonal, etc.)
            ↓
Step 2: Match pattern → method (Holt, Holt-Winters, etc.)
            ↓
Step 3: Generate forecast → compute F_{t+1}
            ↓
Step 4: Compute errors → MAD, MAPE, TS
            ↓
Step 5: Decision (|TS| ≤ 4 ?) → accept or re-tune
```

This decision flow is a **full 14-mark answer** when explained with justifications for each step.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Forecasting System – Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Sources] --> B[Demand Pattern Identification]
    B --> C[Forecasting Method Selection]
    C --> D[Forecast Generation F t+1]
    D --> E[Error Measurement MAD MAPE TS]
    E --> F{TS within plus minus 4?}
    F -- yes --> G[Forecast Accepted]
    F -- no --> H[Bias Detected Retune Model]
    H --> C
    G --> I[Supply Chain Decisions Production Inventory Logistics]
    I --> J[Actual Demand A t]
    J --> E
    K[Time Horizon Short Medium Long] --> C
    L[Level of Aggregation SKU Family Group] --> A
    M[Collaborative Input S and OP CPFR] --> D
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- Data flows in from **multiple sources** (Component 4).
- **Time horizon** and **aggregation level** constrain the choice of method.
- The **feedback loop** (Actual demand → Error) is what makes forecasting a *closed-loop control system*, not a one-shot calculation. This is a critical KTU insight.

---

## 4.2 Demand Pattern Decomposition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Total Demand D t] --> L[Level L t]
    D --> T[Trend T t]
    D --> S[Seasonality S t]
    D --> C[Cycle C t]
    D --> R[Random epsilon t]
    L --> ADD{Additive or Multiplicative Model}
    T --> ADD
    S --> ADD
    C --> ADD
    R --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[Forecast Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU interpretation:** In the *additive* model, components sum up ($D = L + T + S + C + \varepsilon$). In the *multiplicative* model, they multiply ($D = L \times T \times S \times C \times \varepsilon$). Choice depends on whether seasonal swings **grow with the trend** (use multiplicative).

---

## 4.3 Component Relationship Matrix (Visual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK[Input Block]
        A1[Data Sources]
        A2[Time Horizon]

    end

    end
```
