
This **four-block architecture** matches the KTU Module 2 learning outcomes: students must be able to *analyse*, *compute*, *control*, and *apply* forecasts.

## 4.4 Decision Tree – Choosing a Forecasting Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Forecasting Task] --> Q1{Is historical data available?}

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled strictly on **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) with internal choice. Model answers follow KTU valuation key style.

---

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define forecasting. List any four components of forecasting.** *(3 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Forecasting is the process of estimating future demand for products or services using historical data, statistical models, and managerial judgment, to support supply chain planning.
> 
> **Four Components:**
> 1. **Time Horizon** – short, medium, or long-term planning window.
> 2. **Demand Patterns** – level, trend, seasonality, cyclicality, randomness.
> 3. **Forecasting Methods** – qualitative, quantitative (time-series, causal).
> 4. **Error Measurement** – MAD, MAPE, Tracking Signal.

*(For full 3 marks: definition + any 4 components clearly listed.)*

---

### **Q2. What is Tracking Signal? State the rule used to decide if a forecast is acceptable.** *(3 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The Tracking Signal (TS) is a measure used to detect whether a forecast model is exhibiting bias (systematic over- or under-forecasting). It is the ratio of the cumulative sum of forecast errors (RSFE) to the MAD.
> 
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{TS} = \frac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$$
> 
> **Acceptability Rule:** A forecast is considered *in control* and acceptable when
> $$\vert \text{TS} \vert \le 4$$
> If $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$, the forecast has systematic bias and the model must be re-tuned.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Components of Forecasting with Numerical Error Analysis** *(14 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the major components of forecasting in a supply chain context.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The components of forecasting form an integrated system. The major components are:

**1. Time Horizon:**
Forecasts are made for short-term (≤ 3 months), medium-term (3 months – 2 years), and long-term (> 2 years) horizons. Short-term forecasts drive daily replenishment; long-term forecasts support capacity and strategic decisions.

**2. Demand Patterns:**
Every demand series is composed of *level, trend, seasonality, cyclical, and random* elements. Identifying these components is the prerequisite to selecting the right method. For example, a level-only demand uses Simple Exponential Smoothing; a trended demand uses Holt's model.

**3. Forecasting Methods:**
These are classified as *qualitative* (Delphi, jury of executive opinion) for situations with no data, and *quantitative* (time-series, causal, AI-based) for data-rich environments. The choice of method is the heart of the forecasting system.

**4. Data Sources:**
Internal (POS, sales history) and external (macroeconomic, market research) data feed the model. Data quality directly determines forecast quality.

**5. Error Measurement:**
Metrics like MAD, MSE, RMSE, MAPE, and Tracking Signal quantify forecast accuracy. MAPE is industry-preferred because it is scale-independent.

**6. Level of Aggregation:**
Forecasting at higher levels (group, family) is more accurate than at SKU level due to variance reduction. The square-root law $\sigma_{group} = \sigma_{item}/\sqrt{n}$ formalises this.

**7. Forecasting Process:**
A structured 7-step process — purpose definition → data collection → pattern identification → method choice → forecast computation → error measurement → monitoring and control.

**8. Collaborative Input (S&OP / CPFR):**
Forecasts are blended with managerial judgment and shared planning across the supply chain to align supply with realistic demand.

*(Word count approx. 220; with diagrams and bullet structure, this fetches full 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) The actual and forecast demand for a product over 8 months is given below. Compute MAD, MAPE, Bias, and Tracking Signal. Comment on the forecast.** *(7 marks)*

| Month | Actual ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 210 |
| 2 | 220 | 215 |
| 3 | 240 | 230 |
| 4 | 260 | 250 |
| 5 | 280 | 270 |
| 6 | 270 | 280 |
| 7 | 290 | 285 |
| 8 | 310 | 300 |

**Step 1: Compute Error $e_t = A_t - F_t$**

$$e_1 = -10,\ e_2 = 5,\ e_3 = 10,\ e_4 = 10,\ e_5 = 10,\ e_6 = -10,\ e_7 = 5,\ e_8 = 10$$

**Step 2: Absolute Errors and Percentage Errors**

| Month | $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}\times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | -10 | 10 | 5.00% |
| 2 | 5 | 5 | 2.27% |
| 3 | 10 | 10 | 4.17% |
| 4 | 10 | 10 | 3.85% |
| 5 | 10 | 10 | 3.57% |
| 6 | -10 | 10 | 3.70% |
| 7 | 5 | 5 | 1.72% |
| 8 | 10 | 10 | 3.23% |
| **Sum** | **+30** | **70** | **27.51%** |

**Step 3: Compute MAD**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{70}{8} = 8.75\ \text{units}$$

**Step 4: Compute MAPE**

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{27.51}{8} = 3.44\%$$

**Step 5: Compute Bias**

$$\text{Bias} = \frac{+30}{8} = +3.75\ \text{units}$$

**Step 6: Compute Tracking Signal**

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{30}{8.75} \approx 3.43$$

**Step 7: Comment**

Since $\vert \text{TS} \vert = 3.43 \le 4$, the forecast is **acceptable / in control**. However, the positive bias of +3.75 suggests a **slight under-forecasting tendency**, indicating that the model could be marginally improved by increasing the level constant $\alpha$. The MAPE of 3.44% is excellent (industry benchmark for good forecasts is MAPE < 10%).

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): Time-Series Components & Forecasting Methods** *(14 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the components of a time-series demand pattern with suitable diagrams.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A time-series demand pattern is a temporal sequence of demand observations. The classical decomposition identifies five components:

**1. Level ($L_t$):** The baseline average value around which demand fluctuates.

**2. Trend ($T_t$):** The long-term directional movement — increasing, decreasing, or flat. Quantified as the slope of the linear fit.

**3. Seasonality ($S_t$):** Regular, predictable fluctuations within a year (e.g., AC sales peak in summer, umbrellas in monsoon). Captured by seasonal indices.

**4. Cyclicality ($C_t$):** Longer-term oscillations (multi-year) tied to macroeconomic or industry cycles. Less predictable than seasonality.

**5. Random/Irregular ($\varepsilon_t$):** Unpredictable noise from external shocks (strikes, weather extremes).

The classical model combines them either **additively**:
$$D_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$$

or **multiplicatively**:
$$D_t = L_t \times T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times \varepsilon_t$$

*Diagrammatic description (since KTU accepts text-based diagrams):*

```
   D|
    |   /\  /\        /\  /\
    |  /  \/  \      /  \/  \    /\
    | /        \    /        \  /  \
    |/          \  /          \/    \
    |            \/                  \____
    +------------------------------------ t
       |     |     |     |     |     |
     Level Trend  Season Season Cycle
```

---

**(b) Compare qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods. When is each appropriate?** *(7 marks)*

| Aspect | Qualitative Methods | Quantitative Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Data requirement** | Little or no historical data | Requires substantial historical data |
| **Basis** | Judgment, opinion, expertise | Mathematical/statistical models |
| **Speed** | Relatively fast | Slower to build and run |
| **Cost** | Low to moderate | High (data + software) |
| **Accuracy** | Lower for stable demand | Higher when pattern is clear |
| **Examples** | Delphi, Jury of Executive, Sales Force Composite | Naïve, MA, ES, Holt, Holt-Winters, Regression, ARIMA |
| **Used when** | New product, no history, disruptive innovation | Stable demand, mature product, sufficient data |

**Rule of Thumb:**
- **Qualitative** → New product launch, strategic shifts, no historical data.
- **Quantitative** → Mature products, repeatable patterns, large datasets.
- **Hybrid (Ensemble)** → Combine both for robustness (e.g., 70% statistical + 30% judgment).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing "qualitative" with "subjective"** — qualitative is *systematic* (e.g., Delphi is structured). Don't write "qualitative = guesswork."
> 2. **Mixing up MAPE and MAD** in calculations. MAPE uses $A_t$ in the denominator, MAD uses $\vert e_t \vert$ directly.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ rule.** Always state the rule and the comparison.
> 4. **Not showing sub-steps** in 14-mark numericals. Each formula application needs a sub-line of calculation.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment"** in numericals. KTU deducts 1-2 marks for missing the interpretive sentence at the end.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 2: Components of Forecasting – Final Cheat Sheet**

### ✅ Core Definition
Forecasting is the **systematic estimation of future demand** using historical data, statistical models, and managerial judgment.

### ✅ Eight Key Components
1. **Time Horizon** → short / medium / long-term
2. **Demand Patterns** → level, trend, seasonality, cyclical, random
3. **Methods** → qualitative (Delphi, jury) + quantitative (time-series, causal, AI)
4. **Data Sources** → internal (POS, sales) + external (macro, market)
5. **Error Metrics** → MAD, MSE, RMSE, MAPE, TS, Bias
6. **Level of Aggregation** → SKU / family / group (variance reduction)
7. **Process Steps** → 7-step structured cycle
8. **Collaborative Input** → S&OP, CPFR, managerial override

### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas
- $e_t = A_t - F_t$
- $\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n}$
- $\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}\times 100$
- $\text{TS} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$  →  $\vert \text{TS} \vert \le 4$ acceptable
- $\sigma_{group} = \frac{\sigma_{item}}{\sqrt{n}}$  (aggregation law)
- $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(A_t - F_t)$ (Simple ES)

### ✅ Method Selection Rule
| Pattern | Best Method |
|---|---|
| Level only | Naïve / Simple ES |
| Trend | Holt's Linear |
| Trend + Seasonality | Holt-Winters |
| Causal relationship | Linear Regression |
| New product / no data | Delphi / Jury |

### ✅ Numerical Workflow (Always Follow)
**Error → Absolute → MAD → MAPE → Bias → TS → Comment**

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Wrong denominator (MAPE uses $A_t$, not $F_t$).
- Forgetting absolute value bars in MAD.
- Using $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$ as "good" — it's actually **bad**.
- Treating qualitative as "guessing."
- Skipping diagrams in 7-mark descriptive questions.

### ✅ Examiner-Trigger Words (use in answers)
*"Systematic", "Closed-loop control", "Bias detection", "Aggregation benefit", "Pattern-method matching", "Collaborative Planning", "Service level linkage."*

These words are **valuation signal flags** — they signal to the examiner that you understand the syllabus contextually, not just memorised the formulas.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes – Components of Forecasting**
**Prepared in compliance with KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT867) and NEP 2020 outcome-based assessment framework.**

topics.title: Forecasting Methods; Aggregate Planning in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forecasting Methods; Aggregate Planning in Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Forecasting – Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecasting** is the art and science of predicting future demand for a company's products or services by analyzing historical data, market intelligence, and causal relationships. In the KTU 2024 SCM framework, forecasting is the foundational input that drives **every downstream SCOR (Supply Chain Operations Reference) process** — procurement, production, distribution, and inventory planning.

Mathematically stated, a forecast is a *point estimate* of a future random variable:

$$\hat{D}_{t+h} = f(D_t, D_{t-1}, \ldots, D_{t-n}, X_t, X_{t-1}, \ldots)$$

where $\hat{D}_{t+h}$ is the forecast for period $t+h$, $D$ are past actual demands, $X$ are explanatory (causal) variables, and $f$ is the chosen model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Weather Oracle"

Imagine you are planning a beach wedding in Kerala for **December 2026**. You wouldn't decide on an open-air venue based on a single look at today's sky. Instead, you would:
1. Study **past weather patterns** (last 5 Decembers),
2. Apply **seasonal adjustments** (NE monsoon probability),
3. Use a **causal signal** (current El-Niño forecast).

A forecast is exactly that — a **structured "oracle"** that blends history with judgment to minimize the *cost of being wrong* (stockouts vs. overstock). In SCM, this oracle decides **how much to produce, when to order, and how much safety stock to hold**.

## 1.3 Aggregate Planning – Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Planning (AP)** is the medium-range (typically 3–18 months) tactical process of determining the *overall production quantity, workforce level, and inventory position* needed to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost — usually expressed in **family units** (e.g., "litres of paint", "tons of steel") rather than individual SKUs.

In KTU 2024 SCM parlance, AP sits between the **strategic** (long-range capacity) and **operational** (short-range scheduling) layers:

$$\text{Strategic Capacity} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Forecast}}\; \text{Aggregate Plan} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Master Schedule}}\; \text{Shop Floor}$$

### 1.4 Intuition – "The Bakery's Monthly Headache"

Picture *Anna's Bakery* in Kochi. She makes **three product families**: Bread, Cakes, Pastries.
- **December** (Christmas + New Year) → demand doubles for Cakes.
- **June** (school reopens) → Bread demand spikes.

She cannot build a new oven every month (that is *capacity* planning) and she cannot bake 1 cake every hour (that is *detailed scheduling*). What she *can* do is **decide in November**:
- Hire 2 temporary bakers for December? (Chase strategy)
- Bake 2x normal cake volume in November and refrigerate? (Level strategy)
- A mix? (Hybrid strategy)

That monthly family-level decision is **Aggregate Planning**.

## 1.5 Why These Two Topics Are Combined in Module 2

Forecasting provides the **demand input**; Aggregate Planning **consumes that input** to generate production, workforce, and inventory decisions. KTU 2024 groups them because a student cannot master AP without first understanding *where the numbers come from* and *how good those numbers are* (forecast error metrics).

## 1.6 Key Metrics & Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast | $\hat{D}_t$ | Predicted demand for period $t$ | — |
| Actual | $D_t$ | Realized demand for period $t$ | — |
| Forecast Error | $e_t$ | $D_t - \hat{D}_t$ | can be + or − |
| MAD | — | Mean Absolute Deviation | always $\geq 0$ |
| MAPE | — | Mean Absolute Percentage Error | 0 – 100 % |
| MSE | — | Mean Squared Error | always $\geq 0$ |
| Tracking Signal | TS | Cumulative Error / MAD | $\pm 4$ to $\pm 8$ control limits |
| Planning Horizon | $H$ | Months covered by AP | **3–18 months** |
| Aggregate Unit | — | Family-level measure | kg, litres, hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Always carry the **unit** in every aggregate planning table — examiners deduct marks when "100" appears without specifying "100 kg" or "100 man-hours".
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Forecasting Methods

Forecasting methods bifurcate into **Qualitative** (judgmental) and **Quantitative** (mathematical/statistical) categories.

```
Forecasting
├── 2.1.1 Qualitative Methods
│   ├── Executive Opinion (Top-down gut feel)
│   ├── Sales Force Composite (Bottom-up from field)
│   ├── Delphi Method (Iterative anonymous expert panel)
│   └── Market Research / Customer Surveys
├── 2.1.2 Quantitative — Time Series (Univariate)
│   ├── Naïve Forecast
│   ├── Simple Moving Average (SMA)
│   ├── Weighted Moving Average (WMA)
│   ├── Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
│   ├── Holt's Method (Trend, no seasonality)
│   └── Winter's Method (Trend + Seasonality)
└── 2.1.3 Quantitative — Associative / Causal
    └── Linear Regression (Least Squares)
```

### 2.1.1 Qualitative Methods – When Used

| Method | Best Use Case | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Executive Opinion | New product, no history | Bias-prone, political |
| Sales Force Composite | Regional demand patterns | Sales reps may under-forecast to lower quotas |
| Delphi | Long-range (> 3 yr) tech forecasting | Slow (multiple rounds) |
| Market Research | Launch of new variant | Expensive |

### 2.1.2 Quantitative Time-Series Methods

#### (a) Naïve Forecast
The simplest possible model: tomorrow's demand equals today's demand.

$$\hat{D}_{t+1} = D_t$$

#### (b) Simple Moving Average (SMA)
Averages the last $n$ periods; gives equal weight to each.

$$\hat{D}_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}$$

#### (c) Weighted Moving Average (WMA)
Weights sum to 1; recent data weighted more.

$$\hat{D}_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

#### (d) Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
Recursive form — only the previous forecast and actual are needed.

$$\hat{D}_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha)\hat{D}_t, \quad 0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$$

#### (e) Holt's Two-Parameter Method (Trend)
Separately smooths **level** ($L_t$) and **trend** ($T_t$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_t &= \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) \\
T_t &= \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1} \\
\hat{D}_{t+m} &= L_t + m \, T_t
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (f) Winter's Three-Parameter Method (Trend + Seasonality)
Adds a seasonal index $S_t$ with period $p$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_t &= \alpha \frac{D_t}{S_{t-p}} + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) \\
T_t &= \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1} \\
S_t &= \gamma \frac{D_t}{L_t} + (1-\gamma) S_{t-p}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.1.3 Associative (Causal) Method – Linear Regression

$$\hat{y} = a + b x$$

$$
b = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}, \quad
a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}
$$

where $y$ = demand, $x$ = causal driver (e.g., GDP, advertising spend).

### 2.2 Forecast Error Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| MAD | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert D_t - \hat{D}_t \vert$ | Easy interpretation; same unit as data |
| MSE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum (D_t - \hat{D}_t)^2$ | Penalizes large errors heavily |
| MAPE | $\frac{100}{n}\sum \frac{\vert D_t - \hat{D}_t \vert}{D_t}$ | Scale-free comparison across products |
| Tracking Signal | $\frac{\sum (D_t - \hat{D}_t)}{\text{MAD}}$ | Bias detection; control limits $\pm 4$ to $\pm 8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trap:** In the MSE/MAD table, write $\vert x \vert$ as $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking markdown — but in your answer sheet, always write the **absolute value bars clearly**.

### 2.3 Aggregate Planning – Inputs, Outputs, Decisions

**Inputs:**
- Demand forecast (from Section 2.1–2.2)
- Current workforce, inventory, and backorder levels
- Capacity (regular time, overtime)
- Cost data (hiring, firing, holding, backorder, OT, subcontracting)

**Decisions (per period):**
- Production quantity in regular time ($P_t^{RT}$)
- Production in overtime ($P_t^{OT}$)
- Subcontracted units ($P_t^{SC}$)
- Workforce hired ($H_t$) and fired ($F_t$)
- Ending inventory ($I_t$) or backorders ($B_t$)

**Outputs:**
- Production plan (units per period)
- Workforce plan (headcount per period)
- Inventory/backorder plan
- Total cost

### 2.4 Aggregate Planning Strategies

| Strategy | Workforce | Inventory | Best When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chase** | Varies to match demand | Kept low, backorders allowed | Skilled labor easy to hire/fire |
| **Level** | Constant | Builds up in slack, depletes in peak | Stable demand, skilled scarce workforce |
| **Mixed / Hybrid** | Some variation + some inventory | Moderate | Most realistic in practice |
| **Subcontracting** | Constant | Low | Short peak surges |
| **Overtime / Undertime** | Constant | Low | Demand surge is small |

### 2.5 Cost Components in Aggregate Planning

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{H} \Big[ &C_{RT}\,P_t^{RT} + C_{OT}\,P_t^{OT} + C_{SC}\,P_t^{SC} \\
&+ C_H H_t + C_F F_t + C_I I_t + C_B B_t \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Cost Component | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{RT}$ | Regular-time production | ₹/unit |
| $C_{OT}$ | Overtime production | ₹/unit (1.25 – 1.5 × $C_{RT}$) |
| $C_{SC}$ | Subcontracting | ₹/unit (highest variable cost) |
| $C_H$ | Hiring (training + onboarding) | ₹/worker |
| $C_F$ | Firing (severance) | ₹/worker |
| $C_I$ | Inventory holding | ₹/unit-period |
| $C_B$ | Backorder / lost sale | ₹/unit-period |

### 2.6 The Transportation Method of Aggregate Planning

A classic **graphical / linear-programming** approach. Set up a cost matrix where:
- **Rows** = production periods (capacity available)
- **Columns** = demand periods (forecasted requirement)
- **Cell cost** = Regular production cost + Holding cost or Backorder cost for the gap between production period and demand period.

$$
\text{Cell Cost} = C_{RT}(P_t^{RT}) + 
\begin{cases}
C_I \cdot (j-i) & \text{if } j \geq i \quad \text{(holding)} \\
C_B \cdot (i-j) & \text{if } j < i \quad \text{(backorder)}
\end{cases}
$$

Then solve as a **balanced transportation problem** using MODI or stepping-stone.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki)**: AP decides monthly car family volumes; forecast drives the family-level decision while detailed scheduling handles variants.
- **FMCG (e.g., Hindustan Unilever)**: AP synchronizes with seasonal monsoon demand; WMA + Winter's method is industry-standard.
- **Electronics (e.g., Apple iPhone launch)**: Executive opinion + market research dominate because prior history is unavailable.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Concept | Equation | Boundary / Default |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naïve | $\hat{D}_{t+1} = D_t$ | — |
| 2 | SMA (n-period) | $\hat{D}_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}D_{t-i}$ | n = 3 to 6 |
| 3 | WMA | $\hat{D}_{t+1} = \sum w_i D_{t-i+1}$ | $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 4 | SES | $\hat{D}_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{D}_t$ | $0.05 \leq \alpha \leq 0.30$ |
| 5 | Holt level | $L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})$ | $0 \leq \alpha, \beta \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Holt trend | $T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$ | — |
| 7 | Holt forecast | $\hat{D}_{t+m} = L_t + mT_t$ | m = future steps |
| 8 | Regression slope | $b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$ | — |
| 9 | Regression intercept | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | — |
| 10 | MAD | $\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \lvert D_t - \hat{D}_t \rvert$ | — |
| 11 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum (D_t - \hat{D}_t)^2$ | — |
| 12 | MAPE (%) | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n}\sum \frac{\lvert D_t - \hat{D}_t \rvert}{D_t}$ | — |
| 13 | Tracking Signal | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{\sum (D_t - \hat{D}_t)}{\text{MAD}}$ | control: $\pm 4$ to $\pm 8$ |
| 14 | AP total cost | $C = \sum [C_{RT}P^{RT} + C_{OT}P^{OT} + C_H H + C_F F + C_I I + C_B B]$ | per period $t$ |
| 15 | Holding cell cost | $C_{RT} + C_I (j-i)$ | $j \geq i$ |
| 16 | Backorder cell cost | $C_{RT} + C_B (i-j)$ | $j < i$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Moving Average

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Problem]**

**Problem:** A retailer recorded the following weekly demand for a SKU. Compute the 4-period Simple Moving Average forecast for weeks 5 to 8.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand $D_t$ | 120 | 135 | 128 | 142 | 150 | 138 | 160 | 165 |

**Solution – Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Recognize the formula.**

$$\hat{D}_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2} + D_{t-3}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute forecast for week 5** (uses weeks 1–4).

$$\hat{D}_5 = \frac{120 + 135 + 128 + 142}{4} = \frac{525}{4} = 131.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute forecast for week 6** (uses weeks 2–5).

$$\hat{D}_6 = \frac{135 + 128 + 142 + 150}{4} = \frac{555}{4} = 138.75$$

**Step 4 — Compute forecast for week 7** (uses weeks 3–6).

$$\hat{D}_7 = \frac{128 + 142 + 150 + 138}{4} = \frac{558}{4} = 139.50$$

**Step 5 — Compute forecast for week 8** (uses weeks 4–7).

$$\hat{D}_8 = \frac{142 + 150 + 138 + 160}{4} = \frac{590}{4} = 147.50$$

**Step 6 — Tabulate the result.**

| Week $t$ | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $\hat{D}_t$ | Error $e_t = D_t - \hat{D}_t$ | $\lvert e_t \rvert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 150 | 131.25 | 18.75 | 18.75 |
| 6 | 138 | 138.75 | -0.75 | 0.75 |
| 7 | 160 | 139.50 | 20.50 | 20.50 |
| 8 | 165 | 147.50 | 17.50 | 17.50 |
| **Sum** | — | — | 56.00 | 57.50 |

**Step 7 — Compute MAD.**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{57.50}{4} = 14.375 \text{ units}$$

**Step 8 — Compute MAPE.**

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{4}\left(\frac{18.75}{150} + \frac{0.75}{138} + \frac{20.50}{160} + \frac{17.50}{165}\right) = \frac{100}{4}(0.1250 + 0.0054 + 0.1281 + 0.1061) = \frac{100}{4}(0.3546) \approx 8.87\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Step Marks:** Each correctly tabulated row fetches 1 mark. The final MAD and MAPE computations are 2 marks each. Do not forget to **round to 2 decimal places**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Exponential Smoothing (SES)

**Problem:** Using $\alpha = 0.30$ and $\hat{D}_1 = 100$, compute the SES forecast for weeks 2 through 8 from the demand series in Example 3.1.

**Step 1 — Recursive formula.**

$$\hat{D}_{t+1} = 0.30 \, D_t + 0.70 \, \hat{D}_t$$

**Step 2 — Compute iteratively.**

**Week 2:**

$$\hat{D}_2 = 0.30(120) + 0.70(100) = 36 + 70 = 106.00$$

**Week 3:**

$$\hat{D}_3 = 0.30(135) + 0.70(106) = 40.50 + 74.20 = 114.70$$

**Week 4:**

$$\hat{D}_4 = 0.30(128) + 0.70(114.70) = 38.40 + 80.29 = 118.69$$

**Week 5:**

$$\hat{D}_5 = 0.30(142) + 0.70(118.69) = 42.60 + 83.08 = 125.68$$

**Week 6:**

$$\hat{D}_6 = 0.30(150) + 0.70(125.68) = 45.00 + 87.98 = 132.98$$

**Week 7:**

$$\hat{D}_7 = 0.30(138) + 0.70(132.98) = 41.40 + 93.09 = 134.49$$

**Week 8:**

$$\hat{D}_8 = 0.30(160) + 0.70(134.49) = 48.00 + 94.14 = 142.14$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate.**

| Week $t$ | $D_t$ | $\hat{D}_t$ (SES, α = 0.30) | $e_t$ | $\lvert e_t \rvert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 135 | 106.00 | 29.00 | 29.00 |
| 3 | 128 | 114.70 | 13.30 | 13.30 |
| 4 | 142 | 118.69 | 23.31 | 23.31 |
| 5 | 150 | 125.68 | 24.32 | 24.32 |
| 6 | 138 | 132.98 | 5.02 | 5.02 |
| 7 | 160 | 134.49 | 25.51 | 25.51 |
| 8 | 165 | 142.14 | 22.86 | 22.86 |
| **Sum** | — | — | 143.32 | 143.32 |

**Step 4 — Compute MAD for SES.**

$$\text{MAD}_{\text{SES}} = \frac{143.32}{7} \approx 20.47$$

**Step 5 — Compare methods.**

| Method | MAD |
|---|---|
| SMA (4-period) | 14.375 |
| SES (α = 0.30) | 20.47 |

**Conclusion:** For this dataset, **SMA outperforms SES** because the demand series has a strong linear trend — SES lags trends unless upgraded to Holt's method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight Box:** A useful rule of thumb in KTU problems — when MAD of SES is higher than MAD of SMA, the data has a *trend*; switch to Holt's. When SES wins, data is *stationary* (no trend).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Linear Regression Demand Forecast

**Problem:** A company observes the relationship between advertising spend (₹000) and product sales (units) over 6 months:

| Month | x (₹ '000) | y (units) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 200 |
| 2 | 12 | 230 |
| 3 | 14 | 260 |
| 4 | 16 | 280 |
| 5 | 18 | 310 |
| 6 | 20 | 340 |

Compute the regression line $\hat{y} = a + bx$. If the company plans to spend ₹ 25,000 next month, what is the forecast?

**Step 1 — Compute sums.**

| Quantity | Calculation | Value |
|---|---|---|
| n | — | 6 |
| $\sum x$ | 10+12+14+16+18+20 | 90 |
| $\sum y$ | 200+230+260+280+310+340 | 1620 |
| $\sum x^2$ | 100+144+196+256+324+400 | 1420 |
| $\sum xy$ | 2000+2760+3640+4480+5580+6800 | 25260 |
| $\bar{x}$ | 90/6 | 15 |
| $\bar{y}$ | 1620/6 | 270 |

**Step 2 — Compute slope $b$.**

$$
b = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} = \frac{6(25260) - (90)(1620)}{6(1420) - (90)^2}
$$

$$
b = \frac{151560 - 145800}{8520 - 8100} = \frac{5760}{420} = 13.714 \text{ units per ₹ '000}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intercept $a$.**

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 270 - 13.714 \times 15 = 270 - 205.71 = 64.29$$

**Step 4 — Regression equation.**

$$\hat{y} = 64.29 + 13.714\, x$$

**Step 5 — Forecast for x = 25 (₹ 25,000).**

$$\hat{y} = 64.29 + 13.714 \times 25 = 64.29 + 342.86 = 407.15 \approx 407 \text{ units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the **intermediate numerator and denominator** when computing $b$ — examiners want to see the substitution.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Aggregate Planning (Level Strategy Cost)

**Problem:** A firm forecasts demand for the next 4 months: **1200, 1500, 1800, 1500** units. Initial inventory = 100 units, no backorders, no backorders allowed, final inventory desired = 100 units. Regular-time production cost ₹ 10/unit, holding cost ₹ 2/unit/month, hiring cost ₹ 300/worker, firing cost ₹ 400/worker, starting workforce = 40 workers producing 40 units/month each.

Compare (a) **Chase Strategy** and (b) **Level Strategy** on total cost.

### (a) Chase Strategy — Match production to demand

Each worker produces 40 units. Workforce required each month = $\lceil D_t / 40 \rceil$.

| Month | Demand | Workers needed | Hired | Fired |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1200 | 30 | 0 | 10 |
| 2 | 1500 | 38 | 8 | 0 |
| 3 | 1800 | 45 | 7 | 0 |
| 4 | 1500 | 38 | 0 | 7 |

Production = exact demand (no inventory change). Holding = 0. Hiring cost = $(8 + 7) \times 300 = 4500$. Firing cost = $(10 + 7) \times 400 = 6800$. Production cost = $(1200+1500+1800+1500)\times 10 = 60000$.

$$
C_{\text{chase}} = 60000 + 4500 + 6800 + 0 = \mathbf{₹\,71300}
$$

### (b) Level Strategy — Constant workforce, absorb with inventory

Total demand = $1200+1500+1800+1500 = 6000$ units. With initial 100 and final 100, we still produce 6000 over 4 months → **1500 units/month → 38 workers constant**.

Hire from 40 to 38 = fire 2 → cost = $2 \times 400 = 800$.

**Production plan:**

| Month | Demand | Production | Beg Inv | End Inv |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1200 | 1500 | 100 | 400 |
| 2 | 1500 | 1500 | 400 | 400 |
| 3 | 1800 | 1500 | 400 | 100 |
| 4 | 1500 | 1500 | 100 | 100 |

**Holding cost:**
$(400+400+100+100) \times 2 = 1000 \times 2 = 2000$.

**Production cost:** $6000 \times 10 = 60000$.

**Total cost:**
$$
C_{\text{level}} = 60000 + 800 + 2000 = \mathbf{₹\,62800}
$$

### Conclusion

The **Level Strategy is cheaper** by ₹ 8500 in this scenario because hiring/firing costs dominate over holding costs — a textbook KTU outcome.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU problems:** Chase is preferred when **holding cost is high** and **labor cost of change is low** (e.g., unskilled labor). Level is preferred when **hiring/firing is expensive** (skilled labor, unions) and **holding is cheap**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Tracking Signal Interpretation

Using the SMA data from Example 3.1:

**Cumulative Error (RSFE)** = $\sum e_t = 56.00$.

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{56.00}{14.375} \approx 3.90$$

Since $|TS| = 3.90 < 4$ (lower control limit), the forecast is **unbiased** — no systematic over- or under-forecasting. Acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forecasting Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Demand Forecasting Problem]) --> nodeQ1{Is historical data available?}
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeQual[Use Qualitative Methods]
    nodeQual --> nodeDelphi[Delphi Method]
    nodeQual --> nodeExec[Executive Opinion]
    nodeQual --> nodeMR[Market Research]
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2{Is the causal driver known?}
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeReg[Use Linear Regression]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeQ3{Does the series have trend or seasonality?}
    nodeQ3 -- No Stationary --> nodeSES[Simple Exponential Smoothing]
    nodeQ3 -- Trend only --> nodeHolt[Holt Two-Parameter Method]
    nodeQ3 -- Trend plus Seasonality --> nodeWinter[Winter Three-Parameter Method]
    nodeQ3 -- Short series stable --> nodeSMA[Simple Moving Average]
    nodeQ3 -- Short series with recency bias --> nodeWMA[Weighted Moving Average]
    nodeReg --> nodeVal[Validate using MAD MAPE and Tracking Signal]
    nodeSES --> nodeVal
    nodeHolt --> nodeVal
    nodeWinter --> nodeVal
    nodeSMA --> nodeVal
    nodeWMA --> nodeVal
    nodeVal --> nodeDec{TS within plus minus 4 to 8?}
    nodeDec -- Yes --> nodeUse[Deploy Forecast to Aggregate Planning]
    nodeDec -- No --> nodeRecal[Recalibrate Alpha Beta Gamma]
    nodeRecal --> nodeVal
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart is the **first thing** KTU examiners look for in a 14-mark "Discuss forecasting methods" answer. Reproducing a similar flow chart fetches 2–3 easy marks.

---

## 4.2 Aggregate Planning Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Demand Forecast Module 2A] --> nodeB[Capacity and Cost Data]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Aggregate Planning Engine]
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD{Choose Strategy}
    nodeD -- Chase --> nodeE1[Workforce varies each period]
    nodeD -- Level --> nodeE2[Constant workforce plus inventory buffer]
    nodeD -- Hybrid --> nodeE3[Mix of workforce inventory and overtime]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF[Master Production Schedule]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE3 --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Shop Floor Execution]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Procurement Orders]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Distribution Plan]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Feedback Loop back to Forecast Accuracy]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeA
```

---

## 4.3 Forecast Error Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeData[(Historical Demand Database)] --> nodeModel[Forecasting Model]
    nodeModel --> nodeFC[Forecast F t plus 1]
    nodeFC --> nodeCompare[Compare with Actual D t plus 1]
    nodeCompare --> nodeErr[Compute Error e and MAD MAPE]
    nodeErr --> nodeTS[Compute Tracking Signal]
    nodeTS -- Within plus minus 4 to 8 --> nodeOK[Accept Model]
    nodeTS -- Outside Limits --> nodeAdj[Re-tune Alpha Beta or Switch Model]
    nodeAdj --> nodeModel
    nodeOK --> nodeOut[Feed Forecast into Aggregate Planner]
```

---

## 4.4 Aggregate Planning Decision Matrix

| Demand Pattern | Cost Trade-off | Recommended Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Highly seasonal + cheap inventory | Hold inventory | **Level** |
| Highly seasonal + expensive inventory | Vary workforce | **Chase** |
| Mildly varying demand | Avoid extremes | **Hybrid (Mixed)** |
| Short peak only | Use overtime / subcontracting | **Overtime / Subcontract** |
| Declining demand phase | Avoid hiring | **Level + attrition** |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of forecast | Judgment, opinion, intuition | Mathematical/statistical model |
| Data required | No historical data needed | Historical numerical data essential |
| Subjectivity | High | Low |
| Time horizon | Typically long-range | Short to medium range |
| Example | Delphi method, Sales force composite | Moving average, Exponential smoothing, Regression |

> KTU marker awards **1 mark per row** plus 1 mark for at least one valid example.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define the Tracking Signal. What is its significance in forecasting?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Tracking Signal (TS)** is the ratio of the **Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)** to the **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**:

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\sum (D_t - \hat{D}_t)}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}}$$

**Significance:**
1. Detects **forecast bias** — whether forecasts are systematically too high or too low.
2. Acts as an **early warning system**; control limits are typically $\pm 4$ to $\pm 8$ MADs.
3. If TS exceeds limits → model needs **re-tuning** (α, β adjustment) or replacement.

> Examiner usually gives 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for the three significance points.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE of the following.]**

---

### ❖ Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** *A paint manufacturer forecasts the following monthly demand (in '000 litres) for the next 6 months:*

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 100 | 120 | 130 | 110 | 140 | 160 |

**(a)** Compute the **3-month Simple Moving Average** forecast for months 4, 5, 6. Hence calculate **MAD and MAPE** for these forecasts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Simple Exponential Smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.40$ and $\hat{D}_1 = 100$, compute forecasts for months 2 through 6. Compare the **MAD** of SES with that of SMA. Which method is more suitable, and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute SMA(n = 3) forecasts.**

$$\hat{D}_4 = \frac{100 + 120 + 130}{3} = 116.67$$

$$\hat{D}_5 = \frac{120 + 130 + 110}{3} = 120.00$$

$$\hat{D}_6 = \frac{130 + 110 + 140}{3} = 126.67$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate errors.**

| Month | $D_t$ | $\hat{D}_t$ | $e_t = D_t - \hat{D}_t$ | $\lvert e_t \rvert$ | $\frac{\lvert e_t \rvert}{D_t}\times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 110 | 116.67 | -6.67 | 6.67 | 6.06 |
| 5 | 140 | 120.00 | 20.00 | 20.00 | 14.29 |
| 6 | 160 | 126.67 | 33.33 | 33.33 | 20.83 |
| **Sum** | — | — | 46.66 | **60.00** | **41.18** |

**Step 3 — Compute MAD and MAPE.**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{60.00}{3} = 20.00 \text{ '000 litres}$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{41.18}{3} \approx 13.73\%$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply SES recursively.**

$$\hat{D}_{t+1} = 0.40 D_t + 0.60 \hat{D}_t, \quad \hat{D}_1 = 100$$

$\hat{D}_2 = 0.40(100) + 0.60(100) = 100.00$

$\hat{D}_3 = 0.40(120) + 0.60(100) = 48 + 60 = 108.00$

$\hat{D}_4 = 0.40(130) + 0.60(108) = 52 + 64.80 = 116.80$

$\hat{D}_5 = 0.40(110) + 0.60(116.80) = 44 + 70.08 = 114.08$

$\hat{D}_6 = 0.40(140) + 0.60(114.08) = 56 + 68.45 = 124.45$

**Step 2 — Tabulate errors (months 2–6).**

| Month | $D_t$ | $\hat{D}_t$ | $e_t$ | $\lvert e_t \rvert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 120 | 100.00 | 20.00 | 20.00 |
| 3 | 130 | 108.00 | 22.00 | 22.00 |
| 4 | 110 | 116.80 | -6.80 | 6.80 |
| 5 | 140 | 114.08 | 25.92 | 25.92 |
| 6 | 160 | 124.45 | 35.55 | 35.55 |
| **Sum** | — | — | 96.67 | **110.27** |

$$\text{MAD}_{\text{SES}} = \frac{110.27}{5} \approx 22.05$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

| Method | MAD |
|---|---|
| SMA(3) on months 4–6 | **20.00** ✓ |
| SES (α = 0.40) on months 2–6 | 22.05 |

**Conclusion:** SMA is **slightly more accurate** here. The data has an upward trend, so **Holt's method** (which explicitly models trend) would likely outperform both. SES lags the trend because it gives only 40% weight to the latest actual.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** compute MAD with different sample sizes and declare SES worse without acknowledging the 5-vs-3 sample imbalance (1 mark penalty).
> - ❌ **Do NOT** forget to **initialize** $\hat{D}_1$ explicitly — this costs 1 mark.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** round intermediate values to integers; carry **at least 2 decimals** to the next step.
> - ✅ **Do** compare MAE/MAD and explicitly recommend a method with **justification**.

---

### ❖ Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Chase Strategy** and **Level Strategy** of aggregate planning. List **two advantages and two disadvantages** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm has the following demand forecast and cost data. Initial inventory = 50, final inventory = 50, regular production cost = ₹ 12/unit, overtime = ₹ 18/unit, holding = ₹ 1.50/unit/month, hiring = ₹ 500/worker, firing = ₹ 600/worker. One worker produces 1 unit/month. Current workforce = 100 workers.

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 100 | 130 | 150 | 120 |

Compute the **minimum-cost Level Strategy plan** and find the total cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Chase Strategy:**

Workers are hired/fired every period so production = demand exactly. Inventory is held at safety level only.

- *Advantages:*
  1. Inventory holding cost is near-zero.
  2. No risk of stockouts / obsolescence.
- *Disadvantages:*
  1. High hiring and firing costs; morale issues.
  2. Requires a flexible labor pool; training overhead.

**Level Strategy:**

Workforce is kept constant; demand fluctuations are absorbed via inventory buildup and depletion.

- *Advantages:*
  1. Stable workforce, better morale, easier HR planning.
  2. Lower hiring/firing cost.
- *Disadvantages:*
  1. Inventory holding cost rises in slack periods.
  2. Risk of stockouts if forecast is wrong; capital tied up.

> Examiner typically allows a sketch/diagram to fetch **1 mark** in lieu of detailed text.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Total demand** = 100 + 130 + 150 + 120 = 500 units. Net of inventory (initial 50 + final 50 cancel out), production = 500 units.

**Level production rate** = 500 / 4 = **125 units/month** → workforce = **125 workers constant**.

**Transition cost (start = 100, level = 125):** Hire 25 workers → cost = 25 × 500 = ₹ 12,500.

**Production cost:** 500 × 12 = ₹ 6,000.

**Inventory build-up:**

| Month | Demand | Production | Beg Inv | End Inv |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 125 | 50 | 75 |
| 2 | 130 | 125 | 75 | 70 |
| 3 | 150 | 125 | 70 | 45 |
| 4 | 120 | 125 | 45 | 50 |

(Verification: Beg Inv = 50; End Inv₄ = 50 ✓)

**Holding cost:** $(75 + 70 + 45 + 50) \times 1.50 = 240 \times 1.50 = ₹ 360$.

**Total cost:**
$$
C_{\text{total}} = 12500 + 6000 + 360 = \mathbf{₹\,18{,}860}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - ❌ Forgetting to **subtract** initial and final inventory → production = 500 + 50 − 50 = 500 (NOT 600). Loses 1 mark.
> - ❌ Using **ending inventory** alone for holding cost — must use **ending inventory of each period** as the carrying quantity (loses 2 marks).
> - ❌ Forgetting to include the one-time **hiring transition cost** from 100 → 125.

---

## 5.3 Quick-Fire Recall — Important Things to Remember (Board Cram Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Module 2**

### Forecasting
- ☐ Naïve: $\hat{D}_{t+1} = D_t$
- ☐ SMA(n): average of last $n$ periods; weights equal
- ☐ WMA: weights sum to 1; recency biased
- ☐ SES: $\hat{D}_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{D}_t$; $\alpha$ controls responsiveness
- ☐ Holt's = SES + explicit trend component
- ☐ Winter's = Holt's + multiplicative seasonal index
- ☐ Regression slope $b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$
- ☐ MAD = avg of $\lvert e_t \rvert$; MSE = avg of $e_t^2$; MAPE = avg of $\frac{\lvert e_t \rvert}{D_t}\times 100$
- ☐ Tracking Signal = RSFE / MAD; **control limits ±4 to ±8**
- ☐ Qualitative methods used when **no data exists** (new product introduction)

### Aggregate Planning
- ☐ AP horizon = **3 to 18 months**, family-level units
- ☐ Three pure strategies: **Chase, Level, Mixed**
- ☐ Cost components: Production, Hiring, Firing, Holding, Backorder, Overtime, Subcontract
- ☐ Chase → minimize inventory, maximize HR change cost
- ☐ Level → minimize HR change, maximize holding cost
- ☐ Transportation method: rows = production periods, columns = demand periods, cell cost = $C_{RT} + $ holding or backorder cost
- ☐ Always include **inventory continuity equation**: $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$
- ☐ Level rate (per period) = $\dfrac{\sum D_t - I_0 + I_H}{H}$

### Common KTU Board Traps
- ☐ **Carry units** in every aggregate table.
- ☐ **Round MAD/MAPE to 2 decimals** in the final answer.
- ☐ **Show tracking signal calculation** when forecast comparison is asked.
- ☐ **Mention at least one limitation** of any chosen forecasting method (for full marks).
- ☐ When comparing two strategies, conclude with a clear **recommendation + numerical justification**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Forecasting Methods & Aggregate Planning in Supply Chain**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Planning (AP):** A medium-range (3 to 18 months) operational planning activity that establishes a general, high-level blueprint for matching production capacity to demand demand fluctuations across a finite planning horizon, by simultaneously setting **production rates, workforce levels, inventory levels, overtime, subcontracting, backorders, and pricing actions** at an aggregate product-family level (not SKU level).

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off in Aggregate Planning:** A deliberate managerial decision to sacrifice one performance dimension (e.g., low inventory) in exchange for a gain in another dimension (e.g., stable workforce), evaluated through the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** function, which is the algebraic sum of conflicting cost components.

The KTU 2024 syllabus positions *Basic Trade-offs* as the conceptual foundation that explains **why** no single AP strategy is universally optimal — the planner must navigate a set of conflicting cost-and-service objectives to find the **least-cost feasible plan**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bus Reservation Problem

Imagine you are the **fleet manager of a long-distance bus company** in Kerala that runs services between Kochi and Bengaluru. Your buses (capacity) cannot be bought overnight — they take **6 months to procure**. Your drivers (workforce) need **3 weeks of training**. During Onam-Vishu season, demand **doubles**; in monsoon, it **halves**.

- If you buy buses for peak demand → in off-season, **75% of buses sit idle** (high idle-capacity cost).
- If you buy only for average demand → during Onam you **turn away passengers** (lost goodwill, backorder cost).
- You could **hire temporary drivers in peak season** (overtime/hiring cost) — but training them compromises safety.
- You could **use outsourced buses during peak season** (subcontracting cost) — but margins shrink.
- You could **let passengers book in advance and wait** (backorder cost) — but they might switch to trains.

**Aggregate Planning IS exactly this juggling act**, applied to a factory producing 50,000 SKUs aggregated into 5 product families over the next 12 months. The "trade-off" is the deliberate sacrifice on one cost lever to save on another.

---

## 1.3 The Six Fundamental Trade-off Levers in AP

| # | Lever (Decision Variable) | What You Sacrifice | What You Gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Production Rate** | Inventory build-up | Stable employment |
| 2 | **Workforce Level** | Hiring/firing costs | Inventory & overtime savings |
| 3 | **Inventory Level** | Holding cost | Service level & workforce stability |
| 4 | **Overtime / Undertime** | Premium wages | Capacity flexibility |
| 5 | **Subcontracting** | Margin leakage | Capacity without fixed-asset investment |
| 6 | **Backorders / Lost Sales** | Customer goodwill | Inventory carrying savings |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Examiners frequently frame the trade-off as *"a manager wants to keep workers fully employed — explain the cost penalty."* The answer must always reference the **undertime cost** (idle labour = paid but non-productive) versus the **overtime cost** (premium pay at **1.5×** or **2×** regular wage).

---

## 1.4 Three Canonical Aggregate Planning Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus explicitly classifies AP strategies into **three pure strategies** and their **mixed (hybrid) combinations**:

1. **Chase Strategy** — Production matches demand period-by-period; workforce fluctuates via hiring/firing. *Best when: inventory holding cost is very high and hiring cost is low.*
2. **Level Strategy** — Production rate is constant; demand fluctuations absorbed by inventory and backorders. *Best when: hiring/firing cost is high and inventory holding is cheap.*
3. **Mixed (Hybrid) Strategy** — A combination of the above, often optimal in practice. *Best when: cost coefficients are balanced.*

---

## 1.5 Syllabus-Mandated Scope for KTU 2024 Module 2

Based on the PESFT867 Module 2 blueprint, students must be able to:
- **CO2 (Understand):** Explain each trade-off with one real manufacturing example.
- **CO2 (Apply):** Compute total cost of a chase vs. level plan for given demand data.
- **CO3 (Analyse):** Identify the dominant trade-off given a cost-coefficient table.
- **CO3 (Evaluate):** Justify a hybrid plan using marginal cost reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Aggregate Planning Decision

Aggregate Planning sits in the **hierarchical planning pyramid** of any manufacturing/supply-chain enterprise:

$$
\text{Long-range (Strategic)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Medium-range (AP)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Short-range (Disaggregation)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Real-time (Scheduling)}
$$

The AP problem is a **constrained multi-period cost-minimization problem** where the objective is to choose values of decision variables that minimize the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** subject to:
1. **Demand-satisfaction constraint** (with possible backorders)
2. **Workforce-balance constraint**
3. **Capacity constraint** (regular time + overtime ≤ max)
4. **Non-negativity & integer constraints** (workforce is integer)

---

## 2.2 The Eight Standard Cost Components (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet** examiners expect you to reproduce cold. Every cost must be clearly defined with units and the algebraic variable that represents it in the objective function.

| # | Cost Component | Symbol | Unit | Trigger Condition | Typical Magnitude (Indicative) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Regular Production Cost | $C_{P}$ | ₹ / unit | Every unit produced in regular time | ₹80–₹200 / unit |
| 2 | Overtime Production Cost | $C_{OT}$ | ₹ / unit | Units produced beyond regular capacity | **1.5 × $C_{P}$** |
| 3 | Hiring Cost | $C_{H}$ | ₹ / worker | New worker added to workforce | ₹5,000–₹25,000 / hire |
| 4 | Firing / Layoff Cost | $C_{F}$ | ₹ / worker | Worker removed from workforce | ₹10,000–₹50,000 / fire |
| 5 | Inventory Holding Cost | $C_{I}$ | ₹ / unit / period | Ending inventory > 0 | **20–30% of unit cost / yr** |
| 6 | Backorder Cost | $C_{B}$ | ₹ / unit / period | Demand unfilled & carried forward | ₹50–₹500 / unit |
| 7 | Subcontracting Cost | $C_{S}$ | ₹ / unit | Units outsourced | 1.2 × to 1.4 × $C_{P}$ |
| 8 | Idle / Undertime Cost | $C_{U}$ | ₹ / period | Workforce idle (production < capacity) | Wage rate × idle hours |

---

## 2.3 The Trade-off Logic — Cost-vs-Cost

> [!NOTE]
> Every trade-off in AP can be reformulated as: **"Reducing one cost component will increase another."** The planner's job is to find the *equi-marginal point* where the marginal saving on one cost equals the marginal penalty on the other.

### 2.3.1 Trade-off 1 — Workforce Stability vs. Inventory Build-up

$$
\underbrace{\Delta C_{\text{Hire/Fire}}}_{\text{penalty for changing workforce}} \;\;\overset{?}{\underset{?}{=\Longleftrightarrow=}}\;\; \underbrace{\Delta C_{\text{Inventory Holding}}}_{\text{penalty for stable production}}
$$

- **Hire to chase demand** → high $C_H + C_F$, low $C_I$.
- **Level production with buffer stock** → low $C_H + C_F$, high $C_I$.

**Decision rule:** Choose level production if $C_H + C_F > C_I \times (\text{units buffered})$.

### 2.3.2 Trade-off 2 — Overtime vs. Inventory

$$
\Delta C_{OT} \;=\; \Delta C_I
$$

- Paying **1.5× wage** for overtime is cheaper than carrying inventory for **N periods** only if the holding period is short.

### 2.3.3 Trade-off 3 — Subcontracting vs. Inventory vs. Backorder

This is a **three-way** trade-off, the most complex one in AP:

$$
\min \left[ C_S \cdot S_t \;,\; C_I \cdot I_t \;,\; C_B \cdot B_t \right]
$$

A planner compares **per-unit marginal costs** and picks the cheapest source of supply for the marginal unit.

### 2.3.4 Trade-off 4 — Backorders vs. Inventory

$$
C_B \cdot B_t \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; C_I \cdot I_t
$$

- Backorders are usually **cheaper per period** than holding inventory (no warehousing cost, no capital tied up), but they accumulate if demand keeps arriving unmet.
- The crossover period $T^*$ is when $C_B \cdot B_t > C_I \cdot I_t$ → switch to inventory.

### 2.3.5 Trade-off 5 — Make-to-Stock vs. Make-to-Order

- **Make-to-Stock (MTS):** Produce to forecast → inventory risk.
- **Make-to-Order (MTO):** Produce only on confirmed order → capacity underutilization risk.

### 2.3.6 Trade-off 6 — Pricing/Promotion vs. Production

Used to **shape demand** to match supply — discount in slack periods, premium in peak periods.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

| Industry | Dominant Trade-off | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Automobile (e.g., Maruti, KIA)** | Workforce stability vs. inventory | Level strategy with seasonal subcontracting |
| **Apparel (e.g., Myntra/Zara)** | Make-to-stock vs. make-to-order | Postponement strategy |
| **FMCG (e.g., HUL, Nestlé)** | Overtime vs. inventory | Overtime in festival months |
| **Electronics (e.g., Apple Foxconn)** | Subcontracting vs. backorder | Hybrid with strategic buffer |
| **Steel / Cement** | Production rate vs. inventory | Chase due to high holding cost |

---

## 2.5 Marginal Cost Comparison — The Heart of AP Trade-off Analysis

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question (recurring across 2019, 2022, 2023 papers):** *"Given cost coefficients, recommend whether the firm should produce extra units, subcontract, or backorder the demand."*

**Decision Tree (Marginal Unit Logic):**

$$
\text{If } C_{OT} < C_I < C_S \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Use Overtime}
$$
$$
\text{If } C_I < C_{OT} < C_S \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Build Inventory}
$$
$$
\text{If } C_S < C_{OT} < C_I \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Subcontract}
$$
$$
\text{If } C_B < \min(C_{OT}, C_I, C_S) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Backorder (last resort)}
$$

This is the **most testable concept** in KTU 2024 AP trade-off module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Walk-Throughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Formulation of the AP Trade-off Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following LP/MILP formulation is the **canonical KTU answer skeleton** for any "set up the aggregate planning problem" question. Memorize the objective function and the four constraint families.

### 3.1.1 Sets and Indices

$$
T \;=\; \{1, 2, \ldots, n\} \quad \text{(planning periods, typically months)}
$$
$$
t \in T \quad \text{(a single period)}
$$

### 3.1.2 Parameters (Inputs from Module 1 Demand Forecasting)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_t &\;=\; \text{forecasted demand in period } t \quad \text{[units]} \\
C_{P,t} &\;=\; \text{regular production cost per unit in } t \quad \text{[₹/unit]} \\
C_{OT,t} &\;=\; \text{overtime production cost per unit in } t \quad \text{[₹/unit]} \\
C_{H,t} &\;=\; \text{cost to hire one worker in } t \quad \text{[₹/worker]} \\
C_{F,t} &\;=\; \text{cost to lay off one worker in } t \quad \text{[₹/worker]} \\
C_{I,t} &\;=\; \text{inventory holding cost per unit per period} \quad \text{[₹/unit/period]} \\
C_{B,t} &\;=\; \text{backorder cost per unit per period} \quad \text{[₹/unit/period]} \\
C_{S,t} &\;=\; \text{subcontracting cost per unit} \quad \text{[₹/unit]} \\
P_{\max} &\;=\; \text{max regular-time production capacity per period} \quad \text{[units]} \\
W_0 &\;=\; \text{initial workforce size} \quad \text{[workers]} \\
p &\;=\; \text{production per worker per period} \quad \text{[units/worker]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Decision Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &\;=\; \text{units produced in regular time in } t \\
O_t &\;=\; \text{units produced in overtime in } t \\
H_t &\;=\; \text{workers hired at start of } t \\
F_t &\;=\; \text{workers fired at start of } t \\
W_t &\;=\; \text{workforce size in } t \\
I_t &\;=\; \text{ending inventory in } t \quad (I_t>0) \\
B_t &\;=\; \text{ending backorders in } t \quad (B_t>0) \\
S_t &\;=\; \text{units subcontracted in } t
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Objective Function (Total Relevant Cost Minimization)

$$
\min \; Z \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left[ C_{P,t} P_t + C_{OT,t} O_t + C_{H,t} H_t + C_{F,t} F_t + C_{I,t} I_t + C_{B,t} B_t + C_{S,t} S_t \right]
$$

### 3.1.5 Constraints

**(a) Demand-Satisfaction Constraint (Inventory-Backorder Balance):**

$$
I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + P_t + O_t + S_t - I_t + B_t \;=\; D_t \quad \forall \; t \in T
$$

> **Logic:** Period $t$ starts with available inventory $I_{t-1} - B_{t-1}$ (positive net inventory after fulfilling any prior backorders). New supply $P_t + O_t + S_t$ arrives. Period demand $D_t$ is met. The remainder either builds inventory ($I_t$) or accumulates as new backorders ($B_t$). The constraint is an *exact* flow balance, not an inequality — it must be exact equality.

**(b) Workforce-Balance Constraint:**

$$
W_t \;=\; W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t \quad \forall \; t \in T
$$

> **Logic:** The workforce at the start of period $t$ equals the previous period's workforce plus new hires minus layoffs. This is a pure accounting identity.

**(c) Production-Capacity Constraint:**

$$
P_t \;\le\; P_{\max} \quad \text{and} \quad O_t \;\le\; O_{\max} \quad \forall \; t
$$

> **Logic:** Regular-time output is bounded by physical capacity (machines, shifts, line speed). Overtime is bounded by labour law (typically 2 hours/day or 100 hours/quarter).

**(d) Workforce-Production Coupling Constraint:**

$$
P_t \;\le\; p \cdot W_t \quad \forall \; t
$$

> **Logic:** The number of units that can be produced in regular time is the product of the workforce size and the per-worker productivity rate. This is the **most critical** constraint linking workforce decisions to production decisions.

**(e) Non-Negativity & Integrality:**

$$
P_t, O_t, I_t, B_t, S_t \ge 0; \quad W_t, H_t, F_t \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}
$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Walk-Through — KTU-Style Trade-off Cost Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a 14-mark standard problem format.** Examiner will give you 6–12 months of demand data and cost coefficients. You must compute the cost of a chase plan, a level plan, and recommend a hybrid.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement (Worked Example)

> *"A firm produces a single product family. The demand forecast for the next 4 months is:*
>
> | Period $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | Total |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | $D_t$ (units) | 500 | 800 | 1,200 | 700 | 3,200 |
>
> *Initial workforce $W_0 = 10$ workers. Each worker produces 100 units/month. Initial inventory $I_0 = 0$. No backorders allowed. Inventory holding cost = ₹5/unit/month. Hiring cost = ₹300/worker. Firing cost = ₹500/worker. Regular production cost = ₹20/unit (already included in revenue, ignore for trade-off).*
>
> *Compare the **chase** and **level** strategies on the basis of total relevant cost. Comment on the trade-off."*

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Determine Required Workforce Under Each Strategy

**Productivity:** $p = 100$ units/worker/month.

$$
W_t^{\text{required}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{D_t}{p} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{D_t}{100} \right\rceil
$$

| Period $t$ | $D_t$ | $W_t^{\text{chase}}$ | $W_t^{\text{level}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | $\lceil 5 \rceil = 5$ | $\lceil 800/100 \rceil = 8$ |
| 2 | 800 | 8 | 8 |
| 3 | 1,200 | 12 | 8 |
| 4 | 700 | 7 | 8 |

For **level strategy**, the average demand is $3{,}200 / 4 = 800$ units/month, so $W^{\text{level}} = 8$ throughout.

For **chase strategy**, $W_t$ matches $D_t$ exactly each month.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Chase Strategy Cost

Hires and Fires are computed as changes in workforce from previous period:

$$
H_t \;=\; \max(0,\; W_t - W_{t-1}), \quad F_t \;=\; \max(0,\; W_{t-1} - W_t)
$$

| Period | $W_{t-1}$ | $W_t$ | $H_t$ | $F_t$ | Hiring Cost (₹) | Firing Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 × 500 = 2,500 |
| 2 | 5 | 8 | 3 | 0 | 3 × 300 = 900 | 0 |
| 3 | 8 | 12 | 4 | 0 | 4 × 300 = 1,200 | 0 |
| 4 | 12 | 7 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 × 500 = 2,500 |
| **Total** | | | | | **2,100** | **5,000** |

**Inventory under chase** is 0 (production = demand exactly).

$$
\boxed{TC_{\text{chase}} \;=\; 2{,}100 + 5{,}000 \;=\; 7{,}100 \text{ ₹}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Level Strategy Cost

Workforce stays at 8 throughout. No hiring or firing beyond initial adjustment.

- From $W_0 = 10$ to $W_1 = 8$: $F_1 = 2$ → firing cost = 2 × 500 = **₹1,000** (one-time, charged in period 1).
- For $t = 2, 3, 4$: $H_t = 0$, $F_t = 0$.

**Production each month** = 8 × 100 = 800 units.

**Inventory Balance** with $I_0 = 0$:

$$
I_t \;=\; I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t
$$

| Period | $P_t$ | $D_t$ | $I_{t-1}$ | $I_t$ | Holding Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 800 | 500 | 0 | 300 | 300 × 5 = 1,500 |
| 2 | 800 | 800 | 300 | 300 | 300 × 5 = 1,500 |
| 3 | 800 | 1,200 | 300 | -100 | Backorder — not allowed per problem |

> **Issue:** Period 3 demand (1,200) exceeds production (800) and available inventory (300). A pure level strategy is **infeasible** under the no-backorder constraint. This itself is a **trade-off revelation**: the firm must either allow backorders, build pre-season inventory, or hire.

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Modified Level Strategy with Backorders Allowed

Suppose the problem allows backorders at $C_B = ₹10$/unit/period.

Re-do inventory balance allowing $B_t$:

$$
I_t - B_t \;=\; I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + P_t - D_t
$$

| Period | $I_{t-1} - B_{t-1}$ | $P_t$ | $D_t$ | $I_t - B_t$ | Choose split | Backorder Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 800 | 500 | +300 | $I_1=300, B_1=0$ | 0 |
| 2 | 300 | 800 | 800 | +300 | $I_2=300, B_2=0$ | 0 |
| 3 | 300 | 800 | 1,200 | -100 | $I_3=0, B_3=100$ | 100 × 10 = 1,000 |
| 4 | -100 | 800 | 700 | 0 | $I_4=0, B_4=0$ | 0 |

Total holding cost: $(300+300) \times 5 = ₹3{,}000$
Total backorder cost: $₹1{,}000$
Firing cost (period 1): $₹1{,}000$

$$
\boxed{TC_{\text{level+BO}} \;=\; 1{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 1{,}000 \;=\; 5{,}000 \text{ ₹}}
$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Trade-off Comparison & Recommendation

| Strategy | Hiring Cost | Firing Cost | Holding Cost | Backorder Cost | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chase | 2,100 | 5,000 | 0 | 0 | **₹7,100** |
| Level + BO | 0 | 1,000 | 3,000 | 1,000 | **₹5,000** |

$$
\Delta \;=\; 7{,}100 - 5{,}000 \;=\; 2{,}100 \text{ ₹} \;\;\text{saved by level-with-backorders}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Conclusion Statement (write this verbatim in your exam):**
> *"The level strategy with controlled backorders dominates the chase strategy by ₹2,100 over the 4-month horizon, because the per-period holding and backorder costs (₹5 and ₹10 per unit) are smaller than the cumulative hire-fire penalty (₹300 + ₹500 = ₹800 per worker change). However, the firm must weigh the customer-service penalty of backorders against the ₹2,100 saving — this is the **basic trade-off in aggregate planning**."*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Decision Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **decision-rule matrix** that KTU expects in any "explain the trade-off" 7-mark question. Memorize the conditional logic.

| If the cost coefficient condition is… | Then the optimal source of supply is… | Because… |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{OT} < C_I < C_S$ | **Overtime** | Cheapest marginal capacity expansion |
| $C_I < C_{OT} < C_S$ | **Build Inventory** | Holding is cheaper than premium wages |
| $C_S < C_{OT} < C_I$ | **Subcontract** | Outsourcing beats both inventory and overtime |
| $C_B < \min(C_{OT}, C_I, C_S)$ | **Backorder (short-term only)** | Customer-penalty is least per period |
| $C_H + C_F > C_I \times \Delta D$ | **Level production** | Hiring is more expensive than buffering |
| $C_H + C_F < C_I \times \Delta D$ | **Chase demand** | Buffering is more expensive than resizing |

---

## 3.4 The Equi-Marginal Principle (Advanced)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **graduate-level extension** of AP trade-off analysis. For the optimal plan, the marginal cost of producing one more unit from any source should be equal across all sources (otherwise, reallocate one unit from the costlier source to the cheaper one and reduce total cost).

$$
C_{P} \;\le\; C_{P} + C_{I} \;\le\; C_{OT} \;\le\; C_{S}
$$

$$
C_{P} - C_B \;\le\; C_{P} \;\le\; C_{P} + C_{I}
$$

At the optimum:

$$
C_{P}^{\text{regular}} \;=\; C_{P}^{\text{regular}} + C_I \cdot T_{\text{hold}} \;=\; C_{OT} \;=\; C_{S}
$$

This means **the cheapest source is used first, then the next, then the next, until demand is satisfied**. The optimal plan is the one where the *last* unit of supply has equal marginal cost from all active sources.

---

## 3.5 KTU-Frequently-Asked Sub-Question: "When is Backorder Cost NOT a Trade-off?"

**Answer Template (7 marks):**
> *"Backorders cease to be a viable trade-off lever when the customer is a downstream Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) on a JIT contract, because a single missed delivery slot can trigger a contractual penalty of 5×–10× the unit price. In such cases, the firm must shift to a pure level or chase strategy and absorb the higher inventory/hire-fire cost rather than permit backorders. This is why JIT supply chains in the automotive industry (e.g., Bosch → Tata Motors) operate on **vendor-managed inventory (VMI)** rather than backorderable AP."*

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Aggregate Planning Position in the Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stratPlan["STRATEGIC PLANNING<br/>Long Range 1 to 5 years<br/>Capacity Location Layout"]
    aggPlan["AGGREGATE PLANNING<br/>Medium Range 3 to 18 months<br/>Product Families Workforce Inventory"]
    masterSched["MASTER PRODUCTION SCHEDULE<br/>Short Range 1 to 3 months<br/>SKUs End Items"]
    shopFloor["SHOP FLOOR SCHEDULING<br/>Daily Hourly<br/>Machines Operators"]
    dispatch["DISPATCHING EXECUTION<br/>Real Time"]
    stratPlan --> aggPlan
    aggPlan --> masterSched
    masterSched --> shopFloor
    shopFloor --> dispatch
```

> **How to read this:** Each level disaggregates the previous level. AP is the *only* level where the six trade-off levers (production, workforce, inventory, overtime, subcontracting, backorder) are decided jointly. By the time we reach shop-floor scheduling, those levers are *fixed inputs*.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Six Trade-off Levers and Their Cost Conflicts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Production Rate"] <-->|"Trade off 1"| B["Workforce Level"]
    B <-->|"Trade off 2"| C["Inventory Level"]
    C <-->|"Trade off 3"| D["Overtime Undertime"]
    D <-->|"Trade off 4"| E["Subcontracting"]
    E <-->|"Trade off 5"| F["Backorders Lost Sales"]
    F <-->|"Trade off 6"| A
    G["Total Relevant Cost TRC<br/>Objective Function"] -.-> A
    G -.-> B
    G -.-> C
    G -.-> D
    G -.-> E
    G -.-> F
```

> **How to read this:** Each pair of levers is in conflict — improving one worsens the other. The TRC objective is the *balancing function* that maps all six conflicts into a single scalar to be minimized.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Chase vs. Level vs. Mixed Strategy Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start New AP Problem"] --> q1{"Is hiring firing cost<br/>very HIGH"}
    q1 -->|"Yes"| q2{"Is inventory holding<br/>cost LOW"}
    q1 -->|"No"| q3{"Is backorder cost<br/>ACCEPTABLE to customers"}
    q2 -->|"Yes"| level["LEVEL STRATEGY<br/>Constant production rate<br/>Buffer inventory absorbs demand"]
    q2 -->|"No"| mixed1["MIXED STRATEGY<br/>Moderate workforce<br/>Some inventory and backorder"]
    q3 -->|"Yes"| chase["CHASE STRATEGY<br/>Hire fire to match demand<br/>Minimal inventory"]
    q3 -->|"No"| mixed2["MIXED STRATEGY<br/>Stable workforce<br/>Subcontracting and overtime"]
    level --> eval["Compute TRC for all 3 plans"]
    mixed1 --> eval
    mixed2 --> eval
    chase --> eval
    eval --> pick["Pick plan with MIN TRC"]
```

> **How to read this:** The branch conditions use the *qualitative* cost trade-offs from Section 2. In an exam, replace "HIGH"/"LOW" with the **actual ₹/unit numbers** from the cost table.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the AP Trade-off Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        i1["Demand Forecast Dt"]
        i2["Cost Coefficients CP COT CH CF CI CB CS"]
        i3["Capacity Constraints Pmax Omax"]
        i4["Initial Conditions W0 I0 B0"]
    end
    subgraph GEN["PLAN GENERATION BLOCK"]
        g1["Chase Plan Generator"]
        g2["Level Plan Generator"]
        g3["Mixed Plan Generator<br/>Heuristic or LP Solver"]
    end
    subgraph COST["COST EVALUATION BLOCK"]
        c1["Regular Production Cost"]
        c2["Overtime Cost"]
        c3["Hire Fire Cost"]
        c4["Inventory Backorder Cost"]
        c5["Subcontracting Cost"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        o1["Total Relevant Cost per Plan"]
        o2["Plan Comparison Table"]
        o3["Recommended Plan with Min TRC"]
    end
    INPUT --> GEN
    GEN --> COST
    COST --> OUT
    i1 --> g1
    i1 --> g2
    i1 --> g3
    i2 --> c1
    i2 --> c2
    i2 --> c3
    i2 --> c4
    i2 --> c5
```

> **How to read this:** This is a **block-level functional architecture** of a trade-off decision-support system. The three plan generators run in parallel, the cost block evaluates each, and the output block picks the minimum. This is how textbooks (e.g., Chopra & Meindl, 4th ed.) conceptually describe the AP process.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Cost-Coefficient Decision Tree for the Marginal Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need 1 more unit of supply"] --> c1{"C_OT less than C_I"}
    c1 -->|"Yes"| c2{"C_OT less than C_S"}
    c1 -->|"No"| c3{"C_I less than C_S"}
    c2 -->|"Yes"| c4{"C_OT less than C_B"}
    c2 -->|"No"| sub1["SUBCONTRACT this unit"]
    c3 -->|"Yes"| c5{"C_I less than C_B"}
    c3 -->|"No"| sub2["SUBCONTRACT this unit"]
    c4 -->|"Yes"| ot["Use OVERTIME"]
    c4 -->|"No"| bo1["BACKORDER this unit"]
    c5 -->|"Yes"| inv["BUILD INVENTORY"]
    c5 -->|"No"| bo2["BACKORDER this unit"]
```

> **How to read this:** This is the **operational decision tree** for allocating a single marginal unit. A good KTU answer always ends with such a tree, populated with the actual numerical cost values from the question.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for PESFT867):**
> - Part A: 3 questions × 3 marks = 9 marks (answer any 2)
> - Part B: Module-wise choice question × 14 marks (one full question from each module)
> - Module 2 AP Trade-off appears as a **7-mark sub-part** (typically part b of the 14-marker).

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

### **Q1. Define "Trade-off" in Aggregate Planning with one example.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *trade-off* in aggregate planning is the deliberate managerial act of **sacrificing performance on one planning lever to gain on another**, evaluated by comparing the resulting change in the Total Relevant Cost (TRC). For example, a firm may choose to **build inventory in slack months** (incurring holding cost) rather than **lay off workers** (incurring firing cost and losing tacit knowledge). The optimal trade-off is the one that yields the **lowest TRC** while satisfying the demand-satisfaction constraint across the planning horizon.
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for TRC linkage.)*

---

### **Q2. List the three pure aggregate planning strategies and state the dominant cost component in each.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The three pure AP strategies are:
> 1. **Chase Strategy** — Production matches demand period by period. *Dominant cost:* Hiring and firing cost.
> 2. **Level Strategy** — Constant production rate; inventory/backorders absorb demand variation. *Dominant cost:* Inventory holding and/or backorder cost.
> 3. **Mixed (Hybrid) Strategy** — Combination of chase and level. *Dominant cost:* Usually a balance of overtime, subcontracting, and inventory.
> *(1 mark per strategy with its dominant cost.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student answers **ONE** Part B question out of a choice of TWO from the same module. Both alternatives are provided below for full preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Strategy Selection and Cost Computation

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper 1]**
>
> *(a)* Explain the **basic trade-offs** in aggregate planning. Discuss any **four** cost components involved in TRC. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A company forecasts the following demand for the next 6 months: Jan 200, Feb 400, Mar 600, Apr 800, May 600, Jun 400 units. Initial workforce is 8 workers producing 50 units/worker/month. Holding cost = ₹4/unit/month, Backorder cost = ₹8/unit/month, Hiring cost = ₹200/worker, Firing cost = ₹300/worker. Overtime is limited to 25% of regular capacity at ₹6/unit premium.
>
> Compute and compare the **Total Relevant Cost** of a **chase strategy** and a **level strategy with overtime and backorders**. Recommend the lower-cost plan and justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| # | Cost Component | Symbol | Nature of Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Production Cost** | $C_P$ | Regular time vs. overtime |
| 2 | **Workforce Adjustment Cost** | $C_H, C_F$ | Stability vs. agility |
| 3 | **Inventory Holding Cost** | $C_I$ | Service level vs. carrying cost |
| 4 | **Backorder Cost** | $C_B$ | Customer goodwill vs. inventory cost |
| 5 | **Subcontracting Cost** | $C_S$ | Capacity flexibility vs. margin |
| 6 | **Idle / Undertime Cost** | $C_U$ | Workforce stability vs. utilization |

> The planner must reconcile these six costs such that the **TRC is minimized** while all demand is met (in-period or via backorder). The trade-off is essentially a **cost-vs-cost** and **cost-vs-service** decision. *(7 marks distributed: 4 marks for listing with explanation, 3 marks for TRC linkage and minimization logic.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Average Demand and Level Workforce:**

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; \frac{200+400+600+800+600+400}{6} \;=\; \frac{3000}{6} \;=\; 500 \text{ units/month}
$$

$$
W^{\text{level}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{500}{50} \right\rceil \;=\; 10 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 2 — Chase Workforce per Period:**

$$
W_t^{\text{chase}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{D_t}{50} \right\rceil
$$

| Month | $D_t$ | $W_t^{\text{chase}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 200 | 4 |
| Feb | 400 | 8 |
| Mar | 600 | 12 |
| Apr | 800 | 16 |
| May | 600 | 12 |
| Jun | 400 | 8 |

**Step 3 — Chase Strategy Cost (Workforce Changes from $W_0 = 8$):**

| Transition | $W_{t-1} \to W_t$ | Hire $H_t$ | Fire $F_t$ | Hire Cost (₹) | Fire Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 8 → 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 1,200 |
| Feb | 4 → 8 | 4 | 0 | 800 | 0 |
| Mar | 8 → 12 | 4 | 0 | 800 | 0 |
| Apr | 12 → 16 | 4 | 0 | 800 | 0 |
| May | 16 → 12 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 1,200 |
| Jun | 12 → 8 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 1,200 |

$$
TC_{\text{chase}} \;=\; (800 \times 3) + (1{,}200 \times 3) \;=\; 2{,}400 + 3{,}600 \;=\; \boxed{₹6{,}000}
$$

No inventory, no backorders, no overtime (production = demand).

**Step 4 — Level Strategy with Overtime and Backorders:**

Regular production per month = $10 \times 50 = 500$ units.
Overtime capacity = $0.25 \times 500 = 125$ units (premium ₹6/unit).
Max supply per month = 625 units.

**Inventory-Backorder Balance** ($I_0 = 0, B_0 = 0$):

| Month | $D_t$ | Reg Prod | OT | Supply | $I_{t-1} - B_{t-1}$ | Net | Choice |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 200 | 500 | 0 | 500 | 0 | +300 | $I=300$ |
| Feb | 400 | 500 | 0 | 500 | +300 | +400 | $I=400$ |
| Mar | 600 | 500 | 0 | 500 | +400 | +300 | $I=300$ |
| Apr | 800 | 500 | 125 | 625 | +300 | +125 | $I=125$ |
| May | 600 | 500 | 0 | 500 | +125 | +25 | $I=25$ |
| Jun | 400 | 500 | 0 | 500 | +25 | +125 | $I=125$ |

Total ending inventory: $300+400+300+125+25+125 = 1{,}275$ unit-months.

Holding cost: $1{,}275 \times 4 = ₹5{,}100$.
Overtime cost: $125 \times 6 = ₹750$.
Initial firing: $W_0 = 8 \to 10$ is actually a hire, not fire; $H_1 = 2$, hire cost = $2 \times 200 = ₹400$.

$$
TC_{\text{level}} \;=\; 5{,}100 + 750 + 400 \;=\; \boxed{₹6{,}250}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

$$
\Delta \;=\; 6{,}250 - 6{,}000 \;=\; ₹250 \text{ in favor of chase}
$$

The **chase strategy** is cheaper by ₹250 over 6 months. The trade-off insight: **when demand variation is high (range 200 to 800 = 4× variation), the cost of holding inventory across 6 months (₹5,100) outweighs the cost of 9 hire/fire events (₹6,000)**. However, the **costs are nearly equal** (within 4%), which is the classic KTU signal to recommend a **mixed plan** — e.g., hold workforce at 12 workers, use modest overtime in peak, and modest inventory in trough. *(7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Marginal-Cost Trade-off Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**
>
> *(a)* With the help of a **cost-coefficient decision tree**, explain how a planner decides whether to produce, subcontract, or backorder the **marginal unit** of demand. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Given the per-unit cost coefficients: $C_P = ₹20$, $C_{OT} = ₹28$, $C_I = ₹5$/period, $C_S = ₹32$, $C_B = ₹10$/period, and a demand surge of 200 units that must be supplied over the next 2 months. Determine the **optimal source** of supply for the marginal unit, and compute the **least-cost supply mix** for the 200 units. State the dominant trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The planner evaluates four supply sources for the marginal unit, in the order of their per-unit cost:

$$
C_P \;=\; ₹20 \quad < \quad C_B \;=\; ₹10/\text{period effective} \;\; \text{(in-period) }
$$
$$
C_{OT} \;=\; ₹28 \quad < \quad C_S \;=\; ₹32
$$
$$
C_I \cdot T \;=\; ₹5 \times T
$$

**Decision Tree (textual representation for answer sheet):**

1. If demand can be met by **regular production** (capacity available) → produce in-house. Cost = ₹20.
2. Else if **overtime is cheaper than subcontracting** (₹28 < ₹32) → use overtime.
3. Else if **inventory from earlier months is available and holding period is short** → use inventory.
4. Else if **backorder cost is acceptable** → backorder (only if no other option).
5. Else → subcontract (last resort, since ₹32 is the most expensive).
