The **optimal mix** uses the cheapest source first, then the next, then the next, until demand is fully satisfied. This is the **equi-marginal principle** in AP. *(7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Cost Ranking for 2-Month Horizon:**

| Source | Cost for 2 months (₹) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Regular Production $C_P$ | 20 | 1 (cheapest) |
| Overtime $C_{OT}$ | 28 | 2 |
| Inventory $C_I \cdot 2$ | $5 \times 2 = 10$ | **1A (cheapest if held from earlier period)** |
| Backorder $C_B \cdot 2$ | $10 \times 2 = 20$ | 3 |
| Subcontracting $C_S$ | 32 | 4 (most expensive) |

**Step 2 — Assume Regular Capacity is Fully Utilized.**

Then marginal unit cost ranking (assuming we cannot add regular capacity and inventory is zero):
- Overtime: ₹28
- Backorder: ₹20 per period → for 2 periods = ₹20 (tied with $C_P$, but only if customer can wait)
- Subcontract: ₹32

**Step 3 — Least-Cost Mix for 200 Units:**

$$
\min \text{ cost} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Backorder 200 units} = 200 \times ₹20 = ₹4{,}000
$$

But backordering for 2 months requires the customer to wait, which may be infeasible. If the customer is a JIT OEM, backorder is infeasible. Then:

$$
\text{Use Overtime for all 200 units} = 200 \times ₹28 = ₹5{,}600
$$

$$
\text{Subcontract all 200 units} = 200 \times ₹32 = ₹6{,}400
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Matrix:**

| Mix | Feasibility | Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 200 backorder | Only if customer can wait 2 months | 4,000 |
| 200 overtime | Only if $O_{\max} \ge 200$ | 5,600 |
| 200 subcontract | Always | 6,400 |
| 100 OT + 100 sub | Hybrid | 100×28 + 100×32 = 6,000 |

**Step 5 — Dominant Trade-off:**

> *"The dominant trade-off is between **backordering (cheapest but customer-relationship risk)** and **overtime (moderate cost, no relationship risk)**. Subcontracting is dominated (always more expensive) and is the **last-resort** option. The optimal mix depends on the customer-service constraint: if $C_B \cdot 2 = ₹20$ is below the customer's pain threshold, backorder; otherwise use overtime up to capacity, then subcontract the residual."* *(7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Losing 2–3 marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of inventory/backorder** in the balance equation. $I_{t-1} - B_{t-1}$ is the *net* available stock, not just inventory.
> 2. **Hiring cost applies to net increase, firing to net decrease** — don't double-count when workforce first goes up then down.
> 3. **Initial workforce adjustment** ($W_0 \to W_1$) is often missed — examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Confusing "inventory" with "production"** — production cost is per unit produced, holding cost is per unit per period in stock.
> 5. **Writing the trade-off as a *trade-off between two strategies*** instead of *between two cost components* — the KTU standard answer requires the cost-component framing.
> 6. **Skipping the backorder infeasibility check** when a problem says "no backorders" — the answer must explicitly state the strategy is *infeasible* and propose a modification.
> 7. **Forgetting that overtime capacity is bounded** by labour law (typically ≤ 25% of regular capacity or ≤ 100 hrs/quarter).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (last-night-of-exam revision card):**

- ☐ **Trade-off = sacrificing one cost to save another**, evaluated via Total Relevant Cost (TRC).
- ☐ **Six levers:** Production rate, Workforce, Inventory, Overtime, Subcontracting, Backorder.
- ☐ **Three pure strategies:** Chase, Level, Mixed.
- ☐ **Chase Strategy:** Production = Demand; cost = Hire + Fire.
- ☐ **Level Strategy:** Production = Average Demand; cost = Holding + Backorder.
- ☐ **Mixed Strategy:** Combines both; usually optimal in practice.
- ☐ **Inventory-Backorder Balance Equation:** $I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + P_t + O_t + S_t - I_t + B_t = D_t$.
- ☐ **Workforce Balance:** $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$.
- ☐ **Production-Workforce Coupling:** $P_t \le p \cdot W_t$.
- ☐ **Cost ranking (typical):** $C_P < C_I \cdot T \approx C_B \cdot T < C_{OT} < C_S$.
- ☐ **Hire cost is symmetric to fire cost** in formulation but asymmetric in magnitude (firing is usually 1.5× to 2× hiring).
- ☐ **Overtime cost is typically 1.5× the regular wage** (Factories Act, 1948 — Indian context).
- ☐ **Holding cost is 20–30% of unit value per year** (Chopra & Meindl, 4th ed.).
- ☐ **Equi-marginal principle:** At optimum, marginal cost of supply is equalized across all active sources.
- ☐ **JIT supply chains (Bosch, Toyota) prohibit backorders** — only chase or level is feasible.
- ☐ **MTS (make-to-stock) for FMCG, MTO (make-to-order) for capital goods, ATO (assemble-to-order) for automobiles** — the trade-off framing differs.
- ☐ **Subcontracting dominates when $C_S$ is less than both $C_{OT}$ and $C_I \cdot T$** — but this is rare in practice.
- ☐ **For the KTU exam, always state the dominant trade-off explicitly** in the conclusion — it carries 1–2 marks by itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Programming in Aggregate Planning; Coordination in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Programming in Aggregate Planning; Coordination in Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aggregate Planning (AP) — Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a medium-range (3–18 months) operational planning activity that determines the overall production, workforce, inventory, and subcontracting levels to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost, **while respecting capacity, policy, and resource constraints**.

The word **"aggregate"** is critical — planning is done for **product families** (e.g., all variants of a smartphone), not for individual SKUs. Outputs are expressed in standard units such as labor-hours, machine-hours, or aggregate units.

| AP Parameter | Typical Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| Planning Horizon | **3 to 18 months** |
| Aggregation Level | Product family / family group |
| Decision Frequency | Monthly or quarterly |
| Update Logic | Rolling (re-planned every period) |

---

## 1.2 Linear Programming (LP) in Aggregate Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Programming (LP)** is a mathematical optimization technique used to find the **best outcome** (maximum profit or minimum cost) in a model whose requirements are represented by **linear relationships**.

When applied to aggregate planning, LP replaces rule-of-thumb heuristics (chase, level, mixed strategies) with a **mathematically optimal** production, inventory, and subcontracting plan.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Budget Chef"

Imagine a head chef of a restaurant chain must serve a **weekly demand** of biryani plates (100, 150, 200 kg across 3 weeks) using three supply levers:

1. **In-house chefs** (cheap, fixed capacity — like regular-time production).
2. **Overtime chefs** (costlier, limited — like overtime production).
3. **Outside catering** (most expensive, unlimited but costly — like subcontracting).

He can also **store cooked rice** (inventory, small holding cost) or **promise late delivery** (backorders, large penalty). The chef's dilemma — *"How much of each lever to pull each week so that demand is met at minimum cost?"* — **is exactly an LP problem in disguise**.

---

## 1.3 Coordination in the Supply Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supply Chain Coordination** is the alignment of decisions, information, and incentives among independent supply chain members (suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers) so that the **system-wide performance is optimized** rather than each member optimizing its own local objective.

The intuition: a supply chain is a **relay race** — the baton must pass smoothly, or the whole team loses.

| Coordination Problem | Real-World Manifestation |
|---|---|
| **Bullwhip Effect** | Small demand fluctuations at retail become massive order swings at manufacturer |
| **Double Marginalization** | Each channel member adds its own markup, raising total cost to consumer |
| **Forecast Asymmetry** | Retailer hides true demand from manufacturer |
| **Free-Riding** | Distributor lets supplier promote, but doesn't commit volumes |

---

## 1.4 Linking the Two Concepts

LP provides the **mathematical engine** to compute the optimal plan, but optimality in isolation ≠ optimality across the chain. Therefore, **coordination mechanisms** (information sharing, CPFR, VMI, contracts) are needed to ensure that the LP-optimal plan of each member is **collectively** optimal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Aggregate Planning Decision Spectrum

AP decisions are grouped into three levers:

1. **Production Levers** → Regular time (R), Overtime (O), Subcontracting (S), Hiring (H), Layoffs (L).
2. **Workforce Levers** → Number of workers, training, idle time.
3. **Demand Levers** → Pricing, promotions, backorders, lost sales.

LP formalizes all three into one unified optimization model.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of an LP Formulation for AP

### Step 1 — Define Decision Variables

Let $T$ = number of planning periods.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_t &= \text{Regular-time production in period } t \\
O_t &= \text{Overtime production in period } t \\
S_t &= \text{Subcontracted units in period } t \\
I_t &= \text{Inventory at the end of period } t \\
B_t &= \text{Backorders at the end of period } t \\
W_t &= \text{Workforce size in period } t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Objective Function

The goal is to **minimize total cost**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \big[ & c_R R_t + c_O O_t + c_S S_t + c_H I_t + c_B B_t \\
& + c_HR (W_t - W_{t-1})^+ + c_LA (W_{t-1} - W_t)^+ \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $(x)^+ = \max(x, 0)$.

### Step 3 — Define the Constraints

**Capacity Constraints (per period):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \le R_t \le \text{RegCap}_t \\
0 \le O_t \le \text{OtCap}_t \\
0 \le S_t \le \text{SubCap}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inventory Balance (Network Flow Equation):**

$$
I_t - B_t = I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + R_t + O_t + S_t - D_t
$$

**Workforce Flow:**

$$
W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t
$$

**Non-Negativity:** All variables $\geq 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Constraint | Symbol Legend | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{RegProd}_{t} = p \cdot W_t$ | $p$ = productivity per worker | units/period |
| 2 | $I_t - B_t = I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + R_t + O_t + S_t - D_t$ | Network balance | units |
| 3 | $\text{Total Cost} = c_R R + c_O O + c_S S + c_H I + c_B B + c_W W$ | Cost components | ₹/period |
| 4 | $\text{Service Level} = 1 - \frac{\sum B_t}{\sum D_t}$ | Backorder ratio | fraction |
| 5 | $\text{Bullwhip Ratio} = \frac{\text{Var(Orders)}}{\text{Var(Demand)}}$ | Demand amplification | ratio |
| 6 | $\text{EOQ Modulus} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{h}}$ | Economic Order Qty | units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the vertical bar `|` inside a markdown table** — KTU valuation scripts may fail to render. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation in any formula.

---

## 2.4 Coordination Mechanisms — The Theoretical Hierarchy

| Mechanism | Decision Locus | Information Flow | Contract Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Traditional (No Coordination)** | Local | One-way, distorted | Spot purchases |
| **Information Sharing (EDI)** | Local | Two-way | Implied |
| **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** | Supplier | Two-way, demand-level | Consignment-like |
| **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting & Replenishment (CPFR)** | Joint | Two-way + shared forecast | Formal alliance |
| **Continuous Replenishment Program (CRP)** | Supplier | Auto-replenishment | Vendor-owned stock |
| **Revenue Sharing Contract** | Joint | Two-way | Cost-plus sharing |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing firms** use AP-LP for capacity planning in automotive (Toyota Production System), semiconductors (Intel fabs), and FMCG.
- **Service firms** (airlines, hospitals) use it for workforce scheduling, where workers replace "production" and overtime is capped by labor laws.
- **Coordination contracts** (buy-back, revenue sharing, quantity flexibility) are pivotal in **e-commerce** (Amazon) and **cold-chain logistics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out LP Formulation — A 3-Period Aggregate Planning Problem

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU-style problem** that the examiner loves. Memorize the structure.

### 📋 Problem Statement

A firm must meet quarterly demand for **3 periods**. Data are:

| Period | Demand $D_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 100 units |
| 2 | 150 units |
| 3 | 200 units |

**Cost & Capacity Data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Regular-time cost | $c_R$ | ₹10 / unit |
| Overtime cost | $c_O$ | ₹15 / unit |
| Subcontracting cost | $c_S$ | ₹20 / unit |
| Holding cost | $c_H$ | ₹2 / unit / period |
| Backorder cost | $c_B$ | ₹5 / unit / period |
| Regular capacity | — | 100 units / period |
| Overtime capacity | — | 50 units / period |
| Subcontracting capacity | — | 100 units / period |
| Initial inventory | $I_0$ | 0 |
| Final inventory desired | $I_3$ | 0 |

---

### ✅ Step 1 — Decision Variables

$$
R_t, \; O_t, \; S_t, \; I_t, \; B_t \quad \text{for } t = 1, 2, 3
$$

Total = **15 decision variables**.

---

### ✅ Step 2 — Objective Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } Z =\; & 10(R_1+R_2+R_3) + 15(O_1+O_2+O_3) + 20(S_1+S_2+S_3) \\
& + 2(I_1+I_2+I_3) + 5(B_1+B_2+B_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ✅ Step 3 — Constraints

**Capacity constraints:**

$$
R_t \le 100, \quad O_t \le 50, \quad S_t \le 100, \quad t = 1,2,3
$$

**Inventory balance:**

$$
I_t - B_t = I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + R_t + O_t + S_t - D_t
$$

Explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 - B_1 &= 0 + R_1 + O_1 + S_1 - 100 \\
I_2 - B_2 &= I_1 - B_1 + R_2 + O_2 + S_2 - 150 \\
I_3 - B_3 &= I_2 - B_2 + R_3 + O_3 + S_3 - 200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Terminal condition:** $I_3 = B_3 = 0$ (final inventory and backorders both zero).

**Non-negativity:** All variables $\geq 0$.

---

### ✅ Step 4 — Solving by Inspection (LP-Optimal Logic)

Because **regular-time is the cheapest source**, we use it to its full capacity each period.

$$
R_1 = 100, \quad R_2 = 100, \quad R_3 = 100
$$

After meeting period 1 demand entirely from regular time, no inventory is left:

$$
I_1 - B_1 = 100 - 100 = 0
$$

Period 2 demand is 150, but regular capacity is 100 → we need 50 more. Overtime (₹15) is cheaper than subcontracting (₹20) → use **overtime first**.

$$
O_2 = 50, \quad R_2 = 100
$$

Then $I_2 - B_2 = 0 + 150 - 150 = 0$.

Period 3 demand is 200. Regular = 100, Overtime = 50 → still need 50 more. Use **subcontracting**.

$$
R_3 = 100, \; O_3 = 50, \; S_3 = 50
$$

---

### ✅ Step 5 — Total Cost Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Regular cost} &= 10 \times 300 = ₹3{,}000 \\
\text{Overtime cost} &= 15 \times 50 = ₹750 \\
\text{Subcontracting cost} &= 20 \times 50 = ₹1{,}000 \\
\text{Holding cost} &= 2 \times 0 = ₹0 \\
\text{Backorder cost} &= 5 \times 0 = ₹0 \\
\hline
Z_{\min} &= ₹4{,}750
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ✅ Step 6 — Verification of Demand Satisfaction

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 100, \quad D_1 = 100 \Rightarrow \text{OK} \\
P_2 &= 100 + 50 = 150, \quad D_2 = 150 \Rightarrow \text{OK} \\
P_3 &= 100 + 50 + 50 = 200, \quad D_3 = 200 \Rightarrow \text{OK}
\end{aligned}
$$

**All three periods satisfied → LP is feasible and optimal.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Levers vs Cost

| Lever | Symbol | Cost | Capacity | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Regular Time | $R_t$ | Lowest | Fixed | Always first |
| Overtime | $O_t$ | Medium | Limited | Next, if demand > regular |
| Subcontracting | $S_t$ | High | Flexible | Last resort for demand |
| Inventory | $I_t$ | Low holding | Bounded | Buffer demand dips |
| Backorders | $B_t$ | Penalty | — | Avoid; very expensive |

---

## 3.3 Coordination Math — Bullwhip Quantification

Given retailer order $q$ with demand $D$:

$$
\text{Bullwhip Ratio} = \frac{\text{Var}(q)}{\text{Var}(D)}
$$

If ratio $> 1$, the bullwhip effect is present. Common causes:

- **Demand signal processing** (order batching by retailers).
- **Shortage gaming** (over-ordering during scarcity).
- **Order lead-time** amplification.
- **Price fluctuations** (forward buying).

Coordination mechanisms (VMI, CPFR) **reduce this ratio toward 1**, restoring signal fidelity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LP Formulation Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1 Identify Decision Variables] --> stepB[Step 2 Express Demand and Cost Data]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Formulate Objective Function]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Write Capacity Constraints]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Write Inventory Balance Equations]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Add Non Negativity Conditions]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Solve using Simplex or Solver]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Verify Feasibility and Cost]
    stepH --> doneA[Output Optimal Production Plan]
```

---

## 4.2 Aggregate Planning Decision Matrix (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS [Inputs]
        d1[Forecast Demand]
        d2[Current Inventory]
        d3[Workforce Size]
        d4[Cost Parameters]
    end

    subgraph DECISION [Decision Engine LP Model]
        e1[Objective Minimize Total Cost]
        e2[Capacity Constraints]
        e3[Inventory Balance]
        e4[Workforce Flow]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS [Outputs]
        f1[Regular Production Plan]
        f2[Overtime Allocation]
        f3[Subcontracting Schedule]
        f4[Hire and Layoff Plan]
        f5[Inventory Levels]
    end

    d1 --> e1
    d2 --> e3
    d3 --> e4
    d4 --> e1
    d1 --> e3
    e1 --> f1
    e2 --> f1
    e3 --> f5
    e2 --> f2
    e1 --> f3
    e4 --> f4
```

---

## 4.3 Supply Chain Coordination Hierarchy (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    coordRoot[Supply Chain Coordination Mechanisms]

    coordRoot --> lvl1A[No Coordination Spot Market]
    coordRoot --> lvl1B[Information Sharing EDI]
    coordRoot --> lvl1C[Vendor Managed Inventory VMI]
    coordRoot --> lvl1D[CPFR Joint Planning]
    coordRoot --> lvl1E[Contracts Buyback Revenue Sharing]

    lvl1A --> detail1[High Bullwhip High Cost]
    lvl1B --> detail2[Reduced Distortion]
    lvl1C --> detail3[Supplier Owns Replenishment]
    lvl1D --> detail4[Shared Forecast and Plans]
    lvl1E --> detail5[Aligned Incentives]
```

---

## 4.4 CPFR Process Flow (Nine-Step Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpfrA[Step 1 Front End Agreement] --> cpfrB[Step 2 Joint Business Plan]
    cpfrB --> cpfrC[Step 3 Sales Forecast Collaboration]
    cpfrC --> cpfrD[Step 4 Order Forecast Collaboration]
    cpfrD --> cpfrE[Step 5 Order Generation]
    cpfrE --> cpfrF[Step 6 Order Fulfillment]
    cpfrF --> cpfrG[Step 7 Exception Handling]
    cpfrG --> cpfrH[Step 8 Performance Assessment]
    cpfrH --> cpfrI[Step 9 Continuous Improvement Loop]
    cpfrI --> cpfrC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**"Define aggregate planning and list its key inputs and outputs."**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution Below)

Aggregate planning is a **medium-range (3–18 months) tactical planning process** that establishes production rates, workforce levels, inventory holdings, and subcontracting volumes to meet forecasted demand at minimum cost (1 mark).

**Key inputs (1 mark):**
- Demand forecasts (product-family level)
- Current inventory and workforce status
- Cost data (regular, overtime, subcontracting, holding, backorder, hiring, layoff)
- Capacity limits and policy constraints

**Key outputs (1 mark):**
- Aggregate production quantities per period
- Inventory and backorder levels
- Workforce plan (hires, layoffs, overtime hours)
- Total cost of the plan

---

### ❓ Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"What is the bullwhip effect? Mention any two coordination mechanisms that mitigate it."**

#### ✅ Model Answer

The **bullwhip effect** is the phenomenon where **small fluctuations in end-customer demand get amplified as they propagate upstream** in the supply chain, resulting in progressively larger variance in orders placed at each tier (1.5 marks).

Causes include demand signal processing, shortage gaming, order batching, and price promotions.

**Two coordination mechanisms (1.5 marks):**

1. **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI):** The supplier monitors retail inventory and decides replenishment, eliminating the retailer's distorted orders.
2. **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment):** Trading partners share POS data and jointly build a consensus forecast, aligning orders with true demand.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Within a module, students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (full 14 marks). Both are provided here for practice.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

#### (a) Formulate a Linear Programming model for the following aggregate planning scenario. Solve it for the optimal plan and compute total cost. (7 Marks)

**Data:**

| Period | Demand (units) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 300 |
| 3 | 400 |

| Cost Component | Value |
|---|---|
| Regular-time production | ₹8 / unit (max 200 units) |
| Overtime production | ₹12 / unit (max 80 units) |
| Subcontracting | ₹18 / unit (max 150 units) |
| Holding cost | ₹2 / unit / period |
| Backorder cost | ₹6 / unit / period |
| Initial inventory | 0 |
| Final inventory desired | 0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Decision variables:** $R_t, O_t, S_t, I_t, B_t$ for $t = 1, 2, 3$.

**Objective function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } Z =\; & 8(R_1+R_2+R_3) + 12(O_1+O_2+O_3) + 18(S_1+S_2+S_3) \\
& + 2(I_1+I_2+I_3) + 6(B_1+B_2+B_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constraints:** Standard capacity and inventory balance as derived in Section 3.

**Optimal solution by LP logic:**

Use regular time to its max each period:

$$
R_1 = 200, \quad R_2 = 200, \quad R_3 = 200
$$

Period 2 remaining demand = 100. Use overtime (₹12) before subcontracting (₹18):

$$
O_2 = 80 \text{ (max)}, \quad S_2 = 20
$$

Period 3 remaining demand = 200. Use overtime (80) and subcontracting (120):

$$
O_3 = 80, \quad S_3 = 120
$$

**Total cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Regular} &= 8 \times 600 = ₹4{,}800 \\
\text{Overtime} &= 12 \times 160 = ₹1{,}920 \\
\text{Subcontract} &= 18 \times 140 = ₹2{,}520 \\
\text{Holding} &= ₹0 \\
\text{Backorder} &= ₹0 \\
\hline
Z_{\min} &= ₹9{,}240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** All three demands are fully met → feasible and optimal (7 marks — 2 for formulation, 3 for solving logic, 2 for cost computation).

---

#### (b) Discuss the major coordination problems in a supply chain and explain how CPFR overcomes them. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Coordination problems (3 marks):**
   - Bullwhip effect (demand amplification).
   - Double marginalization (each tier marks up price, raising system cost).
   - Information asymmetry (hidden forecasts, gaming).
   - Incentive misalignment (local optimum ≠ global optimum).
   - Free-riding in promotional efforts.

2. **CPFR and its mitigation (4 marks):**
   - **Joint business plan** aligns strategic goals.
   - **Shared POS and inventory data** eliminates the information asymmetry that drives bullwhip.
   - **Collaborative forecasting** dampens demand-signal distortion.
   - **Joint order planning** synchronizes replenishment.
   - **Exception handling** reduces gaming.
   - **Outcome:** lower safety stock, higher service level, reduced total chain cost — closer to the global optimum.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the following supply chain coordination mechanisms with one-line definitions: VMI, CRP, CPFR, Buy-back Contract, Revenue Sharing Contract, Quantity Flexibility Contract. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Mechanism | Definition |
|---|---|
| **VMI (Vendor-Managed Inventory)** | Supplier monitors retailer's inventory and decides replenishment timing & quantity |
| **CRP (Continuous Replenishment Program)** | Supplier automatically replenishes retail stock based on POS-driven triggers |
| **CPFR** | Joint planning, forecasting & replenishment through shared data and consensus |
| **Buy-back Contract** | Manufacturer buys back unsold inventory from retailer at a salvage price, reducing retailer's risk |
| **Revenue Sharing Contract** | Retailer shares a fraction of revenue with supplier in exchange for a lower wholesale price |
| **Quantity Flexibility Contract** | Buyer commits to ±x% of order quantity, allowing demand-aligned adjustments |

(Marks: 1 per mechanism × 6 = 6, plus 1 mark for the comparative table or a concluding sentence)

---

#### (b) The ABC Manufacturing Co. has the following aggregate planning data for 4 periods. Formulate the LP model only (no solving required). (7 Marks)

| Period | Demand |
|---|---|
| 1 | 500 |
| 2 | 700 |
| 3 | 600 |
| 4 | 800 |

Costs: Regular = ₹10, Overtime = ₹14, Subcontract = ₹20, Holding = ₹3, Backorder = ₹8. Capacities: Regular = 500, Overtime = 200, Subcontract = 300. $I_0 = B_0 = 0$, $I_4 = B_4 = 0$.

**Model Solution:**

**Decision variables:** $R_t, O_t, S_t, I_t, B_t$ for $t = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Objective:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } Z =\; & 10\sum_{t=1}^{4}R_t + 14\sum_{t=1}^{4}O_t + 20\sum_{t=1}^{4}S_t \\
& + 3\sum_{t=1}^{4}I_t + 8\sum_{t=1}^{4}B_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Capacity constraints:**

$$
R_t \le 500, \quad O_t \le 200, \quad S_t \le 300, \quad t=1,2,3,4
$$

**Inventory balance:**

$$
I_t - B_t = I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + R_t + O_t + S_t - D_t
$$

**Boundary:** $I_0 = B_0 = 0, \; I_4 = B_4 = 0$.

**Non-negativity:** all variables $\geq 0$.

(7 marks — 2 for variables, 2 for objective, 2 for constraints, 1 for boundary & non-negativity)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in this module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the terminal condition** $I_T = B_T = 0$ → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up the inventory balance sign** — the equation is `I_t − B_t = Previous Net + Production − Demand`. Wrong sign → 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Writing only "Minimize cost"** without specifying cost components → formulation is incomplete → up to 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Confusing CPFR with VMI** — CPFR is *joint* and includes shared forecasting; VMI is *supplier-led* replenishment. Examiners **will** deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 5. **Not converting demand to units** consistent with capacity (e.g., using hours for production but units for demand) → model is dimensionally inconsistent.
> 6. **Skipping non-negativity constraints** in Part (a) of Q-type B → 1 mark deducted.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Aggregate Planning** = tactical, product-family, 3–18 months, cost-minimization.
- **LP is the formal optimization tool** for AP — formulations require variables, objective, and three classes of constraints (capacity, balance, non-negativity).
- **The inventory balance equation** is the heart of the LP model: $I_t - B_t = I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + R_t + O_t + S_t - D_t$.
- **Decision hierarchy of cost levers:** Regular time (cheapest) → Overtime → Subcontracting → Backorders (most expensive).
- **Coordination ≠ Cooperation** — coordination is *system-wide optimal*; cooperation is *willing but not aligned*.
- **Bullwhip effect** is the central symptom of poor coordination; ratio $\text{Var}(\text{orders}) / \text{Var}(\text{demand}) > 1$ indicates distortion.
- **VMI = supplier decides replenishment; CPFR = both decide together; CRP = automatic replenishment trigger.**
- **Contracts (buy-back, revenue sharing, quantity flexibility)** align incentives mathematically.
- **LP can be solved** by Simplex, by Excel Solver, or by logical inspection for small problems — the KTU exam expects the logical-inspection approach for 7-mark sub-questions.
- **Always** state the **optimal cost** and **verify feasibility** (every period's demand must be met) before concluding.
- **CPFR has 9 steps** — memorise: *Agreement → Business Plan → Sales Forecast → Order Forecast → Order Generation → Fulfillment → Exception → Performance → Improvement* (cyclic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Lack of Coordination in the Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impact of Lack of Coordination in the Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supply Chain Coordination** is the strategic alignment of objectives, decisions, information flows, and incentive structures among all independent members (suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers, customers) of a supply chain to optimize the **total system performance** rather than individual member performance.

A **Lack of Coordination** in the chain refers to the systemic misalignment between independent decision-makers in the supply chain, where each entity optimizes its **local objectives** (its own cost, inventory, profit) at the expense of **global chain efficiency**. This creates a chain of suboptimal outcomes, the most documented of which is the **Bullwhip Effect** (also known as the *Forrester Effect* or *Whip Effect*).

$$ \text{Coordination Gap} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \text{Local Optimum}_i - \text{Global Optimum}_{\text{Chain}} \right) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *Bullwhip Effect* is the single most important consequence of lack of coordination. It is defined as the phenomenon where **demand variability amplifies** as it propagates upstream from the end customer to the raw material supplier. Variance at the supplier level can be 5–10 times higher than variance at the customer level.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Relay Race Without a Baton Handover" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a 4 × 100 m relay race where each runner runs as fast as they can but refuses to synchronize with the next runner's stride. The first runner sprints, the second runner starts too early (causing collision/false start), the third runner compensates by running extra slow, and the final runner receives the baton in chaos. The **team finishes poorly** even though **each individual was running fast**.
>
> This is exactly what happens in a poorly coordinated supply chain: each firm acts rationally in isolation, but the **chain collectively fails** because there is no shared rhythm (information), no common goal (incentive), and no synchronized timing (forecasting).

### 1.3 The Classic Reference Case

The phenomenon was popularized by **Procter & Gamble (P&G)** in the early 1990s, who observed that the **variance of orders for Pampers diapers at their manufacturing plant** was **significantly higher** than the variance of actual retail demand. This led to the formal study by supply chain researchers at MIT.

---

### 1.4 Types of Coordination Failures (KTU Taxonomy)

| # | Type of Failure | Root Cause | Stage of Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Failure** | Asymmetric/distorted data sharing | Forecasting & Planning |
| 2 | **Incentive Misalignment** | Local KPIs vs chain KPIs | Procurement & Production |
| 3 | **Operational Mismatch** | Independent inventory policies | Logistics & Warehousing |
| 4 | **Strategic Drift** | Conflicting long-term goals | Sourcing & Distribution |
| 5 | **Financial Disconnect** | Payment terms, pricing volatility | Order Management |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked "What is the impact of lack of coordination?", always structure the answer into **Operational, Financial, and Strategic** impact buckets. Examiners specifically test for this three-fold classification under Module 2 of PESFT867.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bullwhip Effect — Formal Anatomy

The **Bullwhip Effect** can be modeled as a *signal processing distortion* in which each stage of the chain acts as an *amplifier* of upstream demand variability. Mathematically, we observe:

$$ \frac{\text{Var}(Q_{\text{supplier}})}{\text{Var}(D_{\text{customer}})} \;\gg\; 1 $$

Where $Q_{\text{supplier}}$ is the order quantity at the supplier and $D_{\text{customer}}$ is the end-customer demand.

### 2.2 The Four Root Causes (Forrester — Sterman Framework)

These are the **causes of distortion** that produce the bullwhip effect. Note: each cause independently contributes to lack of coordination.

#### Cause 1 — Demand Signal Processing (Demand Forecasting Update Errors)
Each stage uses historical order data (not actual customer demand) to update its forecast. Since orders are already distorted by the previous stage's reactions, **errors compound multiplicatively**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it happens:** Retailer sees demand surge → places large order → wholesaler sees order surge (NOT demand surge) → wholesaler assumes demand has shifted up → places even larger order.

#### Cause 2 — Order Batching
To minimize ordering/transportation costs, firms place orders in **periodic batches** rather than continuously. This creates **lumpy demand patterns** that the supplier perceives as variable demand.

> Real-world example: A retailer who needs 100 units/week but orders 1,200 units quarterly creates a "saw-tooth" pattern that the supplier misinterprets as fluctuating end demand.

#### Cause 3 — Price Fluctuation (Forward Buying / Trade Promotions)
Manufacturers offer **periodic discounts, rebates, and trade promotions**. Buyers (retailers) respond by **stockpiling during promotion periods**, creating artificial demand spikes.

> [!NOTE]
> The forward-buying behavior is rational from the retailer's local perspective (minimizing purchase cost) but distorts the supplier's perception of true end demand.

#### Cause 4 — Shortage Gaming (Hoarding Behavior)
When supply is uncertain, each buyer **inflates orders** to ensure they receive allocation. When supply is restored, the inflated orders are cancelled, creating a demand cliff.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Notation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} \geq 1 + \dfrac{2L}{p} + \dfrac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}}$ | $L$ = lead time, $p$ = review period | Bullwhip amplification (Chen et al. — moving avg + order-up-to) | Estimating upstream variance |
| $\dfrac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} \geq 1 + \dfrac{2L}{p} + \dfrac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha}{2-\alpha}$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing constant | Bullwhip with exponential smoothing | SES-based forecasting |
| $\text{CS}_i = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Stockouts}_i}{\text{Demand}_i}$ | $\text{CS}$ = Customer Service Level | Service degradation metric | KTU Part B numericals |
| $\text{ICR} = \dfrac{\text{Inventory}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Inventory}_{\text{optimal}}}$ | $\text{ICR}$ = Inventory Cost Ratio | Cost of over/under-stocking | Financial impact analysis |
| $\text{TIC} = H \cdot \dfrac{Q}{2} + S \cdot \dfrac{D}{Q} + p_s \cdot \text{Stockouts}$ | $H$ = holding, $S$ = order cost, $p_s$ = stockout penalty | Total Inventory Cost (incl. shortage) | Cost trade-off questions |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Management Utility

Understanding the bullwhip effect and lack of coordination is **directly applied** in:
- **Retail:** Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) systems at Walmart, Amazon
- **Automotive:** Toyota Production System (TPS) — leveling demand (Heijunka)
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Cold-chain coordination between manufacturers and hospitals
- **E-commerce:** Real-time demand sensing (machine learning forecasting)
- **Defense Procurement:** Long-lead-time coordination in aerospace supply chains
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Bullwhip Effect Amplification Factor

We derive the variance amplification ratio for a single-stage supply chain under the **moving average forecast + order-up-to policy** model.

#### Step 1 — Define the End Customer Demand (AR(1) Process)

The customer demand $D_t$ follows a first-order autoregressive process:

$$ D_t = d + \rho \, D_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t $$

Where:
- $d$ = constant mean demand drift
- $\rho$ = autocorrelation coefficient, where $\vert\rho\vert < 1$ ensures stationarity
- $\varepsilon_t$ = i.i.d. white-noise innovation with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}$

#### Step 2 — Demand Variance (Stationary)

For a stationary AR(1) process, the unconditional variance of $D_t$ is:

$$ \text{Var}(D_t) = \frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{1 - \rho^{2}} $$

This is obtained by iteratively substituting $D_{t-1}$ and using the geometric series property $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \rho^{2k} = \frac{1}{1-\rho^{2}}$ for $\vert\rho\vert < 1$.

#### Step 3 — Forecast at the Retailer (Moving Average over $p$ Periods)

The retailer uses a $p$-period moving average to forecast mean demand:

$$ \hat{D}_{t+L} = \frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=0}^{p-1} D_{t-i} $$

Where $L$ is the lead time from retailer to wholesaler.

#### Step 4 — Order-Up-To Level (Base-Stock Style Policy)

The retailer maintains an **order-up-to level** $S_t$ that covers expected lead-time demand plus safety stock:

$$ S_t = \hat{D}_{t+L} + z \, \sigma_{L} $$

For simplicity (ignoring safety stock noise), we take $S_t \approx \hat{D}_{t+L}$.

#### Step 5 — Order Quantity Placed by Retailer

The retailer places an order equal to the change in order-up-to level plus the demand observed during the previous period:

$$ Q_t = S_t - S_{t-1} + D_{t-1} $$

Substituting the moving-average forecast:

$$
Q_t = \frac{1}{p}\sum_{i=0}^{p-1} D_{t-i} - \frac{1}{p}\sum_{i=0}^{p-1} D_{t-1-i} + D_{t-1}
$$

#### Step 6 — Express in Differences

Let $\Delta D_{t} = D_t - D_{t-1}$. After algebraic manipulation:

$$ Q_t = \frac{1}{p}(D_t - D_{t-p}) + D_{t-1} $$

Rewriting using differences:

$$ Q_t = \frac{1}{p}\sum_{k=0}^{p-1} \Delta D_{t-k} + D_{t-1} $$

#### Step 7 — Compute Variance of $Q_t$

Since $D_t$ is AR(1), the increments $\Delta D_{t-k}$ are MA($p$) — they have non-zero covariance only within $p$ lags. Computing:

$$ \text{Var}(Q_t) = \text{Var}(D_t) \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}} \right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Note:** The factor $\left(1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}}\right)$ emerges after expanding the covariances and applying the $\text{MA}(p)$ structure of the demand increments. The lead-time $L$ appears in the linear and quadratic terms because longer lead times force retailers to forecast further ahead, magnifying forecast error variance.

#### Step 8 — The Bullwhip Amplification Ratio

Finally, dividing by $\text{Var}(D)$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} \;=\; 1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}} \;\geq\; 1\;}
$$

This proves that **upstream order variance is always greater than or equal to customer demand variance**, confirming the bullwhip effect mathematically.

#### Step 9 — Limiting Cases (Sanity Checks)

| Case | Condition | Amplification Ratio | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| No lead time | $L = 0$ | $1$ | Variance unchanged (no distortion) |
| Long lead time | $L \gg p$ | $\approx \dfrac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}}$ | Variance explodes as $L^{2}$ |
| Frequent review | $p \to \infty$ | $\to 1$ | Continuous review kills bullwhip |
| Periodic batching | $p = 1$ | $1 + 2L + 2L^{2}$ | Maximum distortion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** The bullwhip effect is a *quadratic* function of lead time and *inverse-quadratic* of the review period. Therefore, **reducing lead time** and **increasing review frequency** are mathematically proven levers to *reduce* the impact of lack of coordination.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** A retailer's end-customer demand has $\text{Var}(D) = 100$ units². The lead time to the wholesaler is $L = 3$ periods, and the retailer's review period is $p = 2$ periods. Find the upstream order variance and the bullwhip ratio.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Plug into the amplification formula:

$$ \frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} = 1 + \frac{2(3)}{2} + \frac{2(3)^{2}}{2^{2}} $$

Step 2 — Evaluate each term:

$$ = 1 + 3 + \frac{18}{4} = 1 + 3 + 4.5 $$

Step 3 — Sum:

$$ \frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} = 8.5 $$

Step 4 — Calculate upstream variance:

$$ \text{Var}(Q) = 8.5 \times 100 = 850 \; \text{units}^{2} $$

Step 5 — Standard deviation interpretation:

$$ \sigma_Q = \sqrt{850} \approx 29.15 \;\text{units}, \quad \sigma_D = \sqrt{100} = 10 \;\text{units} $$

> **Interpretation:** The wholesaler sees demand variability that is **2.92× larger** than what the retailer actually experiences — a classic bullwhip amplification, even in a single stage. In multi-stage chains, this distortion *multiplies geometrically* across each tier.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Cascade of Bullwhip Amplification — Multi-Tier Schematic

The diagram below illustrates how demand variance **amplifies multiplicatively** as it propagates upstream through four supply chain tiers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Tier\nVariance = 100] -->|Demand signal| B[Retailer Tier\nVariance = 100]
    B -->|Order to wholesaler| C[Wholesaler Tier\nVariance = 850]
    C -->|Order to manufacturer| D[Manufacturer Tier\nVariance = 3200]
    D -->|Order to supplier| E[Raw Material Tier\nVariance = 11200]
    
    F[Root Cause 1\nDemand Forecasting] -.-> B
    G[Root Cause 2\nOrder Batching] -.-> C
    H[Root Cause 3\nPrice Fluctuations] -.-> D
    I[Root Cause 4\nShortage Gaming] -.-> E
    
    style A fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2d6a2d,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#cc0000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note the dramatic jump in variance from 100 (customer) → 850 (wholesaler) → 3,200 (manufacturer) → 11,200 (raw material). Each transition represents a *new* layer of coordination failure. The arrows with dotted lines (F, G, H, I) indicate the specific root cause most active at each tier.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Impacts of Lack of Coordination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    coord[Lack of Coordination Trigger] --> info[Information Asymmetry]
    coord --> incentive[Incentive Misalignment]
    coord --> process[Process Mismatch]
    
    info --> opimpact[Operational Impacts]
    incentive --> finimpact[Financial Impacts]
    process --> stratimpact[Strategic Impacts]
    
    opimpact --> op1[Inventory Fluctuations\nExcess and Stockouts]
    opimpact --> op2[Production Schedule\nDisruption]
    opimpact --> op3[Transportation\nInefficiency]
    opimpact --> op4[Bullwhip Effect\nVariance Amplification]
    
    finimpact --> fi1[Increased Working\nCapital Lock-up]
    fiimpact --> fi2[Higher Warehousing\nand Holding Costs]
    finimpact --> fi3[Cash Flow\nVolatility]
    finimpact --> fi4[Lost Sales and\nRevenue Erosion]
    
    stratimpact --> st1[Supplier Relationship\nDamage]
    stratimpact --> st2[Customer Service\nDegradation]
    stratimpact --> st3[Reduced Trust and\nInformation Sharing]
    stratimpact --> st4[Long-Term\nCompetitiveness Loss]
    
    fi1 -.->|snowball effect| st1
    op2 -.->|snowball effect| fi3
    op1 -.->|snowball effect| st2
    
    style coord fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px
    style opimpact fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    style fiimpact fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style stratimpact fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Feedback Loop of Distortion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Stage 1 Customer\nDemand D1] --> s2[Stage 2 Retailer\nOrder Q2]
    s2 --> s3[Stage 3 Wholesaler\nOrder Q3]
    s3 --> s4[Stage 4 Manufacturer\nOrder Q4]
    s4 --> s5[Stage 5 Supplier\nOrder Q5]
    
    s2 -.Distorted forecast\nbased on Q2.-> s3
    s3 -.Distorted forecast\nbased on Q3.-> s4
    s4 -.Distorted forecast\nbased on Q4.-> s5
    
    s5 -.Returns as\ninstability.-> s1
    
    style s1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2d6a2d,stroke-width:2px
    style s2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    style s3 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style s4 fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style s5 fill:#990000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The diagram above illustrates the **closed feedback loop**. Once distortion enters the chain at the retailer, it is *re-interpreted* as new demand information at every upstream stage, and the amplified signal eventually feeds back as supply instability to the customer — creating a self-reinforcing cycle of poor coordination.

---

### 4.4 Quantified Impact Summary (KTU Board Reference Table)

| Impact Category | Specific Metric | Typical Magnitude (Industries) | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inventory Imbalance | Excess-to-shortage ratio | 2:1 to 5:1 in uncoordinated chains | VMI, CPFR |
| Service Level Drop | Stockout frequency | 15–30% increase during disruptions | Safety stock pooling |
| Working Capital | Tied-up inventory $ | 20–40% above optimal | JIT, consignment |
| Order Variability | Var(Q)/Var(D) ratio | 3× to 10× amplification | Information sharing |
| Cost of Coordination Failure | % of revenue | 9.5–13.5% (industry studies) | End-to-end visibility |
| Lead Time Sensitivity | Variance growth rate | Quadratic with $L$ | Postponement, sourcing |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

*Define the Bullwhip Effect. List any FOUR root causes of the Bullwhip Effect in a supply chain.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Bullwhip Effect** is the phenomenon of progressive amplification of demand variance as one moves upstream in a supply chain — from the end customer through retailers, wholesalers, manufacturers, and raw material suppliers. The variance of orders placed at upstream tiers becomes significantly larger than the variance of actual customer demand.

**Four Root Causes:**
1. **Demand Signal Processing** — Each stage updates its forecast based on order history (already distorted), compounding errors.
2. **Order Batching** — Periodic bulk ordering creates lumpy patterns misinterpreted as demand variability.
3. **Price Fluctuations** — Trade promotions and forward-buying distort true demand signals.
4. **Shortage Gaming** — Buyers inflate orders during scarcity, creating artificial peaks followed by cliffs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question, 3 Marks]**

*Distinguish between "Local Optimization" and "Global Optimization" in the context of supply chain coordination. Why does lack of coordination lead to the Bullwhip Effect?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Local Optimization | Global Optimization |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Minimize cost of *one firm* | Minimize cost of *entire chain* |
| **Decision Scope** | Single echelon (e.g., retailer only) | Multi-echelon, end-to-end |
| **Information** | Uses only own data | Uses shared, real-time data |
| **Performance Metric** | Firm-level KPIs (own profit, own inventory) | Chain-level KPIs (service, total cost) |
| **Risk of Sub-optimality** | High — externality imposed on partners | Low — Pareto-optimal outcome |

**Why lack of coordination causes the Bullwhip Effect:**
When each firm optimizes locally, it reacts to *what it observes* (orders from the downstream partner) rather than *what is true* (end-customer demand). This causes over-reaction, over-ordering, and panic — each of which amplifies the demand signal as it propagates upstream. The collective result is the bullwhip effect.

---

### Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [14 Marks — KTU ESE Pattern, Choice Option 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Bullwhip Effect** in a four-stage supply chain. Discuss in detail **any three causes** of the Bullwhip Effect and their corresponding managerial remedies.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A retailer faces customer demand with variance $\text{Var}(D) = 400$ units². The retailer's lead time to the wholesaler is $L = 4$ periods, and the review period is $p = 2$ periods. Using the bullwhip amplification formula, calculate:
  (i) The variance amplification ratio $\frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)}$.
  (ii) The upstream order variance and standard deviation at the wholesaler.
  (iii) Comment on the implication for inventory management.

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Bullwhip Effect Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Diagram (must be drawn in exam):**

```
CUSTOMER → RETAILER → WHOLESALER → MANUFACTURER → SUPPLIER
Variance   Variance   Variance     Variance       Variance
   400       400       3,200        18,000         72,000
           (stable)   (amplified)  (more amplified) (highly amplified)
```

**Explanation:**
The Bullwhip Effect refers to the distortion and amplification of demand information as it travels upstream through the supply chain. Each tier reinterprets signals based on its own local information, leading to compounded variability.

**Three Causes and Remedies:**

1. **Demand Forecasting Errors (Cause) → Information Sharing & Collaborative Forecasting (Remedy):**
   - Cause: Each stage uses historical orders (distorted) for forecasting.
   - Remedy: Use **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)** and **POS data sharing** so each stage bases its forecast on actual end demand.

2. **Order Batching (Cause) → Reducing Order Frequency via EDI and Continuous Replenishment:**
   - Cause: Periodic bulk orders create saw-tooth patterns.
   - Remedy: Adopt **Electronic Data Interchange (EDI)** and **Continuous Replenishment Programs (CRP)** to enable smaller, more frequent orders.

3. **Price Fluctuation / Forward Buying (Cause) → Every Day Low Pricing (EDLP):**
   - Cause: Trade promotions induce stockpiling.
   - Remedy: Shift to **EDLP strategy** (used by Walmart and Costco) to eliminate artificial demand spikes.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given:
- $\text{Var}(D) = 400$ units²
- $L = 4$ periods
- $p = 2$ periods

**(i) Amplification Ratio:**

$$
\frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} = 1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
= 1 + \frac{2(4)}{2} + \frac{2(4)^{2}}{2^{2}} = 1 + 4 + \frac{32}{4} = 1 + 4 + 8 = 13
$$

**$\therefore$ Amplification Ratio = 13**

**(ii) Upstream Variance and Standard Deviation:**

$$
\text{Var}(Q) = 13 \times 400 = 5200 \; \text{units}^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_Q = \sqrt{5200} \approx 72.11 \;\text{units}
$$

Compared to $\sigma_D = \sqrt{400} = 20$ units — a **3.6× amplification**.

**(iii) Managerial Implication:**

The wholesaler must hold safety stock proportional to $\sigma_Q = 72.11$ units, which is 3.6× the safety stock that actual demand variability would justify. This **inflates holding costs, working capital, and stockout risk** simultaneously — a textbook case of the bullwhip effect creating **both over-stocking and under-stocking** in the same chain.

---

**Question B. [14 Marks — KTU ESE Pattern, Choice Option 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **six major operational, financial, and strategic impacts** of lack of coordination in a supply chain. Illustrate with a real-world industry example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing firm observes that its supplier's order variance is $9$ times higher than its own production variance. The firm's lead time to the supplier is $L = 3$ periods and it reviews orders every $p = 1$ period. Verify the bullwhip formula and recommend two coordination strategies.

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Six Major Impacts (7 Marks):**

**Operational Impacts:**
1. **Inventory Fluctuations** — Combination of excess and shortage simultaneously; the bullwhip makes both over-stock and under-stock systemic.
2. **Production Schedule Disruption** — Manufacturers face unplanned ramp-up/ramp-down, leading to overtime, idle time, and quality issues.
3. **Transportation Inefficiency** — Frequent expediting (air freight, partial loads) increases logistics costs.

**Financial Impacts:**
4. **Increased Working Capital** — Higher average inventory ties up cash that could otherwise fund growth.
5. **Higher Warehousing Costs** — More warehouses, longer storage, increased obsolescence risk.

**Strategic Impacts:**
6. **Customer Service Degradation** — Stockouts erode trust and brand loyalty, leading to permanent customer churn.

**Real-World Industry Example:**
*Procter & Gamble's Pampers Diaper Supply Chain (1990s):* P&G observed that retail diaper demand had a stable variance, but **orders placed to manufacturing** had **variance 2–3× higher**. This led to **excess production, obsolescence, and lost sales**. The response was the development of **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** and **CPFR** — two coordination mechanisms now industry-standard.

---

**(b) Numerical Verification (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Amplification ratio = $9$
- $L = 3$ periods
- $p = 1$ period

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical amplification:**

$$
\frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} = 1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}} = 1 + \frac{2(3)}{1} + \frac{2(3)^{2}}{1^{2}}
$$

$$
= 1 + 6 + 18 = 25
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with observed:**

Observed amplification = $9$. Theoretical = $25$.

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The observed ratio ($9$) is **lower than theoretical maximum** ($25$), which suggests the firm has *partial* coordination mechanisms already in place. However, $9$ still indicates a **severe bullwhip effect** — order variability is 9× the actual production variability.

**Two Recommended Coordination Strategies:**

1. **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (CPFR):** Share POS data and forecasts with the supplier so they base production on true end demand rather than distorted order history. This directly addresses the **demand signal processing** root cause.

2. **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI):** Allow the supplier to monitor the firm's inventory level directly and replenish automatically. This eliminates order batching and reduces the lead-time penalty, mathematically reducing the amplification factor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the bullwhip effect with general inventory variability.** Bullwhip specifically refers to *upstream amplification* of *customer-driven* demand variability — not normal stock fluctuations.
> 
> 2. **Always quote the variance amplification formula** $1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}}$ in numerical answers — failing to do so costs 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **In 14-mark questions**, examiners expect **three or more causes** of bullwhip to be discussed with **remedies**. Writing only causes without remedies loses 2–3 marks.
> 
> 4. **In numerical questions**, students often forget to convert variance to standard deviation in the final interpretation. Always show $\sigma_Q = \sqrt{\text{Var}(Q)}$ and provide a managerial comment.
> 
> 5. **Drawing a diagram of the 4-tier supply chain** with variance values at each stage earns 1 extra mark in part (a) — it is considered KTU best practice.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Impact of Lack of Coordination**

- **Definition:** Lack of coordination = misalignment of objectives, information, and incentives among supply chain members, leading to local (not global) optimization.
- **Bullwhip Effect:** Upstream amplification of demand variance — the signature consequence of poor coordination.
- **Four Root Causes:**
  1. Demand signal processing (forecast error compounding)
  2. Order batching (lumpy orders)
  3. Price fluctuation / forward buying (promotions)
  4. Shortage gaming (panic ordering)
- **Amplification Formula:** $\frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)} \geq 1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^{2}}{p^{2}}$ — quadratic in lead time $L$, inverse-quadratic in review period $p$.
- **Impact Categories (always use three buckets):**
  - **Operational:** Inventory, production, transport
  - **Financial:** Working capital, holding costs, lost sales
  - **Strategic:** Relationships, service level, competitiveness
- **Classic Case:** P&G Pampers (1990s) — the textbook origin of bullwhip research.
- **Key Coordination Mechanisms:**
  - Information sharing (POS data, CPFR)
  - Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)
  - Continuous Replenishment (CRP)
  - Every-Day-Low-Pricing (EDLP)
  - Postponement and standardization
- **Lead time $L$ is the most powerful amplifier** — reducing $L$ produces *quadratic* reduction in bullwhip.
- **Review period $p$** — increasing $p$ (more frequent reviews) reduces bullwhip *inversely-quadratically*.
- **Numerical Typical Numbers:** $\text{Var}(D) = 100$, $L = 3$, $p = 2$ → amplification ratio = $8.5$ (use this for mental reference during exam).
- **Exam Tip:** Always end bullwhip answers with a **managerial implication** sentence — examiners reward applied thinking in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacles to Coordination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Obstacles to Coordination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Coordination** in a supply chain refers to the deliberate alignment of decisions and actions among independent entities (suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers, and customers) to improve the **efficiency** of the integrated system as a whole, rather than optimizing the performance of individual stages in isolation.

An **Obstacle to Coordination** is any structural, informational, financial, operational, behavioral, or legal barrier that prevents the supply chain partners from synchronizing their decisions, sharing critical data, or aligning their incentives. These obstacles cause the supply chain to operate at a **sub-optimal level**, often resulting in the *Nash Equilibrium* of the decentralized chain being inferior to the *system-wide optimum* of a centralized chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Must Memorize):**
> In the decentralized supply chain, each stage makes decisions to maximize its **local profit**, ignoring the impact on other stages. The gap between the total profit achievable by the decentralized chain and the integrated (centralized) chain is called the **"Loss of Coordination"** — and every obstacle to coordination contributes to widening this gap.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **relay race team** where four runners are running the same race but are paid *individually* based on how *fast they finish their own leg*, not how *fast the team finishes*. What happens?

- Runner 1 sprints as hard as possible, exhausting himself, and hands the baton to a tired Runner 2.
- Runner 2, knowing he is already far behind, slows down to conserve energy.
- Runners 3 and 4, seeing the team has no chance of winning, jog casually.

The team loses — not because the runners are slow, but because **their incentives were misaligned**. Each runner was optimizing his own segment, not the team's total time. This is *exactly* what happens in a poorly coordinated supply chain. **Obstacles to coordination are the invisible fences that prevent partners from passing the baton smoothly.**

### 1.3 Why Coordination Fails — High-Level Classification

Supply chain coordination failures can be grouped into **seven primary categories**:

1. **Incentive Obstacles** (misaligned reward structures)
2. **Information Obstacles** (distortion, asymmetry, bullwhip effect)
3. **Pricing Obstacles** (Double Marginalization, Batch Discounts)
4. **Operational Obstacles** (mismatched processes and lead times)
5. **Behavioral & Cultural Obstacles** (trust deficits, risk aversion)
6. **Technical / IT Obstacles** (incompatible ERP systems)
7. **Legal & Regulatory Obstacles** (anti-trust laws, contractual rigidity)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "list obstacles to coordination", a student who merely writes "lack of communication" will receive **partial credit only**. Examiners expect **named, textbook-categorized obstacles** with the *underlying economic or operational reason* for each.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Major Obstacles to Coordination — Structured Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Obstacle 1: Incentive Misalignment (Local vs. Global Optimization)

**The "Why":** Each firm in the SC is a *separate legal entity* with its own P&L statement, CEO, and shareholders. Managers are evaluated and rewarded on **local metrics** (own plant utilization, own inventory turns, own ROI). Therefore, a decision that hurts the *chain* but benefits the *firm* is rationally chosen.

**The "How" (Mechanism):**
- Retailer places an order larger than forecast to earn a quantity discount → manufacturer over-produces → inventory piles up at wholesale.
- Manufacturer postpones a small order to keep its line utilization high → retailer faces stockout.
- Distributor refuses to share warehouse capacity data to protect bargaining position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Optimum ≠ Global Optimum.** This is the *First Fundamental Theorem of Decentralized Supply Chains.*

---

#### 2.1.2 Obstacle 2: Information Distortion — The Bullwhip Effect

**Definition (KTU Standard):** The Bullwhip Effect is the phenomenon whereby *demand variability amplifies* as it moves upstream in the supply chain — from retailer to wholesaler to manufacturer to supplier. Small fluctuations in Point-of-Sale (POS) demand at the retail end become large swings in orders placed by manufacturers.

**The "Why":**
- **Demand signal processing** (improper forecasting using past orders, not POS data)
- **Order batching** (periodic ordering to reduce ordering cost — EOQ logic)
- **Price fluctuations** (forward buying during promotions — *trade promotion gaming*)
- **Rationing gaming** (retailers inflate orders when supply is rationed, hoping to receive larger allocation)

**The "How" — Mathematical Amplification:**
Variance amplification is measured as the **Bullwhip Ratio**:

$$
\text{Bullwhip Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)}
$$

where $Q$ = orders placed by an upstream player, and $D$ = actual end-customer demand. A ratio **greater than 1** indicates distortion.

---

#### 2.1.3 Obstacle 3: Pricing Obstacles — Double Marginalization

**Definition:** When two independent firms (e.g., a manufacturer and a retailer) each apply a *markup* over their marginal cost, the *combined* markup is higher than what a single integrated firm would charge. This causes the final retail price to be inflated, demand to fall, and total channel profit to shrink.

**Why It Is an Obstacle:** Both firms act rationally from their *individual* perspective, yet the *joint* outcome is worse than if they had merged. The retailer is "punished" with low margin if it does not raise price, and the manufacturer is "punished" with low volume if the retailer does.

---

#### 2.1.4 Obstacle 4: Operational Mismatches

- **Differing Lead Times** (retailer replenishes daily, manufacturer has a 4-week lead time)
- **Lot-Size Constraints** (minimum order quantities — MOQs)
- **Incompatible Production Schedules**
- **Geographically Dispersed Facilities** (transportation lag)
- **Lack of Synchronization in New Product Introduction (NPI)**

---

#### 2.1.5 Obstacle 5: Behavioral & Cultural Obstacles

- **Lack of Trust** between partners → unwillingness to share forecasts
- **Opportunism** (hold-up problem in transactions)
- **Organizational Inertia** (resistance to changing existing SOPs)
- **Risk Aversion** of middle managers
- **National/Corporate Cultural Differences** in international SCs

---

#### 2.1.6 Obstacle 6: Technical / IT Obstacles

- **Incompatible ERP Systems** (SAP vs. Oracle vs. legacy systems)
- **Lack of Real-Time Data Sharing** (EDI delays, batch data uploads)
- **Cybersecurity Concerns** preventing cloud-based integration
- **Inconsistent Data Standards** (different SKU coding, units of measure)

---

#### 2.1.7 Obstacle 7: Legal & Regulatory Obstacles

- **Anti-Trust Laws** prevent explicit price-fixing or output-allocation agreements
- **Contractual Rigidity** (long-term fixed-price contracts cannot adapt to demand shocks)
- **Import/Export Regulations** and **Tariff Barriers**
- **Product Liability Laws** causing risk-averse information sharing

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Practical Interpretation | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bullwhip Ratio** | $\text{BWR} = \dfrac{\text{Var}(Q)}{\text{Var}(D)}$ | Amplification of demand variance upstream | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Double Marginalization Price** | $p_{\text{retail}} = \dfrac{w(1+\mu_r)}{1-\mu_r}$ where $w = c(1+\mu_m)$ | Two successive markups inflate retail price | Currency |
| 3 | **Loss of Coordination** | $\Delta\Pi = \Pi_{\text{centralized}} - \Pi_{\text{decentralized}}$ | Profit gap between integrated and non-integrated SC | Currency |
| 4 | **EOQ Ordering Frequency** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Drives order batching → contributes to bullwhip | Units |
| 5 | **Total Channel Profit** | $\Pi_{\text{channel}} = (p - c) \cdot D(p)$ | Joint profit to be maximized in coordination | Currency |
| 6 | **Variance of an AR(1) Forecast** | $\text{Var}(\hat{D}) = \dfrac{\sigma_D^2}{1-\rho^2}$ | Higher autocorrelation $\rho$ increases forecast noise | Units² |
| 7 | **Information Value (Shapley)** | $v(S) - \sum_{i \in S} v(\{i\})$ | Marginal value of sharing data with partner | Currency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Safeguard:** The variance expressions above use $\dfrac{}{}$ and $\vert$ deliberately. The vertical bar $\vert$ is **not** used here because it would break the table — but in plain equation lines, $\text{Var}(Q \vert D)$ is acceptable.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

These obstacles are not just textbook abstractions. In industry:
- **Walmart's Retail Link** system was built specifically to overcome Obstacle 2 and 6 (information + IT).
- **Apple's tightly controlled SC** uses Obstacle 7 (exclusive contracts) and Obstacle 1 (aligned incentives via buy-back) to coordinate with Foxconn.
- **Procter & Gamble's Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** program with Walmart directly attacks Obstacle 2 (bullwhip) by giving P&G ownership of the replenishment decision.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Bullwhip Effect (AR(1) Demand Model)

This is the **most frequently asked analytical question** in KTU exams on this topic. The derivation below is exhaustive — every algebraic step is shown.

**Setup:** Suppose end-customer demand $D_t$ follows a simple first-order autoregressive process:

$$
D_t = d + \rho D_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
$$

where $d$ is a constant drift term, $\rho$ is the autocorrelation coefficient ($0 < \rho < 1$), and $\varepsilon_t$ is i.i.d. white noise with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2$.

The retailer uses **Minimum Mean Squared Error (MMSE) forecasting** and places an order equal to its forecast of mean demand over $L$ periods of lead time (the lead-time demand forecast).

**Step 1: Find the unconditional variance of demand $D_t$.**

Variance is taken on both sides:

$$
\text{Var}(D_t) = \rho^2 \, \text{Var}(D_{t-1}) + \sigma_{\varepsilon}^2
$$

Since the process is stationary, $\text{Var}(D_t) = \text{Var}(D_{t-1}) = \sigma_D^2$. Substituting:

$$
\sigma_D^2 = \rho^2 \, \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_{\varepsilon}^2
$$

Solving for $\sigma_D^2$:

$$
\sigma_D^2 \, (1 - \rho^2) = \sigma_{\varepsilon}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_D^2 = \dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1 - \rho^2}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the lead-time demand forecast variance.**

The retailer must forecast demand over the $L$-period lead time. The lead-time demand is:

$$
D_t^L = D_{t+1} + D_{t+2} + \dots + D_{t+L}
$$

Using the MMSE forecast, the variance of this sum is:

$$
\text{Var}(D_t^L) = L \cdot \sigma_D^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho} \right) - \dfrac{2\rho(1-\rho^L)}{(1-\rho)^2} \, \sigma_D^2
$$

For simplicity (and KTU board-level approximation), when $L$ is small, this reduces to:

$$
\text{Var}(D_t^L) \;\approx\; L \, \sigma_D^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho} \right)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the variance of orders placed by the retailer.**

The retailer uses a base-stock policy: order up to the forecast of lead-time demand. The order quantity $Q_t$ equals the forecast plus a safety stock adjustment. The variance of $Q_t$ is therefore approximately the variance of the lead-time demand forecast:

$$
\text{Var}(Q_t) = \text{Var}(D_t^L) = L \, \sigma_D^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho} \right)
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Bullwhip Ratio.**

$$
\text{BWR} = \dfrac{\text{Var}(Q_t)}{\text{Var}(D_t)} = \dfrac{L \, \sigma_D^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho} \right)}{\dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1 - \rho^2}}
$$

Substitute $\sigma_D^2 = \dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1 - \rho^2}$:

$$
\text{BWR} = \dfrac{L \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1 - \rho^2} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho}}{\dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1 - \rho^2}}
$$

Cancel $\dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1-\rho^2}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
\text{BWR} = L \cdot \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{BWR} = L \cdot \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $\rho = 0$ (no autocorrelation), $\text{BWR} = L$. Variance scales with lead time — no bullwhip.
- If $\rho \to 1$ (highly persistent demand), $\text{BWR} \to \infty$. **Catastrophic bullwhip.**
- Lead time $L$ is a **multiplier** of bullwhip intensity.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Double Marginalization

**Setup:** A manufacturer with marginal cost $c$ sells to a retailer at wholesale price $w$. The retailer then sells to consumers at price $p$. Demand is linear: $D(p) = a - b p$.

**Step 1: Retailer's problem.** The retailer chooses $p$ to maximize its own profit:

$$
\Pi_r = (p - w) \cdot (a - b p)
$$

Taking derivative and setting to zero:

$$
\dfrac{d\Pi_r}{dp} = (a - b p) + (p - w)(-b) = 0
$$

$$
a - b p - b p + b w = 0
$$

$$
a + b w = 2 b p
$$

$$
p^*(w) = \dfrac{a + b w}{2b}
$$

**Step 2: Manufacturer's problem.** The manufacturer anticipates $p^*(w)$ and chooses $w$:

$$
\Pi_m = (w - c) \cdot (a - b p^*) = (w - c) \cdot \left( a - b \cdot \dfrac{a + bw}{2b} \right)
$$

Simplify the demand term:

$$
a - \dfrac{a + bw}{2} = \dfrac{2a - a - bw}{2} = \dfrac{a - bw}{2}
$$

So:

$$
\Pi_m = (w - c) \cdot \dfrac{a - bw}{2}
$$

Taking derivative with respect to $w$:

$$
\dfrac{d\Pi_m}{dw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ (a - bw) + (w - c)(-b) \right] = 0
$$

$$
a - bw - bw + bc = 0
$$

$$
a + bc = 2bw
$$

$$
\boxed{w^* = \dfrac{a + bc}{2b}}
$$

**Step 3: Substituting back.**

$$
p^* = \dfrac{a + b \cdot \dfrac{a+bc}{2b}}{2b} = \dfrac{a + \dfrac{a+bc}{2}}{2b} = \dfrac{\dfrac{2a + a + bc}{2}}{2b} = \dfrac{3a + bc}{4b}
$$

**Step 4: Compare to centralized optimum.**

Under centralization, a single decision-maker chooses $p$ to maximize $(p - c)(a - bp)$:

$$
p_{\text{cent}} = \dfrac{a + bc}{2b}
$$

Comparing:

$$
p^* = \dfrac{3a + bc}{4b} \; > \; p_{\text{cent}} = \dfrac{a + bc}{2b} = \dfrac{2a + 2bc}{4b}
$$

Because $3a + bc > 2a + 2bc \iff a > bc$, which is true whenever demand is positive. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{p^* > p_{\text{cent}}}
$$

The decentralized price is **strictly higher** than the centralized price, reducing demand and total channel profit. This is the **double marginalization obstacle**.

---

### 3.3 Loss-of-Coordination Quick-Check Matrix

| Decision Variable | Local Optimum (Decentralized) | Global Optimum (Centralized) | Gap Source | Recommended Coordination Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Order Quantity (EOQ) | $\sqrt{2DS/H}$ (per stage) | Joint lot sizing | Ordering cost vs. holding cost misaligned | **Quantity Discounts, VMI** |
| Retail Price | $p^* = (3a+bc)/4b$ | $(a+bc)/2b$ | Successive markups | **Two-Part Tariff, Resale Price Maintenance** |
| Safety Stock | Per-stage $z\sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_D^2}$ | Chain-wide pooling | Risk-pooling ignored | **Information Sharing, CPFR** |
| Production Quantity | Per-firm EOQ | Aggregate planning | Forecast distortion | **Vendor Managed Inventory** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Seven Obstacles — Causal Map

The following Mermaid block maps the **causal structure** of how each obstacle leads to a loss of coordination in the supply chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Chain Decentralization] --> B1[Obstacle 1 Incentive Misalignment]
    A --> B2[Obstacle 2 Information Distortion]
    A --> B3[Obstacle 3 Pricing Obstacles]
    A --> B4[Obstacle 4 Operational Mismatch]
    A --> B5[Obstacle 5 Behavioral Cultural]
    A --> B6[Obstacle 6 IT Technical]
    A --> B7[Obstacle 7 Legal Regulatory]

    B1 --> C1[Local Optimization Dominates]
    B2 --> C2[Bullwhip Effect Variance Amplification]
    B3 --> C3[Double Marginalization Inflated Prices]
    B4 --> C4[Lead Time Mismatch Stockouts Excess]
    B5 --> C5[Trust Deficit Reduced Collaboration]
    B6 --> C6[Data Silos Inaccurate Forecasts]
    B7 --> C7[Rigid Contracts Anti Trust Limits]

    C1 --> D[Suboptimal Channel Performance]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D

    D --> E[Loss of Coordination Profit Gap]
    E --> F[Coordination Mechanisms Required]
    F --> G1[Incentive Alignment Contracts]
    F --> G2[Information Sharing CPFR]
    F --> G3[Pricing Coordination Two Part Tariff]
    F --> G4[Process Synchronization VMI]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart shows the seven obstacles as parallel root causes converging into a single outcome: *Loss of Coordination*. Each obstacle maps to a corresponding coordination mechanism (right branch) used in industry to counter it.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The Bullwhip Amplification Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    consumer[End Consumer Demand D]
    consumer -->|Small Fluctuation sigma_D| retailer[Retailer]
    retailer -->|Order Variance sigma_Q_r| wholesaler[Wholesaler]
    wholesaler -->|Order Variance sigma_Q_w| manufacturer[Manufacturer]
    manufacturer -->|Order Variance sigma_Q_m| supplier[Component Supplier]

    retailer -.->|Forecast Update +1| retailer
    wholesaler -.->|Forecast Update +2| wholesaler
    manufacturer -.->|Forecast Update +3| manufacturer
    supplier -.->|Forecast Update +4| supplier

    note1[Note Variance Amplifies Upstream]:::warn
    retailer -.- note1
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Double Marginalization — Sequential Markup Distortion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Marginal Cost c] -->|Markup mu_m| W[Wholesale Price w]
    W -->|Markup mu_r| P[Retail Price p]
    P --> D[Demand Falls]
    D --> LP[Loss of Channel Profit]

    alt[Centralized Firm would set]:::opt
    C -->|Single Markup mu_star| P2[Retail Price p_cent]
    P2 --> D2[Higher Demand]
    D2 --> HP[Higher Channel Profit]
    end

    classDef warn fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#008000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Bullwhip Effect" in the context of supply chain coordination. List any four root causes of the bullwhip effect.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bullwhip Effect** is a phenomenon in which the *variability of orders* placed by upstream supply chain members is *significantly greater* than the variability of actual end-customer demand. Small fluctuations in point-of-sale demand become progressively amplified as one moves upstream from retailer → wholesaler → manufacturer → supplier.

**Four root causes:**
1. **Demand forecast updating** — each stage uses past orders (not POS data) for forecasting, amplifying noise.
2. **Order batching** — periodic ordering to minimize transaction costs (EOQ-driven).
3. **Price fluctuations / trade promotions** — forward buying and gaming of discounts.
4. **Shortage gaming / rationing** — retailers inflate orders when supply is rationed.

*(Award 1 mark for definition; 0.5 each for 4 causes.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is "Double Marginalization"? Why is it considered an obstacle to supply chain coordination?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Double Marginalization** occurs when two successive firms in a supply chain (e.g., manufacturer and retailer) each apply a positive markup over their marginal cost. The combined markup inflates the final retail price *above* the level a centralized firm would set, thereby reducing total demand and total channel profit.

**Why it is an obstacle:** Although each firm acts *individually rationally* (maximizing its own profit), the *joint* outcome is *worse* than the centralized optimum. This is a classic case of **local optimization defeating global optimization**, a structural obstacle to coordination that cannot be resolved without an explicit coordination mechanism (e.g., two-part tariff, resale price maintenance, or vertical integration).

*(Award 1.5 marks for definition; 1.5 marks for the obstacle explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Choice)

> **Note for Students:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B Module-2 questions offer an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Both are given below.

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the major obstacles to coordination in a supply chain. Discuss how each obstacle prevents the supply chain from achieving its global optimum.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the Bullwhip Ratio for a supply chain where end-customer demand follows a first-order autoregressive (AR(1)) process. Show all steps of the derivation clearly.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Obstacles to Coordination — Detailed Explanation (7 marks)**

Coordination failures in a supply chain arise from the following obstacles:

1. **Incentive Misalignment (1 mark):** Each firm optimizes its *own* profit (local optimum). The retailer's bonus is tied to *its* sales; the manufacturer's bonus is tied to *its* plant utilization. Decisions that increase channel profit but reduce local profit are *rejected*.
