The resulting Nash Equilibrium of the decentralized chain is *strictly inferior* to the system optimum — a gap called **Loss of Coordination** $\Delta\Pi = \Pi_{\text{cent}} - \Pi_{\text{decent}}$.

2. **Information Obstacles (1 mark):** Distortion of demand signals (Bullwhip Effect) means upstream stages see *amplified* variance and make *exaggerated* capacity/inventory decisions, causing stockouts and obsolete inventory.

3. **Pricing Obstacles (1 mark):** Double marginalization, lack of cooperative pricing, and trade-promotion gaming all inflate prices or distort order patterns.

4. **Operational Mismatches (1 mark):** Different lead times, MOQs, and production cycles cause desynchronization — a small delay at one stage cascades into bullwhip at the next.

5. **Behavioral & Cultural Obstacles (1 mark):** Trust deficits, opportunism, and risk aversion of middle managers cause partners to hoard data and over-protect their own turf.

6. **Technical / IT Obstacles (1 mark):** Incompatible ERP systems and absence of real-time data sharing prevent timely coordination.

7. **Legal & Regulatory Obstacles (1 mark):** Anti-trust laws prevent explicit coordination agreements; contractual rigidity prevents adaptive responses.

The combined effect of all seven is a **profit gap** between the decentralized and centralized chain, which is the defining symptom of a lack of coordination.

---

**(b) Derivation of Bullwhip Ratio (7 marks)**

Assume end-customer demand follows an AR(1) process:

$$
D_t = d + \rho D_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
$$

where $\rho \in (0,1)$ and $\varepsilon_t$ is i.i.d. with variance $\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute unconditional variance.

$$
\sigma_D^2 = \rho^2 \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_{\varepsilon}^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\sigma_D^2 = \dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1 - \rho^2}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Lead-time demand variance for an $L$-period replenishment lead time:

$$
\text{Var}(D_t^L) = L \, \sigma_D^2 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Under a base-stock / order-up-to policy, the order variance equals the lead-time forecast variance:

$$
\text{Var}(Q_t) = L \, \sigma_D^2 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Bullwhip Ratio:

$$
\text{BWR} = \dfrac{\text{Var}(Q_t)}{\text{Var}(D_t)} = \dfrac{L \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1-\rho^2} \cdot \dfrac{1+\rho}{1-\rho}}{\dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1-\rho^2}}
$$

Cancelling $\dfrac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2}{1-\rho^2}$:

$$
\boxed{\text{BWR} = L \cdot \dfrac{1 + \rho}{1 - \rho}}
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** **Interpretation.** The Bullwhip Ratio is amplified by (i) the lead time $L$ (longer replenishment cycles increase distortion) and (ii) the autocorrelation $\rho$ (more persistent demand signals cause worse amplification). When $\rho \to 1$, BWR $\to \infty$, indicating severe bullwhip. This result *quantifies* the information obstacle.

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative]**

**(a)** *With the help of a suitable model, explain the phenomenon of Double Marginalization in a two-echelon supply chain. Show that the decentralized retail price exceeds the centralized retail price.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the role of information sharing, incentive alignment, and operational synchronization as the three pillars of supply chain coordination. For each pillar, give one real-world industry example.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Double Marginalization (7 marks)**

Consider a manufacturer (marginal cost $c$) selling through a retailer. Demand: $D(p) = a - bp$.

**Retailer's problem (2 marks):**

$$
\Pi_r = (p - w)(a - bp) \;\Rightarrow\; p^*(w) = \dfrac{a + bw}{2b}
$$

**Manufacturer's problem (2 marks):** Substituting $p^*(w)$:

$$
\Pi_m = (w - c) \cdot \dfrac{a - bw}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; w^* = \dfrac{a + bc}{2b}
$$

**Decentralized retail price (1 mark):**

$$
p^* = \dfrac{3a + bc}{4b}
$$

**Centralized retail price (1 mark):** Solving $\max (p - c)(a - bp)$:

$$
p_{\text{cent}} = \dfrac{a + bc}{2b}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):** Since $a > bc$ for positive demand, $3a + bc > 2(a + bc)$, hence:

$$
p^* = \dfrac{3a + bc}{4b} \;>\; \dfrac{a + bc}{2b} = p_{\text{cent}}
$$

The decentralized price strictly exceeds the centralized price, proving double marginalization. The remedy is a *two-part tariff* $(w, F)$ such that the joint profit is split, e.g., $w = c$ and $F = \Pi_{\text{channel}} - 0$.

---

**(b) Three Pillars of Coordination (7 marks)**

**Pillar 1: Information Sharing (2.5 marks).** Real-time sharing of POS data, forecasts, and inventory levels among partners. Reduces bullwhip and improves forecast accuracy. **Industry Example:** *Walmart's Retail Link* with P&G (and later 16,000+ suppliers) allows vendors to see POS data and replenish directly.

**Pillar 2: Incentive Alignment (2.5 marks).** Contracts that make the profit of one party depend on the profit of another (e.g., buy-back, revenue sharing, quantity discounts). Aligns local decisions with global welfare. **Industry Example:** *Apple's buy-back and component commitments* with Foxconn ensure that Foxconn's investment in capacity for iPhones is rewarded even if demand fluctuates.

**Pillar 3: Operational Synchronization (2 marks).** Matching lead times, production cycles, and replenishment frequencies. **Industry Example:** *Dell-Taiwan Semiconductor (TSMC)* — TSMC's wafer production is synchronized to Dell's daily order stream through integrated IT, allowing 7-day order-to-delivery cycles.

These three pillars jointly close the **loss-of-coordination gap** by attacking the root obstacles (information, incentive, operational).

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Before You Write the Exam!):**
>
> 1. **"List obstacles" trap:** Writing *only* "lack of communication" or "no trust" will fetch **at most 1 out of 7 marks**. Examiners expect the **seven categorized obstacles** with the *mechanism* of each (i.e., *why* it blocks coordination), not just the label.
>
> 2. **Bullwhip derivation trap:** Students often **skip the AR(1) variance step** ($\sigma_D^2 = \sigma_{\varepsilon}^2 / (1-\rho^2)$) and directly write $\text{BWR} = L(1+\rho)/(1-\rho)$. Examiners will not award full marks — *always show the variance derivation explicitly*.
>
> 3. **Double marginalization trap:** A common mistake is to claim the manufacturer *also* sells at retail. No — in a two-echelon chain, the manufacturer sells *wholesale only*. The retailer is the one setting $p$. Mis-attributing the markup loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Formula formatting trap:** In the answer sheet, avoid `|x|` for absolute value — use proper notation or write *"the absolute value of x"*. Same for the Bullwhip Ratio — write it as a *fraction*, not a vertical stack.
>
> 5. **Centrally- vs. Decentralized-Price sign error:** When comparing $p^*$ and $p_{\text{cent}}$, students frequently flip the inequality. **Memory aid:** Decentralization = *double markup* = *higher* price. Always.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Obstacles to Coordination"**

- **The Seven Obstacles (memorize verbatim):**
  1. Incentive Misalignment
  2. Information Distortion (Bullwhip Effect)
  3. Pricing Obstacles (Double Marginalization)
  4. Operational Mismatches
  5. Behavioral & Cultural Obstacles
  6. Technical / IT Obstacles
  7. Legal & Regulatory Obstacles

- **Core Numerical Result (Bullwhip):**
  $\;\text{BWR} = L \cdot \dfrac{1+\rho}{1-\rho}\;$ — amplification grows with lead time and demand autocorrelation.

- **Core Numerical Result (Double Marginalization):**
  $\;p_{\text{decent}} = \dfrac{3a+bc}{4b} \;>\; p_{\text{cent}} = \dfrac{a+bc}{2b}\;$

- **Definition of Loss of Coordination:**
  $\;\Delta\Pi = \Pi_{\text{centralized}} - \Pi_{\text{decentralized}}\;$ — the **profit gap** that coordination aims to close.

- **Three Pillars of Coordination (Question B favorite):**
  Information Sharing + Incentive Alignment + Operational Synchronization.

- **Real-world examples to quote (examiners love them):**
  - Walmart + P&G → Information Sharing
  - Apple + Foxconn → Incentive Alignment via buy-back
  - Dell + TSMC → Operational Synchronization

- **One-liner rule:** *Decentralized supply chains are Nash-Equilibrium sub-optimal.* Coordination is the act of moving the system *from* this sub-optimal Nash point *toward* the centralized global optimum via contracts, information, and process alignment.

- **High-yield keywords for 14-mark answers:** *Nash Equilibrium, Global Optimum, Variance Amplification, Autoregressive AR(1), Successive Markups, Loss of Coordination, Three Pillars, Two-Part Tariff, CPFR, VMI.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managerial Levers to Improve Coordination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Managerial Levers to Improve Coordination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supply Chain Coordination** is the process of integrating and managing the interactions between successive stages of a supply chain to ensure that all parties involved work together in a synchronized, efficient, and mutually beneficial manner. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *Supply Chain and Logistics Management*, **Managerial Levers** are the set of strategic, tactical, and operational actions that supply chain managers can deliberately deploy to align the objectives, decisions, and information flows of independent entities (suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers) so as to eliminate or reduce the **bullwhip effect**, **double marginalization**, **local optimization**, and **misaligned incentives** that degrade total supply chain profit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT867 – Module 2):**
> Managerial levers are the counter-measures to the *obstacles to coordination*. The KTU Board expects students to map each obstacle to its corresponding lever. Memorizing this mapping is worth **direct 3-mark questions** in the ESE.

Mathematically, the degree of coordination can be expressed as:

$$
C_{sc} = \frac{\Pi_{integrated} - \Pi_{decentralized}}{\Pi_{integrated}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{sc}$ = **Coordination Index** of the supply chain (0 = no coordination, 1 = perfect coordination)
- $\Pi_{integrated}$ = **Profit** when the entire chain is owned by a single decision-maker
- $\Pi_{decentralized}$ = **Profit** when each stage optimizes only its own local cost/profit

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Orchestra Without a Conductor

Imagine a group of brilliant musicians, each the best violinist, flutist, and percussionist in the city, all sitting together to perform a symphony. Each musician is an **independent stage** of the orchestra (your supplier, manufacturer, distributor). Every individual player is technically perfect — but without a **conductor** (the supply chain manager using *managerial levers*) waving the baton, the music descends into chaos. Each player plays *louder, faster, and on their own tempo* trying to "win" their personal sound.

> The **managerial levers** are the conductor's baton, sheet-music, tuning instructions, and the rulebook that turns chaos into harmony. They do not change *who* the players are — they change *how* they coordinate.

A more relatable real-world example: **Zara's fast-fashion supply chain.** Zara shares Point-of-Sale (POS) data *daily* with its fabric suppliers (an *information lever*), uses *aligned incentives* (penalty for late delivery, bonus for quick response), and employs a *centralized organizational structure*. The result: lead times of 2–3 weeks versus the industry standard of 6 months.

## 1.3 Why Coordination Fails — The Five Obstacle Classes

Before discussing levers, the KTU 2024 syllabus insists on understanding the **obstacles** that the levers are designed to overcome:

| # | Obstacle Class | Root Cause | Real-World Symptom |
|---|----------------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Incentive Obstacle** | Each stage maximizes *its own* profit | Retailer orders more than needed to bulk-discount |
| 2 | **Information Processing Obstacle** | Information is distorted as it moves upstream | Bullwhip effect (variance amplification) |
| 3 | **Operational Obstacle** | Actions that improve one stage hurt another | Excess inventory at one stage, stockouts at next |
| 4 | **Pricing Obstacle** | Pricing structure rewards bad behaviour | Quantity discounts encouraging forward buying |
| 5 | **Behavioural Obstacle** | Local optimization & functional silos | Sales team over-promises dates, manufacturing misses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition — "Lever":**
> A *lever* is a managerial action whose *primary purpose* is to overcome one or more of the above obstacles. A lever is not a logistics activity (truck loading, warehousing) — it is a **coordination mechanism**.

## 1.4 The Managerial Levers — The Big Picture

The KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum (aligned with Chopra & Meindl) recognizes **seven principal managerial levers**:

1. **Information Sharing**
2. **Information Technology (IT) Enablement**
3. **Organizational Structure Alignment**
4. **Aligned Incentives (Incentive Alignment)**
5. **Pricing & Efficient Consumer Response (ECR)**
6. **Alliances / Long-Term Partnerships**
7. **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting & Replenishment (CPFR) / Joint Decision-Making**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:**
> When asked "*List the managerial levers*" — list **all seven** in any order. When asked "*How does lever X overcome obstacle Y?*" — answer in the *Obstacle → Lever* mapping format. This is the single most tested pattern in PESFT867 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why → How" Framework for Each Lever

### Lever 1 — Information Sharing

- **Why it works:** Information asymmetry is the *root cause* of the bullwhip effect. When downstream demand data is shared upstream, suppliers can plan with the *true* customer demand signal rather than a distorted retailer's order.
- **How it works:**
  - Share **Point-of-Sale (POS)** data, inventory levels, promotion calendars.
  - **Early warning** of stockouts, recalls, demand surges.
  - Reduces **Information Processing Obstacle** by 60–80% in case studies.
- **Real-world utility:** Walmart's Retail Link system gives Procter & Gamble real-time access to every Walmart register's sales — P&G's replenishment decisions now match *actual* demand, not distorted orders.

### Lever 2 — Information Technology (IT)

- **Why it works:** IT is the *infrastructure* that makes Lever 1 scalable, fast, and cheap.
- **How it works:**
  - **EDI (Electronic Data Interchange):** Standardized B2B document exchange.
  - **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning):** Integrates internal functions.
  - **SCM Software (SAP, Oracle, Kinaxis):** End-to-end visibility.
  - **Blockchain & IoT sensors:** Tamper-proof, real-time tracking.
- **Real-world utility:** Maersk's *TradeLens* (blockchain) reduced document-processing time for a single shipment from **5 days to under 1 hour**.

### Lever 3 — Organizational Structure Alignment

- **Why it works:** If buyer and seller have *cross-functional* contacts (not just sales-to-sales), coordination quality rises.
- **How it works:**
  - Create **cross-company teams** (e.g., Toyota-GM NUMMI joint teams).
  - Restructure internally around **processes, not functions**.
  - **Co-location** of supplier engineers at buyer's site.
- **Real-world utility:** Boeing embeds supplier engineers in *its* design centers for the 787 — coordination is structural, not contractual.

### Lever 4 — Aligned Incentives

- **Why it works:** Incentives are the *real* drivers of behaviour. If a supplier is paid by *unit volume*, the supplier will *push* volume regardless of true demand.
- **How it works:**
  - Switch to **performance-based contracts** (on-time delivery %, quality %, fill rate).
  - Use **Buyback contracts**, **Revenue-sharing contracts**, **Quantity-flexibility contracts**.
  - Pay for **service level**, not for *units shipped*.
- **Real-world utility:** Cisco rewards contract manufacturers (Flex, Jabil) on **yield and cycle time**, not piece-count. Result: faster ramp, lower scrap.

### Lever 5 — Pricing & Efficient Consumer Response (ECR)

- **Why it works:** Misaligned pricing (e.g., deep quantity discounts) is the single largest *operational* driver of forward buying and the bullwhip effect.
- **How it works:**
  - Replace **quantity discounts** with **every-day-low-pricing (EDLP)**.
  - Use **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** — supplier sets retail replenishment.
  - Apply **Category Management** — jointly plan SKU assortments.
- **Real-world utility:** P&G's "Power Pricing" initiative with Walmart eliminated forward buying and saved the pair **hundreds of millions of USD** in the late 1990s.

### Lever 6 — Alliances / Long-Term Partnerships

- **Why it works:** Repeated-game behaviour encourages *cooperation* over *defection*. Trust reduces contract-monitoring cost.
- **How it works:**
  - Sign **multi-year contracts** with clear SLAs.
  - Pool **forecasting** and **capacity planning**.
  - Share **R&D and innovation pipelines**.
- **Real-world utility:** Toyota's keiretsu network with Denso, Aisin, etc. — partners co-design cars from day zero.

### Lever 7 — CPFR & Joint Decision-Making

- **Why it works:** Joint planning synchronizes *forecasts, replenishment, and promotions* across the chain.
- **How it works:**
  - **Collaborative Forecasting** — joint demand planning.
  - **Joint Replenishment** — coordinated order triggers.
  - **Joint Promotions** — synchronized marketing calendars.
  - **Exception Management** — shared alerts on forecast deviations.
- **Real-world utility:** Walmart & P&G's CPFR pilot in 1995 cut stockouts from 11% to <2% and reduced inventory by 30%.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Table for PESFT867 ESE — Memorize These Equations:**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Coordination Index | $C_{sc} = \dfrac{\Pi_{int} - \Pi_{dec}}{\Pi_{int}}$ | Dimensionless; 0 → 1 | Measures how close decentralized chain is to integrated optimum |
| 2 | Bullwhip Effect Ratio | $BW = \dfrac{Var(Q_{order}) / E(Q_{order})}{Var(D) / E(D)}$ | Ratio ≥ 1 | Demand variance vs. order variance |
| 3 | Newsvendor Order Quantity | $Q^* = F^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c_u}{c_u + c_o}\right)$ | $c_u$ underage, $c_o$ overage cost (₹/unit) | Single-period inventory; basis of incentive design |
| 4 | Buyback Contract Parameter | $w_b = w - b$ | $w$ wholesale, $b$ buyback price (₹) | Manufacturer pays retailer $b$ per unsold unit |
| 5 | Revenue-Sharing Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{\text{Revenue to Retailer}}{\text{Total Revenue}}$ | $0 < \phi < 1$ | Retailer keeps fraction $\phi$, supplier keeps $1-\phi$ |
| 6 | Double Marginalization Loss | $DML = \Pi_{int} - \Pi_{stack}$ | Profit units (₹) | Loss from each stage adding its own markup |
| 7 | Information Sharing Value | $V_{IS} = \Pi_{sc}^{shared} - \Pi_{sc}^{no\ share}$ | Profit units (₹) | Marginal value of sharing demand data |
| 8 | Lead-Time Variance Reduction | $\sigma_{LT}^{new} = \sigma_{LT}^{old} \cdot (1 - r)$ | $r \in [0,1]$ reduction factor | IT lever impact on demand forecast error |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipeline Safeguard:** All table entries use `\dfrac` and `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe character `|`. The KTU answer-sheet template uses standard markdown renderers that *break* on raw pipes inside math. Using `\dfrac` and `\vert` guarantees your answer renders correctly in any KTU digital portal.

## 2.3 Obstacle → Lever Mapping Matrix (Essential for KTU)

| Obstacle ↓ \ Lever → | Info Share | IT | Org. Structure | Incentives | Pricing/ECR | Alliances | CPFR |
|----------------------|:----------:|:--:|:--------------:|:----------:|:-----------:|:---------:|:----:|
| Incentive | ○ | ○ | ● | ●●● | ●● | ●● | ● |
| Information Processing | ●●● | ●●● | ○ | ○ | ○ | ● | ●●● |
| Operational | ● | ●● | ●● | ●● | ●●● | ●● | ●● |
| Pricing | ○ | ● | ○ | ●● | ●●● | ● | ●● |
| Behavioural | ● | ○ | ●●● | ●● | ○ | ●●● | ●● |

*(Legend: ●●● = Primary lever, ●● = Strong secondary, ● = Supporting, ○ = Limited effect)*

This matrix is the **most-tested artefact** in PESFT867 — the KTU Board uses it in ESE Part B questions worth 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Coordination Index $C_{sc}$

**Step 1 — Define the integrated supply chain profit.**

Assume a single-decision-maker controls a two-stage chain: a manufacturer (Stage 1) supplying a retailer (Stage 2). The integrated profit is:

$$
\Pi_{int} = (p - c_m - c_r) \cdot D(p)
$$

Where:
- $p$ = retail price (₹/unit)
- $c_m$ = manufacturer marginal cost (₹/unit)
- $c_r$ = retailer marginal cost (₹/unit)
- $D(p)$ = market demand function (units)

**Step 2 — Define the decentralized (stacked) profit.**

Each stage independently adds its own markup. Manufacturer charges retailer $w$ per unit; retailer sells at $p$.

$$
\Pi_{mfr} = (w - c_m) \cdot D(p)
$$

$$
\Pi_{ret} = (p - w - c_r) \cdot D(p)
$$

**Step 3 — Sum to get decentralized chain profit.**

$$
\Pi_{dec} = \Pi_{mfr} + \Pi_{ret} = (p - c_m - c_r) \cdot D(p)
$$

Wait — observe that *if both stages are at optimal $w$ and $p$* the sum is *identical* to $\Pi_{int}$. The real loss appears because each stage chooses its own optimum *independently* — the manufacturer's optimal $w^*$ is *higher* than the chain-optimal $w^{opt}$.

**Step 4 — Solve for the chain-optimal wholesale price.**

The chain maximizes $\Pi_{int}$ w.r.t. $p$ only. The manufacturer and retailer, however, maximize *sequentially* using Stackelberg (leader-follower) game theory.

**Step 5 — Compute the Coordination Index.**

$$
C_{sc} = \frac{\Pi_{int} - \Pi_{dec}^{*}}{\Pi_{int}}
$$

Where $\Pi_{dec}^{*}$ is the *decentralized equilibrium* (Stackelberg) profit, not the arithmetic sum. This is the formal definition of the **gap created by double marginalization**.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Double Marginalization with Numbers

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
> A retailer faces market demand $D(p) = 1000 - 5p$. Manufacturer's cost $c_m = 20$ ₹/unit. Retailer's cost $c_r = 10$ ₹/unit.
> **(a)** Compute the integrated optimal price and profit.
> **(b)** Compute the decentralized equilibrium and the resulting Coordination Index.
> **(c)** Suggest a contract that *perfectly coordinates* the chain.

### Part (a) — Integrated Chain

$$
\Pi_{int}(p) = (p - 20 - 10)(1000 - 5p) = (p - 30)(1000 - 5p)
$$

$$
\Pi_{int}(p) = 1000p - 5p^2 - 30000 + 150p = -5p^2 + 1150p - 30000
$$

Take derivative and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d\Pi_{int}}{dp} = -10p + 1150 = 0
$$

$$
p^{opt} = 115 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

Corresponding demand:

$$
D^{opt} = 1000 - 5(115) = 425 \text{ units}
$$

Integrated profit:

$$
\Pi_{int}^{*} = (115 - 30)(425) = 85 \times 425 = 36125 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Part (b) — Decentralized Equilibrium

**Retailer's problem** (given wholesale price $w$): choose $p$ to maximize $(p - w - 10)(1000 - 5p)$.

First-order condition:

$$
\frac{d\Pi_{ret}}{dp} = 1000 - 5p - 5(p - w - 10) = 0
$$

$$
1000 - 5p - 5p + 5w + 50 = 0
$$

$$
1050 + 5w = 10p
$$

$$
p^*(w) = 105 + \frac{w}{2}
$$

**Manufacturer's problem**: substitute retailer's reaction into manufacturer's profit.

$$
\Pi_{mfr}(w) = (w - 20) \cdot D(p^*) = (w - 20)\left[1000 - 5\left(105 + \frac{w}{2}\right)\right]
$$

$$
= (w - 20)\left[1000 - 525 - \frac{5w}{2}\right] = (w - 20)\left(475 - \frac{5w}{2}\right)
$$

Take derivative w.r.t. $w$:

$$
\frac{d\Pi_{mfr}}{dw} = \left(475 - \frac{5w}{2}\right) + (w - 20)\left(-\frac{5}{2}\right) = 0
$$

$$
475 - \frac{5w}{2} - \frac{5w}{2} + 50 = 0
$$

$$
525 - 5w = 0
$$

$$
w^{Stackelberg} = 105 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
p^* = 105 + \frac{105}{2} = 157.5 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

$$
D^* = 1000 - 5(157.5) = 212.5 \text{ units}
$$

Decentralized profits:

$$
\Pi_{mfr} = (105 - 20)(212.5) = 85 \times 212.5 = 18062.5 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\Pi_{ret} = (157.5 - 105 - 10)(212.5) = 42.5 \times 212.5 = 9031.25 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\Pi_{dec} = 18062.5 + 9031.25 = 27093.75 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Coordination Index:**

$$
C_{sc} = \frac{36125 - 27093.75}{36125} = \frac{9031.25}{36125} = 0.25
$$

**Interpretation:** 25% of the chain's potential profit is **destroyed** by lack of coordination.

### Part (c) — A Coordinating Contract

A **two-part tariff** with $w = c_m = 20$ and franchise fee $F = 18062.5$ ₹ (paid retailer → manufacturer) makes the retailer indifferent between behaving locally and behaving as a chain-agent, achieving $\Pi_{ret} = 9031.25 - 18062.5 = ...$ — but in practice, the fee is set so the retailer earns at least its decentralized profit while the manufacturer captures the rest. This is the classic **wholesale-price-only vs. two-part tariff** lever.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Levers vs. Cost of Implementation

| Lever | Coordination Strength | Implementation Cost | Time to Deploy | Best For |
|-------|:---------------------:|:-------------------:|:--------------:|----------|
| Information Sharing | Medium-High | Low–Medium | Short | Bullwhip, demand variability |
| IT Enablement | High (enabler) | High | Medium–Long | Scaling Lever 1 |
| Org. Structure | Medium | Medium | Medium | Cultural change |
| Aligned Incentives | Very High | Low | Short | Mis-aligned KPIs |
| Pricing / ECR | Very High | Low | Short | Forward buying, SKU proliferation |
| Alliances | High | Medium | Long | Strategic coordination |
| CPFR / Joint Decisions | Very High | High | Medium–Long | Forecast & replenishment sync |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Heuristic:**
> When asked to *recommend* a lever in a case study, the Board expects you to (1) identify the obstacle, (2) cross-reference with the mapping matrix in §2.3, (3) justify using the **cost–benefit trade-off** of the comparison table above. Skipping any step loses 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Master Coordination Framework — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **Obstacle → Lever → Outcome** architecture of coordination. It is the canonical schematic for KTU Module 2 and is worth 5 marks in any descriptive question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Chain Stages: Supplier, Manufacturer, Distributor, Retailer, Customer] --> B{Independent Local Optimization}
    B --> C1[Incentive Obstacle]
    B --> C2[Information Processing Obstacle]
    B --> C3[Operational Obstacle]
    B --> C4[Pricing Obstacle]
    B --> C5[Behavioural Obstacle]
    C1 --> D1[Double Marginalization]
    C2 --> D2[Bullwhip Effect]
    C3 --> D3[Inventory Misalignment]
    C4 --> D4[Forward Buying]
    C5 --> D5[Functional Silos]
    D1 --> E[Reduced Total Chain Profit]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Managerial Levers Activated]
    F --> F1[Information Sharing]
    F --> F2[Information Technology]
    F --> F3[Organizational Structure]
    F --> F4[Aligned Incentives]
    F --> F5[Pricing and ECR]
    F --> F6[Alliances and Partnerships]
    F --> F7[CPFR and Joint Decisions]
    F1 --> G[Restored Coordination]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G
    G --> H[Maximum Integrated Chain Profit]
    H --> I[Improved Service Level, Lower Cost, Higher Resilience]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Lever — Cross-Functional View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Information Sharing Lever]
        IS1[POS Data]
        IS2[Inventory Levels]
        IS3[Promotion Calendar]
        IS1 --> ISX[Shared Demand Signal]
        IS2 --> ISX
        IS3 --> ISX
        ISX --> ISY[Reduced Bullwhip]
    end
    subgraph L2[Pricing and ECR Lever]
        PR1[Quantity Discounts]
        PR2[EDLP]
        PR3[VMI]
        PR1 --> PRX[Pricing Models]
        PR2 --> PRX
        PR3 --> PRX
        PRX --> PRY[Eliminated Forward Buying]
    end
    subgraph L3[Aligned Incentives Lever]
        AI1[Unit Volume Based]
        AI2[Service Level Based]
        AI3[Buyback Contracts]
        AI1 --> AIX[Contract Type]
        AI2 --> AIX
        AI3 --> AIX
        AIX --> AIY[Aligned KPIs]
    end
    ISY --> OUT[Chain-Wide Profit Maximization]
    PRY --> OUT
    AIY --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Coordination Maturity Ladder (Stage-Wise View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: No Coordination - Each Stage Independent] --> S2[Stage 2: Information Sharing - Basic Data Exchange]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Process Alignment - Joint SOPs]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Incentive and Contract Alignment - Two Part Tariff, VMI]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Strategic Alliance - Co Design, CPFR, Risk Sharing]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Full Integration - Virtual or Real Vertical Integration]
    S6 -.feedback.-> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Viva:**
> The maturity ladder above is the *Chopra-Meindl 5-stage model* (with an added Stage 6 for full M&A integration). The KTU Board commonly asks: "*A firm is at Stage 2 — recommend the next two levers to climb to Stage 4.*" — The textbook answer is **Pricing/ECR → Aligned Incentives**.

## 4.4 The Information Sharing Causality Loop (Bullwhip Dissection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CD[True Customer Demand - Low Variance] --> RD[Retailer Orders - Medium Variance]
    RD --> WD[Wholesaler Orders - High Variance]
    WD --> MD[Manufacturer Orders - Very High Variance]
    MD --> P1[Excess Production]
    MD --> P2[Capacity Stress]
    P1 --> L1[Inventory Bloat]
    P2 --> L2[Service Failure]
    L1 --> CB[Cost of Coordination Failure]
    L2 --> CB
    CD -. POS data shared upstream .-> MD
    MD -. Dampened orders .-> L1
    MD -. Planned capacity .-> L2
    CB --> LEV[Activate Information Sharing Lever]
    LEV -.-> CD
```

This loop is the *bullwhip effect* — the information processing obstacle visualized. Note the dashed lines showing how the **Information Sharing Lever** short-circuits the amplification chain by feeding the true demand signal directly to the manufacturer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for PESFT867:**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark questions (short, direct, recall-based).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with internal choice (a 7 + b 7).
> - **Module Choice Restriction:** The paper setter gives you a choice *within* Module 2, not across modules.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define "Supply Chain Coordination". List any three obstacles to coordination.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Supply Chain Coordination is the process of synchronizing the decisions and actions of all stages of a supply chain (supplier, manufacturer, distributor, retailer) so as to maximize total chain profit rather than individual stage profit. The KTU textbook (Chopra & Meindl framework) defines it as *"the alignment of objectives, decisions, and information flows across supply chain stages."*

**Three Obstacles (any three of the following — full list fetches higher marks):**
1. **Incentive Obstacle** — Each stage maximizes *its own* profit, leading to **double marginalization**.
2. **Information Processing Obstacle** — Distorted demand signals moving upstream cause the **bullwhip effect**.
3. **Operational Obstacle** — Actions optimizing one stage (e.g., lot-sizing) hurt others (e.g., retail stockouts).
4. **Pricing Obstacle** — Mis-aligned pricing structures (deep quantity discounts) encourage **forward buying**.
5. **Behavioural Obstacle** — Functional silos and local optimization bias.

*(Each obstacle: ½ mark for naming + ½ mark for explaining the chain reaction = 1 mark per obstacle. Definition: ½ mark.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between "Information Sharing" and "Information Technology" as managerial levers. Why is IT considered an "enabler" lever?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Information Sharing | Information Technology |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Definition** | The *practice* of exchanging demand, inventory, and forecast data across stages. | The *infrastructure* (software, hardware, networks) that enables the exchange. |
| **Nature** | Behavioural / Process | Technological / Tool |
| **Example** | Sharing POS data with supplier. | Deploying EDI, ERP, or a blockchain ledger. |
| **Independence** | Can occur informally (phone calls, emails). | Can exist without active sharing (idle ERP). |
| **KTU Tag** | *Primary lever* against Information Processing Obstacle. | *Enabler lever* — it amplifies Levers 1, 4, 5, 6, 7. |

**Why IT is an "enabler":**
IT does not, by itself, *change* a partner's behaviour. It only *makes* the other levers (especially Information Sharing) feasible at scale, in real-time, and at low marginal cost. An EDI system is useless if the retailer refuses to share POS data; conversely, a "data-sharing contract" without a secure IT backbone is impractical. Hence KTU categorizes IT as the **enabling infrastructure** for the *active* levers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **Question A (a) [7 Marks]:**
> *"Explain in detail the five obstacles to supply chain coordination. For each obstacle, identify the most appropriate managerial lever to overcome it."*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Incentive Obstacle [1.5 marks]**
- **Cause:** Each supply chain stage independently maximizes its own profit, ignoring the impact on the rest of the chain. This leads to **double marginalization** — the manufacturer sets a high wholesale price, the retailer adds a high retail markup, and the chain sells far less than the joint-optimum quantity.
- **Symptom:** Stockouts at retail even when manufacturer inventory is high; sub-optimal total chain profit.
- **Best Lever:** **Aligned Incentives** (Lever 4) — redesign contracts using buyback, revenue-sharing, or two-part tariffs so both stages optimize *joint* profit.

**2. Information Processing Obstacle [1.5 marks]**
- **Cause:** Forecasts and orders are revised at each stage using local information, amplifying variance upstream — the **bullwhip effect**.
- **Symptom:** Manufacturer's order variance is 4–5× retail demand variance.
- **Best Lever:** **Information Sharing** (Lever 1) supported by **IT** (Lever 2) — share POS data, inventory levels, and forecasts in real time.

**3. Operational Obstacle [1 mark]**
- **Cause:** Cost structures or lot-sizing rules optimized locally (e.g., full-truck-load shipments from manufacturer) cause uneven flow to the next stage.
- **Symptom:** Inventory piling at one stage, stockouts at the next.
- **Best Lever:** **Organizational Structure Alignment** (Lever 3) and **CPFR** (Lever 7) — joint planning of order frequencies, safety stock, and replenishment cycles.

**4. Pricing Obstacle [1 mark]**
- **Cause:** Pricing structures (deep quantity discounts) reward stages to *batch orders*, causing forward buying and end-of-quarter demand spikes.
- **Symptom:** Periodic inventory gluts followed by stockouts.
- **Best Lever:** **Pricing & ECR** (Lever 5) — switch to EDLP, VMI, and category management.

**5. Behavioural Obstacle [1 mark]**
- **Cause:** Functional silos, lack of trust, sales-vs-operations friction.
- **Symptom:** Local optimization, finger-pointing, contract renegotiation churn.
- **Best Lever:** **Alliances / Long-Term Partnerships** (Lever 6) and **Organizational Structure** (Lever 3) — co-locate teams, multi-year contracts, shared KPIs.

**Mapping Summary Table [1 mark]**

| Obstacle | Primary Lever |
|----------|---------------|
| Incentive | Aligned Incentives |
| Information Processing | Information Sharing (+ IT) |
| Operational | Org. Structure + CPFR |
| Pricing | Pricing / ECR |
| Behavioural | Alliances + Org. Structure |

---

> **Question A (b) [7 Marks]:**
> *"Compute the coordination index for a two-stage supply chain where the integrated optimum profit is ₹5,00,000 and the decentralized equilibrium profit (after double marginalization) is ₹3,75,000. What percentage of potential profit is lost? Suggest a contractual lever to close 100% of this gap."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Coordination Index.**

$$
C_{sc} = \frac{\Pi_{int} - \Pi_{dec}}{\Pi_{int}} = \frac{500000 - 375000}{500000} = \frac{125000}{500000}
$$

$$
C_{sc} = 0.25 \text{ (or 25%)}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the gap.**

25% of the supply chain's *potential* profit is destroyed by lack of coordination. In absolute terms, the chain is losing **₹1,25,000 per period**.

**Step 3 — Identify the contractual lever to close the gap.**

A **two-part tariff** contract (also called a *fixed-fee + marginal-cost* contract) can perfectly coordinate the chain. The manufacturer sets:
- Wholesale price $w = c_m$ (manufacturer's marginal cost)
- Franchise fee $F = \Pi_{mfr}^{dec}$ (the manufacturer's lost profit recovery)

With $w = c_m$, the retailer faces the *true* chain cost and chooses the chain-optimal retail price. The franchise fee compensates the manufacturer. The resulting $C_{sc} = 0$ (i.e., 100% coordination achieved).

**Step 4 — Why two-part tariff (not buyback / revenue-sharing)?**
- **Buyback contracts** (Cachon 2003) coordinate only for specific demand distributions.
- **Revenue sharing** coordinates if and only if $\phi$ (retailer's revenue share) is chosen carefully.
- **Two-part tariff** is the *most general* coordinating contract and is the KTU-preferred textbook answer.

**Step 5 — Practical limitations [1 mark]**
- The franchise fee must be feasible for the retailer (cannot exceed retailer's outside option).
- Requires *trust* and *legal enforceability* — Lever 6 (Alliances) supports the contractual lever.
- Regulatory scrutiny in some markets (resale price maintenance).

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

> **Question B (a) [7 Marks]:**
> *"With a neat block diagram, explain the seven managerial levers to improve supply chain coordination. Use a real-world example for each lever."*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram [3 marks]:**

*(Draw the Master Coordination Framework from §4.1 — Mermaid-equivalent on paper. KTU examiners award full marks if all 7 levers and their link to obstacles are shown.)*

**Seven Levers — One Real-World Example Each [4 marks — ½ mark per lever for name + example, rest for linking to obstacle]:**

| # | Lever | Real-World Example | Obstacle Overcome |
|---|-------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Information Sharing | **Walmart ↔ P&G** share daily POS data via Retail Link. | Information Processing (Bullwhip) |
| 2 | IT Enablement | **Maersk TradeLens** blockchain for shipping documents. | Operational + Information |
| 3 | Org. Structure Alignment | **Boeing 787** embeds supplier engineers in its design center. | Behavioural + Operational |
| 4 | Aligned Incentives | **Cisco** pays Flex/Jabil on yield & cycle time, not piece-count. | Incentive (Double Marginalization) |
| 5 | Pricing & ECR | **P&G-Walmart "Power Pricing"** replaces quantity discounts with EDLP. | Pricing (Forward Buying) |
| 6 | Alliances | **Toyota keiretsu** with Denso, Aisin for joint R&D. | Behavioural + Incentive |
| 7 | CPFR | **Walmart-P&G CPFR pilot (1995)** cut stockouts 11% → <2%. | All three — Incentive, Info, Operational |

**Conclusion [0.5 mark]:**
The seven levers are *not mutually exclusive* — in practice, the most successful supply chains (Walmart, Zara, Toyota, Amazon) deploy *all seven simultaneously*. KTU examiners reward integration logic, not just listing.

---

> **Question B (b) [7 Marks]:**
> *"A two-stage supply chain has market demand $D = 1000 - 2p$. Manufacturer's cost is ₹30/unit, retailer's cost is ₹20/unit. Calculate: (i) the integrated optimal price and chain profit, (ii) the Stackelberg decentralized equilibrium price, quantity, and profits, and (iii) the coordination index. Suggest the wholesale price that perfectly coordinates the chain."*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Integrated optimum:**

$$
\Pi_{int}(p) = (p - 30 - 20)(1000 - 2p) = (p - 50)(1000 - 2p)
$$

$$
= 1000p - 2p^2 - 50000 + 100p = -2p^2 + 1100p - 50000
$$

$$
\frac{d\Pi_{int}}{dp} = -4p + 1100 = 0 \Rightarrow p^{opt} = 275 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
D^{opt} = 1000 - 2(275) = 450 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\Pi_{int}^{*} = (275 - 50)(450) = 225 \times 450 = 101250 \text{ ₹}
$$

**(ii) Stackelberg equilibrium:**

Retailer reaction: maximize $(p - w - 20)(1000 - 2p)$:

$$
\frac{d}{dp} = 1000 - 2p - 2(p - w - 20) = 0
$$

$$
1000 - 2p - 2p + 2w + 40 = 0 \Rightarrow p^*(w) = 260 + \frac{w}{2}
$$

Manufacturer maximizes: $(w - 30)\left[1000 - 2\left(260 + \frac{w}{2}\right)\right] = (w - 30)(480 - w)$

$$
\frac{d\Pi_{mfr}}{dw} = (480 - w) + (w - 30)(-1) = 510 - 2w = 0
$$

$$
w^{Stackelberg} = 255 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
p^* = 260 + 127.5 = 387.5 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
D^* = 1000 - 2(387.5) = 225 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\Pi_{mfr} = (255 - 30)(225) = 225 \times 225 = 50625 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\Pi_{ret} = (387.5 - 255 - 20)(225) = 112.5 \times 225 = 25312.5 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\Pi_{dec} = 50625 + 25312.5 = 75937.5 \text{ ₹}
$$

**(iii) Coordination Index:**

$$
C_{sc} = \frac{101250 - 75937.5}{101250} = \frac{25312.5}{101250} = 0.25
$$

**Perfectly coordinating wholesale price:**

For full coordination, set $w = c_m = 30$ ₹/unit (marginal-cost pricing) and recover manufacturer's profit via a **lump-sum franchise fee** $F = 50625$ ₹. The retailer's net profit becomes $25312.5 - 50625 = -25312.5$ — clearly unacceptable unless the manufacturer's decentralization profit is also shared. Therefore, a *balanced* two-part tariff is:

$$
w = 30, \quad F = \text{negotiated value} \leq 25312.5
$$

So the retailer earns at least its Stackelberg profit. This is the **KTU textbook answer** for a coordination-achieving contract.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the *Coordination Index* with the *Bullwhip Ratio*.** The first is a profit-gap metric, the second is a variance-amplification metric. Mixing them up costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the franchise fee in two-part tariffs.** Setting $w = c_m$ *alone* is not enough — students must mention the lump-sum $F$ that compensates the manufacturer, otherwise the contract is unworkable.
> 3. **Listing levers without mapping them to obstacles.** KTU awards **1 mark** explicitly for the obstacle-lever mapping. A bare list gets at most 3 of 7 marks.
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in Stackelberg differentiation.** The most common is forgetting the retailer's reaction function has *both* $p$ and $w$ inside. Differentiate carefully.
> 5. **Omitting the assumption of demand function form.** Always state $D(p) = a - bp$ explicitly at the start of a numerical answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Topic: Managerial Levers to Improve Coordination**

- ☐ **Definition:** Coordination = alignment of decisions, incentives, and information across all SC stages to maximize *total* (not local) profit.
- ☐ **Coordination Index formula:** $C_{sc} = (\Pi_{int} - \Pi_{dec}) / \Pi_{int}$ — a number between 0 and 1.
- ☐ **Five Obstacles to Coordination:** (1) Incentive, (2) Information Processing, (3) Operational, (4) Pricing, (5) Behavioural. Remember all five.
- ☐ **Seven Managerial Levers:** (1) Information Sharing, (2) IT, (3) Org. Structure, (4) Aligned Incentives, (5) Pricing/ECR, (6) Alliances, (7) CPFR/Joint Decisions.
- ☐ **Mapping is essential:** Every obstacle has a *primary* lever and *secondary* levers (use the matrix in §2.3).
- ☐ **Double Marginalization:** Independent stage-level markups destroy 20–40% of chain profit on average. Coordination Index typically 0.20–0.35 in real cases.
- ☐ **Stackelberg Game:** Manufacturer is the *leader* (sets $w$ first), retailer is the *follower* (sets $p$ after). Always derive retailer's reaction function *first*.
- ☐ **Perfect Coordination Contract:** Two-part tariff with $w = c_m$ + franchise fee $F$. KTU's textbook answer.
- ☐ **Bullwhip Effect:** Demand variance amplifies upstream. Quantified by the ratio $BW = [Var(Q)/E(Q)] / [Var(D)/E(D)]$. Information Sharing is the *primary* counter-lever.
- ☐ **Real-world anchors to remember:** Walmart-P&G (POS sharing + CPFR), Toyota keiretsu (alliances + co-design), Maersk TradeLens (IT lever), Cisco-Flex (aligned incentives), Zara (info + org structure).
- ☐ **Maturity Ladder:** Stage 1 (None) → Stage 2 (Info Share) → Stage 3 (Process Alignment) → Stage 4 (Contract Alignment) → Stage 5 (Strategic Alliance) → Stage 6 (Full Integration). KTU exam tip: always recommend the *next* stage-appropriate lever, not the *latest* one.
- ☐ **Valuation Pitfalls:** Don't confuse coordination index with bullwhip ratio; always include franchise fee in two-part tariffs; always state the demand function explicitly in numerical problems; map levers to obstacles explicitly (it is a 1-mark line item in KTU ESE).

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Managerial Levers to Improve Coordination**
> **Total Estimated Study Time:** 6–8 hours for first read, 2 hours for revision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cycle Inventory, Aggregating Multiple Products in a Single Order and Quantity Discounts; Managing Uncertainty in a Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cycle Inventory, Aggregating Multiple Products in a Single Order and Quantity Discounts; Managing Uncertainty in a Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cycle Inventory — The Foundational Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle Inventory** is the average inventory a firm holds to satisfy demand between successive orders (replenishment cycles). It is the **working inventory** that constantly cycles between a maximum (order quantity $Q$) and zero.

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Cycle Inventory is the portion of total inventory that results from ordering (or producing) in lots of size $Q$ to satisfy the steady demand $D$ over a year. If a firm places an order of size $Q$ units and demand is consumed uniformly, the average inventory held during the cycle equals half of the order quantity.

$$\text{Cycle Inventory} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

where $Q$ is the order quantity in units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cycle inventory is *not* safety stock. Cycle inventory exists because of the **fixed-cost structure** of ordering/transportation — it disappears only in a frictionless world where every unit could be produced/replenished exactly when demanded.

---

## 1.2 Aggregating Multiple Products in a Single Order (Joint Replenishment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Order Aggregation** (also called *joint ordering* or *multi-item coordination*) is the practice of placing **one combined order** for several SKUs (Stock-Keeping Units) from the same supplier or on the same truck, rather than placing separate orders per SKU.

**Why Aggregation Exists:** A truck that has space for 26 pallets often travels whether it carries 4 pallets or 26. If a retailer splits orders across many small trucks (Less-Than-Truckload / LTL), the *transportation cost per unit* is very high. By **consolidating** shipments to Full-Truckload (FTL), the same truck cost is spread over more units.

---

## 1.3 Quantity Discounts (Trade Promotions / Volume Pricing)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantity Discount** is a pricing schedule in which the **unit purchase price ($P$) decreases** as the order quantity ($Q$) increases. It is a *demand-shaping tool* used by suppliers to:
> 1. Shift cycle inventory **upstream** (to the buyer / manufacturer).
> 2. Reduce the supplier's own ordering and shipping costs.
> 3. Lock the buyer into larger, less-frequent purchases.

There are two principal structures covered in KTU:

| Type | Description | KTU Jargon |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **All-Units Discount** | The lower price applies to **ALL** units in the order once $Q \geq$ break quantity $Q_b$. | "Step discount" |
| **Incremental Discount** | Lower price applies **only to units beyond** each break point; earlier units are billed at higher prices. | "Marginal / tiered discount" |

---

## 1.4 Managing Uncertainty — Safety Inventory

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Inventory (SI)** (also called *buffer stock* or *safety stock*) is inventory held to **absorb variability** in demand and/or supply lead time, thereby protecting the firm against stockouts.

Safety inventory is computed using the **in-stock probability** (also called *cycle service level* or *Type-1 service level*), which is the probability of not stocking out during a replenishment lead time.

**Key Inputs (highlighted):**
- **$\sigma_d$** = standard deviation of daily/weekly demand.
- **$\sigma_{LT}$** = standard deviation of lead time.
- **$z$** = z-score corresponding to the desired in-stock probability (e.g., $z = 1.65$ for **95%** in-stock probability, $z = 2.33$ for **99%**).
- **$L$** = lead time (in same time unit as demand).
- **$\bar{D}$** = average demand rate.

---

## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Combining All Four Sub-Topics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Kitchen-Pantry Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a small grocery store in Kerala stocking **rice, sugar, and tea powder**. These three SKUs come from the same supplier in Kochi.
>
> 1. **Cycle Inventory:** The shopkeeper orders rice every 2 weeks (because transporting rice is expensive per trip). So on average there is half an order of rice sitting in the back room — that is cycle inventory.
>
> 2. **Aggregation:** Instead of ordering rice, sugar, and tea powder on three separate days (three truck hires), the shopkeeper **waits until Friday, places ONE combined order, and ONE truck brings all three**. This is aggregation — fixed ordering cost is spread over more products.
>
> 3. **Quantity Discount:** The supplier says, *"If you order ≥ 200 kg of rice at once, I will reduce the price from ₹50/kg to ₹45/kg."* The shopkeeper must now weigh the **discount savings** against the **extra holding cost** of storing more rice.
>
> 4. **Safety Inventory:** During Onam, rice demand is unpredictable — sometimes 50 customers come, sometimes 200. The shopkeeper keeps an **extra 30 kg buffer** so he never runs out. This buffer is safety inventory, sized to absorb demand uncertainty.
>
> **Together, these four ideas answer ONE question:** *How much should we order, how often, from whom, and how much buffer do we keep to stay profitable and customer-satisfied?*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cycle Inventory & The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

### 2.1.1 The Trade-Off (The Heart of EOQ)

When a firm orders quantity $Q$:

- **Ordering cost per year** $= \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$  
  → Goes **down** as $Q$ rises (fewer orders).
- **Holding cost per year** $= \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$  
  → Goes **up** as $Q$ rises (more stock sitting in warehouse).
- **Purchase cost per year** $= D \cdot P$  
  → **Independent of $Q$** for a fixed unit price (so it is ignored when minimising variable cost).

The **optimal $Q$** is the one that **minimises the sum** of ordering + holding cost.

### 2.1.2 The KTU Assumptions of the EOQ Model

1. Demand $D$ is **known, constant, and deterministic**.
2. Lead time $L$ is **known and constant**.
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate).
4. No **quantity discounts** (initially).
5. Only one product is being ordered.
6. No stockouts allowed.
7. Ordering cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are **independent of $Q$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 questions, you must **explicitly list the assumptions** before deriving EOQ. Examiners award a dedicated sub-mark for this.

---

## 2.2 Aggregating Multiple Products — The Coordination Logic

### 2.2.1 The Key Idea: Square-Root Aggregation

When $n$ independent products are aggregated into a single joint order, the **cycle inventory of the entire system grows only with the square-root of $n$**, while the cycle inventory of each individual product is unchanged.

**Aggregation Effect on Cycle Inventory:**

$$\text{Total Cycle Inventory (aggregated)} = \sqrt{n} \cdot \frac{Q^{*}}{2}$$

where $Q^{*}$ is the individual EOQ (assuming all $n$ products have similar demand/order characteristics).

**Why square root?** Because the joint order, if all $n$ products share the same order frequency, comes every $Q^{*}/D$ cycles for **one** order cycle, but each product is replenished less frequently. The combined inventory variance averages out partially.

### 2.2.2 The Power of Transportation Aggregation

A KTU-favourite example is the **LTL → FTL** switch:

- LTL cost ≈ ₹4 per unit (small shipment, high fixed cost per unit).
- FTL cost ≈ ₹0.50 per unit (full truck, low fixed cost per unit).
- By consolidating, the **per-unit transportation cost can drop by 80% or more**, even though holding cost rises.

**Aggregation-Saving Formula (KTU board favourite):**

$$\text{Saving} = (n - \sqrt{n}) \cdot \frac{Q^{*}}{2} \cdot H_{\text{avoided}}$$

This is the *extra* inventory held vs the un-aggregated case; the trade-off must be evaluated against the ordering/transportation saving.

---

## 2.3 Quantity Discounts — Two KTU-Relevant Structures

### 2.3.1 All-Units Discount Schedule

A typical schedule:

| Discount Range | Unit Price $P_i$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $0 \leq Q < Q_1$ | $P_1$ (highest) |
| $Q_1 \leq Q < Q_2$ | $P_2$ |
| $Q_2 \leq Q < Q_3$ | $P_3$ |
| $Q \geq Q_3$ | $P_4$ (lowest) |

Once the order quantity crosses $Q_i$, the lower price $P_i$ applies to **all** units in that order.

### 2.3.2 Incremental (Marginal) Discount Schedule

For an order of $Q$ units where $Q_1 \leq Q < Q_2$:

$$\text{Total Purchase Cost} = (Q_1 - 0) \cdot P_1 + (Q - Q_1) \cdot P_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, **All-Units Discounts** appear far more frequently than incremental because the calculation involves a clean break-even comparison.

---

## 2.4 Managing Uncertainty — Safety Inventory Formula Family

### 2.4.1 Case A: Uncertainty in Demand ONLY (Lead Time $L$ constant)

$$\text{Safety Stock (SS)} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

The Reorder Point (ROP) is:

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{D} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

### 2.4.2 Case B: Uncertainty in Lead Time ONLY (Demand constant)

$$\text{SS} = z \cdot \bar{D} \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \bar{D} \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

### 2.4.3 Case C: Uncertainty in BOTH Demand AND Lead Time (KTU's Favourite Case)

The variance of demand over the lead time is:

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT} = z \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{2}}$$

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{2}}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Must-Memorise Table.** Master every row; the valuation key penalises missing units.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **EOQ** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}$ | $D$=annual demand, $S$=order cost/order, $H$=holding cost/unit/yr | units |
| 2 | **Cycle Inventory** | $CI = Q/2$ | Average inventory in a cycle | units |
| 3 | **Number of Orders / yr** | $N = D/Q$ | $N$ is dimensionless | orders |
| 4 | **Time Between Orders** | $T = Q/D$ | $T$ is in years (multiply by 52 for weeks) | yr |
| 5 | **Total Annual Cost (no discount)** | $TC = DP + \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H$ | $P$=unit price | ₹/yr |
| 6 | **EOQ with all-units discount** | $TC_i = D P_i + \frac{D}{Q_i}S + \frac{Q_i}{2}H_i$ where $H_i = h P_i$ | $i$ indexes the price bracket | ₹/yr |
| 7 | **Break-even quantity for discount** | $Q_b = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H \cdot (1 - P_{low}/P_{high})}}$ | Used to compare costs across brackets | units |
| 8 | **Aggregated cycle inventory** | $CI_{agg} = \sqrt{n} \cdot \frac{Q^{*}}{2}$ | $n$ = number of SKUs aggregated | units |
| 9 | **Safety Stock — demand variability only** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $\sigma_d$ = std dev of daily demand, $L$ = lead time in days | units |
| 10 | **Safety Stock — both variabilities** | $SS = z \cdot \sqrt{L \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2} \sigma_{LT}^{2}}$ | $\sigma_{LT}$ = std dev of lead time | units |
| 11 | **Reorder Point** | $ROP = \bar{D} L + SS$ | Demand during lead time + buffer | units |
| 12 | **z-score (95% service)** | $z = 1.645$ | In-stock probability ≈ 95% | dimensionless |
| 13 | **z-score (97.5% service)** | $z = 1.96$ | In-stock probability ≈ 97.5% | dimensionless |
| 14 | **z-score (99% service)** | $z = 2.33$ | In-stock probability ≈ 99% | dimensionless |
| 15 | **Cycle Service Level** | $CSL = P(\text{no stockout in LT})$ | Probability, $0 < CSL < 1$ | dimensionless |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering supply-chain practice (KTU NEP 2020 Outcome):**
> - **Manufacturing plants:** EOQ determines raw-material lot sizes that minimise the joint cost of machine set-up (ordering) and warehouse occupancy (holding).
> - **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Aggregation logic drives how items are picked from fulfilment centres and consolidated on a delivery truck.
> - **Retailers (Reliance, Lulu):** Quantity discounts inform vendor negotiation and the "buy in bulk" campaigns that customers see during Onam and Ramadan.
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Safety-stock formulas with **dual variability** are used to keep insulin and critical drugs available despite fluctuating demand and global supply shocks.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the EOQ Formula (Harris-Wilson Model)

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Annual Total Cost Function

Let $D$ = annual demand (units/year), $Q$ = order quantity (units), $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order), $H$ = annual holding cost per unit (₹/unit/year), $P$ = unit price (₹/unit).

**Annual Purchase Cost:**

$$\text{APC} = D \cdot P$$

This is fixed w.r.t. $Q$ when $P$ is constant. We **ignore** it when locating the optimal $Q$.

**Annual Ordering Cost:**

A firm places $D/Q$ orders per year, each costing $S$. Hence:

$$\text{AOC} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

**Annual Holding Cost:**

If demand is consumed uniformly, average inventory over a cycle = $Q/2$. Holding cost per unit per year = $H$. Therefore:

$$\text{AHC} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

**Annual Total Relevant Cost (TRC):**

$$\text{TRC}(Q) = \frac{D S}{Q} + \frac{H Q}{2}$$

### 3.1.2 Minimising TRC — First-Order Condition

Differentiate TRC with respect to $Q$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{TRC})}{dQ} = -\frac{D S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

Solve for $Q$:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D S}{Q^{2}}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2 D S}{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}}$$

### 3.1.3 Second-Order Confirmation (Minimum, Not Maximum)

$$\frac{d^{2}(\text{TRC})}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{for all } Q > 0$$

Since the second derivative is **positive**, TRC is **convex** and $Q^{*}$ is a **global minimum**. ✓

### 3.1.4 The Famous EOQ Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> A retailer sells **$D = 50{,}000$ units/year** of a product. Each order costs **$S = ₹200$**. Holding cost is **$H = ₹5$/unit/year**. Find the EOQ, number of orders, cycle inventory, and time between orders.

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ:**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 50{,}000 \cdot 200}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{4{,}000{,}000} = 2000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Number of Orders per Year:**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{50{,}000}{2000} = 25 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 3 — Time Between Orders:**

$$T = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{2000}{50{,}000} = 0.04 \text{ years} = 0.04 \times 52 = 2.08 \text{ weeks}$$

So roughly **one order every 2 weeks**.

**Step 4 — Cycle Inventory:**

$$CI = \frac{Q^{*}}{2} = 1000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum Total Relevant Cost:**

$$\text{TRC}_{\min} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 200}{2000} + \frac{2000 \cdot 5}{2} = 5000 + 5000 = ₹10{,}000/\text{year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A beautiful **KTU property**: at the optimum, **ordering cost = holding cost**. Verify: $DS/Q = H Q/2 = ₹5{,}000$. They are equal — this is the universal EOQ equilibrium.

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation of the Optimal Quantity Under All-Units Discount

### 3.2.1 The Critical Insight

When the unit price $P$ drops to $P'$, the holding cost (which is often expressed as a fraction $i$ of price: $H = iP$) also drops. So a *new* EOQ must be computed for **each** price bracket:

$$Q_{i}^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H_i}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{i P_i}}$$

### 3.2.2 The KTU Decision Rule (Exam-Favourite Procedure)

> **Step 1:** For the **lowest price bracket**, compute $Q^{*}$.  
> **Step 2:** If $Q^{*} \geq$ break-quantity $Q_b$ of that bracket, then $Q^{*}$ is feasible. Use it.  
> **Step 3:** If $Q^{*} < Q_b$, then $Q^{*}$ is *not feasible* — the buyer would not buy enough to get the discount. So **adjust upward** to $Q_b$ and recompute total cost.  
> **Step 4:** Compare total costs across all feasible brackets. Pick the **minimum**.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example (All-Units Discount)

> $D = 10{,}000$ units/yr, $S = ₹50$/order, holding cost rate $i = 20\%$ of unit price. Discount schedule:

| Quantity Range | Price $P_i$ (₹) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $0 \leq Q < 1000$ | $10$ |
| $1000 \leq Q < 5000$ | $9$ |
| $Q \geq 5000$ | $8$ |

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ at the lowest price $P_3 = ₹8$:**

$$Q_3^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 50}{0.20 \cdot 8}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1.6}} = \sqrt{625{,}000} \approx 790.6 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Feasibility check:**

$Q_3^{*} \approx 791 < 5000$, so the buyer does **not** qualify for the $₹8$ discount. We must adjust **up** to $Q = 5000$.

**Step 3 — Total cost at $Q = 5000$ at $P_3 = ₹8$:**

$$H_3 = 0.20 \cdot 8 = ₹1.60/\text{unit/yr}$$

$$\text{TC}_3 = 10{,}000 \cdot 8 + \frac{10{,}000}{5000} \cdot 50 + \frac{5000}{2} \cdot 1.60$$

$$\text{TC}_3 = 80{,}000 + 100 + 4000 = ₹84{,}100$$

**Step 4 — Compute EOQ at $P_2 = ₹9$:**

$$Q_2^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 50}{0.20 \cdot 9}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1.8}} \approx 745.4 \text{ units}$$

$745.4 < 1000$, so not feasible. Adjust to $Q = 1000$:

$$H_2 = 0.20 \cdot 9 = ₹1.80/\text{unit/yr}$$

$$\text{TC}_2 = 10{,}000 \cdot 9 + \frac{10{,}000}{1000} \cdot 50 + \frac{1000}{2} \cdot 1.80$$

$$\text{TC}_2 = 90{,}000 + 500 + 900 = ₹91{,}400$$

**Step 5 — Compute EOQ at $P_1 = ₹10$:**

$$Q_1^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 50}{0.20 \cdot 10}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{2}} \approx 707.1 \text{ units}$$

Feasible (in range $[0, 1000)$).

$$H_1 = 0.20 \cdot 10 = ₹2.00/\text{unit/yr}$$

$$\text{TC}_1 = 10{,}000 \cdot 10 + \frac{10{,}000}{707.1} \cdot 50 + \frac{707.1}{2} \cdot 2$$

$$\text{TC}_1 = 100{,}000 + 707.1 + 707.1 = ₹101{,}414$$

**Step 6 — Pick Minimum:**

| Option | Quantity | Price | Total Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_3$, $Q = 5000$ | 5000 | ₹8 | **₹84,100** ← MIN |
| $P_2$, $Q = 1000$ | 1000 | ₹9 | ₹91,400 |
| $P_1$, $Q = 707.1$ | 707 | ₹10 | ₹101,414 |

**Decision:** Buy **5000 units** at **₹8/unit** — total cost = **₹84,100/yr**.

---

## 3.3 Full Derivation of Safety Stock With Demand Uncertainty Only

### 3.3.1 Setup

Let daily demand $d$ be **normally distributed** with mean $\bar{d}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_d$. Lead time $L$ is fixed (in days). We want the firm to **not stock out** during lead time with probability $CSL$.

### 3.3.2 Demand Over Lead Time

Demand over $L$ days is normally distributed with:

$$\text{Mean} = \bar{d} \cdot L$$

$$\text{Standard Deviation} = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

(The square root appears because independent daily demands are summed, and variances add — variances add linearly, standard deviations add via square root.)

### 3.3.3 Reorder Point Equation

The ROP must cover **average demand over LT + safety buffer**:

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

The safety stock is the second term:

$$\boxed{SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}}$$

### 3.3.4 Numerical Example

> A supermarket's daily demand for milk is $\bar{d} = 100$ litres with $\sigma_d = 15$ litres. Lead time $L = 4$ days. Target in-stock probability = 95%, so $z = 1.645$.

**Step 1 — Safety Stock:**

$$SS = 1.645 \cdot 15 \cdot \sqrt{4} = 1.645 \cdot 15 \cdot 2 = 49.35 \text{ litres}$$

**Step 2 — ROP:**

$$\text{ROP} = 100 \cdot 4 + 49.35 = 449.35 \approx 450 \text{ litres}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** When stock reaches **450 litres**, place a new order. The 50-litre buffer protects against unusually high demand during the 4-day wait.

---

## 3.4 Full Derivation of Safety Stock With Combined Uncertainty (Demand + Lead Time)

### 3.4.1 Variance Addition Principle

If demand and lead time are **independent**, the variance of demand during lead time is the **sum** of two components:

- Variance from demand variability during $L$ days: $L \cdot \sigma_d^{2}$.
- Variance from lead-time variability at average demand $\bar{D}$: $\bar{D}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{2}$.

### 3.4.2 Combined Standard Deviation

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{2}}$$

### 3.4.3 Safety Stock Equation

$$\boxed{SS = z \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{2}}}$$

### 3.4.4 Numerical Example

> $\bar{D} = 100$ units/day, $\sigma_d = 10$ units/day, $\bar{L} = 6$ days, $\sigma_{LT} = 1.5$ days, target $CSL = 97.5\%$ → $z = 1.96$.

**Step 1 — Plug values:**

$$SS = 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{6 \cdot 100 + 100^{2} \cdot 2.25}$$

$$SS = 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{600 + 22{,}500} = 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{23{,}100}$$

$$SS = 1.96 \cdot 151.99 \approx 297.9 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — ROP:**

$$\text{ROP} = 100 \cdot 6 + 297.9 = 897.9 \approx 898 \text{ units}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EOQ Cost Trade-Off Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Annual Demand D] --> B[Choose Order Quantity Q]
    S[Ordering Cost S] --> B
    H[Holding Cost Rate i times Price P] --> B
    B --> C[Compute Annual Ordering Cost D times S over Q]
    B --> D[Compute Annual Holding Cost Q over 2 times H]
    C --> E[Sum TRC of Q]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Derivative equals zero?}
    F -->|Yes| G[EOQ Formula Qstar equals sqrt 2DS over H]
    F -->|No| B
    G --> H[Validate Qstar via second derivative greater than zero]
    H --> I[Optimal Order Policy]
```

## 4.2 Quantity Discount Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Start Discount Analysis] --> A[Step 1 Compute EOQ at Lowest Price Pn]
    A --> B{Qstar greater than or equal to Break Qn}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Qstar Feasible]
    B -->|No| D[Set Q equals Qn Adjust Upward]
    C --> E[Compute Total Cost TCn]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Repeat for all price brackets i equals 1 to n]
    F --> G[Tabulate all TC values]
    G --> H[Select Minimum TC as Optimal]
    H --> I[End Optimal Decision]
```

## 4.3 Order Aggregation Logic (LTL to FTL Switch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Demand for SKU 1] --> M[Merge into Joint Order]
    B[Demand for SKU 2] --> M
    C[Demand for SKU n] --> M
    M --> D[Single Truck Single Invoice]
    D --> E{Is Truck Full FTL or Partial LTL}
    E -->|LTL| F[High Cost Per Unit but Low Holding]
    E -->|FTL| G[Low Cost Per Unit but High Holding]
    F --> H[Evaluate Holding vs Transport Savings]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Decide Aggregation Frequency]
```

## 4.4 Safety Stock With Uncertainty Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DF[Demand Forecast] --> DV[Compute sigma d]
    LTF[Lead Time Info] --> LTV[Compute sigma LT]
    DV --> CB[Combine via Variance Addition]
    LTV --> CB
    CB --> SDL[sigma DLT equals sqrt L sigma d squared plus D bar squared sigma LT squared]
    Z[Choose In Stock Probability CSL] --> ZT[Look up z score from Normal Table]
    ZT --> SS[Compute SS equals z times sigma DLT]
    SDL --> SS
    SS --> ROP[Compute ROP equals D bar times L plus SS]
    ROP --> OP[Issue Order When On Hand equals ROP]
```

## 4.5 Integrated Inventory Policy Architecture (Cycle + Safety + Aggregation + Discount)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A Forecast Demand per SKU] --> B[Step B Decide Aggregation Level]
    B --> C[Step C Apply Discount Schedule]
    C --> D[Step D Compute EOQ for each SKU]
    D --> E[Step E Compute Cycle Inventory equals Q over 2]
    E --> F[Step F Add Safety Stock from z sigma]
    F --> G[Step G Compute Total Inventory equals Cycle plus Safety]
    G --> H[Step H Compute Reorder Point]
    H --> I[Final Policy Qstar, ROP, CSL, Aggregated Trucks]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in formula substitution (₹/yr vs ₹/unit/yr). Deduct 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Ignoring feasibility check** after computing EOQ under discount → loses 2 marks easily.
> 3. **Using $z = 1.65$ for 99%** service level — correct value is $z = 2.33$. Read the question carefully!
> 4. **Forgetting $\sqrt{L}$** when deriving safety stock from demand variability.
> 5. **Mixing up "all-units" and "incremental"** discount purchase cost formulas.
> 6. **Not listing EOQ assumptions** explicitly → loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define cycle inventory. Why does it exist even when demand is perfectly known?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Cycle inventory is the average inventory a firm carries due to ordering in lots. If a firm orders $Q$ units and demand is consumed uniformly, the average inventory over a replenishment cycle is $Q/2$.
>
> **Why it exists even with perfect demand knowledge (1.5 marks):** Cycle inventory arises because of the **fixed cost structure** of ordering and transportation. Even if we know tomorrow's demand exactly, we cannot economically deliver one unit at a time (each delivery has a fixed cost $S$). To amortise this fixed cost across many units, we order in batches $Q$, creating a saw-tooth inventory that averages $Q/2$. It is a *cost-driven* phenomenon, not an *uncertainty-driven* one.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three commonly used z-scores corresponding to in-stock probabilities in safety-stock calculations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| In-Stock Probability (Cycle Service Level) | z-Score |
| :--- | :--- |
| **90%** | **$z = 1.28$** |
| **95%** | **$z = 1.645$** |
| **97.5%** | **$z = 1.96$** |
| **99%** | **$z = 2.33$** |
| **99.9%** | **$z = 3.09$** |

(Any three with correct pairing earn full marks — 1 mark each.)
