## Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Solve **EITHER** Question 1 OR Question 2. Each carries **14 marks** split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question 1A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the EOQ formula from the total annual cost function. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b)** A retailer faces the following all-units quantity discount for a product with annual demand $D = 8000$ units, order cost $S = ₹150$ per order, and holding cost rate $i = 25\%$ of unit price:

| Quantity Range | Unit Price (₹) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $0 \leq Q < 500$ | $20$ |
| $500 \leq Q < 2000$ | $18$ |
| $Q \geq 2000$ | $16$ |

Determine the optimal order quantity and the resulting minimum total annual cost.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question 1A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of EOQ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Assumptions of EOQ (2 marks):**

1. Demand rate $D$ is deterministic and constant.
2. Lead time $L$ is constant and known.
3. Order quantity arrives instantaneously (infinite replenishment rate).
4. No quantity discounts; unit price $P$ is constant.
5. Only one product (or independent products).
6. No stockouts are permitted.
7. Ordering cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are independent of $Q$.

**Step 2 — Total Annual Relevant Cost (2 marks):**

$$\text{TRC}(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D S}{Q}}_{\text{Annual Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{H Q}{2}}_{\text{Annual Holding Cost}}$$

**Step 3 — First-Order Condition (2 marks):**

$$\frac{d(\text{TRC})}{dQ} = -\frac{D S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2 D S}{H} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}$$

**Step 4 — Second-Order Verification (1 mark):**

$$\frac{d^{2}(\text{TRC})}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^{3}} > 0$$

Hence TRC is convex and $Q^{*}$ is a global minimum. ∎

---

#### Part (b) — Quantity Discount Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $D = 8000$ units/yr, $S = ₹150$/order, $i = 0.25$.

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ at the lowest price $P_3 = ₹16$ (1 mark):**

$$Q_3^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 8000 \cdot 150}{0.25 \cdot 16}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}400{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{600{,}000} \approx 774.6 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Feasibility Check at $P_3$ (1 mark):**

$Q_3^{*} = 774.6 < 2000$, so **not feasible** for the $₹16$ bracket. Adjust up to $Q = 2000$.

**Step 3 — Total Cost at $Q = 2000$ (₹16) (1.5 marks):**

$$H_3 = 0.25 \cdot 16 = ₹4$$

$$\text{TC}_3 = 8000 \cdot 16 + \frac{8000}{2000} \cdot 150 + \frac{2000}{2} \cdot 4$$

$$\text{TC}_3 = 128{,}000 + 600 + 4000 = ₹132{,}600$$

**Step 4 — EOQ at $P_2 = ₹18$ (1 mark):**

$$Q_2^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 8000 \cdot 150}{0.25 \cdot 18}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}400{,}000}{4.5}} \approx 730.3 \text{ units}$$

Not feasible ($< 500$ is in $P_1$, but $730 < 1000$... wait $730.3 \geq 500$, so feasible!). Adjust to $Q_2^{*}$ if feasible. $Q_2^* = 730.3$ lies in $[500, 2000)$ ✓ feasible.

$$\text{TC}_2 = 8000 \cdot 18 + \frac{8000}{730.3} \cdot 150 + \frac{730.3}{2} \cdot 4.5$$

$$\text{TC}_2 = 144{,}000 + 1643.4 + 1643.4 \approx ₹147{,}287$$

**Step 5 — EOQ at $P_1 = ₹20$ (1 mark):**

$$Q_1^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 8000 \cdot 150}{0.25 \cdot 20}} = \sqrt{600{,}000} \approx 774.6 \text{ units}$$

Feasible (in $[0, 500)$? No, $774 > 500$... so NOT feasible for $P_1$ bracket). Adjust down to $Q = 500$ (max of $P_1$ bracket).

Actually, since $Q_1^* = 774.6$ does not fall in $[0, 500)$, the **highest feasible quantity** at $P_1$ is $Q = 500$:

$$\text{TC}_1 = 8000 \cdot 20 + \frac{8000}{500} \cdot 150 + \frac{500}{2} \cdot 5$$

$$\text{TC}_1 = 160{,}000 + 2400 + 1250 = ₹163{,}650$$

**Step 6 — Decision (0.5 marks):**

| Bracket | $Q$ used | TC (₹/yr) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_3 = ₹16$ | 2000 | **₹132,600** ← MIN |
| $P_2 = ₹18$ | 730 | ₹147,287 |
| $P_1 = ₹20$ | 500 | ₹163,650 |

**Optimal Order Quantity = 2000 units, Total Annual Cost = ₹132,600.**

---

### ❓ Question 1B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **aggregating multiple products in a single order**. With the help of a numerical example, show how aggregation leads to cost savings in a supply chain.

**(b)** A company sells a product with average daily demand $\bar{D} = 200$ units, daily demand standard deviation $\sigma_d = 25$ units, mean lead time $\bar{L} = 5$ days, and lead-time standard deviation $\sigma_{LT} = 1$ day. The company targets a 97.5% in-stock probability. Compute the **safety stock** and the **reorder point**.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question 1B

#### Part (a) — Order Aggregation (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

Order aggregation (joint replenishment) refers to consolidating orders for multiple SKUs into a single shipment. Since each truck trip / purchase order carries a fixed cost $S$ (driver wages, paperwork, fuel fixed component), shipping products together amortises $S$ over more units. The two main benefits are:

1. **Lower per-unit ordering/transportation cost** (especially LTL → FTL).
2. **Possible volume discounts** because the consolidated quantity is larger.

The downside is **higher holding cost** because aggregated shipments mean each SKU is held longer between replenishments.

**Numerical Illustration (4 marks):**

Consider three SKUs A, B, C with:
- EOQ each: $Q^{*}_A = Q^{*}_B = Q^{*}_C = 1000$ units.
- Holding cost each: $H_A = H_B = H_C = ₹10$/unit/yr.

**Un-aggregated:**
- 3 separate orders, 3 × $S$ = $3S$ cost.
- Cycle inventory per SKU = 500; total = **1500 units**.

**Aggregated** (one combined order for all three):
- 1 order, $1S$ cost.
- Total cycle inventory = $\sqrt{3} \cdot 500 \approx 866$ units.

**Saving:** $1500 - 866 = 634$ units of inventory eliminated.
**Annual holding-cost saving** $= 634 \times 10 = ₹6340$.
**Plus** ordering cost reduced from $3S$ to $1S$.

**Conclusion:** Aggregation yields substantial savings when fixed ordering/transport costs dominate.

---

#### Part (b) — Safety Stock with Combined Uncertainty (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\bar{D} = 200$/day, $\sigma_d = 25$/day, $\bar{L} = 5$ days, $\sigma_{LT} = 1$ day, $CSL = 97.5\% \Rightarrow z = 1.96$.

**Step 1 — Combined Standard Deviation of Demand over LT (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{5 \cdot 625 + 200^{2} \cdot 1}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{3125 + 40{,}000} = \sqrt{43{,}125} \approx 207.67 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Safety Stock (2 marks):**

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT} = 1.96 \cdot 207.67 \approx 407.04 \approx 408 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Reorder Point (2 marks):**

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + SS = 200 \cdot 5 + 408 = 1000 + 408 = 1408 \text{ units}$$

**Answer:** Safety stock ≈ **408 units**, Reorder point ≈ **1408 units**. When stock falls to 1408 units, place a new order.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

### 🔹 Cycle Inventory Essentials
- Cycle Inventory $= Q/2$; exists due to **fixed ordering/transportation costs**.
- Optimal $Q$ minimises the sum of **ordering cost** + **holding cost**.
- EOQ formula: $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — at the optimum, **ordering cost = holding cost**.
- Always list the **seven EOQ assumptions** in long-answer questions.

### 🔹 Aggregation Essentials
- Joint ordering consolidates $n$ SKUs into one shipment.
- Aggregated cycle inventory $= \sqrt{n} \cdot (Q^{*}/2)$, **smaller** than $n \cdot (Q^{*}/2)$.
- Aggregation saves on **fixed ordering/transport cost** but increases **holding cost**.
- Common trigger: switch from **LTL to FTL** shipping.

### 🔹 Quantity Discount Essentials
- **All-units** discount: lower price applies to **all** units once $Q \geq Q_b$.
- **Incremental** discount: lower price applies only to units **beyond** $Q_b$.
- KTU decision rule: **compute EOQ at each price**, check feasibility, adjust upward to break-point if needed, then **pick minimum total cost**.
- The all-units TC formula: $TC_i = DP_i + (D/Q_i)S + (Q_i/2) \cdot iP_i$.

### 🔹 Uncertainty / Safety Stock Essentials
- Safety stock absorbs **demand and/or lead-time variability**.
- $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ (demand variability only).
- $SS = z \cdot \bar{D} \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ (lead-time variability only).
- $SS = z \cdot \sqrt{L\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{D}^{2}\sigma_{LT}^{2}}$ (both — KTU favourite).
- $\text{ROP} = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + SS$.
- Memorise z-scores: $z(90\%) = 1.28$, $z(95\%) = 1.645$, $z(97.5\%) = 1.96$, $z(99\%) = 2.33$, $z(99.9\%) = 3.09$.

### 🔹 KTU-Common Numerical Pattern (One-Line Mnemonic)
> *"EOQ for cycle, break-even for discount, $z\sigma\sqrt{L}$ for safety."*

### 🔹 Pitfalls to Avoid (Re-stated for Exam Day)
- Don't forget the **square root of $L$** in demand-only safety stock.
- Don't apply $z = 1.96$ for 95% — that's $z = 1.645$.
- Don't forget to **adjust upward** to $Q_b$ when EOQ falls short of the discount break.
- Don't ignore **units** in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Inventory: Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability; Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory; Impact of Aggregation on Safety Inventory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Safety Inventory: Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability; Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory; Impact of Aggregation on Safety Inventory # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Safety Inventory? **Safety Inventory (Safety Stock)** is the **excess inventory carried over and above the expected demand** during lead time, held as a buffer to protect the supply chain against **demand variability** and **supply uncertainty**. It is a strategic insurance policy against stockouts, ensuring a target level of product availability (service level) is maintained. Formally, according to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (Chopra & Meindl Framework):** > *Safety Inventory is the inventory held to satisfy demand that exceeds the amount expected during the lead time. It acts as a cushion against the natural uncertainty present in both demand and supply processes within a supply chain.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Water Tank Buffer" Imagine your home has an **overhead water tank** that feeds your taps. The municipal supply is irregular — sometimes water comes at full pressure, sometimes there's a complete cut. You install a **small reserve tank (safety buffer)** at the top to ensure you never run dry. - **Expected daily water usage** = Average demand - **Municipal pipeline variability** = Supply uncertainty - **Reserve tank capacity** = Safety inventory - **Your comfort level (never running out)** = Desired product availability (service level) The bigger the variability in supply (broken pipes, irregular timings), the **bigger the reserve tank** you need. If you combine water from multiple sources (aggregation), one big reserve tank can serve many houses, instead of each house having its own small one. This is **risk pooling** in essence. ### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Terminology > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Terminology — Memorize These:** > - **Service Level (β):** Probability of not stocking out during a lead time cycle. Expressed as a percentage (e.g., **95%**). > - **Type 1 Service Level:** Probability of satisfying all demand within a cycle (no backorders). > - **Type 2 Service Level:** Expected fraction of demand met from stock (fill rate). > - **Z-score (z):** Number of standard deviations from the mean corresponding to the desired service level. > - **Lead Time (L):** Time between placing an order and receiving it. > - **Reorder Point (ROP):** Inventory level that triggers a new order = $\bar{D}\bar{L} + SS$. > - **Standard Deviation of Demand during Lead Time (σ_DL):** The key variability measure. ### 1.4 Product Availability — The Strategic Goal **Product Availability** is defined as the ability of a supply chain to have the right product, in the right quantity, at the right time, for the right customer. It is the **outcome variable** that safety inventory is designed to protect. | Availability Level | Service Level (β) | Z-Score (z) | Typical Use | |:------------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------:|:------------| | Low | 85% | 1.04 | Low-margin commodities | | Moderate | 95% | 1.65 | Standard retail SKUs | | High | 97.5% | 1.96 | Critical spare parts | | Very High | 99% | 2.33 | Medical / Aerospace | | Extreme | 99.87% | 3.00 | Six-Sigma operations | ### 1.5 Why Does Safety Inventory Exist? Three fundamental reasons: 1. **Demand Uncertainty** — Actual customer demand deviates from the forecast. 2. **Supply Uncertainty** — Lead times, supplier reliability, and quality vary. 3. **Product Variety / Aggregation Trade-offs** — The decision to centralize or decentralize inventory. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability The optimal level of product availability is a **strategic decision** determined by the trade-off between the **cost of carrying additional safety stock** and the **cost of being out of stock**. KTU 2024 emphasizes the following factors: ### 2.1.1 Demand-Side Factors - **Demand Variability (σ_D):** Higher variability in daily/weekly demand requires more safety stock to absorb the peaks. CV (coefficient of variation) is often a better measure than standard deviation alone. - **Forecast Accuracy:** Better forecasts → narrower demand distribution → lower safety stock. - **Seasonality and Trend:** Products with strong seasonal peaks require more buffer to prevent lost sales during peaks. - **Product Life Cycle Stage:** New products have highly uncertain demand, requiring higher safety stock. - **Customer Behavior:** Loyalty, switching behavior, and price sensitivity affect stockout tolerance. ### 2.1.2 Supply-Side Factors - **Lead Time Variability (σ_L):** Suppliers with inconsistent lead times force higher safety stock. - **Supplier Reliability:** On-time delivery percentage, defect rate, and capacity flexibility. - **Order Frequency & Lot Sizing:** Larger order quantities (EOQ) → more inventory carrying but more time between order risks. - **Transportation Reliability:** Mode of transport variability. - **Geographic Sourcing Distance:** Longer supply lines typically = more uncertainty. ### 2.1.3 Cost-Trade-off Factors - **Stockout Cost (C_s):** Lost margin, lost customer goodwill, line stoppages in B2B. - **Carrying Cost (h):** Typically **20–30%** of unit cost per year (capital + storage + obsolescence). - **Replenishment Lead Time:** Longer lead times → higher required safety stock. - **Reorder Point Position:** ROP = Expected demand during lead time + Safety Stock. ### 2.1.4 Product & Market Factors - **Profit Margin:** High-margin items justify higher availability investments. - **Criticality:** Spare parts for critical machinery → near-100% availability. - **Substitutability:** If substitutes exist, lower safety stock per SKU is acceptable. - **Shelf Life:** Perishables cannot hold large safety stocks. - **ABC Classification:** 'A' items (high value) get higher service levels than 'C' items. ### 2.1.5 Strategic / Network Factors - **Aggregation Decisions (Centralization):** Pooled inventory at central DCs reduces total safety stock (square root law). - **Postponement Strategy:** Delaying differentiation reduces variety-based safety stock. - **Information Sharing:** VMI, CPFR, RFID reduce bullwhip and hence safety stock needs. - **Number of Echelons:** Multi-echelon chains compound safety stock requirements. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD:** These are the standard KTU-board expected formulas. Memorize the variable definitions and boundary conditions. | # | Situation | Formula | Key Variables | KTU Mark Weight | |:-:|:----------|:--------|:--------------|:---------------:| | 1 | Deterministic (no uncertainty) | $SS = 0$ | — | Low | | 2 | Variable Demand, Constant Lead Time | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$ | $\sigma_D$ = std dev of daily demand; $\bar{L}$ = avg lead time (days) | **High** | | 3 | Constant Demand, Variable Lead Time | $SS = z \cdot \bar{D} \cdot \sigma_L$ | $\bar{D}$ = avg daily demand; $\sigma_L$ = std dev of lead time | **High** | | 4 | Variable Demand AND Variable Lead Time | $SS = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_L^2}$ | Most realistic case | **Highest** | | 5 | Reorder Point | $ROP = \bar{D}\bar{L} + SS$ | Trigger for new order | **High** | | 6 | Square Root Law (Aggregation) | $SS_{pooled} = \sqrt{n} \cdot SS_{individual}$ | n = number of identical facilities | **Highest** | | 7 | General Aggregation | $SS_{pooled} = \sqrt{\sum_i n_i \cdot SS_i^2}$ | n_i = number per category; SS_i = individual SS | High | | 8 | Fill Rate (Type 2 Service) | $\beta_2 = 1 - \frac{SS \cdot f(k)}{Q}$ | Q = order quantity; f(k) = standard normal pdf | Medium | | 9 | Z-score lookup | $z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = stockout probability | **High** | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Z-Table Values to Memorize for KTU Exam:** > - 90% service → $z = 1.28$ > - 95% service → $z = 1.65$ > - 97.5% service → $z = 1.96$ > - 99% service → $z = 2.33$ > - 99.9% service → $z = 3.09$ ### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Formulas In KTU industry context (Kerala-based FMCG, marine spare parts, Cochin port logistics, Kochi-based spice exports, IT services), these formulas are used in: - **MRP/ERP Software** (SAP MM, Oracle SCM) for automated ROP and SS calculation. - **Spare Parts Planning** for the Cochin Shipyard and marine industry. - **Pharmaceutical Distribution** in Kerala's large retail pharmacy networks. - **Apparel and Footwear** in Kannur/Kozhikode-based export houses. - **Tea/Spice Exports** where lead time to Europe/US is 25–40 days, demanding high safety stock. ## 2.4 Factors Affecting Optimal Availability — Quick Decision Matrix | Factor | Increases Safety Stock Need? | Why? | |:-------|:----------------------------:|:-----| | Higher demand variability (↑σ_D) | ✅ Yes | Wider demand distribution | | Longer/less reliable lead time (↑σ_L) | ✅ Yes | More uncertainty in replenishment | | Higher desired service level (↑z) | ✅ Yes | More buffer for high availability | | Higher stockout cost | ✅ Yes | Worth investing in buffer | | Lower carrying cost | ✅ Yes | Buffer is cheap to hold | | Better forecast accuracy | ❌ No | Reduces σ_D | | Aggregation/Centralization | ❌ No | Risk pooling effect | | Product substitutability | ❌ No | Customers can switch SKUs | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Safety Stock with Variable Demand, Constant Lead Time ### Step 1 — Setup the Demand Distribution Let $D_i$ be the daily demand on day $i$, assumed to be a normal random variable with: - Mean: $\bar{D}$ units/day - Standard deviation: $\sigma_D$ units/day ### Step 2 — Demand over Lead Time If lead time $L$ is **constant** (in days), the demand during lead time is the sum of $L$ independent daily demands: $$ D_{LT} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} D_i $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Sum of Independent Normals For a sum of independent normal random variables, the resulting mean is the sum of means, and the variance is the sum of variances: $$ E[D_{LT}] = L \cdot \bar{D} $$ $$ Var(D_{LT}) = L \cdot \sigma_D^2 $$ ### Step 4 — Standard Deviation of Demand During Lead Time $$ \sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{Var(D_{LT})} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_D^2} = \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{L} $$ ### Step 5 — Apply the Z-Score Safety Stock Definition Safety stock is the buffer required to cover demand that exceeds the mean by $z$ standard deviations: $$ SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DL} $$ ### Step 6 — Final Formula $$ \boxed{SS = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Check:** Safety stock grows with the **square root** of lead time, not linearly. Doubling lead time increases SS by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ times, not 2 times. This is because averaging over more days reduces relative variability. ## 3.2 Derivation: Variable Demand AND Variable Lead Time (Most General) ### Step 1 — Acknowledge Lead Time Randomness Now let $L$ itself be a random variable: - Mean: $\bar{L}$ days - Standard deviation: $\sigma_L$ days Daily demand remains: $\bar{D}, \sigma_D$. ### Step 2 — Mean Demand During Lead Time $$ E[D_{LT}] = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} $$ ### Step 3 — Variance via the "Pooled" Variance Formula For a product of two random variables (or a sum where one factor varies): $$ Var(D_{LT}) = \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2 $$ This result comes from the variance of a product / conditional variance identity. The intuition: - $\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2$: variability from demand swings *over the average lead time*. - $\bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2$: variability from lead time swings *at the average demand rate*. ### Step 4 — Standard Deviation and Safety Stock $$ \sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_L^2} $$ $$ \boxed{SS = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_L^2}} $$ ### Step 3.3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A retailer sells a product with average daily demand $\bar{D} = 100$ units, daily demand std dev $\sigma_D = 25$ units. Average lead time $\bar{L} = 8$ days, std dev of lead time $\sigma_L = 2$ days. Desired service level = 95% (so $z = 1.65$). **Step 1:** Compute variance from demand variability: $$ \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2 = 8 \times 25^2 = 8 \times 625 = 5000 $$ **Step 2:** Compute variance from lead time variability: $$ \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2 = 100^2 \times 2^2 = 10000 \times 4 = 40000 $$ **Step 3:** Sum and take square root: $$ \sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{5000 + 40000} = \sqrt{45000} \approx 212.13 \text{ units} $$ **Step 4:** Multiply by z-score: $$ SS = 1.65 \times 212.13 \approx 350.02 \text{ units} $$ **Step 5:** Reorder Point: $$ ROP = \bar{D}\bar{L} + SS = 100 \times 8 + 350.02 = 800 + 350.02 = 1150.02 \approx 1150 \text{ units} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Square Root Law of Aggregation ### Step 1 — Independent Facility Setup Suppose a firm has $n$ independent retail outlets (or warehouses). Each faces: - Mean demand: $\bar{D}_i$ - Std dev of demand: $\sigma_{D_i}$ ### Step 2 — Total Safety Stock at Decentralized Level Each facility carries its own safety stock: $$ SS_{decentralized} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} SS_i = z \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_{D_i} $$ For identical facilities ($\sigma_{D_i} = \sigma$ for all $i$): $$ SS_{decentralized} = n \cdot z \cdot \sigma $$ ### Step 3 — Pooled (Centralized) Demand Distribution When all facilities are served from a single central warehouse, the total demand is: $$ D_{pooled} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i $$ For independent demands: $$ \sigma_{D_{pooled}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_{D_i}^2} $$ For identical facilities: $$ \sigma_{D_{pooled}} = \sqrt{n \cdot \sigma^2} = \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} $$ ### Step 4 — Pooled Safety Stock $$ SS_{pooled} = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} $$ ### Step 5 — The Square Root Law $$ \boxed{\frac{SS_{pooled}}{SS_{decentralized}} = \frac{z \cdot \sigma \sqrt{n}}{n \cdot z \cdot \sigma} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}} $$ Equivalently: $$ \boxed{SS_{pooled} = \frac{SS_{decentralized}}{\sqrt{n}} = SS_{individual} \cdot \sqrt{n}} $$ ### Step 6 — Numerical Illustration Suppose 4 retail outlets each hold 200 units of safety stock. **Decentralized total:** $4 \times 200 = 800$ units. **Pooled safety stock:** $200 \times \sqrt{4} = 200 \times 2 = 400$ units. **Reduction:** $800 - 400 = 400$ units, i.e., **50% reduction** in safety inventory — while maintaining the same service level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** Aggregation reduces safety stock by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$, NOT by a factor of $n$. This is the famous **Square Root Law** and is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU exams for this module. ## 3.5 General Aggregation Formula (Heterogeneous Items) When items or facilities are not identical: $$ SS_{pooled} = z \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_{D_i}^2} = z \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{SS_i}{z}\right)^2} $$ Simplified (with same z): $$ \boxed{SS_{pooled} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} SS_i^2}} $$ This is the **heterogeneous aggregation formula** preferred by KTU 2024 examiners for mixed-product problems. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of Safety Inventory in a Supply Chain The following Mermaid block diagram maps the interactions between the factors that determine safety inventory levels, providing a functional architecture flow: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Supply Chain Network] --> B[Demand Side Variables] A --> C[Supply Side Variables] A --> D[Cost Trade-off Variables] B --> B1[Mean Daily Demand: D-bar] B --> B2[Std Dev of Demand: sigma-D] B --> B3[Forecast Accuracy Level] B --> B4[Seasonality Factor] C --> C1[Average Lead Time: L-bar] C --> C2[Std Dev of Lead Time: sigma-L] C --> C3[Supplier Reliability Score] C --> C4[Order Lot Size: Q] D --> D1[Stockout Cost: Cs] D --> D2[Carrying Cost: h] D --> D3[Service Level Target: beta] B2 --> E[Variance Computation Block] C1 --> E C2 --> E B1 --> E D3 --> F[Z-score Lookup Table] F --> G[Sigma-DL Calculator] E --> G G --> H[Safety Stock Output: SS] D1 --> H D2 --> H H --> I[Reorder Point: ROP] I --> J[Order Trigger to Supplier] J --> K[Inventory Position Update] K --> L[Cycle Restarts] M[Aggregation Decision] --> N[Number of Locations: n] N --> O[Square Root Law Application] O --> H style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style H fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style I fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style L fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style O fill:#ea580c,stroke:#ea580c,color:#ffffff ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The left side captures **inputs** (Demand, Supply, Cost variables). - The center executes the **mathematical computation** of variance and z-score. - The right side outputs the **decision variable** (Safety Stock, ROP, Order Trigger). - The bottom branch shows the **aggregation effect** that can override the SS calculation via the Square Root Law. ## 4.2 Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Stock (Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stable Supplier
Low sigma-L] --> B[Small Safety Stock
SS = 35 units] C[Moderate Supplier
Medium sigma-L] --> D[Medium Safety Stock
SS = 85 units] E[Unreliable Supplier
High sigma-L] --> F[Large Safety Stock
SS = 250 units] G[Same Demand
D-bar = 100] --> B G --> D G --> F H[Same Service Level
z = 1.65] --> B H --> D H --> F style A fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style C fill:#eab308,stroke:#eab308,color:#ffffff style E fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style B fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a style D fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#eab308 style F fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626 ``` **Interpretation:** All else being equal, doubling the lead-time standard deviation from 1 day to 2 days (and then to 4 days) **more than triples** the safety stock required — demonstrating the **non-linear (and very expensive) impact of supply uncertainty**. ## 4.3 Aggregation / Risk Pooling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DECENTRALIZED[Decentralized Network] R1[Retailer 1
SS = 200] R2[Retailer 2
SS = 200] R3[Retailer 3
SS = 200] R4[Retailer 4
SS = 200] T1[Total SS = 800] end R1 --> T1 R2 --> T1 R3 --> T1 R4 --> T1 subgraph CENTRALIZED[Centralized Network] CW[Central Warehouse
SS = 400] T2[Total SS = 400] end CW --> T2 DECENTRALIZED ==> CENTRALIZED SAVINGS[50 percent Savings
400 units freed up] CENTRALIZED --> SAVINGS style DECENTRALIZED fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626 style CENTRALIZED fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a style SAVINGS fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style CW fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Takeaway:** Centralizing 4 identical retailers' safety stock into one pool **halves** the required safety inventory while maintaining identical service levels. This is the **Risk Pooling Effect**, a foundational concept in supply chain network design. ## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Safety Stock Formula ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Identify Variability Sources] --> Q1{Is lead time constant?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is demand variable?} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is demand variable?} Q2 -- No --> SS0[SS = 0] Q2 -- Yes --> F1[SS = z times sigma-D times sqrt of L] Q3 -- No --> F2[SS = z times D-bar times sigma-L] Q3 -- Yes --> F3[SS = z times sqrt of L-bar sigma-D squared plus D-bar squared sigma-L squared] F1 --> CHECK{Centralize?} F2 --> CHECK F3 --> CHECK CHECK -- Yes --> POOL[Apply Square Root Law: SS new = SS individual times sqrt of n] CHECK -- No --> KEEP[Keep Individual Safety Stocks] style START fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style F1 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style F2 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style F3 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style POOL fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Safety Inventory `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Safety Inventory (or Safety Stock) is the **excess inventory** carried over and above the expected demand during lead time to protect against demand and supply variability. It serves as a buffer to maintain a target level of product availability and prevent stockouts. > > **Key Roles:** > (1) Absorbs demand variability (forecast errors) > (2) Absorbs supply variability (lead time uncertainty, supplier unreliability) > (3) Maintains desired service level (product availability) > > **Mathematically:** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DL}$, where $z$ is the z-score for the desired service level and $\sigma_{DL}$ is the standard deviation of demand during lead time. --- ### Question 2: State and explain the Square Root Law of Safety Inventory. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Square Root Law** states that when safety inventory from $n$ independent facilities is aggregated (centralized) into a single pool, the required total safety stock reduces from $n \cdot SS_{individual}$ to $\sqrt{n} \cdot SS_{individual}$. > > **Formula:** $SS_{pooled} = SS_{individual} \cdot \sqrt{n}$ > > **Significance:** It quantifies the **risk pooling effect**. For example, centralizing 9 identical warehouses reduces safety inventory to one-third (from $9 \times SS$ to $3 \times SS$), achieving a **66.7% reduction** while maintaining the same service level. This law underpins the economic incentive for centralization in supply chain network design. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Discuss the various factors affecting the optimal level of product availability in a supply chain. `[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The optimal level of product availability is determined by balancing the **cost of carrying safety stock** against the **cost of stockouts**. The following factors influence this decision: **1. Demand-Side Factors:** - Demand variability (σ_D) — Higher variability → higher safety stock - Forecast accuracy — Better forecasts reduce required safety stock - Seasonality — Peak season requires larger buffers - Product life-cycle stage — New products have higher uncertainty **2. Supply-Side Factors:** - Lead time variability (σ_L) — Unreliable suppliers demand more safety stock - Supplier reliability score (on-time delivery, defect rate) - Order quantity and lot size - Transportation mode reliability **3. Cost Trade-off Factors:** - Stockout cost — Lost sales, customer goodwill, production downtime - Inventory carrying cost — Capital, storage, obsolescence (typically 20–30% of unit cost) - Order cost — Influences lot size, indirectly affects cycle stock **4. Product & Market Factors:** - Profit margin — High-margin items warrant high availability - Criticality — Medical/aerospace items require near-100% availability - Substitutability — If substitutes exist, less safety stock per SKU needed - Shelf life — Perishables limit safety stock quantities - ABC classification — 'A' items get higher service levels **5. Network & Strategic Factors:** - Aggregation decisions (centralization) — Reduces total safety stock via risk pooling - Information sharing — VMI, CPFR lower bullwhip effect - Postponement — Delays differentiation, reducing safety stock variety - Number of supply chain echelons **Conclusion:** The optimal availability level is not a single number but a **tailored decision per product-market combination** based on the trade-off between carrying costs and stockout costs. --- #### (b) Explain the impact of supply uncertainty on safety inventory. Derive the formula for safety stock under variable demand and lead time. `[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Impact of Supply Uncertainty:** Supply uncertainty (manifested as variability in lead time, supplier quality, or delivery reliability) has a **direct, non-linear, and significant impact** on safety inventory. Even a small increase in lead-time standard deviation causes a disproportionately large increase in required safety stock. **Derivation:** Let: - $\bar{D}$ = mean daily demand, $\sigma_D$ = std dev of daily demand - $\bar{L}$ = mean lead time, $\sigma_L$ = std dev of lead time **Step 1:** The mean demand during lead time is: $$ E[D_{LT}] = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} $$ **Step 2:** Using the conditional variance identity, the variance of demand during lead time is: $$ Var(D_{LT}) = \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2 $$ **Step 3:** Taking the square root: $$ \sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_L^2} $$ **Step 4:** Safety stock to achieve service level corresponding to z-score: $$ \boxed{SS = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_L^2}} $$ **Special Cases:** - If $\sigma_L = 0$ (constant lead time): $SS = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$ - If $\sigma_D = 0$ (constant demand): $SS = z \cdot \bar{D} \cdot \sigma_L$ **Real-world impact:** In Indian pharma supply chains, switching from a domestic supplier (σ_L = 1 day) to an overseas supplier (σ_L = 5 days) can **multiply safety stock by 3 to 5 times** — making supply chain design decisions critical. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) Explain the impact of aggregation on safety inventory. Derive the Square Root Law and discuss its practical implications. `[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Concept of Aggregation (Risk Pooling):** Aggregation refers to **centralizing inventory** from multiple locations, products, or echelons into a single pool. The fundamental insight is that the safety stock required to cover aggregated demand grows with the **square root of the number of items aggregated**, not linearly. **Derivation of Square Root Law:** Consider $n$ independent facilities, each with: - Demand standard deviation $\sigma_{D_i}$ **Step 1 — Decentralized safety stock:** $$ SS_{decentralized} = z \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_{D_i} $$ For identical facilities ($\sigma_{D_i} = \sigma$): $$ SS_{decentralized} = n \cdot z \cdot \sigma $$ **Step 2 — Pooled demand standard deviation:** $$ \sigma_{D_{pooled}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_{D_i}^2} = \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} \text{ (for identical)} $$ **Step 3 — Pooled safety stock:** $$ SS_{pooled} = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} $$ **Step 4 — Ratio:** $$ \boxed{\frac{SS_{pooled}}{SS_{decentralized}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}} $$ **Practical Implications:** 1. **Significant Cost Savings:** Centralizing 16 warehouses reduces safety stock to 1/4 (75% savings). 2. **Higher Service Levels Possible:** Same budget can fund higher service levels. 3. **Strategic Tool:** Justifies regional distribution centers over many local warehouses. 4. **Limitations:** Works only for **substitutable** or **independent** products. Lead time to customers may increase (the **transportation cost** vs **inventory cost** trade-off). 5. **Information Dependency:** Requires accurate demand data and good ERP integration. **Example:** Amazon India consolidates inventory in few mega-warehouses rather than thousands of small stores — applying the Square Root Law to dominate service-level economics. --- #### (b) A company operates 4 regional warehouses. Each warehouse currently holds a safety stock of 250 units. The company is considering centralizing the inventory at one location. Calculate: (i) the new pooled safety stock, and (ii) the percentage reduction in safety stock. Assume all warehouses face identical demand variability and service level. `[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Number of warehouses: $n = 4$ - Individual safety stock: $SS_{individual} = 250$ units - All identical (same $\sigma_D$, same service level z) **Step 1 — Total decentralized safety stock:** $$ SS_{decentralized} = n \cdot SS_{individual} = 4 \times 250 = 1000 \text{ units} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Square Root Law:** $$ SS_{pooled} = SS_{individual} \cdot \sqrt{n} $$ $$ SS_{pooled} = 250 \cdot \sqrt{4} = 250 \times 2 = 500 \text{ units} $$ **Step 3 — Reduction in safety stock:** $$ \Delta SS = SS_{decentralized} - SS_{pooled} = 1000 - 500 = 500 \text{ units} $$ **Step 4 — Percentage reduction:** $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{\Delta SS}{SS_{decentralized}} \times 100 = \frac{500}{1000} \times 100 = 50\% $$ **Step 5 — Final Answer Summary:** | Parameter | Decentralized | Centralized | Change | |:----------|:------------:|:-----------:|:------:| | Safety Stock | 1000 units | 500 units | **−50%** | | Service Level | Same | Same | No change | | Number of Locations | 4 | 1 | −3 | **Interpretation:** The company can **halve its safety inventory** by centralizing — freeing up 500 units of working capital — while maintaining identical service levels. However, the company must evaluate the **increase in transportation cost to customers** from the central location before deciding. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:** > 1. **Do not** compute pooled SS as $SS_{individual} / n$ (linear reduction). The law is $\sqrt{n}$, not $1/n$. > 2. **Do not** forget to state that the service level (z) is unchanged — the question often tests whether you understand that the *level* of protection remains the same. > 3. **Do not** assume centralization is always beneficial — always discuss the transportation cost trade-off. > 4. **Show the formula explicitly** before substitution. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the formula is missing. > 5. In numerical problems, **carry forward** intermediate results if the same value is used in multiple sub-parts. > 6. **Always state assumptions** (e.g., "assuming independent demands, identical variability"). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Safety Inventory (Module 3)** ### Core Concepts - [ ] Safety inventory = buffer for demand and supply variability - [ ] Product availability is the **outcome**; safety inventory is the **means** - [ ] Optimal availability balances carrying cost vs stockout cost - [ ] Service level (β) is the probability of NOT stocking out in a cycle ### Critical Formulas to Memorize - [ ] $SS = z \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$ (variable demand, constant lead time) - [ ] $SS = z \cdot \bar{D} \cdot \sigma_L$ (constant demand, variable lead time) - [ ] $SS = z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_L^2}$ (most general) - [ ] $ROP = \bar{D}\bar{L} + SS$ - [ ] $SS_{pooled} = SS_{individual} \cdot \sqrt{n}$ (Square Root Law) ### Must-Know Z-Values - [ ] 90% → 1.28 | 95% → 1.65 | 97.5% → 1.96 | 99% → 2.33 ### Factors Affecting Optimal Availability (5 Categories) - [ ] Demand-side (variability, accuracy, seasonality, life cycle) - [ ] Supply-side (lead time variability, supplier reliability, lot size) - [ ] Cost trade-offs (stockout cost vs carrying cost) - [ ] Product/market (margin, criticality, substitutability, shelf life) - [ ] Network/strategic (aggregation, postponement, information sharing) ### Supply Uncertainty Impact — Key Insights - [ ] Non-linear relationship: doubling σ_L doesn't double SS — it increases by √(1 + new contribution) - [ ] Lead time variance term ($\bar{D}^2 \sigma_L^2$) is often **dominant** when $\bar{D}$ is large - [ ] Improving supplier reliability is one of the **highest-ROI** safety stock reduction strategies ### Aggregation / Risk Pooling — Key Insights - [ ] Square root law applies only to **independent** demand streams - [ ] Heterogeneous items use $SS_{pooled} = \sqrt{\sum SS_i^2}$ - [ ] Centralization trades **transportation cost** for **inventory cost** - [ ] Risk pooling is a foundational concept for **network design decisions** - [ ] Works exceptionally well for **substitutable products** (e.g., variants of a model) ### KTU Exam Strategy Tips - [ ] Always **define variables** before substituting into formulas - [ ] Always **state assumptions** (e.g., normal distribution, independence) - [ ] Always **comment on managerial implications** in long-answer questions - [ ] In numerical problems, show **all intermediate steps** - [ ] For 14-mark questions, include a **diagram** wherever possible (Mermaid or hand-drawn equivalent) — KTU awards 1–2 marks for visual representation

topics.title: Logistics Management and its Components; Modes of Transportation and their Performance Characteristics and Transportation Infrastructure Policies; Design Options for a Transportation and Logistics Network; Trade-offs in Transportation Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Logistics Management and its Components; Modes of Transportation and their Performance Characteristics and Transportation Infrastructure Policies; Design Options for a Transportation and Logistics Network; Trade-offs in Transportation Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Logistics Management — Formal Definition

**Logistics Management** is the part of supply chain management that plans, implements, and controls the efficient, effective forward and reverse flow and storage of goods, services, and related information between the point of origin and the point of consumption in order to meet customer requirements.

*(Source-aligned definition, CSCMP / Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize**
> "Logistics Management is the process of strategically managing the movement, storage, and flow of goods, services, and information from origin to consumption to satisfy customer demands, while optimizing cost, speed, and reliability."

Logistics is the **operational backbone** of any supply chain. While Supply Chain Management (SCM) is the strategic orchestration of *all* partners, channels, and activities, Logistics Management is the **executive arm** that physically moves the product.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body Analogy"

Imagine your body as a company:
- The **brain** = Strategic SCM (planning, partnerships)
- The **heart** = Logistics Management (pumps blood — the equivalent of moving goods)
- The **veins & arteries** = Transportation networks
- The **warehouses (liver, lungs)** = Storage & inventory nodes
- The **nervous system** = Information flow (orders, tracking)

Just as a healthy body requires the heart to pump blood efficiently to every organ, a healthy supply chain needs logistics to deliver the right product, in the right quantity, in the right condition, to the right place, at the right time — at the right cost (**the 7 R's of Logistics**).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7 R's of Logistics (Key Framework)**
> 1. **Right Product**
> 2. **Right Quantity**
> 3. **Right Condition**
> 4. **Right Place**
> 5. **Right Time**
> 6. **Right Customer**
> 7. **Right Cost (Right Price)**

## 1.2 The 7 R's — Expanded View

| R # | R-Name | Real-World Example (Flipkart / Amazon) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Right Product | Delivering a Samsung phone, not a Nokia |
| 2 | Right Quantity | 1 unit ordered → 1 unit delivered |
| 3 | Right Condition | No physical damage, working battery |
| 4 | Right Place | Customer's address in Kochi |
| 5 | Right Time | Within 24-hour delivery promise |
| 6 | Right Customer | Verified recipient |
| 7 | Right Cost | Free delivery or priced correctly |

## 1.3 Components of Logistics Management

Logistics is composed of several interconnected operational activities, often abbreviated and grouped differently by KTU textbooks. Below is the **canonical 6-component framework** used across KTU 2024 reference material:

### 1.3.1 Primary (Key) Components

1. **Transportation** — Movement of goods between locations (factories, warehouses, customers). The single largest logistics cost (~**60%** of total logistics cost).
2. **Inventory Management** — Holding raw materials, WIP, and finished goods to buffer demand-supply mismatches.
3. **Warehousing & Storage** — Holding inventory at fixed locations; cross-docking, fulfillment centers.
4. **Order Processing** — Capturing, transmitting, and fulfilling customer orders (the information trigger).
5. **Materials Handling** — Movement of goods *within* a facility (forklifts, conveyors, AGVs).
6. **Packaging** — Protection, identification, and unitization of goods for safe transit.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember the 6 Components — "T-I-W-O-M-P"**
> **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **W**arehousing, **O**rder Processing, **M**aterials Handling, **P**ackaging.

### 1.3.2 Supporting Components

- **Facility Location (Network Design)** — Where to place plants, warehouses, DCs
- **Sourcing & Procurement** — Inbound logistics
- **Reverse Logistics** — Returns, recycling, repairs
- **Information Systems** — WMS, TMS, ERP
- **Demand Planning & Forecasting**

## 1.4 Logistics Cost Structure — The Big Picture

Logistics cost is typically **7–10% of GDP** in developed economies; in India ~**14–16% of GDP** (World Bank reports). The dominant cost bucket is transportation.

| Cost Bucket | % of Total Logistics Cost | KTU Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Transportation | **55–60%** | Highest contributor |
| Inventory Carrying | **20–25%** | Includes capital + obsolescence |
| Warehousing | **8–10%** | Fixed + variable |
| Order Processing | **5–7%** | IT + labor |
| Materials Handling | **3–5%** | Mostly in-plant |
| Packaging | **2–3%** | Lowest visible cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake**
> Students often treat "Logistics = Transportation only." This is a **definition error** worth 2–3 marks. Always state: *Logistics = Transportation + Inventory + Warehousing + Order Processing + Materials Handling + Packaging (+ Information Flow).*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Each Component

### 2.1.1 Transportation
The physical movement of goods from origin to destination. It is the most visible logistics activity and the largest cost driver.

- **In-bound (procurement) logistics**: Raw materials to factory
- **Out-bound (distribution) logistics**: Finished goods to customer
- **Internal transport**: Inter-facility movement
- **Reverse transport**: Returns, recycling

### 2.1.2 Inventory Management
Balancing availability against cost. KTU focuses on three inventory decisions:
- **Cycle stock** — working inventory from order cycles
- **Safety stock** — buffer against demand/supply variability
- **Pipeline stock** — in-transit inventory

### 2.1.3 Warehousing
Fixed-location storage that enables consolidation, break-bulk, postponement, and value-added services. KTU distinguishes:
- **Storage warehouses** — long-term holding
- **Distribution centers (DCs)** — fast-moving, cross-docking
- **Fulfillment centers** — e-commerce, picker-to-box
- **Bonded warehouses** — customs-related

### 2.1.4 Order Processing
The information trigger of the entire chain. Includes:
- Order entry → order transmission → order picking → order shipping → invoicing
- KTU emphasizes **Order Cycle Time (OCT)** as a key performance metric

### 2.1.5 Materials Handling
Efficient movement *within* a warehouse/factory. Driven by the **3 R's**: Right quantity, Right place, Right time (internally).

### 2.1.6 Packaging
Three functions:
- **Containment** — holding the product
- **Protection** — physical + environmental
- **Communication** — labeling, branding, regulatory

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Load Concept (KTU Frequently Asked)**
> A *unit load* groups multiple individual items into a single handling unit (e.g., a pallet of 48 cases, a container of 24 pallets). The goal is to **minimize the number of handling operations** to reduce labor cost and damage.

## 2.2 Modes of Transportation — Performance Characteristics

KTU 2024 Scheme requires a comparative understanding of all five primary modes.

### 2.2.1 The Five Modes — Overview

1. **Road (Truck) Transport**
2. **Rail Transport**
3. **Air Transport**
4. **Water (Maritime/Inland) Transport**
5. **Pipeline Transport**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Modes of Transport Performance Matrix (High-Yield Table)**
> Memorize this **2×5** matrix for 7 to 14-mark questions.

| Performance Criterion | Road | Rail | Air | Water | Pipeline |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Fast (regional) | Medium | Fastest (long-haul) | Slowest | Continuous (medium) |
| **Cost** | Moderate–High | Low (long-haul) | Highest | Lowest (bulk) | Very Low |
| **Reliability** | Medium | High | Very High | Low (weather) | Very High |
| **Availability** | Very High (door-to-door) | Medium (track-bound) | Medium (airports) | Low (ports/rivers) | Lowest (fixed) |
| **Flexibility** | Highest | Low | Medium | Lowest | Lowest |
| **Capacity** | Limited | High | Lowest | Highest | Highest |
| **Frequency** | High | Medium | High | Low | Continuous |
| **Product Suitability** | FMCG, manufactured goods | Bulk raw materials, coal, grain | High-value, perishables, urgent | Bulky, low-value, non-perishable | Liquids, gases, slurries |
| **Environmental Impact** | High (CO₂) | Low–Medium | Very High | Low (per ton-km) | Lowest |
| **Infrastructure Cost** | Roads (moderate) | Tracks (high) | Airports (very high) | Ports (very high) | Pipelines (very high) |

### 2.2.2 Detailed Performance Characteristics

**A. Road Transport (Trucking)**
- **Strengths**: Door-to-door service, last-mile flexibility, low capital requirement for truck owner
- **Weaknesses**: High pollution, traffic congestion, driver shortage, accident rate
- **Best for**: Short to medium hauls (<500 km), high-value, time-sensitive
- **Indian context**: Carries **~60% of freight by ton-km** in India (highest modal share by far)

**B. Rail Transport**
- **Strengths**: Economical for long hauls, energy-efficient, high capacity
- **Weaknesses**: Limited door service, fixed routes, schedule rigidity
- **Best for**: Coal, iron ore, grain, cement, fertilizer, bulk liquids
- **Indian context**: Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFC), Gati Shakti initiative

**C. Air Transport**
- **Strengths**: Fastest, reliable for time-critical
- **Weaknesses**: Highest cost per kg, capacity limit, weather dependent, security restrictions
- **Best for**: Perishables, high-value-low-weight (electronics, pharma), emergency spares

**D. Water Transport (Maritime + Inland Waterways)**
- **Strengths**: Lowest cost for bulk, large capacity (ULCVs), eco-friendly per ton-km
- **Weaknesses**: Very slow, weather risk, port congestion, theft in some regions
- **Best for**: Crude oil, containers (FCL/LCL), iron ore, grain, cement
- **Modes**: Ocean shipping, coastal shipping, inland waterways (India: NW-1, NW-2, NW-3, NW-4)

**E. Pipeline Transport**
- **Strengths**: Continuous, low operating cost, low labor, weather-independent
- **Weaknesses**: High capital cost, single-commodity, fixed route, security risk
- **Best for**: Crude oil, natural gas, LPG, refined products, slurry (coal-water), water

### 2.2.3 Intermodal & Multimodal Transport

| Term | Definition | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unimodal** | Single mode throughout | Full truckload (FTL) by road |
| **Multimodal** | Multiple modes; one carrier arranges all | Maersk ocean+road door-to-door |
| **Intermodal** | Multiple modes; each leg separate carrier | Road (origin) → Rail (long-haul) → Road (destination) |
| **Co-modal** | Optimal mode selection, possibly single | Strategic mix based on cost-time-service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Containerization (Revolution in Logistics)**
> Standardized **ISO containers (20 ft TEU, 40 ft FEU)** enabled seamless intermodal transfer. A 20 ft container holds ~33 m³; the world moves **>800 million TEU annually** (UNCTAD 2023).

## 2.3 Transportation Infrastructure Policies (India-Focused, KTU Expectation)

KTU 2024 expects awareness of national policies. The key ones:

| Policy / Initiative | Launch Year | Key Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **National Logistics Policy (NLP)** | 2022 | Reduce logistics cost from 14–16% to ~9% of GDP |
| **PM Gati Shakti – National Master Plan** | 2021 | Integrated multimodal infrastructure planning |
| **Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFC)** | Eastern + Western | High-capacity rail freight |
| **Sagarmala Programme** | 2015 | Port modernization, coastal shipping |
| **Bharatmala Pariyojana** | 2017 | Highway + expressway network |
| **UDAN (Ude Desh ka Aam Nagrik)** | 2016 | Regional airport connectivity |
| **Jal Marg Vikas Project** | 2018 | Inland waterways (NW-1 on Ganga) |
| **PM Urja Ganga** | 2016 | Gas pipeline infrastructure |
| **Multi-Modal Logistics Parks (MMLPs)** | Ongoing | Integrated freight handling hubs |
| **Logistics Performance Index (LPI)** | World Bank | India's rank 2023: **38** (improved from 44 in 2018) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Definitions to Memorize**
> - **Logistics Cost (% of GDP)**: Total logistics spend / National GDP
> - **LPI (Logistics Performance Index)**: World Bank ranking on Customs, Infrastructure, Shipments, Logistics Quality, Timeliness, Tracking
> - **Gati Shakti**: GIS-based platform for 16 ministries to coordinate infrastructure projects

## 2.4 Design Options for a Transportation & Logistics Network

A logistics network is the set of facilities (plants, warehouses, DCs, cross-docks) and the transport links between them. KTU 2024 focuses on **4 classical design options** plus modern variants.

### 2.4.1 The Four Classical Network Designs

| # | Design Option | Facility Footprint | Inventory Level | Transport Outbound | Lead Time | Cost Profile |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Direct Shipping** | Many plants → Direct to customer | Lowest | High (small vehicles) | Shortest | High transport |
| 2 | **Direct Shipping with Warehousing** | Many plants → Some warehouses → Customers | Low | Medium (consolidated) | Medium | Balanced |
| 3 | **Milk Run / Consolidation** | Many suppliers → One DC → Plant | Low inbound | High outbound | Medium | Low inbound |
| 4 | **Centralized Hub (All-in-One)** | 1 Plant → 1 Warehouse → All customers | High | High (long-haul) | Longest | High inventory |

### 2.4.2 Expanded Design Options (Modern / KTU Expectation)

**Option 5: Cross-Docking Network**
- Inventory *temporarily* held at a hub
- Goods received, sorted, and dispatched within **<24 hours**
- Minimal or zero storage; pure transit
- Used by: Walmart, Amazon (sortation centers)

**Option 6: Hybrid / Tiered Network**
- Centralized inventory at one hub
- Regional distribution centers (RDCs) feed local
- Common in e-commerce (Amazon's FC → Last-mile)

**Option 7: Postponement Network**
- Generic products stored centrally
- Customization (labeling, packaging, kitting) at the *last possible moment*
- Reduces obsolete inventory risk

**Option 8: Decentralized / Saturated Network**
- Multiple regional plants serving regional markets
- Highest inventory; lowest transport cost
- Used by: Bakeries, dairy, newspapers

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet is mandatory for 14-mark design/numerical questions. Memorize units and notations.**

| # | Formula | Variables | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TC = FC + VC \cdot Q$ | $TC$ = Total Cost, $FC$ = Fixed, $VC$ = Variable per unit, $Q$ = Quantity | Break-even / facility sizing |
| 2 | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = Annual demand, $S$ = Order cost, $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year | Economic order quantity (logistics cost optimization) |
| 3 | $Q^\* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H \cdot (1 - r/p)}}$ | Same + $r$ = Reorder point terms | Production Order Quantity (POQ) |
| 4 | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $z$ = service-level z-score, $\sigma_{LT}$ = std dev of demand, $L$ = lead time (periods) | Safety stock calculation |
| 5 | $DC = D \cdot L$ | $D$ = avg demand per period, $L$ = lead time | Reorder point / cycle stock |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{Q}{D}$ | $T$ = Cycle time, $Q$ = Order qty, $D$ = Demand rate | Order frequency |
| 7 | $N = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot h}{2 \cdot s \cdot k}}$ | $D$ = annual demand, $h$ = holding, $s$ = setup, $k$ = vehicle capacity | # of deliveries / vehicle routing (Harris-Wilson) |
| 8 | $T_e = T_d + T_p + T_s + T_t$ | Order entry, pick, ship, transport | Order Cycle Time (OCT) |
| 9 | $Inventory\ Carrying\ Cost = C \cdot i$ | $C$ = unit cost, $i$ = carrying rate (typically 0.20–0.30) | Annual holding cost per unit |
| 10 | $Transport\ Cost\ per\ unit = \dfrac{FC + VC \cdot D}{D}$ | FC = freight fixed, VC = variable per ton-km | Logistics cost minimization |
| 11 | $C_{t,i} = a_i \cdot d_{ij}$ | Cost proportional to distance | Weber facility location |
| 12 | $C_{ij} = R \cdot D_{ij} + F$ | $R$ = rate/km, $D$ = distance, $F$ = fixed freight | Tariff models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation note:** KTU uses both $D$ (demand) and $Q$ (quantity) interchangeably in textbooks; the meaning is contextual. Always read the question's "Given Data" carefully.

## 2.6 Why These Design Options Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Direct shipping** is used in **ready-mix concrete** (short time-window) and **fresh dairy** (very short shelf life).
- **Cross-docking** is used in **Walmart's apparel** and **Amazon Pantry** for fast-moving groceries.
- **Centralized hub** is used in **fashion e-commerce (Myntra, Zalando)** for size/return management.
- **Hybrid tiered** dominates **e-commerce giants** like Amazon (FC → Sortation → Last-mile).
- **Postponement** is the **Dell model** — generic PC built in Asia, customized at regional hub.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Approach for 14-Mark Design Question**
> 1. State the design chosen (1 mark)
> 2. Justify using 3 criteria (cost, speed, inventory) (3 marks)
> 3. Sketch the network (2 marks)
> 4. Calculate key metrics (EOQ, OCT, transport cost) (5 marks)
> 5. Discuss trade-offs and risks (3 marks)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — The Wilson Formula

The EOQ formula gives the order quantity that minimizes the **Total Logistics Cost** = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost.

### Step 1 — Set Up Cost Functions

Let:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units/order)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)

**Annual Ordering Cost (OC):**

$$OC = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

(Explanation: Number of orders per year = $D \div Q$; multiply by cost per order = $S$.)

**Annual Holding Cost (HC):**

$$HC = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

(Explanation: Average inventory = $Q \div 2$ since inventory linearly depletes from $Q$ to $0$. Multiply by holding cost per unit per year.)

**Total Logistics Cost (TLC):**

$$TLC = OC + HC = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Minimize TLC with Respect to $Q$

Take the first derivative of $TLC$ w.r.t. $Q$ and set it to zero.

$$\dfrac{d(TLC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $Q$

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}$$

$$Q^\* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}$$

**This is the classical Wilson-Harris EOQ formula** — derivable for 2 to 3 marks in a KTU 14-mark question.

### Step 4 — Verify Minimum (Second Derivative Test)

$$\dfrac{d^2(TLC)}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{Q^3} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, the function is convex, confirming a **minimum** at $Q^\*$.

### Step 5 — Substitute Back to Get Minimum Total Cost

Plug $Q^\*$ into the TLC equation:

$$TLC_{min} = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}} + \dfrac{H}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$

$$TLC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

A clean, elegant result: the **minimum total cost = $\sqrt{2DSH}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At $Q^\*$, the ordering cost equals the holding cost.
> $OC = HC \Rightarrow \dfrac{DS}{Q} = \dfrac{HQ}{2} \Rightarrow Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H}$. Verified.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Transportation Network Cost

**Problem (KTU 2024 Module 4 Style):**

A company needs to ship 50,000 tons/year from Plant $P$ to Warehouse $W$. Three mode options exist. Compute total logistics cost for each and recommend the optimal mode.

| Mode | Rate (₹/ton-km) | Fixed Annual Cost (₹) | Distance (km) | Lead Time (days) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Road | 3.5 | 200,000 | 600 | 2 |
| Rail | 1.5 | 800,000 | 600 | 4 |
| Pipeline | 0.8 | 5,000,000 | 600 | Continuous |

### Step 1 — Compute Variable Cost (VC) per Mode

$$VC_{Road} = 3.5 \times 600 = 2{,}100 \text{ ₹/ton}$$

$$VC_{Rail} = 1.5 \times 600 = 900 \text{ ₹/ton}$$

$$VC_{Pipeline} = 0.8 \times 600 = 480 \text{ ₹/ton}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total Annual Cost per Mode

$$TC_{mode} = (D \times VC_{mode}) + FC_{mode}$$

$$TC_{Road} = (50{,}000 \times 2{,}100) + 200{,}000 = 105{,}200{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$TC_{Rail} = (50{,}000 \times 900) + 800{,}000 = 45{,}800{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$TC_{Pipeline} = (50{,}000 \times 480) + 5{,}000{,}000 = 29{,}000{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 3 — Build Comparison Table

| Mode | Total Annual Cost (₹ Cr) | Lead Time | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Road | 10.52 | 2 days | Costliest, fastest |
| Rail | 4.58 | 4 days | Moderate |
| Pipeline | **2.90** | Continuous | Cheapest if commodity compatible |

### Step 4 — Add Inventory Carrying Cost (Pipeline Scenario)

Continuous flow = pipeline stock ≈ 0 → **no extra inventory carrying**.

For Rail, average pipeline stock = $(D \times L) / 365 = (50{,}000 \times 4) / 365 = 548$ units.
Inventory carrying cost @ 25% of ₹5,000/unit = $548 \times 1{,}250 = 685{,}000$ ₹/year.

$$TC_{Rail}^{adjusted} = 4.58 + 0.0685 = 4.65 \text{ Cr ₹}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Rule:** Even with adjusted cost, **Pipeline is cheapest** if the product is liquid/gas. For non-liquid goods, **Rail wins** with a comfortable margin.

## 3.3 Derivation: Safety Stock (Service-Level Approach)

Customer demand fluctuates; lead time varies. KTU expects the safety stock formula:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

Where:
- $z$ = Standard normal z-score for desired service level (e.g., 95% → $z = 1.65$, 97.5% → $z = 1.96$, 99% → $z = 2.33$)
- $\sigma_d$ = Standard deviation of daily demand
- $L$ = Lead time in days

**Worked Numerical:**

Demand = 100 units/day, $\sigma_d = 15$ units, $L = 10$ days, service level = 95%.

$$SS = 1.65 \times 15 \times \sqrt{10}$$

$$SS = 1.65 \times 15 \times 3.1623$$

$$SS \approx 78.27 \approx 79 \text{ units}$$

**Reorder Point (ROP):**

$$ROP = \overline{d} \cdot L + SS = 100 \times 10 + 79 = 1{,}079 \text{ units}$$

## 3.4 Trade-offs in Transportation Design — The Core Dilemma

There are **four canonical trade-offs** KTU 2024 expects students to articulate:

### Trade-off 1: Transport Cost vs. Inventory Cost
- **More transport (direct shipping)** → Lower inventory, higher transport
- **Less transport (consolidated hub)** → Higher inventory, lower transport
- **Sweet spot**: $TC_{transport} = TC_{inventory}$

### Trade-off 2: Number of Facilities vs. Customer Service
- **More warehouses** → Closer to customers, faster delivery, but more fixed cost
- **Fewer warehouses** → Lower fixed cost, but slower, higher transport

### Trade-off 3: Speed vs. Cost
- **Air (fast, costly)** vs. **Water (slow, cheap)** — the classic pyramid
- Decision based on product value, perishability, urgency

### Trade-off 4: Centralization vs. Decentralization
- **Centralization**: Lower inventory, scale economies, but risk of single point of failure
- **Decentralization**: Resilience, fast response, but duplicated inventory

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Network Design

KTU 2024 frequently tests: "Given scenario X, choose the design." The decision matrix:

| Scenario Indicator | Recommended Design |
|---|---|
| High demand density, low variety | Direct Shipping |
| High demand density, high variety | Direct + Warehousing |
| Many low-volume sources | Milk Run / Consolidation |
| Low demand density, high variety | Centralized Hub |
| Need for very fast response | Cross-Docking |
| High customization at customer end | Postponement Network |
| Bulky/fragile products | Hybrid Tiered |
| Perishables | Direct Shipping (cold chain) |
| E-commerce (B2C) | Hybrid (FC → Last Mile) |
| National distribution (B2B) | Multi-tier (Plant → RDC → Customer) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Decision Rule (Memorize)**
> When in doubt, apply the **"Cost-Service-Speed" triangle**:
> 1. **Cost** → minimizes total landed cost
> 2. **Service** → meets customer SLA
> 3. **Speed** → meets lead time
> Pick the design that **balances all three** — and explicitly mention the trade-off you accepted.

## 3.6 Consolidated Analytical Comparison Table — All Design Options

| Criterion | Direct Shipping | Direct + Warehouse | Milk Run | Centralized Hub | Cross-Dock | Hybrid | Postponement |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory Level** | Lowest | Low | Low | Highest | Very Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Transport Cost** | Highest | High | Low inbound | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Facility Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Low | High | Medium | High | High |
| **Lead Time** | Shortest | Short | Medium | Longest | Short | Medium | Medium |
| **Flexibility** | High | High | Low | Lowest | High | High | Highest |
| **Best for Product** | High value, urgent | Standard | Sourcing | Long-tail | FMCG | E-com | Customized |
| **Risk** | Capacity | Stockout | Single point | Long lead | Damage | Complex | Obsolescence |
| **Indian Example** | Cold-chain dairy | FMCG distribution | Auto ancillaries | Apparel e-com | BigBasket grocery | Amazon India | Custom laptops |
