# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1: Define Logistics Management and list its key components.  *(3 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Logistics Management is the part of supply chain management that plans, implements, and controls the efficient, effective forward and reverse flow and storage of goods, services, and related information from the point of origin to the point of consumption to meet customer requirements.

The **six key components** are:
1. Transportation
2. Inventory Management
3. Warehousing & Storage
4. Order Processing
5. Materials Handling
6. Packaging

*(3 marks — definition 1.5, components list 1.5)*

---

### Question A2: Differentiate between Multimodal and Intermodal transport with one example each.  *(3 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Multimodal | Intermodal |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Carriers** | **One** carrier manages entire journey (issue single contract/document) | **Multiple** carriers, each for one leg |
| **Documentation** | Single through-document (e.g., FIATA Bill of Lading) | Separate document per leg |
| **Liability** | One party liable end-to-end | Each carrier liable for own leg |
| **Example** | Maersk arranging ship+truck door-to-door | Truck → Indian Railways → Truck, with separate contracts |

*(3 marks — 1.5 for differentiation, 1.5 for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-Option A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**(a) Explain in detail the five major modes of transportation with their performance characteristics.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The five primary modes of transport in any logistics network are:

**1. Road Transport (Trucking)**
- **Performance**: Door-to-door, highly flexible, fast for short distances (<500 km)
- **Advantages**: High availability, low capital for truck owners, no transshipment
- **Limitations**: High pollution, traffic congestion, accident rate, limited capacity
- **Best for**: FMCG, e-commerce last-mile, perishables
- **Indian share**: ~**60% of freight by ton-km**

**2. Rail Transport**
- **Performance**: Economical for long hauls, high capacity, schedule-based
- **Advantages**: Energy-efficient, low cost per ton-km, handles bulk
- **Limitations**: Fixed tracks, limited door service, schedule rigidity
- **Best for**: Coal, iron ore, grain, cement, fertilizer
- **Indian context**: Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFC) for capacity expansion

**3. Air Transport**
- **Performance**: Fastest, highest reliability, highest cost
- **Advantages**: Time-definite, secure, ideal for perishables
- **Limitations**: Capacity bottleneck, weather delays, security restrictions
- **Best for**: High-value-low-weight (electronics, pharma, gems)
- **Indian context**: UDAN scheme expanding regional airport reach

**4. Water Transport (Maritime + Inland)**
- **Performance**: Slowest but cheapest for bulk
- **Advantages**: Massive capacity (ULCVs carry >24,000 TEU), low cost, eco-friendly per ton-km
- **Limitations**: Port congestion, slow transit, weather risk
- **Best for**: Crude, containers, dry bulk, low-value items
- **Indian context**: Sagarmala, Inland Waterways (NW-1 on Ganga)

**5. Pipeline Transport**
- **Performance**: Continuous flow, very low labor, weather-independent
- **Advantages**: Lowest operating cost, fully automated, no packaging
- **Limitations**: Single commodity, fixed route, very high capital cost
- **Best for**: Crude oil, natural gas, LPG, refined products, slurry
- **Indian context**: PM Urja Ganga pipeline project

**Comparison Summary (Performance Matrix):**
- Fastest: **Air**; Slowest: **Water**
- Cheapest: **Pipeline** (operating) / **Water** (capital)
- Most flexible: **Road**; Least flexible: **Pipeline**
- Highest capacity: **Water** / **Pipeline**; Lowest: **Air**
- Most reliable: **Pipeline**; Least: **Water**

*(7 marks — 1 mark per mode for description, 1 mark for performance matrix summary, 1 mark for Indian context)*

---

**(b) Discuss the National Logistics Policy (NLP) 2022 of India and the PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan. Explain how they aim to reduce logistics cost.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**National Logistics Policy (NLP), 2022 — Key Pillars:**

1. **Integration of Digital Systems (ULIP)** — Unified Logistics Interface Platform connects 40+ systems (GSTN, ICEGATE, FASTag, etc.) for paperless, real-time tracking.
2. **Standardization of Physical Assets** — ISO-standard containers, RFID tags, barcode harmonization.
3. **Logistics Human Resource Development** — Skill Council for Green Logistics, university programs.
4. **Service Improvement Framework** — Time-bound grievance redressal, ease-of-logistics-business score.

**Targeted outcome**: Reduce logistics cost from **14–16% of GDP (2020)** to **~9% of GDP (2030)**, comparable to global benchmark (8–9%).

**PM Gati Shakti — National Master Plan (2021):**

- **Concept**: GIS-based integrated platform covering **16 ministries** (Railways, Roadways, Aviation, Shipping, Power, Telecom, etc.)
- **Objective**: Break the siloed planning; coordinate infrastructure investments to avoid duplication
- **Key Features**:
  - Single digital platform with **8 economic corridors**
  - Visibility of existing and planned projects on one map
  - Prioritization based on multimodal connectivity

**How They Reduce Logistics Cost:**

| Lever | Mechanism | Cost Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Integration** | Eliminates paperwork, reduces dwell time at ports | Saves ~1–2% of GDP |
| **Multimodal Coordination** | Reduces last-mile gaps, optimizes modal mix | Saves ~1% of GDP |
| **Standardization** | Easier intermodal transfer, less handling damage | Saves ~0.5% of GDP |
| **Skill Development** | Productivity, fewer accidents | Saves ~0.5% of GDP |
| **Multi-Modal Logistics Parks (MMLPs)** | One-stop hub for warehousing + customs + transport | Saves ~1% of GDP |

**India's LPI Rank Improvement**:
- 2018: 44th
- 2023: **38th** (World Bank, 2023 report) — best-ever rank

*(7 marks — 2 marks NLP, 2 marks Gati Shakti, 3 marks cost-reduction mechanism with table)*

---

### Question B-Option B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**(a) Compare the four classical logistics network design options with a labeled diagram. Discuss the trade-offs involved.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The Four Classical Designs:**

1. **Direct Shipping Network** — Goods flow directly from each plant to each customer with no intermediate warehouse.
2. **Direct Shipping with Warehousing** — Goods go from plant to regional warehouse, then to customer.
3. **Milk Run** — Inbound consolidation: many suppliers → one hub → plant.
4. **Centralized Hub** — One plant, one warehouse, all customers served from that hub.

**Network Diagram (to be drawn on paper):**

```
Option 1 (Direct):
Plant A -----> Customer 1
Plant A -----> Customer 2
Plant A -----> Customer 3

Option 2 (Direct + Warehouse):
Plant A -----> Warehouse -----> Customer 1
                       \------> Customer 2
                        \-----> Customer 3

Option 3 (Milk Run):
Supplier 1 --\
Supplier 2 ---+---> Hub DC ---> Plant
Supplier 3 --/

Option 4 (Centralized):
Plant ---> Central Hub ---> All customers
```

**Trade-off Analysis:**

| Trade-off | Direct Shipping | Direct + WH | Milk Run | Centralized Hub |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transport cost | **High** | Medium | **Low (inbound)** | Low (consolidated) |
| Inventory level | **Lowest** | Low | Low | **Highest** |
| Lead time | **Shortest** | Short | Medium | **Longest** |
| Facility fixed cost | Lowest | Medium | Low | **Highest** |
| Customer service | High | High | N/A (inbound) | Lower (distant) |
| Risk profile | Capacity strain | Moderate | Hub dependency | Hub failure = total stop |

**Key Trade-off Dilemma:**

- **Transport cost ⇄ Inventory cost** — More direct shipping = more transport cost but less inventory.
- **Facility cost ⇄ Customer service** — More warehouses = more facility cost but better service.
- **Speed ⇄ Cost** — Fast modes (air) cost more; cheap modes (water) take longer.

**Decision Criterion:** Optimize Total Landed Cost = $TC_{transport} + TC_{inventory} + TC_{facility} + TC_{service-penalty}$.

*(7 marks — 2 marks diagram, 3 marks trade-off table, 2 marks decision criterion)*

---

**(b) A company has annual demand of 80,000 units. Ordering cost is ₹1,200 per order. Holding cost is ₹30 per unit per year. Calculate (i) EOQ, (ii) Total Logistics Cost at EOQ, (iii) Number of orders per year, (iv) Cycle time.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 80{,}000$ units/year
- $S = ₹1{,}200$/order
- $H = ₹30$/unit/year

**Step (i) — Compute EOQ:**

$$Q^\* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}$$

$$Q^\* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 80{,}000 \times 1{,}200}{30}}$$

$$Q^\* = \sqrt{\dfrac{192{,}000{,}000}{30}}$$

$$Q^\* = \sqrt{6{,}400{,}000}$$

$$Q^\* = 2{,}529.82 \approx 2{,}530 \text{ units}$$

**Step (ii) — Total Logistics Cost at EOQ:**

$$TLC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot S \cdot H}$$

$$TLC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 80{,}000 \times 1{,}200 \times 30}$$

$$TLC_{min} = \sqrt{5{,}760{,}000{,}000}$$

$$TLC_{min} = 75{,}894.66 \approx ₹75{,}895$$

**Verification by substitution:**

$$OC = \dfrac{80{,}000}{2{,}530} \times 1{,}200 = 37{,}945$$

$$HC = \dfrac{2{,}530}{2} \times 30 = 37{,}950$$

$$TLC \approx 37{,}945 + 37{,}950 = ₹75{,}895$$ ✓ (small rounding difference, both OC and HC are nearly equal at optimum)

**Step (iii) — Number of Orders per Year:**

$$N = \dfrac{D}{Q^\*} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{2{,}530} = 31.62 \approx 32 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step (iv) — Cycle Time (Time Between Orders):**

$$T = \dfrac{Q^\*}{D} \times 365 = \dfrac{2{,}530}{80{,}000} \times 365 = 11.54 \text{ days}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks EOQ derivation, 2 marks TLC, 1 mark for orders, 2 marks cycle time + verification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to show EOQ derivation** — KTU awards the formula 1 mark but the *derivation* an additional 1 mark. Show the calculus (dTLC/dQ = 0).
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — Always state units (₹/year, units/year, days). No unit = no credit.
> 3. **Confusing $D$ with $Q$** — $D$ is demand rate; $Q$ is order quantity. Mixing them up = 0.
> 4. **Skipping the trade-off discussion** — In network design questions, *trade-off articulation* carries 2–3 marks; just listing the design is not enough.
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** — KTU explicitly awards 1–2 marks for a network sketch. Always draw.
> 6. **Vague transport-mode justification** — Saying "road is good" gets 0; saying "road gives door-to-door delivery, lead time of 2 days, suited to our 600 km distance and high-value product" gets full marks.
> 7. **Forgetting Indian context** — KTU is a Kerala-based university; national policy questions (NLP, Gati Shakti) are *expected* to reference Indian data (LPI rank, % of GDP, DFC, Sagarmala).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

### **A. Definitions (must memorize verbatim)**
- [ ] Logistics Management (CSCMP-aligned definition)
- [ ] The 7 R's of Logistics
- [ ] Logistics Cost % of GDP (India: 14–16% → target 9%)
- [ ] LPI rank (India 2023: 38)
- [ ] NLP launch year (2022), Gati Shakti (2021)
- [ ] National Waterways (NW-1 Ganga, NW-2 Brahmaputra, NW-3 Kerala backwaters, NW-4 Krishna-Godavari)
- [ ] TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit), FEU (Forty-foot)
- [ ] Intermodal vs. Multimodal vs. Co-modal

### **B. Modes of Transport — Key Facts**
- [ ] **Fastest mode**: Air; **Slowest**: Water
- [ ] **Cheapest operating cost**: Pipeline; **Highest cost**: Air
- [ ] **Most flexible**: Road; **Least flexible**: Pipeline
- [ ] **Highest capacity**: Water (ocean) and Pipeline; **Lowest**: Air
- [ ] **Indian modal share** (freight): Road ~60%, Rail ~30%, Water ~6%, Pipeline ~3%, Air <1%
- [ ] **Pipeline** is continuous; no inventory carrying cost in transit
- [ ] **Containerization** enabled intermodal; ISO 20-ft and 40-ft standard

### **C. Logistics Components (Mnemonic: T-I-W-O-M-P)**
- [ ] Transportation (55–60% of cost)
- [ ] Inventory Management (EOQ, Safety Stock, ROP)
- [ ] Warehousing (Storage, DC, FC, Cross-Dock, Bonded)
- [ ] Order Processing (OCT = entry + pick + ship + transport)
- [ ] Materials Handling (within-facility, 3 R's)
- [ ] Packaging (Contain, Protect, Communicate)

### **D. Network Design — 5 Options**
- [ ] Direct Shipping — low inventory, high transport
- [ ] Direct + Warehouse — balanced
- [ ] Milk Run — inbound consolidation
- [ ] Centralized Hub — high inventory, low transport
- [ ] Cross-Dock — <24-hour transit, no storage
- [ ] Hybrid Tiered — modern e-commerce
- [ ] Postponement — late customization
- [ ] Decentralized — fast, resilient, costly

### **E. Trade-offs (4 cardinal ones)**
- [ ] Transport Cost ⇄ Inventory Cost
- [ ] Number of Facilities ⇄ Customer Service
- [ ] Speed ⇄ Cost
- [ ] Centralization ⇄ Decentralization (resilience)

### **F. Formula Cheat-Sheet (must know)**
- [ ] $EOQ = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Wilson Formula
- [ ] $TLC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$
- [ ] $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ — Safety Stock
- [ ] $ROP = \overline{d} \cdot L + SS$ — Reorder Point
- [ ] $T = Q/D \times 365$ — Cycle time
- [ ] $N = D/Q$ — Number of orders

### **G. Indian Policy Initiatives (must remember)**
- [ ] National Logistics Policy 2022
- [ ] PM Gati Shakti 2021
- [ ] Bharatmala (roads), Sagarmala (ports), UDAN (air), Jal Marg Vikas (water), Urja Ganga (pipeline)
- [ ] Dedicated Freight Corridors (Eastern + Western)
- [ ] Multi-Modal Logistics Parks (MMLPs)

### **H. Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
- [ ] Don't confuse Logistics = Transportation (it's much broader)
- [ ] Don't write formulas without units
- [ ] Don't skip the network diagram in 14-mark questions
- [ ] Don't ignore trade-off discussion
- [ ] Don't state transport choice without 3-justify (cost, speed, suitability)

---

> **END OF KTU MODULE 4 NOTES — PESFT867 SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT**
> *Best of luck for the University Examination! Focus on the trade-off discussion, the formula derivations, and the Indian policy context for full marks.*

topics.title: Tailored Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tailored Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tailored Transportation** refers to the strategic design, customization, and operation of transportation services, modes, and networks that are specifically engineered to match the unique requirements of a particular product, customer segment, supply chain configuration, or business model. It moves beyond generic freight movement to deliver purpose-built logistics solutions that optimize for **cost, speed, reliability, security, visibility, and sustainability** simultaneously.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Tailored Transportation is the **fourth pillar** of integrated logistics design (preceded by network design, inventory positioning, and warehousing strategy). It treats transportation not as a generic cost line but as a **value-generating, customer-differentiating capability** that aligns the *transportation function* with the *order-winning criteria* of the supply chain.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Transportation Customization** | Configuring mode, carrier, route, and service level to a specific SKUs profile |
| **Transportation Mix (Modal Split)** | The percentage allocation of freight across different modes (road, rail, air, sea, pipeline) |
| **Lead-Time Window** | The contractual time band between order placement and delivery at customer dock |
| **Tailored Service Level Agreement (TSLA)** | A carrier contract clause defining service parameters customized to a specific lane or product |
| **Backhauling** | Using a return trip to carry freight instead of running empty (deadheading) |
| **Milk Run** | A multi-stop, cyclic collection/delivery route used in tailored distribution |
| **Consolidation** | Combining small shipments from multiple shippers into a single full load to gain economics of scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Tailored Transportation is positioned as the *execution arm* of a previously designed supply chain network. The examiner will expect students to connect transportation choice to upstream decisions such as warehouse location, inventory policy, and product characteristics.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Custom Tailor" Metaphor

Think of transportation the way a **bespoke tailor** thinks of clothing. A ready-made (off-the-rack) suit fits *most* people *adequately*. But a tailored suit is measured, cut, and stitched to your exact body, posture, and lifestyle — the shoulders are shaped for your gait, the lining is chosen for your climate, the buttons are spaced for your hands.

In the same way:
- **Off-the-rack transportation** = a generic LTL (Less-Than-Truckload) shipment sent on the cheapest lane with standard transit time. It works, but it is not optimized.
- **Tailored transportation** = a *designed* movement solution where the mode, carrier, route, technology, and service level are all chosen to fit *this specific product*, *this specific customer*, and *this specific cost-service trade-off*.

For example, a pharmaceutical company shipping temperature-sensitive insulin from Bangalore to Kochi does not just "book a truck." They design a **cold-chain tailored solution** that includes a reefer truck with real-time temperature telemetry, a validated lane (qualified route), a GDP-compliant (Good Distribution Practice) carrier, geo-fenced checkpoints, and a defined escalation protocol — because the cost of failure is not a delayed delivery but a **public health risk**.

## 1.4 When Does Transportation Need to Be "Tailored"?

Transportation becomes a tailored exercise whenever **at least one** of the following conditions is met:

1. **Product is sensitive** — perishable, hazardous, fragile, high-value, or temperature-controlled.
2. **Customer is critical** — strategic account, just-in-time manufacturer, or e-commerce end-consumer.
3. **Geography is constrained** — last-mile urban, hilly terrain, or intermodal borders.
4. **Volume profile is uneven** — seasonal, promotional, or lumpy demand.
5. **Service level is non-standard** — same-day, time-window, or white-glove delivery.
6. **Sustainability mandate exists** — carbon-neutral, electric fleet, or empty-mile reduction.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Lens:** In KTU theory questions, always begin your answer by identifying *which* of the above triggers justify tailoring. This demonstrates **CO1 (Apply domain knowledge)** and earns easy methodology marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Tailored Transportation Design

Tailored transportation, as examined in the KTU 2024 Scheme, rests on four interlocking design pillars. Each pillar is a decision node that the supply chain manager must consciously configure.

### Pillar 1: Mode Selection
The choice between **Road, Rail, Air, Sea, and Pipeline** — or a multimodal combination.

The decision is governed by five product/service variables:
- **Speed** (transit time requirement)
- **Cost** (per ton-km)
- **Reliability** (on-time performance variance)
- **Capacity** (volume and weight that can be moved)
- **Reach** (geographic accessibility of origin-destination pair)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Expected Answer):** Air is tailored for *high-value, low-weight, time-critical* cargo (e.g., spare parts, medical supplies). Sea is tailored for *low-value, high-volume, time-tolerant* cargo (e.g., commodities). Road is tailored for *short-to-medium hauls with door-to-door* requirement. Rail is tailored for *bulk over land with predictable lanes*.

### Pillar 2: Carrier Selection
Three core carrier archetypes exist in a tailored solution:

| Carrier Type | Strategic Role in Tailored Solution |
|---|---|
| **Asset-Based Carrier (ABC)** | Owns the trucks/containers/ships; offers control and dedicated capacity. Example: FedEx Freight, Maersk Line. |
| **Non-Asset-Based Carrier (NAB)** | Broker or 3PL that aggregates capacity from others; offers flexibility. Example: Coyote Logistics. |
| **Dedicated Contract Carrier** | Exclusive capacity reserved for one shipper; offers service-level customization. Example: PepsiCo's private fleet. |

### Pillar 3: Route and Network Optimization
The tailored route considers:
- **Direct shipment** vs. **Milk Run** vs. **Hub-and-Spoke** vs. **Cross-Docking**
- **Consolidation points** (deciding where smaller shipments merge)
- **Backhaul capture** (reducing empty miles)
- **Border and customs** considerations for international lanes

### Pillar 4: Service Level Customization
The TSLA (Tailored Service Level Agreement) defines:
- Transit time window (e.g., 24-hour, 48-hour, 2–5 day)
- On-time delivery target (e.g., 95%, 98%, 99.5%)
- Real-time visibility requirement
- Exception handling protocol
- Performance-based penalty/reward structure

## 2.2 The Transportation Cost-Trade-off Matrix

Every tailoring decision involves a fundamental trade-off between **five competing objectives**:

$$
\text{Tailored Transport Objective} = f(\text{Cost}, \text{Speed}, \text{Reliability}, \text{Flexibility}, \text{Sustainability})
$$

The **efficient frontier** of transportation design is the set of solutions where no single objective can be improved without worsening another. A *tailored* solution explicitly picks a point on this frontier that aligns with the firm's competitive strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A cost-leadership strategy (e.g., Walmart) tailors for *minimum cost per case*. A differentiation strategy (e.g., Amazon Prime) tailors for *minimum transit time*. The *same physical product* therefore has *different tailored transportation designs* under different competitive strategies.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula that may appear in a KTU 2024 ESE question on Tailored Transportation. All formulas are mathematically verifiable and use standard logistics notation.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Freight Cost per Unit** | $C_u = \dfrac{C_t}{Q}$ | $C_u$ = cost per unit (₹/unit), $C_t$ = total freight cost (₹), $Q$ = shipment quantity (units) | Comparing carrier quotes on different shipment sizes |
| 2 | **Freight Cost per Ton-Km** | $C_{tk} = \dfrac{C_t}{W \times D}$ | $C_{tk}$ = cost per ton-km (₹/ton-km), $W$ = weight in tons, $D$ = distance in km | Mode comparison and benchmarking |
| 3 | **Total Transportation Cost (TTC)** | $TTC = C_{line\text{-}haul} + C_{loading} + C_{unloading} + C_{inventory\text{-}in\text{-}transit} + C_{admin}$ | All costs in ₹ | Full cost accounting for a tailored lane |
| 4 | **Inventory-in-Transit Cost** | $C_{IIT} = V \times p \times \dfrac{D}{S} \times i$ | $V$ = shipment value (₹), $p$ = proportion in transit, $D$ = demand (units/year), $S$ = order size, $i$ = holding cost rate (decimal) | Captures the *opportunity cost* of goods in transit |
| 5 | **Total Cost of a Transport Decision** | $TC = F + vQ + \dfrac{cV}{2} \cdot \dfrac{Q}{D}$ | $F$ = fixed cost, $v$ = variable cost per unit, $c$ = carrying cost, $V$ = annual volume | The classic *total cost curve* for transport-inventory trade-off |
| 6 | **Break-Even Distance for Mode Switch** | $D_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{fix,A} - C_{fix,B}}{c_{B} - c_{A}}$ | $C_{fix}$ = fixed cost of switching to mode, $c$ = variable cost per km of each mode | Decision: at what distance does rail beat road? |
| 7 | **Vehicle Utilization Ratio** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\text{Loaded km}}{\text{Total km driven}} \times 100\%$ | Loaded km vs. total km, expressed as % | Measures backhaul efficiency |
| 8 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %** | $OTD = \dfrac{\text{Deliveries on time}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \times 100\%$ | Counts or shipments, expressed as % | Carrier KPI in a TSLA |
| 9 | **Order Fill Rate** | $F_r = \dfrac{\text{Orders filled completely}}{\text{Total orders}} \times 100\%$ | Counts, expressed as % | Reliability measure |
| 10 | **Carbon Emission per Ton-Km** | $E = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ emitted (kg)}}{W \times D}$ | $CO_2$ in kg, $W$ in tons, $D$ in km | Sustainability-tailoring metric |
| 11 | **Consolidation Savings** | $S_c = n \cdot C_{LTL} - C_{TL}$ | $n$ = number of LTL shipments consolidated, $C_{LTL}$ = cost per LTL, $C_{TL}$ = cost of consolidated truckload | Justifies a tailored consolidation program |
| 12 | **Lead Time Variance** | $\sigma_{LT}^2 = \sigma_{transit}^2 + \sigma_{handling}^2 + \sigma_{customs}^2$ | Standard deviations in days | Used in safety stock for in-transit inventory |
| 13 | **Number of Vehicles Required (Milk Run)** | $N_v = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_{shift} - t_{setup}} \right\rceil$ | $t_i$ = stop time, $T_{shift}$ = shift length, $t_{setup}$ = depot load time | Sizing a tailored milk-run fleet |
| 14 | **Hub-and-Spoke Delay Factor** | $D_f = 1 + (h-1) \cdot \phi$ | $h$ = number of hubs, $\phi$ = transshipment delay factor (0 to 1) | Adjusts expected transit in hub designs |
| 15 | **Cost of Empty Miles** | $C_{empty} = D_{empty} \times c_{km} \times N_{trips}$ | Empty km × per-km cost × number of trips | Quantifies the cost of poor backhaul planning |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute-value, magnitude, and conditional separators inside the table above use semantic LaTeX commands (such as `\left\lceil` / `\right\rceil` and `\cdot`) instead of the raw pipe character `\vert`. This is to prevent markdown-table syntax corruption, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.4 The Tailored Transportation Design Process (KTU-Expected Logical Flow)

When a 14-mark question asks you to *"explain the design of a tailored transportation system,"* the following five-step logic is the *gold-standard* answer structure:

### Step 1 — Segment the Demand
Group shipments by product, customer, lane, and service level. **Why?** Because treating all freight as homogeneous is the antithesis of tailoring.

### Step 2 — Profile Each Segment
For every segment, record volume, weight, density, value, perishability, hazard class, required transit time, and order frequency. **How?** Using historical TMS (Transport Management System) data and customer interviews.

### Step 3 — Map Segment to Mode and Carrier
Match each segment's profile to the optimal mode + carrier combination using the **Total Cost of Ownership** framework, not just line-haul rate. **Why?** Because the cheapest quote often has hidden cost in reliability failures.

### Step 4 — Design the Network and Routes
Decide on direct vs. consolidated, dedicated vs. shared, and hub vs. point-to-point routing. **How?** Using optimization software (e.g., Oracle TMS, SAP TM, Llamasoft) for large networks and heuristics for smaller ones.

### Step 5 — Define the TSLA and Performance Loop
Codify service expectations, embed KPIs, build dashboards, and create continuous improvement rituals (monthly carrier reviews, quarterly RFPs). **Why?** Tailoring is not a one-time design — it is a *living* capability.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** This five-step process is the *exact* framework used by companies like Unilever, Nestlé, and Amazon when they redesign their Indian or ASEAN distribution networks. In a KTU viva or placement interview, narrating this process with a real brand example will significantly boost your score.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Question 1 — Mode Selection Using Break-Even Distance

### Problem Statement
> A KTU study case: A logistics manager in Kerala must choose between **road transport** and **intermodal (road + rail)** for a freight lane from Kochi to Delhi.
>
> Given:
> - Road transport: Fixed cost $C_{fix,R} = ₹15,000$ per shipment; variable cost $c_R = ₹2.50$ per ton-km.
> - Intermodal (road+rail): Fixed cost $C_{fix,I} = ₹45,000$ per shipment; variable cost $c_I = ₹1.20$ per ton-km.
> - Shipment weight $W = 10$ tons.
> - Distance $D = 2{,}400$ km.
>
> **Determine the break-even distance at which intermodal becomes cheaper than road.** Also compute total cost under each mode for the given shipment.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Establish the cost equation for each mode.**

Total cost of road transport for one shipment:

$$
C_R = C_{fix,R} + c_R \cdot W \cdot D
$$

Total cost of intermodal transport for one shipment:

$$
C_I = C_{fix,I} + c_I \cdot W \cdot D
$$

**Step 2 — Define the break-even condition.**

At the break-even distance $D_{BE}$, the total cost of both modes is equal:

$$
C_R = C_I
$$

$$
C_{fix,R} + c_R \cdot W \cdot D_{BE} = C_{fix,I} + c_I \cdot W \cdot D_{BE}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $D_{BE}$ algebraically.**

$$
C_{fix,R} - C_{fix,I} = c_I \cdot W \cdot D_{BE} - c_R \cdot W \cdot D_{BE}
$$

$$
C_{fix,R} - C_{fix,I} = W \cdot D_{BE} \cdot (c_I - c_R)
$$

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{fix,R} - C_{fix,I}}{W \cdot (c_I - c_R)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{15{,}000 - 45{,}000}{10 \cdot (1.20 - 2.50)}
$$

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{-30{,}000}{10 \cdot (-1.30)}
$$

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{-30{,}000}{-13.0}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{BE} = 2{,}307.69 \text{ km (approximately)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Insight:** Both numerator and denominator are negative because intermodal has *higher* fixed cost but *lower* variable cost. The negative ratio gives a *positive* break-even distance, which is mathematically correct. Students often make sign errors here — losing 2 marks.

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

Since the actual distance $D = 2{,}400$ km is **greater** than $D_{BE} = 2{,}307.69$ km, **intermodal transport is more economical** for this shipment. The variable-cost advantage of intermodal now outweighs its higher fixed cost.

**Step 6 — Verify by computing total costs.**

For Road:

$$
C_R = 15{,}000 + 2.50 \cdot 10 \cdot 2{,}400
$$

$$
C_R = 15{,}000 + 60{,}000 = ₹75{,}000
$$

For Intermodal:

$$
C_I = 45{,}000 + 1.20 \cdot 10 \cdot 2{,}400
$$

$$
C_I = 45{,}000 + 28{,}800 = ₹73{,}800
$$

**Savings by using intermodal:**

$$
\Delta C = C_R - C_I = 75{,}000 - 73{,}800 = ₹1{,}200
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Mark Allocation:**
> - Step 1 & 2 (Equations setup): **3 marks**
> - Step 3 (Algebraic isolation): **3 marks**
> - Step 4 (Numerical substitution and evaluation): **3 marks**
> - Step 5 & 6 (Interpretation and verification): **5 marks**
>
> Total: 14 marks. Note how interpretation carries 5 marks — KTU rewards **commercial understanding** over mere arithmetic.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Question 2 — Transportation-Inventory Total Cost Curve

### Problem Statement
> A KTU exam question (Part B, 14 marks) presents the following scenario:
>
> A FMCG company supplies a product from a single warehouse to a retail customer. The annual demand $D = 50{,}000$ units. The unit value $V = ₹200$. The holding cost rate $i = 18\%$ per year. The ordering cost (which is effectively the freight cost per order) is $S = ₹4{,}000$ per order. The freight cost per unit $c_f = ₹8$ per unit.
>
> **Derive the total cost function $TC(Q)$ as a function of order size $Q$, and find the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) that minimizes the total cost. Then compute the minimum total cost.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Decompose the total cost into its components.**

The total annual cost comprises three parts:
1. **Annual freight (transport) cost:** $\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S + c_f \cdot D$
2. **Annual inventory holding cost:** $\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot V \cdot i$
3. **Combined function:**

$$
TC(Q) = \underbrace{\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q}}_{\text{transport/ordering}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{Q \cdot V \cdot i}{2}}_{\text{holding}} + \underbrace{c_f \cdot D}_{\text{unit-freight, constant}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The third term $c_f \cdot D$ does not depend on $Q$ and therefore does not affect the optimal $Q$. We can ignore it for *minimization* but must include it when reporting the *total cost* figure.

**Step 2 — Differentiate $TC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$.**

$$
\dfrac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \dfrac{V \cdot i}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Set the derivative to zero for the optimum.**

$$
-\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \dfrac{V \cdot i}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q$.**

$$
\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} = \dfrac{V \cdot i}{2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{V \cdot i}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{V \cdot i}}
$$

**Step 5 — This is the classic Wilson EOQ formula, with $S$ representing the transport-driven ordering cost.**

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 50{,}000 \cdot 4{,}000}{200 \cdot 0.18}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400{,}000{,}000}{36}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{11{,}111{,}111.11}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^{*} \approx 3{,}333.33 \text{ units (per order)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the number of orders per year.**

$$
N = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{3{,}333.33} = 15 \text{ orders per year}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the minimum total cost.**

First, the variable part of TC:

$$
TC_{var}(Q^{*}) = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^{*}} + \dfrac{Q^{*} \cdot V \cdot i}{2}
$$

$$
TC_{var}(Q^{*}) = \dfrac{50{,}000 \cdot 4{,}000}{3{,}333.33} + \dfrac{3{,}333.33 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.18}{2}
$$

$$
TC_{var}(Q^{*}) = 60{,}000 + 60{,}000 = ₹1{,}20{,}000
$$

Then, the constant unit-freight cost:

$$
c_f \cdot D = 8 \cdot 50{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000
$$

Total minimum cost:

$$
\boxed{TC_{min} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}20{,}000}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transport-Inventory Trade-off Insight:** Notice that at the optimum, **transport cost = holding cost = ₹60,000 each**. This is the famous "intersection at optimum" property of the EOQ model. If a KTU question gives you a Q *not* at the EOQ and asks "is the system optimal?", you can answer by checking whether transport cost equals holding cost.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Suitability by Product Type

| Product / Service Profile | Tailored Mode | Rationale (Why) | Typical Lead Time | Cost Tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E-commerce parcels (<5 kg)** | Air + last-mile road | Speed and reach outweigh cost | 1–3 days | High |
| **Pharmaceuticals (cold-chain)** | Reefer road or reefer air | Temperature integrity is critical | 1–2 days | Premium |
| **Automotive assemblies (JIT)** | Dedicated road (milk run) | Sequence and zero-defect transit | 4–12 hours | High |
| **FMCG (mass consumption)** | Road + rail intermodal | Volume and cost balance | 2–4 days | Medium |
| **Bulk grain / cement** | Rail or pipeline | Economies of scale | 3–7 days | Low |
| **Crude oil / gases** | Pipeline | Continuous, low marginal cost | Continuous | Lowest per ton-km |
| **Apparel (seasonal peak)** | Road TL with backhaul | Flexibility to scale fleet | 2–5 days | Medium |
| **Electronics (high value, low weight)** | Air + secure road | Insurance and speed | 1–2 days | Premium |
| **E-commerce (same-day)** | Local dark stores + e-bike | Hyperlocal optimization | <4 hours | High per parcel |
| **Project cargo (ODC)** | Multi-modal (sea + road + rail) | Weight and dimension exceed single mode | 5–30 days | Custom quote |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Tailored Transportation Design Framework

The following Mermaid block presents a **block-level functional architecture flow** of how a tailored transportation solution is engineered from demand signal to continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[STEP 0 STRATEGIC INTENT] --> A1[Identify Competitive Priority Cost Speed Reliability Sustainability]
    A1 --> B0[STEP 1 DEMAND SEGMENTATION]
    B0 --> B1[Segment by Product Customer Lane Volume]
    B1 --> C0[STEP 2 SEGMENT PROFILING]
    C0 --> C1[Record Volume Weight Density Value Perishability Service Level]
    C1 --> D0[STEP 3 MODE SELECTION]
    D0 --> D1{Road Rail Air Sea Pipeline or Multimodal}
    D1 --> E0[STEP 4 CARRIER SELECTION]
    E0 --> E1[Asset Based Non Asset Based Dedicated Contract]
    E1 --> F0[STEP 5 NETWORK AND ROUTE DESIGN]
    F0 --> F1[Direct Milk Run Hub and Spoke Cross Dock]
    F1 --> G0[STEP 6 TSLA DEFINITION]
    G0 --> G1[Transit Window OTD Target Visibility Exception Protocol]
    G1 --> H0[STEP 7 PERFORMANCE LOOP]
    H0 --> H1[Dashboard Monthly Review Quarterly RFP Continuous Improvement]
    H1 --> A0
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — The Transportation Decision Tree

This tree maps a typical "which-mode-to-pick" decision the way a logistics manager would walk through it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[START Shipment Request Received] --> P1{Is shipment time critical less than 24 hours}
    P1 --> P1Y[YES] --> P1A[Choose AIR FREIGHT]
    P1 --> P1N[NO] --> P2{Is it international cross border}
    P2 --> P2Y[YES] --> P2A{Is volume a full container load}
    P2A --> P2AY[YES] --> P2A1[Choose SEA FREIGHT FCL]
    P2A --> P2AN[NO] --> P2A2[Choose SEA FREIGHT LCL or AIR if urgent]
    P2 --> P2N[NO] --> P3{Is shipment distance over 500 km and weight over 5 tons}
    P3 --> P3Y[YES] --> P3A{Is there a rail terminal within 50 km of origin and destination}
    P3A --> P3AY[YES] --> P3A1[Choose INTERMODAL Road plus Rail]
    P3A --> P3AN[NO] --> P3A2[Choose ROAD TRANSPORT TL or dedicated]
    P3 --> P3N[NO] --> P4[Choose ROAD TRANSPORT LTL or Parcel]
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — The Cost Service Trade-off (Efficient Frontier)

The following is a **conceptual representation** of the efficient frontier. Each curve is plotted in *cost-vs-service* space, with a low service level on the left and high service level on the right.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[LOW COST LOW SERVICE Standard LTL] --> Q2[MEDIUM COST MEDIUM SERVICE LTL with tracking] --> Q3[HIGH COST HIGH SERVICE Air freight with same day]
    Q2 -.-> R1[INEFFICIENT Wasteful Combination High cost low service]
    Q2 -.-> R2[INEFFICIENT Wasteful Combination Low cost high service Impossible]
    Q1 -.-> Q3
    style Q1 fill:#90ee90
    style Q2 fill:#ffd700
    style Q3 fill:#ff7f7f
    style R1 fill:#d3d3d3
    style R2 fill:#d3d3d3
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** The *green* node is the cost-leadership option. The *gold* node is the balance option. The *red* node is the service-leadership option. The *grey* nodes represent *inefficient* points — combinations that achieve neither cost nor service leadership. A well-tailored solution always lands on the **green-gold-red diagonal**, never in the grey zone.

### 4.4 Diagram D — Hub-and-Spoke vs. Direct Shipment

A network-level comparison showing how a tailored solution may pick different architectures for different lanes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hub and Spoke
        HS_O[ORIGIN Plant A] --> HS_H[HUB Central Warehouse]
        HS_H --> HS_D1[Customer 1]
        HS_H --> HS_D2[Customer 2]
        HS_H --> HS_D3[Customer 3]
    end
    subgraph Direct Shipment
        DS_O[ORIGIN Plant A] --> DS_D1[Customer 1]
        DS_O --> DS_D2[Customer 2]
        DS_O --> DS_D3[Customer 3]
    end
    subgraph Milk Run
        MR_O[ORIGIN Plant A] --> MR_D1[Customer 1]
        MR_D1 --> MR_D2[Customer 2]
        MR_D2 --> MR_D3[Customer 3]
        MR_D3 --> MR_O
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **When to use which (KTU-expected answer):**
> - **Hub-and-Spoke:** When demand points are geographically dispersed and volumes per lane are small.
> - **Direct Shipment:** When volume per lane is large and customer is willing to wait.
> - **Milk Run:** When customers are clustered and order frequencies are similar.
> - **Cross-Docking:** When products need to be re-sorted but not stored.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define "Tailored Transportation" in the context of supply chain management. Mention any two scenarios where it is preferred over generic freight movement.**

> **Model Answer (KTU Key Pattern):**
>
> **Definition:** Tailored Transportation is the strategic design of transportation services, modes, carriers, routes, and service-level agreements that are customized to the specific product, customer, lane, or business model requirements of a supply chain, rather than applying a one-size-fits-all freight solution.
>
> **Scenario 1 — Pharmaceutical Cold-Chain:** A pharmaceutical company shipping temperature-sensitive vaccines requires a tailored reefer solution with validated lanes, real-time temperature telemetry, and a GDP-compliant carrier. Generic dry-cargo trucking would risk product spoilage and regulatory failure.
>
> **Scenario 2 — E-commerce Same-Day Delivery:** A quick-commerce firm like Zepto or Blinkit must design a tailored hyperlocal delivery model using dark stores, e-bikes, and gig-rider networks, because the standard long-haul freight network cannot deliver within the 10–15 minute customer expectation.
>
> *(Word count: ~110 words. Time to write: 6 minutes. Marks breakdown: Definition 1.5, Scenarios 1.5.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Distinguish between Less-Than-Truckload (LTL) and Full-Truckload (FTL) transportation. When is LTL more cost-effective than FTL?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Dimension | LTL | FTL |
> |---|---|---|
> | Shipment size | Small to medium (typically < 12 tons) | Large (≥ 12 tons, full truck capacity) |
> | Cost structure | Per-unit or per-kg tariff, often with accessorial fees | Flat per-truck or per-km rate |
> | Handling | Multiple stops, multiple handling points | Single origin, single destination |
> | Transit time | Longer (consolidation adds days) | Faster (direct movement) |
> | Damage risk | Higher (more touch points) | Lower |
>
> **LTL is more cost-effective when:**
> 1. Shipment weight is **insufficient to fill a truck** (e.g., 2–8 tons).
> 2. The freight can be **consolidated** with other shippers' cargo on the same lane.
> 3. The customer can tolerate the **longer transit time** in exchange for lower freight cost.
> 4. The product is **not highly time-sensitive** and not excessively fragile.
>
> *(Word count: ~120 words. Time to write: 6 minutes.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions in Module 4 carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**A(a)** Explain the **five-step design process of a tailored transportation system** in supply chain management. (7 marks)

**A(b)** A logistics company must decide between **road TL (Truckload)** and **intermodal (road + rail)** for a freight lane of 1,500 km. The shipment weight is 8 tons. The road TL cost structure is: fixed cost ₹12,000 per shipment, variable cost ₹3.00 per ton-km. The intermodal cost structure is: fixed cost ₹38,000 per shipment, variable cost ₹1.50 per ton-km. **Compute the break-even distance** and recommend the appropriate mode. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1 — Segment the Demand (1 mark):** Group shipments by product type, customer, lane, volume, and service requirement. *Why:* To enable targeted design.
>
> **Step 2 — Profile Each Segment (1 mark):** Capture volume, weight, density, value, perishability, hazard class, and required lead time for each segment.
>
> **Step 3 — Map Segment to Mode and Carrier (2 marks):** Match each segment's profile to the optimal mode (road/rail/air/sea) and carrier (asset-based, non-asset, dedicated) using a Total Cost of Ownership framework, not just line-haul rate.
>
> **Step 4 — Design the Network and Routes (1.5 marks):** Choose between direct, milk run, hub-and-spoke, and cross-docking based on lane volume and geography.
>
> **Step 5 — Define the TSLA and Performance Loop (1.5 marks):** Codify service expectations, embed KPIs (OTD, fill rate, cost per ton-km), build dashboards, and operate continuous improvement through monthly reviews and quarterly RFPs.
>
> **Conclusion:** A tailored transportation system is a *living* capability, not a one-time design, and integrates with the firm's broader supply chain strategy.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write the total cost functions.**

$$
C_{TL} = C_{fix,TL} + c_{TL} \cdot W \cdot D
$$

$$
C_{IM} = C_{fix,IM} + c_{IM} \cdot W \cdot D
$$

**Step 2 — Set the break-even condition.**

$$
C_{fix,TL} + c_{TL} \cdot W \cdot D_{BE} = C_{fix,IM} + c_{IM} \cdot W \cdot D_{BE}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $D_{BE}$.**

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{fix,TL} - C_{fix,IM}}{W \cdot (c_{IM} - c_{TL})}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{12{,}000 - 38{,}000}{8 \cdot (1.50 - 3.00)}
$$

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{-26{,}000}{8 \cdot (-1.50)}
$$

$$
D_{BE} = \dfrac{-26{,}000}{-12.0} = 2{,}166.67 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with actual distance.**

Actual $D = 1{,}500$ km $< D_{BE} = 2{,}166.67$ km.

Therefore, **road TL is the recommended mode** for this shipment, because the distance is too short to recover the intermodal's higher fixed cost.

**Step 6 — Verify with total cost computation.**

$$
C_{TL} = 12{,}000 + 3.00 \cdot 8 \cdot 1{,}500 = 12{,}000 + 36{,}000 = ₹48{,}000
$$

$$
C_{IM} = 38{,}000 + 1.50 \cdot 8 \cdot 1{,}500 = 38{,}000 + 18{,}000 = ₹56{,}000
$$

Road TL is ₹8,000 cheaper — confirming the recommendation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Valuation Warning):** Students often forget to **include the sign** when calculating $D_{BE}$. The numerator and denominator are *both negative* (because intermodal has higher fixed cost but lower variable cost), and the result is *positive*. Marks are deducted if the negative ratio is reported as the answer without sign interpretation. Additionally, KTU penalizes students who skip the **interpretation** step (Step 5). Always state which mode is preferred and why.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**B(a)** Discuss the **role of 3PL and 4PL providers** in designing a tailored transportation solution. (7 marks)

**B(b)** The annual demand for a product is $D = 24{,}000$ units. The unit value is $V = ₹500$. The holding cost rate is $i = 20\%$ per year. The freight cost per order (which acts as the ordering cost $S$) is $₹6{,}000$. **Calculate the EOQ** that minimizes the total transport-inventory cost and verify that at EOQ, transport cost equals holding cost. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

> **3PL (Third-Party Logistics) Role (3.5 marks):**
> A 3PL provider executes transportation on behalf of the shipper. In a tailored solution, the 3PL's value lies in:
> 1. **Aggregated capacity** — Small and mid-sized shippers gain access to fleet and lane capacity they could not afford to own.
> 2. **Specialized expertise** — A 3PL focused on cold-chain or hazardous goods brings regulatory and operational know-how.
> 3. **Technology platform** — TMS, real-time visibility, and analytics are provided as a service.
> 4. **Scalability** — Seasonal peaks can be absorbed without long-term asset commitment.
>
> *Example:* DHL Supply Chain manages tailored cold-chain transportation for pharmaceutical clients in India.
>
> **4PL (Fourth-Party Logistics) Role (3.5 marks):**
> A 4PL is a *consultative, orchestrating* layer above the 3PLs. In a tailored solution, the 4PL's value lies in:
> 1. **Network design and strategy** — Designing the optimal transportation network rather than executing it.
> 2. **Multi-3PL coordination** — Managing a portfolio of 3PLs and integrating them.
> 3. **Continuous optimization** — Running data-driven design changes, RFPs, and KPI reviews.
> 4. **Strategic alignment** — Ensuring transportation strategy follows the firm's broader supply chain strategy.
>
> *Example:* Accenture's 4PL practice orchestrates tailored transportation for global FMCG majors.
>
> **Conclusion:** 3PLs execute, 4PLs orchestrate and optimize. In a truly tailored solution, both play complementary roles.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write the total cost function.**

$$
TC(Q) = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \dfrac{Q \cdot V \cdot i}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and set to zero.**

$$
\dfrac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \dfrac{V \cdot i}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the EOQ.**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{V \cdot i}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 24{,}000 \cdot 6{,}000}{500 \cdot 0.20}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{288{,}000{,}000}{100}} = \sqrt{2{,}880{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^{*} \approx 1{,}697.06 \text{ units per order}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute transport cost at EOQ.**

$$
TC_{transport} = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{24{,}000 \cdot 6{,}000}{1{,}697.06} \approx ₹84{,}852
$$

**Step 6 — Compute holding cost at EOQ.**

$$
TC_{holding} = \dfrac{Q^{*} \cdot V \cdot i}{2} = \dfrac{1{,}697.06 \cdot 500 \cdot 0.20}{2} \approx ₹84{,}853
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transport and holding costs are *virtually identical* (rounding causes a ₹1 difference). This confirms the EOQ's optimality property. (7 marks total)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Tailored Transportation Questions (Module 4)**
>
> 1. **Definition only, no segmentation** — A 7-mark question on "tailored transportation design" requires the *five-step process* with each step explained. A mere definition earns only 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the holding cost component** — In transport-inventory problems, students often compute only the transport cost. KTU explicitly tests the *total* cost integration. **Always** add the holding cost.
> 3. **Sign error in break-even calculation** — The negative-numerator / negative-denominator case is the most common error. State the sign logic explicitly.
> 4. **No interpretation of result** — A computed $D_{BE}$ is meaningless without stating: *"Since the actual distance is greater / less than $D_{BE}$, the recommended mode is..."*. This single line of interpretation is worth 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing LTL with parcel** — LTL is freight shipping; parcel is courier-grade. Mixing them up costs marks on carrier-type questions.
> 6. **Omitting the TSLA** — A tailored solution *must* end with a TSLA, KPIs, and a performance loop. Forgetting this loses the final 1.5–2 marks.
> 7. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — For your own answer sheets, always use $\lvert x \rvert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ to avoid formatting issues in any digital submission.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Tailored Transportation (Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Core Concept:** Tailored transportation is *not* a mode — it is a *design philosophy* that aligns mode, carrier, route, network, and service level to a specific product-customer-lane requirement.
>
> ✅ **Five-Step Design Process:** Segment demand → Profile segments → Map to mode + carrier → Design network + routes → Define TSLA + performance loop.
>
> ✅ **Five Competing Objectives:** Cost, Speed, Reliability, Flexibility, Sustainability. The *efficient frontier* defines what's achievable.
>
> ✅ **Mode Triggers:** Air = high-value + urgent. Road = door-to-door + medium range. Rail = bulk + long-haul + predictable lane. Sea = low-value + high-volume + international. Pipeline = continuous liquid/gas flow.
>
> ✅ **Carrier Types:** Asset-based (control), non-asset (flexibility), dedicated contract (exclusivity). The choice is part of the tailoring.
>
> ✅ **Network Archetypes:** Direct shipment, Milk run, Hub-and-spoke, Cross-docking — each fits a different demand pattern.
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
> - $C_{tk} = \dfrac{C_t}{W \cdot D}$ — cost per ton-km
> - $D_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{fix,A} - C_{fix,B}}{W \cdot (c_B - c_A)}$ — break-even distance
> - $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{V \cdot i}}$ — EOQ (Wilson)
> - $OTD = \dfrac{\text{On-time deliveries}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \times 100\%$ — reliability KPI
> - $C_{IIT} = V \cdot p \cdot \dfrac{D}{S} \cdot i$ — inventory-in-transit cost
>
> ✅ **TSLA Components:** Transit window, OTD target, visibility, exception handling, performance-based penalty/reward.
>
> ✅ **3PL vs. 4PL:** 3PL *executes*; 4PL *orchestrates and optimizes*. Both are valid in a tailored solution, often layered.
>
> ✅ **Sustainability Tailoring:** Carbon per ton-km, backhaul capture, electric fleet, empty-mile reduction are no longer optional in KTU 2024 Scheme answers.
>
> ✅ **Highest-Yield Exam Tip:** Always close your answer with a **strategic linkage** sentence — *"Thus, tailored transportation converts the transportation function from a cost center into a customer-differentiating capability, aligned with the firm's competitive priority."* This is a one-line **3-mark booster** that examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Sustainability in a Supply Chain; Sustainability Connected Supply Chain Drivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT867 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Role of Sustainability in a Supply Chain; Sustainability Connected Supply Chain Drivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainability in a Supply Chain (KTU 2024 Definition):** Sustainability in a supply chain refers to the strategic integration of **environmental stewardship, social responsibility, and economic viability** across all interconnected activities — from raw material sourcing, production, warehousing, transportation, and distribution, to end-of-life product management — ensuring that present-day operational demands are met **without compromising the ability of future generations** to meet their own needs (Brundtland Commission principle adapted to SCM).

The **World Commission on Environment and Development (WCED, 1987)** defines sustainable development as *"development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."* In a supply chain context, this means every node in the value network — suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers, customers, and reverse logistics partners — must operate in a way that balances the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**.

### The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — Elkington (1994)

| Pillar | Focus | Key Metric | Supply Chain Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **People (Social)** | Human rights, labor welfare, community impact | Employee safety index, fair-wage compliance | Ethical sourcing from Fair-Trade certified suppliers |
| **Planet (Environmental)** | Carbon footprint, waste, resource depletion | CO₂ emissions (tonnes/year), % renewable energy | Switching to electric delivery fleets |
| **Profit (Economic)** | Long-term financial viability | ROI on green initiatives, eco-cost savings | Reducing fuel costs via route optimization |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three-Legged Stool" Analogy:**
> Imagine a stool with three legs — **People, Planet, and Profit**. If any one leg is broken or too short, the stool (your supply chain) collapses. A supply chain that is **highly profitable but pollutes rivers** (Planet leg broken) will eventually face lawsuits, shutdowns, and loss of license. A supply chain that is **green but bankrupt** (Profit leg broken) cannot sustain its mission. True sustainability requires **all three legs to be strong, balanced, and equal in length**.

### Real-World Intuitive Example

Consider a **smartphone manufacturer** (e.g., a hypothetical Indian electronics company):
- **Without Sustainability:** Sources cobalt from conflict mines → social harm; dumps e-waste in landfills → environmental harm; but cheaper production → short-term profit. Long-term: brand damage, regulatory fines, supply disruption.
- **With Sustainability:** Sources cobalt from audited, conflict-free mines; runs take-back/recycling programs; uses solar-powered assembly plants. Slightly higher cost, but **lower risk, better brand equity, regulatory compliance, and long-term resilience**.

## 1.3 Why Sustainability Now? — KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasizes sustainability because modern supply chains operate in a **VUCA world** (Volatile, Uncertain, Complex, Ambiguous). Climate change regulations (e.g., **EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism - CBAM**), consumer awareness, and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) investing have made sustainability a **boardroom priority**, not a peripheral CSR activity.

**Key standards/metrics** used in KTU board examinations:
- **GRI (Global Reporting Initiative)** — sustainability reporting
- **ISO 14001** — environmental management systems
- **ISO 26000** — social responsibility
- **UN SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals)** — 17 global goals
- **GHG Protocol** — Scope 1, 2, 3 emissions accounting
- **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** — circular design philosophy
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Role of Sustainability in a Supply Chain

Sustainability is no longer a "nice-to-have" — it is a **strategic imperative** that affects every functional area of the supply chain. The role can be broken down into **five strategic pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Risk Mitigation and Resilience
- **Climate-related supply disruptions** (floods, droughts) can halt raw material supply.
- **Reputational risks** from unethical suppliers can destroy brand value overnight.
- Sustainable practices (supplier diversification, local sourcing, circular buffers) build **resilience**.

### Pillar 2: Cost Reduction and Operational Efficiency
- **Lean + Green = Efficient.** Reducing waste reduces cost.
- Energy-efficient warehouses (LED lighting, solar rooftops) cut electricity bills by **30–50%**.
- Optimized logistics routing reduces fuel consumption and emissions simultaneously.

### Pillar 3: Regulatory Compliance
- Carbon taxes, EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility), plastic bans.
- Non-compliance → fines, import bans, license revocation.

### Pillar 4: Brand Equity and Customer Loyalty
- **73% of millennials** (Nielsen global survey) willing to pay more for sustainable brands.
- Sustainability is a **competitive differentiator**.

### Pillar 5: Investor Confidence and Access to Capital
- ESG funds now exceed **$30 trillion** globally.
- Companies with high ESG ratings enjoy **lower cost of capital**.

## 2.2 Sustainability-Connected Supply Chain Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** *Supply chain sustainability drivers* are the **internal and external forces** that motivate a firm to adopt sustainable practices across its supply chain operations. These drivers translate abstract sustainability goals into **concrete operational decisions**.

The drivers are classified into **three interconnected categories**:

### A. Internal Drivers (Originating within the firm)

| Driver | Description | Operational Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost Reduction** | Lower energy, waste disposal, and raw material costs | Lean manufacturing, energy audits |
| **Risk Management** | Avoiding supply disruption from climate/regulatory shocks | Supplier diversification, dual sourcing |
| **Brand Value & Reputation** | Building green brand image | Sustainability marketing, eco-labels |
| **Innovation Pressure** | Need for new eco-friendly products/processes | Green R&D, biomaterials |
| **Top Management Commitment** | CEO/CXO-level sustainability vision | ESG-linked executive bonuses |

### B. External Drivers (Originating outside the firm)

| Driver | Description | Operational Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Government Regulations** | Carbon tax, EPR, pollution control norms | Compliance audits, reporting |
| **Customer/Consumer Pressure** | Demand for green products | Eco-labeling, transparency |
| **Investor/Shareholder Pressure** | ESG-linked investing | Sustainability reports (GRI/TCFD) |
| **NGO & Media Activism** | Public campaigns against polluting firms | Supplier code of conduct |
| **Industry Peer Pressure** | Competitors adopting green practices | Industry coalitions, pacts |

### C. Performance Drivers (TBL-based outcomes)

| Driver | Dimension | Example Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Environmental Drivers** | Planet | CO₂ reduction %, renewable energy %, water reuse % |
| **Social Drivers** | People | Worker safety index, supplier audit pass %, community spend |
| **Economic Drivers** | Profit | Eco-cost savings, green revenue %, ROI on green capex |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE for PESFT867. Memorize the formulas, units, and interpretations.

| # | Metric / Formula | Equation | Unit | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Carbon Footprint (CF)** | $CF = AD \times EF$ | tonnes CO₂e | $AD$ = Activity Data (e.g., liters of fuel), $EF$ = Emission Factor (kg CO₂e/liter) |
| 2 | **CO₂ Emissions from Transport** | $E_t = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \times w_i \times EF_i$ | tonnes CO₂e | $d_i$ = distance, $w_i$ = weight, $EF_i$ = emission factor per ton-km |
| 3 | **Eco-Efficiency (EE)** | $EE = \dfrac{\text{Product/Service Value}}{\text{Environmental Impact}}$ | dimensionless (or ₹/kg CO₂) | Higher EE = more value per unit environmental harm |
| 4 | **Sustainability Index (SI)** | $SI = w_1 P + w_2 E + w_3 S$ where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ | index (0 to 1) | Weighted score: $P$ = Profit, $E$ = Environment, $S$ = Social KPIs normalized |
| 5 | **Reverse Logistics Recovery Rate** | $RR = \dfrac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{sold}} \times 100$ | % | $Q_{recovered}$ = quantity returned/recycled, $Q_{sold}$ = quantity sold |
| 6 | **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Used (MJ)}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | MJ/unit | Lower is better |
| 7 | **Water Footprint** | $WF = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | m³/product | Blue = surface/ground, Green = rain, Grey = polluted water |
| 8 | **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) in CO₂e** | $CO_2e = \sum_g (Mass_g \times GWP_g)$ | tonnes CO₂e | $GWP$ = Global Warming Potential (CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265 over 100 years) |
| 9 | **Return on Sustainability Investment (ROSI)** | $ROSI = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit of Green Initiative}}{\text{Total Green Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Quantifies financial gain from sustainability projects |
| 10 | **% Renewable Energy Use** | $\%RE = \dfrac{E_{renewable}}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Share of clean energy in total energy mix |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics managers** use Carbon Footprint (CF) formulas to select low-emission transport modes (rail vs road vs air).
- **Warehouse designers** use Energy Intensity (EI) to compare solar vs grid power alternatives.
- **Procurement officers** use Sustainability Index (SI) to score and rank suppliers.
- **Finance teams** use ROSI to justify green capex to the board.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **worked problems** are typical KTU ESE numerical/narrative questions. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no skips.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Carbon Footprint of a Logistics Network

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A company operates a fleet of 40 diesel trucks. Each truck consumes **15 liters of diesel per 100 km** and runs an average of **120 km/day** for **300 days/year**. The emission factor of diesel is **2.68 kg CO₂e/liter**. Calculate the **total annual carbon footprint** of the fleet in tonnes CO₂e.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters**
- Number of trucks, $N = 40$
- Fuel consumption rate, $C = 15$ L per 100 km
- Average daily distance, $D_d = 120$ km/day
- Operating days, $T = 300$ days/year
- Emission factor, $EF = 2.68$ kg CO₂e/L

**Step 2: Calculate daily fuel consumption per truck**
Daily fuel per truck is calculated by converting the consumption rate to a per-km value and multiplying by the daily distance.

$$
\text{Fuel per truck per day} = \frac{C}{100} \times D_d
$$

$$
\text{Fuel per truck per day} = \frac{15}{100} \times 120 = 18 \text{ L/day}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate annual fuel consumption per truck**
Multiply daily fuel by the number of operating days in a year.

$$
\text{Fuel per truck per year} = 18 \times 300 = 5{,}400 \text{ L/year}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate annual fuel consumption for the entire fleet**
Multiply the per-truck annual consumption by the total number of trucks.

$$
\text{Total fleet fuel} = 5{,}400 \times 40 = 216{,}000 \text{ L/year}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Carbon Footprint formula**
The carbon footprint is the product of activity data (fuel used) and the emission factor.

$$
CF = AD \times EF
$$

$$
CF = 216{,}000 \text{ L} \times 2.68 \text{ kg CO₂e/L} = 578{,}880 \text{ kg CO₂e}
$$

**Step 6: Convert kilograms to tonnes**
Since 1 tonne = 1000 kg, divide the result by 1000 to get tonnes.

$$
CF = \frac{578{,}880}{1{,}000} = 578.88 \text{ tonnes CO₂e/year}
$$

**Final Answer:** The total annual carbon footprint of the fleet is **578.88 tonnes CO₂e/year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sustainability Recommendation:** If the firm switches to **electric vehicles** (EF ≈ 0.05 kg CO₂e/kWh, with grid mix), the CF could drop by **60–80%**, simultaneously reducing fuel cost and ESG exposure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Sustainability Index (SI) Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A firm has three sustainability KPIs (each normalized to a 0–1 scale):
- Economic (Profit) score: $P = 0.80$
- Environmental score: $E = 0.65$
- Social score: $S = 0.75$

The board assigns weights: $w_1 = 0.5$ (Profit), $w_2 = 0.3$ (Environment), $w_3 = 0.2$ (Social). Calculate the **Sustainability Index (SI)** and interpret it.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Verify weights sum to 1**
The Sustainability Index formula requires the weights to sum to 1, which acts as a sanity check on the weight assignment.

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Apply the SI formula**
The Sustainability Index is the weighted sum of the three normalized KPI scores, where each score is multiplied by its assigned weight.

$$
SI = w_1 P + w_2 E + w_3 S
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the given values**
Plug in the normalized KPI scores and their respective weights into the SI formula.

$$
SI = (0.5 \times 0.80) + (0.3 \times 0.65) + (0.2 \times 0.75)
$$

**Step 4: Compute each weighted contribution**
Calculate the contribution of each dimension to the overall sustainability index.

$$
SI = 0.400 + 0.195 + 0.150
$$

**Step 5: Sum the weighted contributions**
Add all three weighted contributions to obtain the final Sustainability Index value.

$$
SI = 0.745
$$

**Final Answer:** $SI = 0.745$ (or **74.5%**).

**Interpretation:** A Sustainability Index of **0.745** indicates a **good** sustainability performance, with the **economic dimension being the strongest** (0.80) and the **environmental dimension being the weakest** (0.65). The firm should prioritize **green initiatives** (renewable energy, waste reduction) to improve its weakest pillar.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Eco-Efficiency Comparison

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A factory produces **50,000 units/year**. Under **Process A**, it uses **2.5 MJ/unit** of energy and generates **₹800/unit** of value. Under **Process B** (green process), it uses **1.6 MJ/unit** and generates **₹780/unit** of value. Compare the **Eco-Efficiency (EE)** of both processes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the Eco-Efficiency formula**
Eco-Efficiency is defined as the ratio of product/service value generated to the environmental impact incurred. A higher EE value indicates a more sustainable process.

$$
EE = \frac{\text{Value}}{\text{Environmental Impact}} = \frac{\text{₹/unit}}{\text{MJ/unit}}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate EE for Process A (conventional)**
Substitute the value and energy intensity values of Process A into the EE formula.

$$
EE_A = \frac{800}{2.5} = 320 \text{ ₹/MJ}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate EE for Process B (green)**
Substitute the value and energy intensity values of Process B into the EE formula.

$$
EE_B = \frac{780}{1.6} = 487.5 \text{ ₹/MJ}
$$

**Step 4: Compare the two Eco-Efficiency values**
Since Process B generates more value per unit of energy consumed, it is the more eco-efficient option despite slightly lower per-unit value.

$$
EE_B > EE_A \implies 487.5 > 320
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the percentage improvement in Eco-Efficiency**
The percentage improvement quantifies how much more eco-efficient Process B is compared to Process A.

$$
\%\text{Improvement} = \frac{EE_B - EE_A}{EE_A} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Improvement} = \frac{487.5 - 320}{320} \times 100 = 52.34\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Process B is **52.34% more eco-efficient** than Process A. Even though it generates **₹20 less per unit**, it consumes **36% less energy**, making it the **sustainable choice**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — Structural Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **Three Pillars of Sustainable Supply Chain Management** and their interconnected drivers. Each pillar is a sub-system, and the arrows show the interdependencies — a change in one pillar directly affects the other two.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["SUSTAINABLE SUPPLY CHAIN"] --> B["PEOPLE - SOCIAL PILLAR"]
    A --> C["PLANET - ENVIRONMENTAL PILLAR"]
    A --> D["PROFIT - ECONOMIC PILLAR"]

    B --> B1["Fair Labor Practices"]
    B --> B2["Worker Safety"]
    B --> B3["Community Welfare"]
    B --> B4["Human Rights Compliance"]

    C --> C1["Carbon Footprint Reduction"]
    C --> C2["Waste Minimization"]
    C --> C3["Renewable Energy Use"]
    C --> C4["Water Conservation"]

    D --> D1["Cost Optimization"]
    D --> D2["Green Revenue Streams"]
    D --> D3["ROI on Sustainability"]
    D --> D4["Long-Term Viability"]

    B1 -.-> C1
    C2 -.-> D1
    D2 -.-> B3
    B2 -.-> D3
    C3 -.-> D4
    B3 -.-> C1
```

## 4.2 Internal vs External Sustainability Drivers — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INT["INTERNAL DRIVERS ORIGINATING WITHIN THE FIRM"]
        I1["Cost Reduction Pressure"]
        I2["Risk Management Needs"]
        I3["Brand Value Enhancement"]
        I4["Top Management Vision"]
        I5["Innovation in Green Tech"]
    end

    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL DRIVERS ORIGINATING OUTSIDE THE FIRM"]
        E1["Government Regulations"]
        E2["Consumer Green Demand"]
        E3["Investor ESG Pressure"]
        E4["NGO and Media Activism"]
        E5["Industry Peer Standards"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["SUSTAINABLE SUPPLY CHAIN OUTCOMES"]
        O1["Lower Carbon Footprint"]
        O2["Higher Brand Equity"]
        O3["Regulatory Compliance"]
        O4["Resilient Operations"]
        O5["Long-Term Profitability"]
    end

    I1 --> O1
    I2 --> O4
    I3 --> O2
    I4 --> O3
    I5 --> O1
    E1 --> O3
    E2 --> O2
    E3 --> O4
    E4 --> O2
    E5 --> O1

    O1 --> SC["SUSTAINABLE COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE"]
    O2 --> SC
    O3 --> SC
    O4 --> SC
    O5 --> SC
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop (Circular) Sustainable Supply Chain — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["RAW MATERIAL SUSTAINABLE SOURCING"] --> P1["GREEN MANUFACTURING - CLEAN ENERGY"]
    P1 --> D1["ECO-FRIENDLY DISTRIBUTION - LOW EMISSION TRANSPORT"]
    D1 --> U1["CONSUMER USE - PRODUCT LONGEVITY"]
    U1 --> R1["REVERSE LOGISTICS - PRODUCT TAKE BACK"]
    R1 --> RP1["REFURBISH OR REMANUFACTURE"]
    R1 --> RC1["RECYCLE MATERIALS"]
    R1 --> DS1["SAFE DISPOSAL OR INCINERATION WITH ENERGY RECOVERY"]
    RP1 --> P1
    RC1 --> S1
    DS1 --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The closed-loop model ensures that materials **never become waste** — they are continuously cycled back as inputs. This is the **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** philosophy. Each arrow represents a real supply chain function: sourcing → production → logistics → consumption → return → reprocessing.
