# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **confuse "drivers" with "barriers"** of sustainability. Drivers = forces that **push** toward sustainability; Barriers = forces that **resist** it. Do not mix them.
> - When asked to "explain the role of sustainability," **always structure your answer around the TBL (People, Planet, Profit)** — examiners award marks for systematic coverage of all three pillars.
> - For numerical problems, **always show units and final unit conversion** (e.g., kg → tonnes). Marks are specifically reserved for unit consistency in the final step.
> - Do **not** write essays without examples. A 14-mark question **demands diagrams, real-world case studies, and quantitative justification**.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**Define the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) concept in the context of sustainable supply chain management. List the three pillars with one supply chain example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**, introduced by **John Elkington in 1994**, is a sustainability framework that measures an organization's performance across three interconnected dimensions:

1. **People (Social Pillar):** Focuses on the welfare of workers, communities, and society at large. *Supply chain example:* Sourcing raw materials from **Fair-Trade certified suppliers** to ensure ethical labor practices.

2. **Planet (Environmental Pillar):** Focuses on minimizing the ecological footprint — reducing emissions, waste, and resource depletion. *Supply chain example:* Using **electric delivery vehicles** in last-mile logistics to cut carbon emissions.

3. **Profit (Economic Pillar):** Focuses on ensuring long-term financial viability and value creation. *Supply chain example:* Investing in **solar-powered warehouses** to reduce long-term electricity costs and improve ROI.

The TBL principle states that a supply chain is truly sustainable **only when all three pillars are simultaneously satisfied** — economic growth should not come at the cost of social inequity or environmental degradation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**List and briefly explain any FOUR internal drivers of sustainability in a supply chain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four key internal drivers of supply chain sustainability are:

1. **Cost Reduction:** Sustainable practices such as energy-efficient operations, waste minimization, and lean logistics directly reduce operational costs (e.g., LED lighting in warehouses cuts electricity bills).

2. **Risk Management:** Climate change, resource scarcity, and reputational scandals can disrupt supply chains. Adopting sustainable practices (diversified sourcing, ethical audits) builds **resilience** against such shocks.

3. **Brand Value and Reputation:** Modern consumers prefer eco-conscious brands. Sustainability investments enhance **brand equity** and customer loyalty, translating into higher market share.

4. **Top Management Commitment:** When CXOs and board members champion sustainability, it cascades down to operational decisions — ESG-linked executive bonuses, sustainability KPIs in performance reviews, etc.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**(a)** Discuss the **role of sustainability** in modern supply chain management. Explain with reference to the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a **closed-loop (circular) supply chain**. How does it differ from a traditional linear supply chain? Illustrate with a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Sustainability has evolved from a peripheral CSR activity to a **core strategic priority** in modern supply chain management. Driven by climate change, regulatory pressure, and consumer awareness, firms are now integrating environmental, social, and economic goals into every supply chain function.

**Role 1 — Risk Mitigation and Resilience (1.5 marks):**
Climate-related disruptions (floods, droughts) and reputational risks (supplier labor scandals) can cripple supply chains. Sustainable practices — supplier diversification, local sourcing, ethical audits — build **resilience** and ensure business continuity.

**Role 2 — Cost Reduction and Efficiency (1.5 marks):**
The synergy of **Lean (waste reduction) and Green (environmental care)** drives operational efficiency. Examples: route optimization cuts fuel costs; energy-efficient warehouses cut electricity bills by 30–50%; reverse logistics recovers value from used products.

**Role 3 — Regulatory Compliance and Market Access (1 mark):**
Compliance with carbon taxes, EPR laws, and ESG reporting is mandatory for market access, especially in the EU and developed markets.

**Role 4 — Brand Equity, Customer Loyalty, and Investor Confidence (1 mark):**
Sustainability differentiates brands, attracts ESG-conscious investors, and unlocks access to green capital at lower interest rates.

**TBL Conclusion (1 mark):**
The **Triple Bottom Line (People, Planet, Profit)** framework ensures that supply chain decisions are evaluated holistically, balancing **social equity, environmental integrity, and economic prosperity**. A truly sustainable supply chain is one where no pillar is sacrificed for another.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Definition of Closed-Loop Supply Chain (1.5 marks):**
A **closed-loop supply chain (CLSC)**, also called a **circular supply chain**, is a system in which products and materials are **continuously cycled back** into the supply chain after their useful life through **reverse logistics** — for reuse, refurbishment, remanufacturing, or recycling. It embodies the **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** philosophy, where waste from one process becomes the input for another.

**Key Activities in CLSC (2 marks):**
1. **Product Take-Back:** Manufacturers collect end-of-life products from customers.
2. **Reverse Logistics:** Transportation of used products back to collection/inspection centers.
3. **Sorting & Inspection:** Products are graded for refurbishment, remanufacturing, or recycling.
4. **Value Recovery:** Refurbished products are resold; materials are recycled into raw inputs.

**Linear vs Closed-Loop Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Supply Chain | Closed-Loop Supply Chain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Flow Direction** | One-way (forward only) | Circular (forward + reverse) |
| **End-of-Life** | Waste/landfill | Reuse, recycle, remanufacture |
| **Resource Model** | Take → Make → Dispose | Take → Make → Use → Return → Remake |
| **Environmental Impact** | High waste, high emissions | Low waste, low emissions |
| **Value Recovery** | None | Significant (refurbished goods, recycled materials) |
| **Philosophy** | Cradle-to-Grave | Cradle-to-Cradle |

**Real-World Example (1.5 marks):**
**Apple Inc.'s iPhone recycling program** — Apple offers trade-in for old iPhones, which are then refurbished and resold in emerging markets, or disassembled to recover gold, cobalt, and rare earth metals for use in new products. This reduces mining demand, cuts e-waste, and creates a new revenue stream, exemplifying the **circular economy** in action.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]
**(a)** Classify and explain the **internal and external drivers** of sustainability in a supply chain. Give at least **two examples** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **environmental and social drivers** of sustainability with suitable supply chain examples. How are these measured using **key performance indicators (KPIs)**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Supply chain sustainability drivers are the **forces that motivate** firms to adopt sustainable practices. They are classified into **Internal Drivers** (originating within the firm) and **External Drivers** (originating from the external environment).

**Internal Drivers (3 marks):**
1. **Cost Reduction:** Sustainable practices (energy efficiency, waste minimization, lean logistics) directly cut operational costs. *Example:* Installing solar panels on warehouse rooftops reduces electricity expenses by 30–50% over 5 years.
2. **Risk Management:** Proactive sustainability reduces exposure to climate, regulatory, and reputational risks. *Example:* Diversifying suppliers across geographies to avoid climate-driven disruptions.
3. **Brand Value and Reputation:** Sustainability differentiates the firm and builds customer loyalty. *Example:* Patagonia's "Don't Buy This Jacket" campaign built strong brand equity among eco-conscious consumers.
4. **Top Management Commitment:** CEO-level vision cascades down to operations. *Example:* Unilever's Sustainable Living Plan, led by the CEO, embedded sustainability into every supply chain decision.
5. **Innovation Pressure:** Sustainability drives R&D in green materials, circular design, and clean tech.

**External Drivers (3 marks):**
1. **Government Regulations:** Carbon tax, EPR, plastic bans, emissions caps. *Example:* The EU's **Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)** imposes a carbon levy on imported goods, forcing firms to reduce Scope 3 emissions.
2. **Consumer/Societal Pressure:** Modern consumers demand transparency and ethical sourcing. *Example:* The boycott of brands using conflict minerals has reshaped electronics supply chains.
3. **Investor/Shareholder Pressure:** ESG-linked investing and green bonds. *Example:* BlackRock's CEO Larry Fink has mandated ESG integration in all portfolio decisions.
4. **NGO and Media Activism:** Public campaigns expose unsustainable practices. *Example:* Greenpeace's campaign against palm oil deforestation pushed global FMCG firms to adopt RSPO-certified palm oil.
5. **Industry Peer Pressure:** Firms adopt best practices to remain competitive. *Example:* The **Race to Zero** campaign by the UN has united thousands of firms in committing to net-zero supply chains.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Environmental Drivers (3 marks):**
Environmental drivers relate to the **Planet** pillar of TBL — they motivate firms to reduce their ecological footprint.

1. **Climate Change and Carbon Emissions:** Need to reduce **Scope 1, 2, 3 GHG emissions** across the supply chain. *Example:* Walmart's **Project Gigaton** aims to avoid 1 gigaton of CO₂ emissions across its global supply chain by 2030.
2. **Resource Scarcity and Depletion:** Finite raw materials (rare earths, water, fossil fuels) force circular thinking. *Example:* Adidas's **Parley shoes** use recycled ocean plastic, reducing dependence on virgin polyester.
3. **Waste Generation and Pollution:** Plastic waste, e-waste, and industrial effluents. *Example:* HP's **closed-loop ink cartridge** program uses 65% recycled plastic in new cartridges.

**Environmental KPIs (1 mark):**
- Carbon footprint (tonnes CO₂e/year)
- % renewable energy use
- Water footprint (m³/unit produced)
- Waste diversion rate from landfill (%)
- Energy intensity (MJ/unit produced)

**Social Drivers (2 marks):**
Social drivers relate to the **People** pillar of TBL — they motivate firms to protect human welfare and community interests.

1. **Labor Rights and Worker Safety:** *Example:* Apple's **Supplier Responsibility Reports** audit every supplier factory for fair wages, working hours, and safety conditions.
2. **Community Welfare and Human Rights:** *Example:* The **Fair-Trade certification** ensures coffee/farmers in developing nations receive fair prices and community development premiums.
3. **Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI):** *Example:* Many global firms now mandate supplier diversity programs, sourcing from women-owned and minority-owned businesses.

**Social KPIs (1 mark):**
- Worker safety index (LTIFR — Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate)
- % suppliers audited for labor compliance
- Community investment (₹/year)
- Employee training hours on sustainability
- Supplier diversity ratio (%)

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Both environmental and social drivers are essential for **long-term supply chain resilience** and are increasingly mandated by **regulations, investors, and customers**. Firms that measure these drivers with **quantitative KPIs** can track progress, communicate transparently, and continuously improve their sustainability performance.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Module 4**

- ✅ **Sustainability** in SCM = integrating environmental, social, and economic goals into all supply chain functions.
- ✅ **Brundtland Commission (1987)** — defines sustainable development (use this in definitions).
- ✅ **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — coined by **John Elkington (1994)** — three pillars: **People, Planet, Profit**.
- ✅ The three pillars are **interdependent** — weakening one collapses the others.
- ✅ **Closed-loop supply chain** = Cradle-to-Cradle; uses **reverse logistics** for value recovery.
- ✅ **Drivers of sustainability** = forces that **push** a firm toward sustainable practices.
- ✅ **Internal drivers:** Cost, Risk, Brand, Top Management, Innovation.
- ✅ **External drivers:** Regulation, Consumer, Investor, NGO, Industry Peers.
- ✅ **Environmental drivers** relate to **Planet** — emissions, waste, energy, water.
- ✅ **Social drivers** relate to **People** — labor, safety, community, human rights.
- ✅ **Economic drivers** relate to **Profit** — cost savings, ROI, green revenue.
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $CF = AD \times EF$
  - $EE = \dfrac{\text{Value}}{\text{Environmental Impact}}$
  - $SI = w_1 P + w_2 E + w_3 S$ (weights sum to 1)
  - $ROSI = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Green Investment}} \times 100$
  - $CO_2e = \sum (Mass \times GWP)$
- ✅ **Key standards:** GRI, ISO 14001, ISO 26000, GHG Protocol, UN SDGs, CBAM, EPR.
- ✅ **GWP values to remember:** CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265 (100-year horizon).
- ✅ Always show **units** in numerical answers and **convert** to standard units (kg → tonnes).
- ✅ Always use **diagrams** (TBL, drivers, closed-loop) in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Real-world examples** (Apple, Walmart, Patagonia, Adidas) score extra marks in KTU valuation.
- ✅ The TBL framework is the **most-tested concept** in this module — practice drawing it.
- ✅ **Closed-loop vs linear** is a high-frequency comparison question.
- ✅ Remember: **Drivers push toward sustainability; Barriers resist it** — don't confuse the two.
